name: Human Anatomy
description: Comprehensive human anatomy deck — 17 subdecks covering all body systems, clinical anatomy, embryology, regional anatomy, histology, and 129 visual image-based quizzes. Built for medical students with encyclopedic gross anatomy coverage.

facts.quizQuestion: How many chambers does the human heart have?
facts.explanation: The heart has four chambers: the right atrium and right ventricle on one side, and the left atrium and left ventricle on the other. This double-pump design keeps oxygenated and deoxygenated blood separated.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section diagram of heart showing four labeled chambers

variants.quizQuestion: The heart is divided into how many distinct chambers?
variants.explanation: Two atria (upper receiving chambers) and two ventricles (lower pumping chambers) make up the heart's four-chamber structure.

facts.quizQuestion: Which heart chamber has the thickest muscular wall?
facts.explanation: The left ventricle has the thickest wall because it must generate enough pressure to pump oxygenated blood throughout the entire body — a far greater workload than pumping blood only to the nearby lungs.
facts.visualDescription: Heart cross-section with left ventricle wall highlighted as thickest

variants.quizQuestion: Which chamber pumps oxygenated blood out to the body and has the thickest wall?
variants.explanation: The left ventricle drives systemic circulation and must overcome the high resistance of the entire body's vasculature, requiring a very thick myocardium.

facts.quizQuestion: Which heart chamber receives deoxygenated blood from the superior and inferior vena cava?
facts.explanation: The right atrium collects oxygen-depleted blood returning from the body via the superior vena cava (upper body) and inferior vena cava (lower body), then passes it to the right ventricle.
facts.visualDescription: Heart diagram with SVC and IVC arrows pointing into right atrium

variants.quizQuestion: The superior vena cava and inferior vena cava both drain into which heart chamber?
variants.explanation: Both venae cavae empty deoxygenated blood into the right atrium, the entry point of systemic venous return.

facts.quizQuestion: Which heart chamber receives oxygenated blood from the four pulmonary veins?
facts.explanation: After gas exchange in the lungs, oxygenated blood returns via four pulmonary veins — two from each lung — draining into the left atrium before flowing to the left ventricle.
facts.visualDescription: Heart diagram with four pulmonary vein arrows feeding into left atrium

facts.quizQuestion: Which valve with three cusps controls blood flow from the right atrium to the right ventricle?
facts.explanation: The tricuspid valve (tri = three) sits between the right atrium and right ventricle. Its three leaflets are anchored by chordae tendineae to papillary muscles, preventing the valve from inverting during ventricular contraction.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of tricuspid valve with three cusps labeled between RA and RV

variants.quizQuestion: The right atrioventricular valve is also called the _____ valve.
variants.explanation: The right AV valve has three cusps, earning it the name 'tricuspid.' The left AV valve has two cusps and is called the mitral (or bicuspid) valve.

facts.quizQuestion: Which two-cusped AV valve guards the opening between the left atrium and left ventricle?
facts.explanation: The mitral valve (also called the bicuspid valve) has just two leaflets — unlike the three-leaflet tricuspid on the right side. Its shape resembles a bishop's mitre, which is how it got its name. Like all AV valves, it has chordae tendineae.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of mitral valve with two cusps between LA and LV

variants.quizQuestion: The left atrioventricular valve is also known as the bicuspid or _____ valve.
variants.explanation: The mitral valve is the only AV valve with two cusps. It sits between the left atrium and left ventricle and is the most commonly diseased valve in rheumatic heart disease.

facts.quizQuestion: What fibrous cord structures anchor the AV valve leaflets to the papillary muscles, preventing prolapse?
facts.explanation: Chordae tendineae — literally 'tendinous cords' — are tough fibrous strings that run from the papillary muscles to the edges of the tricuspid and mitral valve leaflets. They act like parachute strings, keeping the valves from flipping backward into the atria during systole.
facts.visualDescription: Close-up of mitral valve showing chordae tendineae as cord-like attachments

variants.quizQuestion: AV valves are prevented from prolapsing back into the atria by papillary muscles and _____ tendineae.
variants.explanation: Chordae tendineae are the fibrous cords that tether AV valve leaflets. Semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary) have no chordae — they open and close solely by blood pressure changes.

facts.quizQuestion: Which valves open by blood pressure alone and have no chordae tendineae?
facts.explanation: The semilunar valves — the pulmonary valve (RV to pulmonary trunk) and aortic valve (LV to aorta) — each have three half-moon-shaped cusps. They are pushed open by ventricular pressure and snap shut when pressure drops at the end of systole. No chordae needed.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of aortic and pulmonary semilunar valves with three half-moon cusps

facts.quizQuestion: The closing of the AV valves at the start of systole produces which heart sound?
facts.explanation: S1 — the 'lub' — occurs when the mitral and tricuspid valves snap shut as ventricular pressure rises at the beginning of systole. S2 ('dub') follows when the semilunar valves close at the end of systole.
facts.visualDescription: Phonocardiogram wave labeled S1 and S2 with systole and diastole phases

variants.quizQuestion: In the 'lub-dub' of the heartbeat, what does the 'lub' (S1) represent?
variants.explanation: S1 marks the beginning of systole when the mitral and tricuspid valves close. The 'dub' (S2) marks the end of systole when the aortic and pulmonary valves close.

facts.quizQuestion: Which heart sound is produced by the closing of the semilunar valves, marking the beginning of diastole?
facts.explanation: S2 — the 'dub' — is produced when the aortic and pulmonary valves snap shut as ventricular pressure falls below aortic/pulmonary pressure at the end of systole. This is the start of diastole, when ventricles begin to relax and refill.
facts.visualDescription: Cardiac cycle diagram with S2 marked at semilunar valve closure

facts.quizQuestion: Which coronary artery is nicknamed the 'widow maker' because its occlusion is the most lethal?
facts.explanation: The LAD (left anterior descending artery) supplies the anterior left ventricle and anterior interventricular septum — the bulk of the heart's pumping muscle. Blockage here causes a massive anterior MI with very high mortality, earning its grim nickname.
facts.visualDescription: Heart diagram with LAD highlighted descending on anterior surface

variants.quizQuestion: The LAD (left anterior descending) artery supplies the anterior left ventricle and anterior interventricular _____ .
variants.explanation: The LAD travels down the anterior interventricular groove, supplying the anterior wall of the LV and the anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum. Its occlusion is called the 'widow maker' MI.

facts.quizQuestion: Which coronary artery branches into the LAD and the left circumflex artery?
facts.explanation: The left main coronary artery is a short but critical vessel that quickly divides into the LAD (supplying the anterior heart) and the left circumflex artery (supplying the lateral and posterior left ventricle).
facts.visualDescription: Coronary artery diagram showing left main bifurcating into LAD and circumflex

facts.quizQuestion: Which artery supplies the SA node in about 60% of people and the AV node in about 85% of people?
facts.explanation: The right coronary artery (RCA) supplies the right atrium, right ventricle, SA node (in ~60% of people), and AV node (in ~85% of people). In right-dominant hearts (85% of people), the RCA also gives rise to the posterior descending artery (PDA).
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of RCA traveling in right atrioventricular groove with SA/AV node supply labeled

variants.quizQuestion: In right-dominant hearts (85% of people), which artery gives rise to the posterior descending artery (PDA)?
variants.explanation: In right-dominant coronary anatomy — the most common pattern — the RCA gives rise to the PDA, which supplies the inferior ventricular wall and the posterior third of the interventricular septum.

facts.quizQuestion: Which branch of the left main coronary artery supplies the lateral and posterior left ventricle?
facts.explanation: The left circumflex artery wraps around the left side of the heart in the atrioventricular groove, supplying the lateral wall and posterior wall of the left ventricle. It branches off the left main coronary artery alongside the LAD.
facts.visualDescription: Heart diagram with left circumflex artery curving around lateral/posterior LV

facts.quizQuestion: Which structure is known as the heart's primary pacemaker?
facts.explanation: The sinoatrial (SA) node is a cluster of specialized cells in the right atrium near the junction with the superior vena cava. It spontaneously depolarizes at 60–100 times per minute, setting the normal heart rate. All other conduction structures have slower intrinsic rates and follow the SA node's lead.
facts.visualDescription: Heart diagram with SA node highlighted in upper right atrium near SVC

variants.quizQuestion: The SA node is located in which heart chamber, near the junction with the superior vena cava?
variants.explanation: The SA node sits in the wall of the right atrium near where the superior vena cava empties in. Its spontaneous firing rate of 60–100 bpm establishes normal sinus rhythm.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the intrinsic firing rate of the SA node?
facts.explanation: The SA node fires spontaneously at 60–100 beats per minute, which defines the normal resting heart rate range. If the SA node fails, the AV node takes over at its slower rate of 40–60 bpm, and if that fails, the Purkinje fibers maintain a very slow rate of 20–40 bpm.
facts.visualDescription: Table comparing intrinsic rates: SA 60-100, AV 40-60, Purkinje 20-40 bpm

facts.quizQuestion: Which structure is the only normal electrical connection between the atria and ventricles, and delays the signal by about 0.1 seconds?
facts.explanation: The AV node sits in the interatrial septum and intentionally slows conduction by about 0.1 seconds. This delay lets the atria finish contracting and emptying into the ventricles before the ventricles contract — critical for efficient cardiac output.
facts.visualDescription: Conduction system diagram with AV node labeled in interatrial septum and delay arrow

variants.quizQuestion: Why does the AV node delay the electrical impulse by ~0.1 seconds?
variants.correctAnswer: To allow atria to finish contracting before the ventricles contract
variants.explanation: The AV node's delay ensures the atria have fully emptied blood into the ventricles before ventricular systole begins, maximizing stroke volume.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the intrinsic backup pacing rate of the AV node if the SA node fails?
facts.explanation: If the SA node fails, the AV node takes over as a secondary pacemaker at its intrinsic rate of 40–60 bpm. This is slower than normal but still sustains life. Purkinje fibers provide the last-resort backup at just 20–40 bpm.
facts.visualDescription: Conduction system backup hierarchy diagram: SA (60-100) → AV (40-60) → Purkinje (20-40)

facts.quizQuestion: Which conduction structure passes through the interventricular septum, connecting the AV node to the bundle branches?
facts.explanation: The bundle of His (pronounced 'hiss') is a tract of specialized cardiac muscle fibers that travels through the fibrous skeleton of the heart and into the interventricular septum, where it splits into the left and right bundle branches.
facts.visualDescription: Conduction diagram showing Bundle of His in interventricular septum between AV node and bundle branches

facts.quizQuestion: Which cells are the fastest-conducting in the heart and spread the electrical impulse to the ventricular myocardium?
facts.explanation: Purkinje fibers are specialized cardiomyocytes that conduct electrical impulses very rapidly through the ventricular walls, ensuring the ventricles contract in a coordinated, efficient squeeze from apex to base. Their intrinsic pacemaker rate is just 20–40 bpm — a last-resort backup.
facts.visualDescription: Conduction diagram with Purkinje fiber network spreading through ventricular walls

variants.quizQuestion: Purkinje fibers serve as a pacemaker of last resort with an intrinsic rate of how many bpm?
variants.explanation: Purkinje fibers fire at only 20–40 bpm if both the SA and AV nodes fail — slow enough to sustain minimal circulation but not comfortable. Their primary role is rapid impulse distribution, not pacing.

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the phase of the cardiac cycle when the ventricles contract and pump blood out?
facts.explanation: Systole is the contraction phase: the ventricles squeeze, pressure rises, the semilunar valves open, and blood is ejected into the aorta and pulmonary trunk. The opposite phase — ventricular relaxation and filling — is called diastole.
facts.visualDescription: Cardiac cycle diagram with systole and diastole phases labeled

variants.quizQuestion: The 'relaxation and filling' phase of the cardiac cycle is called _____ .
variants.explanation: During diastole, the ventricles relax and fill with blood from the atria. The AV valves are open and the semilunar valves are closed. Diastole is longer than systole at normal resting heart rates.

acceptableAlternatives: Heart rate multiplied by stroke volume
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for cardiac output?
facts.explanation: Cardiac output (CO) = Heart Rate (HR) × Stroke Volume (SV). At rest, a typical CO is about 5 L/min: roughly 70 beats/min × 70 mL/beat. During intense exercise, CO can rise to 20–25 L/min in trained athletes.
facts.visualDescription: Formula card: CO = HR × SV with normal values annotated

variants.quizQuestion: At a resting heart rate of 70 bpm and stroke volume of 70 mL, what is the approximate cardiac output?
variants.explanation: 70 bpm × 70 mL = 4,900 mL/min ≈ 5 L/min. This is the typical resting cardiac output, meaning the entire blood volume is pumped approximately once per minute.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the normal range for left ventricular ejection fraction?
facts.explanation: Ejection fraction (EF) is the percentage of blood in the left ventricle that is ejected with each heartbeat. Normal EF is 55–70%. An EF below 40% indicates heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), a serious condition.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing ventricle at end-diastole and end-systole with EF percentage labeled

facts.quizQuestion: Which of the three heart wall layers is the thick muscular middle layer responsible for pumping?
facts.explanation: The heart wall has three layers: the epicardium (outer), myocardium (middle), and endocardium (inner lining). The myocardium is by far the thickest — it is the cardiac muscle itself, and its contraction powers every heartbeat.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section of heart wall showing three layers: epicardium, myocardium, endocardium

variants.quizQuestion: Name the three layers of the heart wall from outer to inner.
variants.explanation: The epicardium (= visceral pericardium) is the outer layer; the myocardium is the thick muscular middle layer; the endocardium is the smooth inner lining that contacts blood in the chambers.

facts.quizQuestion: The space between the parietal and visceral layers of the serous pericardium normally contains about how much lubricating fluid?
facts.explanation: The pericardial cavity — between the parietal pericardium and the visceral pericardium (epicardium) — normally holds about 20 mL of serous fluid. This fluid reduces friction as the heart beats. In pericardial effusion, this fluid can accumulate to dangerous volumes and compress the heart.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of pericardial layers with ~20mL fluid volume labeled in cavity

variants.quizQuestion: The pericardium has two main layers: the outer fibrous pericardium and the inner _____ pericardium (which itself has parietal and visceral layers).
variants.explanation: The serous pericardium has two layers: the parietal layer lining the fibrous pericardium, and the visceral layer (epicardium) covering the heart surface. The pericardial cavity between them contains ~20 mL of lubricating fluid.

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the largest artery in the human body, originating from the left ventricle and carrying oxygenated blood to the systemic circulation?
facts.explanation: The aorta is the largest artery in the body. It arises from the left ventricle and is divided into four segments: the ascending aorta, the aortic arch, the thoracic descending aorta, and the abdominal aorta. All systemic arteries ultimately branch off from it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of the chest showing a large red vessel arching up from the heart, branching into smaller arteries, labeled diagram style

variants.question: The ___ is the largest artery in the body, originating from the left ventricle.
True or false: The aorta originates from the right ventricle.

facts.quizQuestion: Three major arteries supplying blood to the head, neck, and upper limbs branch off which segment of the aorta?
facts.explanation: The aortic arch gives rise to three great vessels: the brachiocephalic trunk (which then divides into the right common carotid and right subclavian arteries), the left common carotid artery, and the left subclavian artery. These branches supply the head, neck, brain, and both upper limbs.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art diagram of the aortic arch curving over the heart, with three labeled branches emerging from its top surface in sequence

variants.question: The brachiocephalic trunk, left common carotid, and left subclavian arteries all arise from which structure?

facts.quizQuestion: The first and largest branch off the aortic arch divides into the right common carotid and right subclavian arteries. What is this vessel called?
facts.explanation: The brachiocephalic trunk (also called the innominate artery) is the first branch of the aortic arch. It travels only a short distance before dividing into the right common carotid artery (supplying the right side of the brain and face) and the right subclavian artery (supplying the right arm). There is no equivalent vessel on the left — the left common carotid and left subclavian arise separately and directly from the arch.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art closeup of aortic arch showing the brachiocephalic trunk as the first and largest branch, splitting into two vessels like a Y

variants.question: The ___ is the first branch of the aortic arch and divides into the right common carotid and right subclavian arteries.
True or false: The left side of the aortic arch also has a brachiocephalic trunk equivalent.

facts.quizQuestion: The common carotid artery bifurcates at the level of vertebra C3/C4 into the external carotid (supplying face and neck) and which other branch that supplies the brain?
facts.explanation: At the level of C3/C4, the common carotid artery splits into two: the external carotid artery, which supplies the face, scalp, and neck structures, and the internal carotid artery, which ascends without branching in the neck and enters the skull to supply the brain. The carotid bifurcation contains the carotid body and sinus, important chemoreceptor and baroreceptor sites.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art neck anatomy showing a vessel splitting into two labeled branches — one going toward the face, one ascending into the skull

variants.question: Which artery supplies blood to the brain and is a branch of the common carotid at the C3/C4 level?

facts.quizQuestion: The brachial artery divides at the cubital fossa into two branches. Which lateral branch, running along the thumb side of the forearm, is routinely used for taking a pulse at the wrist?
facts.explanation: At the cubital fossa (the hollow in front of the elbow), the brachial artery bifurcates into the radial artery (lateral, thumb side) and the ulnar artery (medial, little-finger side). The radial artery continues down the lateral forearm and onto the wrist, where it lies superficially between the flexor carpi radialis tendon and the radial styloid — the standard site for measuring the pulse in clinical practice.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art forearm and wrist showing two parallel arteries; the lateral one highlighted with a pulsing icon at the wrist

variants.question: True or false: The radial artery is on the medial (little-finger) side of the forearm.
The brachial artery splits at the cubital fossa into the ___ artery (lateral) and the ulnar artery (medial).

facts.quizQuestion: Which unpaired branch of the abdominal aorta supplies the foregut organs — stomach, liver, spleen, and pancreas?
facts.explanation: The celiac trunk is the first major unpaired branch of the abdominal aorta. It divides almost immediately into three main branches: the left gastric artery (stomach and lower esophagus), the splenic artery (spleen and pancreas), and the common hepatic artery (liver, gallbladder, duodenum). Together these branches supply all foregut-derived structures.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art abdominal diagram with a short wide artery branching into three labeled vessels toward stomach, liver, and spleen

variants.question: The left gastric, splenic, and common hepatic arteries are all branches of which vessel?

facts.quizQuestion: Which abdominal aorta branch supplies the midgut — including the small intestine and the ascending and transverse colon?
facts.explanation: The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) arises from the abdominal aorta just below the celiac trunk and supplies all of the small intestine, the cecum, appendix, ascending colon, and most of the transverse colon — the embryological midgut. It is a critical vessel; SMA occlusion causes extensive bowel ischemia. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) supplies the hindgut (descending colon, sigmoid, rectum).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art abdominal diagram with branching arteries to the looping small intestine and right colon, labeled SMA

variants.question: True or false: The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) supplies the hindgut, including the descending colon and rectum.

facts.quizQuestion: The abdominal aorta bifurcates into the left and right common iliac arteries at the level of which lumbar vertebra?
facts.explanation: The abdominal aorta ends at the level of the L4 vertebra, where it bifurcates into the right and left common iliac arteries. Each common iliac then divides into the internal iliac artery (supplying pelvic organs) and the external iliac artery (continuing as the femoral artery to supply the lower limb). The L4 bifurcation is a landmark used in aortic surgery and vascular imaging.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art pelvis and lumbar spine diagram showing the aorta forking into two vessels at a labeled vertebral level

variants.question: The abdominal aorta bifurcates into the common iliac arteries at the level of vertebra ___.

facts.quizQuestion: The external iliac artery passes under the inguinal ligament and continues as which major artery supplying the thigh?
facts.explanation: As the external iliac artery passes under the inguinal ligament, it becomes the femoral artery, which is the main arterial supply to the thigh. The femoral artery runs through the femoral triangle and adductor canal, then passes through the adductor hiatus to become the popliteal artery behind the knee. It is the standard site for arterial access in cardiac catheterization.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lower limb showing a vessel continuing from the pelvis down through the thigh, labeled at the groin

variants.question: Which artery is the continuation of the external iliac artery distal to the inguinal ligament?

facts.quizQuestion: The anterior tibial artery continues onto the dorsum of the foot as which artery, whose pulse can be palpated on the top of the foot and is routinely checked in vascular assessments?
facts.explanation: The anterior tibial artery crosses the ankle joint and continues onto the dorsum of the foot as the dorsalis pedis artery. Its pulse is palpable on the dorsum of the foot, lateral to the extensor hallucis longus tendon. Clinicians routinely check this pulse alongside the posterior tibial pulse to assess peripheral vascular disease in the lower limb.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art top-down view of a foot with a highlighted artery running along the dorsum, pulsing indicator near the ankle

variants.question: True or false: The dorsalis pedis artery is a continuation of the posterior tibial artery.

facts.quizQuestion: Which large vein drains deoxygenated blood from the head, neck, upper limbs, and thorax into the right atrium?
facts.explanation: The superior vena cava (SVC) is formed by the union of the right and left brachiocephalic veins and drains the upper half of the body — head, neck, both upper limbs, and the thoracic wall — into the right atrium. The inferior vena cava (IVC) drains the lower body. Together they return all systemic deoxygenated blood to the right heart.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art chest diagram with a large blue vessel descending from the neck into the upper right chamber of the heart, labeled SVC

variants.question: The ___ drains the head, neck, upper limbs, and thorax into the right atrium.

facts.quizQuestion: On each side, the internal jugular vein and the subclavian vein merge to form which vessels that then unite to create the superior vena cava?
facts.explanation: On each side of the neck, the internal jugular vein (draining brain and face) and the subclavian vein (draining the arm) merge to form the brachiocephalic vein (also called the innominate vein). The right and left brachiocephalic veins then join behind the right sternal border to form the superior vena cava, which drains into the right atrium.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art neck/chest diagram showing two Y-shaped vessel mergers on each side converging into a single large vessel entering the heart

variants.question: The right and left brachiocephalic veins each form from the union of which two vessels?

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the longest vein in the human body, running along the medial leg from the ankle to drain into the femoral vein at the saphenous opening, and commonly harvested for coronary artery bypass grafts?
facts.explanation: The great saphenous vein (GSV) is the longest vein in the body. It begins at the medial side of the foot, ascends along the medial leg and thigh, and drains into the femoral vein through the saphenous opening (an aperture in the fascia lata) in the upper thigh. Because of its length, accessibility, and caliber, it is the most commonly used conduit for coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) when a surgeon needs to bypass blocked coronary arteries.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art full-length leg with a highlighted blue vessel running along the inner side from ankle to groin

variants.question: True or false: The great saphenous vein is commonly used as a graft in coronary artery bypass surgery.
The ___ vein is the longest vein in the body and drains into the femoral vein at the saphenous opening.

facts.quizQuestion: Which vein carries nutrient-rich blood from the gastrointestinal tract to the liver for processing, rather than returning it directly to the heart?
facts.explanation: The hepatic portal vein carries blood from the capillary beds of the stomach, intestines, spleen, and pancreas to the liver's sinusoids, where nutrients, toxins, and drugs are processed before blood returns to the systemic circulation via the hepatic veins and IVC. This arrangement — where one capillary bed drains into a vein that feeds a second capillary bed before reaching the heart — defines a portal system. It is the only major portal system in the body.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art abdominal diagram showing blood flowing from the intestines into a vessel leading to the liver, then to the heart via another vessel

variants.question: True or false: The hepatic portal vein carries blood directly back to the right atrium without passing through the liver.

facts.quizQuestion: Which vein drains the thoracic wall (posterior intercostal veins) and empties into the superior vena cava, serving as an important collateral route if the IVC is obstructed?
facts.explanation: The azygos vein runs along the right side of the vertebral column, draining the posterior intercostal veins and thoracic wall, and arches over the right lung root to empty into the posterior SVC. It communicates with the ascending lumbar veins below, linking it to the IVC territory. If the IVC is obstructed (e.g., by a tumor or thrombus), the azygos system provides an alternative route for lower-body venous return — making it clinically important as a collateral pathway.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior chest showing a vessel running vertically along the spine, arching forward to join the superior vena cava near the heart

variants.question: Which vein drains the thoracic wall and empties into the SVC, acting as an IVC collateral when obstructed?

facts.quizQuestion: Which circulatory loop carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs for gas exchange, then returns oxygenated blood to the left atrium via the pulmonary veins?
facts.explanation: Pulmonary circulation is the circuit between the heart and the lungs. Deoxygenated blood leaves the right ventricle via the pulmonary trunk, splits into right and left pulmonary arteries, passes through the lungs where CO2 is exchanged for O2, and returns via four pulmonary veins (two from each lung) to the left atrium. It is a low-pressure, high-flow circuit.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art heart-lung diagram with blue arrows going right ventricle → lungs and red arrows returning from lungs → left atrium

variants.question: ___ carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs and returns oxygenated blood to the left atrium.

facts.quizQuestion: Unlike systemic arteries which carry oxygenated blood, the pulmonary arteries carry what type of blood from the right ventricle to the lungs?
facts.explanation: The pulmonary arteries are the only arteries in the adult body that carry deoxygenated blood. By convention, arteries are defined as vessels carrying blood away from the heart — not as vessels carrying oxygenated blood. The pulmonary trunk and its right and left pulmonary artery branches carry oxygen-poor blood from the right ventricle to the pulmonary capillaries for gas exchange. Conversely, the pulmonary veins are the only veins that carry oxygenated blood.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art diagram with a blue-shaded vessel labeled 'pulmonary artery' carrying dark blood away from the right heart toward lungs
distractors: Blood high in CO2 and O2

variants.question: True or false: The pulmonary arteries carry oxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs.

facts.quizQuestion: After gas exchange in the lungs, how many pulmonary veins return oxygenated blood to the left atrium — two from the right lung and two from the left lung?
facts.explanation: There are four pulmonary veins in total: the right superior and right inferior pulmonary veins (from the right lung) and the left superior and left inferior pulmonary veins (from the left lung). All four drain oxygenated blood into the left atrium. This is clinically relevant because the pulmonary vein orifices are the origin sites of many atrial fibrillation triggers, and pulmonary vein isolation is a common ablation procedure.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior view of the left atrium showing four vessel openings labeled in pairs from each lung

variants.question: ___ pulmonary veins (two from each lung) return oxygenated blood to the left atrium.

facts.quizQuestion: In the systemic circulation, arterioles lead to microscopic vessels where gas and nutrient exchange occurs between blood and tissues. What are these exchange vessels called?
facts.explanation: Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels — typically just one endothelial cell thick — and are the site of all gas, nutrient, and waste exchange between the blood and body tissues. Blood flows from arteries → arterioles → capillaries → venules → veins. The capillary wall's extreme thinness and enormous total surface area enable efficient diffusion of O2, CO2, glucose, and metabolic waste.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art microscopic view of branching tiny vessels connecting a small artery to a small vein, surrounded by tissue cells

variants.question: True or false: Gas and nutrient exchange between blood and body tissues occurs primarily in arterioles.

facts.quizQuestion: Which circulatory system is unique in having one capillary bed draining via a vein into a second capillary bed — specifically, GI capillaries draining into the liver sinusoids before returning to the heart?
facts.explanation: The hepatic portal system is the only portal system in the body: blood from the capillary beds of the GI tract drains into the portal vein, which then divides into the sinusoids of the liver (a second capillary bed) for nutrient processing, detoxification, and drug metabolism. The blood then exits via the hepatic veins into the IVC. No other major circuit in the body has this capillary-to-vein-to-capillary arrangement.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art flowchart: intestine capillaries → portal vein → liver sinusoids → hepatic vein → IVC, with an arrow loop indicating two capillary beds

variants.question: In which system does a capillary bed drain via a vein into a second capillary bed, rather than directly returning blood to the heart?

facts.quizQuestion: Systolic blood pressure is the peak arterial pressure recorded during ventricular contraction. What is the normal resting systolic value in a healthy adult?
facts.explanation: Normal systolic blood pressure is approximately 120 mmHg. It represents the maximum pressure in the large arteries during ventricular systole (contraction). Values consistently at or above 130 mmHg are classified as Stage 1 hypertension (per 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines). Blood pressure is measured in millimeters of mercury (mmHg).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art blood pressure gauge showing the needle at 120, with the top number highlighted in red and the bottom in blue

variants.question: Normal resting systolic blood pressure in a healthy adult is approximately ___ mmHg.

facts.quizQuestion: Diastolic blood pressure is the minimum arterial pressure measured during ventricular relaxation. What is the normal resting diastolic value in a healthy adult?
facts.explanation: Normal diastolic blood pressure is approximately 80 mmHg. It is the pressure remaining in the arteries between heartbeats, when the ventricles are relaxing and refilling. A blood pressure reading of 120/80 mmHg is considered optimal. Consistently elevated diastolic pressure (90+ mmHg) indicates hypertension and increased cardiovascular risk.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art blood pressure cuff on an arm, readout showing 120/80 with the bottom number highlighted

variants.question: True or false: Normal resting diastolic blood pressure is approximately 80 mmHg.

facts.quizQuestion: Pulse pressure is the difference between systolic and diastolic blood pressure. Given a normal blood pressure of 120/80 mmHg, what is the normal pulse pressure?
facts.explanation: Pulse pressure = systolic pressure − diastolic pressure = 120 − 80 = 40 mmHg. It represents the force the heart generates with each beat. A widened pulse pressure (e.g., >60 mmHg) may indicate aortic regurgitation, severe anemia, or high output states; a narrowed pulse pressure (e.g., <25 mmHg) may signal cardiac tamponade or heart failure.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art waveform showing two peaks with the gap between systolic (high) and diastolic (low) labeled as pulse pressure

variants.question: With a blood pressure of 120/80 mmHg, the pulse pressure (systolic minus diastolic) is ___ mmHg.

facts.quizQuestion: Mean arterial pressure (MAP) approximates the average perfusion pressure driving blood to organs. Using the formula MAP ≈ diastolic + 1/3(pulse pressure) with a blood pressure of 120/80 mmHg, what is the approximate MAP?
facts.explanation: MAP ≈ diastolic + 1/3 × (systolic − diastolic) = 80 + 1/3 × (120 − 80) = 80 + 1/3 × 40 = 80 + 13.3 ≈ 93 mmHg. MAP is weighted toward diastole because the heart spends more time in diastole than systole at normal heart rates. A MAP below 60–65 mmHg is considered insufficient for adequate organ perfusion and defines circulatory shock thresholds.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art pressure graph with a horizontal dashed line slightly above the midpoint between systolic and diastolic peaks, labeled MAP

variants.question: Using MAP ≈ diastolic + 1/3(pulse pressure), a BP of 120/80 gives a MAP of approximately ___ mmHg.
True or false: Mean arterial pressure (MAP) equals the simple average of systolic and diastolic pressure.

facts.quizQuestion: Which superficial vein runs along the posterior leg and drains into the popliteal vein behind the knee — the smaller counterpart to the great saphenous vein?
facts.explanation: The small saphenous vein (also called the short saphenous vein) begins at the lateral side of the foot and runs up the posterior calf to drain into the popliteal vein in the popliteal fossa behind the knee. It is a common site for varicose vein formation. In contrast, the great saphenous vein runs along the medial leg and drains into the femoral vein at the groin.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior leg view with a highlighted vessel running up the back of the calf to the knee, where it joins a deeper vessel

variants.question: Which superficial vein of the posterior leg drains into the popliteal vein behind the knee?

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate total blood volume in a healthy adult?
facts.explanation: An adult human body contains roughly 5 litres of blood, accounting for about 7–8% of body weight. Blood maintains a slightly alkaline pH of 7.35–7.45. Even a loss of 15–20% (around 750 mL) triggers measurable physiological responses.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional diagram of a blood-filled vessel with total volume annotation

variants.quizQuestion: What is the normal pH range of human blood?
variants.explanation: Blood is maintained within the narrow range of 7.35–7.45 by buffer systems, the respiratory system, and the kidneys. A pH below 7.35 is acidosis; above 7.45 is alkalosis — both are life-threatening if severe.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the liquid component of blood that makes up approximately 55% of its volume?
facts.explanation: Plasma is the straw-coloured liquid matrix of blood. It is roughly 92% water and carries proteins (albumin, globulins, fibrinogen), electrolytes, nutrients, hormones, waste products, and dissolved gases. The remaining 45% of blood volume is formed elements (cells and platelets).
facts.visualDescription: Centrifuged blood tube showing pale yellow plasma layer above a thin buffy coat and a red RBC pellet

variants.quizQuestion: What percentage of blood plasma is water?
variants.explanation: About 92% of plasma is water, which acts as the solvent for all dissolved substances. The remaining 8% includes proteins, electrolytes, nutrients, hormones, and metabolic waste products.

variants.quizQuestion: What term describes the 45% of blood volume occupied by red blood cells, measured after centrifugation?
variants.explanation: Hematocrit (packed cell volume) is the percentage of blood volume consisting of red blood cells after spinning. Normal ranges are roughly 41–53% in males and 36–46% in females. Low hematocrit indicates anaemia; high values may indicate polycythaemia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which plasma protein is the most abundant, making up ~54% of all plasma proteins, and is chiefly responsible for maintaining osmotic pressure?
facts.explanation: Albumin accounts for about 54% of plasma proteins and is the primary contributor to oncotic (colloid osmotic) pressure, which keeps fluid from leaking out of capillaries. It also transports fatty acids, hormones, and drugs. Globulins (38%) include antibodies; fibrinogen (7%) is essential for clotting.
facts.visualDescription: Protein composition pie chart of plasma showing albumin, globulins, and fibrinogen fractions

variants.quizQuestion: Which plasma protein, accounting for ~7% of plasma proteins, is the inactive precursor to fibrin in blood clotting?
variants.explanation: Fibrinogen is converted to fibrin by thrombin during the coagulation cascade. Fibrin strands form the structural mesh of a blood clot. Fibrinogen is synthesised in the liver, as are most clotting factors.

facts.quizQuestion: What type of blood cell is a biconcave disc, has no nucleus when mature, and carries oxygen via haemoglobin?
facts.explanation: Red blood cells (erythrocytes) are the most numerous blood cells: 4.5–5.5 million per microlitre. Their biconcave shape maximises surface area for gas exchange. They shed their nucleus during maturation so more space is available for haemoglobin, which binds and transports oxygen.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of biconcave red blood cells viewed from the side and above, showing the characteristic disc shape

variants.quizQuestion: Approximately how many red blood cells are found in one microlitre (µL) of blood?
variants.explanation: A single microlitre of blood contains 4.5–5.5 million erythrocytes. Because they are so numerous, RBCs account for nearly the entire formed-element fraction of blood and give blood its red colour.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the average lifespan of a red blood cell?
facts.explanation: Red blood cells circulate for about 120 days before being removed by macrophages in the spleen, liver, and bone marrow. Haemoglobin is then recycled — the iron is reused for new RBCs, and the porphyrin ring becomes bilirubin, excreted via bile.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of the RBC lifecycle from bone marrow production to splenic destruction and haemoglobin recycling

variants.quizQuestion: What process describes the production of red blood cells in red bone marrow, stimulated by the hormone EPO?
variants.explanation: Erythropoiesis is the production of red blood cells. The kidneys sense low oxygen levels and release erythropoietin (EPO), which stimulates stem cells in red bone marrow to differentiate into erythrocytes. Athletes have exploited synthetic EPO as a performance-enhancing drug.

facts.quizQuestion: What oxygen-carrying protein fills red blood cells, consisting of four subunits each containing an iron-bearing heme group?
facts.explanation: Haemoglobin (Hb) is composed of 4 globin chains (2 alpha + 2 beta), each with a heme group containing an Fe²⁺ iron atom. Each heme group binds one O₂ molecule, so each Hb molecule can carry up to 4 oxygen molecules. Normal Hb levels are 13.5–17.5 g/dL in males and 12–16 g/dL in females.
facts.visualDescription: Molecular ribbon diagram of haemoglobin showing four coloured subunits, each with a heme disc at the centre

variants.quizQuestion: How many oxygen molecules can a single haemoglobin molecule carry at maximum capacity?
variants.explanation: Each of haemoglobin's four subunits has one heme group, and each heme group can bind one O₂ molecule. So one Hb molecule transports up to 4 O₂ molecules. This cooperative binding (each O₂ increases affinity for the next) is called the Hill effect.

variants.quizQuestion: Why does carbon monoxide poisoning prevent oxygen delivery to tissues?
variants.correctAnswer: CO binds haemoglobin 200–250× more strongly than O₂, blocking O₂ binding sites
variants.explanation: Carbon monoxide (CO) occupies the same heme binding sites as oxygen but with 200–250 times greater affinity. This forms carboxyhaemoglobin, which cannot carry O₂ and also shifts the O₂ dissociation curve left, impairing release of any remaining bound oxygen.

facts.quizQuestion: What class of blood cells, present at 4,500–11,000 per µL, forms the cellular arm of immune defence?
facts.explanation: White blood cells (leukocytes) are the immune cells of the blood. They are far less numerous than RBCs but are essential for defence against pathogens, foreign particles, and abnormal cells. The normal count is 4,500–11,000 per µL; values outside this range suggest infection, inflammation, or immune disorders.
facts.visualDescription: Microscope blood smear showing a large purple-staining leukocyte alongside much smaller red blood cells

facts.quizQuestion: Which leukocyte type is the most abundant (60–70% of WBCs) and acts as a first-responder phagocyte against bacterial infection?
facts.explanation: Neutrophils are granulocytes that arrive first at sites of infection. They engulf and destroy bacteria via phagocytosis, release antimicrobial enzymes, and form neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs). They are short-lived (hours to days) and their remnants form pus.
facts.visualDescription: Multi-lobed neutrophil nucleus visible in a blood smear, characteristic of the polymorphonuclear appearance

variants.quizQuestion: Which WBC type (2–4% of leukocytes) is elevated in parasitic infections and allergic reactions?
variants.explanation: Eosinophils are granulocytes named for their affinity for the acidic dye eosin. They release cytotoxic granules to combat parasites and modulate allergic inflammatory responses. Counts rise dramatically during helminth infections and in conditions like asthma.

facts.quizQuestion: Which rarest granulocyte (0.5–1% of WBCs) releases histamine and heparin, contributing to allergic and inflammatory responses?
facts.explanation: Basophils are the least common granulocyte. Their granules contain histamine (which dilates blood vessels and increases permeability during inflammation) and heparin (an anticoagulant). They play a role in allergic reactions and are closely related to tissue mast cells.
facts.visualDescription: Basophil in blood smear showing large dark-blue granules that often obscure the bilobed nucleus

facts.quizQuestion: Which agranulocyte type (20–40% of WBCs) includes T cells, B cells, and natural killer cells?
facts.explanation: Lymphocytes are the primary cells of adaptive immunity. B cells produce antibodies; T cells include cytotoxic T cells (kill infected/cancerous cells) and helper T cells (coordinate immune responses); natural killer (NK) cells attack tumour cells and virus-infected cells without prior sensitisation.
facts.visualDescription: Small round lymphocyte with large nucleus filling most of the cell, contrasted against red blood cells in a blood smear

variants.quizQuestion: Which WBC type (2–8% of leukocytes) migrates into tissues and differentiates into macrophages?
variants.explanation: Monocytes are large agranulocytes that circulate briefly in blood and then enter tissues, where they mature into macrophages. Macrophages are long-lived phagocytes that engulf pathogens, dead cells, and debris, and also present antigens to T cells to activate adaptive immunity.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the small, anucleate cell fragments, derived from megakaryocytes, that are essential for forming a primary clot?
facts.explanation: Platelets (thrombocytes) are not true cells — they are cytoplasmic fragments pinched off from large bone-marrow cells called megakaryocytes. Normal count is 150,000–400,000 per µL. They circulate for 8–10 days and aggregate at sites of vessel injury to form a platelet plug, the first step in haemostasis.
facts.visualDescription: Electron micrograph showing small irregularly shaped platelets between much larger red blood cells

variants.quizQuestion: What is the normal platelet count range per microlitre of blood?
variants.explanation: A count below 150,000/µL is thrombocytopenia (bleeding risk); above 400,000/µL is thrombocytosis (clotting risk). Platelets survive 8–10 days. Low counts can result from bone marrow failure, immune destruction, or drugs.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the term for the three-step process — vascular spasm, platelet plug formation, and coagulation cascade — that stops bleeding?
facts.explanation: Haemostasis is the body's mechanism to stop bleeding. Step 1: vascular spasm (immediate vasoconstriction reduces blood flow). Step 2: platelet plug (platelets adhere and aggregate at the injury). Step 3: coagulation cascade (a series of clotting factor reactions produce fibrin, reinforcing the plug into a stable clot).
facts.visualDescription: Three-panel diagram showing vascular spasm, platelet aggregation, and fibrin mesh formation at a vessel breach

variants.quizQuestion: Which vitamin is required for the liver to produce clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X?
variants.explanation: Vitamin K is a fat-soluble vitamin essential for the gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors II (prothrombin), VII, IX, and X, which enables them to bind calcium and activate. Warfarin (a blood thinner) works by antagonising vitamin K, reducing production of these factors.

facts.quizQuestion: What insoluble protein forms the mesh that reinforces a platelet plug into a stable blood clot?
facts.explanation: Fibrin is produced when thrombin cleaves fibrinogen (a soluble plasma protein). Fibrin strands polymerise into a mesh that traps red blood cells and platelets, solidifying the clot. The intrinsic and extrinsic coagulation pathways converge on a common pathway that generates thrombin, which then produces fibrin.
facts.visualDescription: Scanning electron micrograph of red blood cells trapped in a fibrin mesh, forming a blood clot

facts.quizQuestion: Which blood type is known as the universal red blood cell donor because its RBCs carry no ABO or Rh antigens?
facts.explanation: Type O negative RBCs have no A, B, or Rh D antigens on their surface. Any recipient's immune system has nothing to recognise as foreign, so O- can be transfused safely in emergencies regardless of the recipient's blood type. About 7% of the US population is O-.
facts.visualDescription: Blood type compatibility chart highlighting O- as the sole universal RBC donor pointing to all other types

variants.quizQuestion: Which blood type is the universal recipient, able to receive red blood cells from any ABO/Rh donor?
variants.explanation: AB+ individuals have A, B, and Rh antigens on their RBCs and carry no anti-A, anti-B, or anti-Rh antibodies in their plasma. This means their immune system will not react against any donated RBCs. About 3% of the US population is AB+.

facts.quizQuestion: In the ABO blood system, what antigens are on the red blood cells and what antibodies are in the plasma of a Type A person?
facts.explanation: Type A blood has A antigens displayed on RBC surfaces and carries anti-B antibodies in the plasma. If Type B blood were transfused into a Type A person, the anti-B antibodies would attack the incoming B-antigen RBCs, causing a haemolytic transfusion reaction.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of a Type A red blood cell showing A-antigen spikes on surface with anti-B antibodies floating in surrounding plasma

variants.quizQuestion: In the ABO system, what antigens and antibodies does Type AB blood have?
variants.correctAnswer: A and B antigens; no ABO antibodies
variants.explanation: Type AB individuals express both A and B antigens on their RBCs and produce neither anti-A nor anti-B antibodies (the immune system recognises both as 'self'). Because their plasma has no ABO antibodies to attack donor RBCs, AB individuals can receive from any ABO type.

acceptableAlternatives: No antigens, anti-A and anti-B antibodies
no ABO antigens; anti-A + anti-B antibodies
facts.quizQuestion: What is the antigen and antibody profile of Type O blood?
facts.explanation: Type O RBCs carry no A or B antigens, which is why they can be donated to any ABO type without triggering an antigen-driven immune attack. However, Type O plasma contains both anti-A and anti-B antibodies, so Type O individuals can only receive Type O red blood cells. Type O has no A or B antigens on RBCs but carries both anti-A and anti-B antibodies.
facts.visualDescription: Type O red blood cell shown with no surface antigen projections, contrasted with A- and B-type cells

facts.quizQuestion: Which blood type is the most common in the United States, found in approximately 38% of the population?
facts.explanation: O+ is the most frequent blood type in the US at ~38%, followed by A+ (~34%), B+ (~9%), O- (~7%), A- (~6%), AB+ (~3%), B- (~2%), and AB- (~1%). O+ is also highly valuable for transfusion as it can be given to any Rh+ recipient — the vast majority of people.
facts.visualDescription: Pie chart of US blood type distribution with O+ as the largest segment

variants.quizQuestion: What is the rarest blood type in the United States by ABO/Rh classification?
variants.explanation: AB- is present in only ~1% of the US population, making it the rarest of the eight common blood types. Despite its rarity, AB- plasma (which contains no ABO or Rh antibodies) is valuable as a universal plasma donor.

facts.quizQuestion: What surface antigen determines whether a blood type is positive (+) or negative (−) in the Rh blood group system?
facts.explanation: The Rh system is defined primarily by the presence (Rh+, ~85% of people) or absence (Rh−, ~15%) of the Rh D antigen on RBC surfaces. Unlike ABO antibodies, anti-Rh antibodies only develop after exposure to Rh+ blood — through transfusion or pregnancy.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram comparing an Rh+ RBC (D antigen present on surface) with an Rh- RBC (smooth surface, no D antigen)

variants.quizQuestion: What percentage of the human population is Rh positive?
variants.explanation: Approximately 85% of people have the Rh D antigen on their RBCs and are Rh+. The remaining ~15% are Rh−. Prevalence varies by ethnicity — certain African populations have near-100% Rh+ frequency, while Rh− is more common in people of Basque descent.

facts.quizQuestion: What condition occurs when an Rh− mother develops anti-Rh antibodies that cross the placenta and destroy red blood cells of a subsequent Rh+ fetus?
facts.explanation: During a first Rh-incompatible pregnancy, fetal RBCs can enter the mother's circulation at delivery, causing her immune system to produce anti-Rh (anti-D) antibodies. In a subsequent Rh+ pregnancy, these IgG antibodies cross the placenta and attack fetal RBCs, causing haemolytic disease. It is prevented by giving the mother RhoGAM (anti-D immunoglobulin) within 72 hours of delivery.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing Rh- mother with anti-D antibodies crossing the placenta to attack Rh+ fetal red blood cells in a second pregnancy

variants.quizQuestion: What injection is given to an Rh− mother after delivery of an Rh+ baby to prevent sensitisation in future pregnancies?
variants.explanation: RhoGAM contains anti-D antibodies that neutralise any Rh+ fetal RBCs that entered the mother's bloodstream before her immune system can mount a response. Administered within 72 hours of delivery (and sometimes at 28 weeks gestation), it has made haemolytic disease of the newborn rare in developed countries.

facts.quizQuestion: Which blood type is the universal plasma donor because its plasma contains no ABO antibodies that could attack a recipient's red blood cells?
facts.explanation: AB+ plasma carries no anti-A or anti-B antibodies (AB individuals recognise both antigens as 'self') and no Rh antibodies, so it can be transfused to any patient without risk of an antibody-mediated reaction. This makes AB+ plasma particularly valuable in massive transfusions before blood typing is possible.
facts.visualDescription: Transfusion compatibility chart highlighting AB+ as the universal plasma donor with arrows pointing to all other blood types

variants.quizQuestion: Why is Type O negative considered the safest choice for emergency red blood cell transfusion before a patient's blood type is known?
variants.correctAnswer: O- RBCs have no A, B, or Rh antigens, so no recipient antibodies can trigger a haemolytic reaction
variants.explanation: O- red blood cells lack all three major surface antigens (A, B, and Rh D) that trigger transfusion reactions. This means any patient — regardless of their blood type — can receive O- RBCs without a compatibility test, making it the default emergency donor type kept in trauma bays.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the arterial anastomosis at the base of the brain that provides collateral blood flow if one of its contributing vessels is occluded?
facts.explanation: The Circle of Willis is a ring-shaped anastomosis formed by the two anterior cerebral arteries joined by the anterior communicating artery, the two internal carotid arteries, the two posterior communicating arteries, and the two posterior cerebral arteries (which arise from the basilar artery). It allows collateral flow between the anterior and posterior cerebral circulations so that if one vessel is blocked, adjacent territories can still be perfused.
facts.visualDescription: Inferior view of the brain with the Circle of Willis highlighted: ICA entering on each side, ACA + ACoA across the front, PCoA linking to PCA on each side, basilar artery entering from below

variants.quizQuestion: The Circle of Willis is formed by contributions from two pairs of vertebral-derived vessels and one unpaired midline trunk. What is the unpaired trunk called?
variants.explanation: The two vertebral arteries merge at the pontomedullary junction to form the single basilar artery, which then bifurcates into the two posterior cerebral arteries — the posterior portion of the Circle of Willis.

facts.quizQuestion: Which short artery connects the two anterior cerebral arteries across the midline, completing the anterior portion of the Circle of Willis?
facts.explanation: The anterior communicating artery (ACoA) is a short vessel (~3 mm) bridging the left and right anterior cerebral arteries just above the optic chiasm. It completes the anterior arc of the Circle of Willis. It is also the most common site of intracranial berry aneurysm formation, accounting for roughly 30–35% of all cerebral aneurysms.
facts.visualDescription: Close-up diagram of the anterior Circle of Willis: two ACA trunks running parallel with a short horizontal bridge labeled ACoA

variants.quizQuestion: The anterior communicating artery is the most common site of which intracranial vascular abnormality?
variants.explanation: Berry (saccular) aneurysms form at arterial bifurcations and weak points. The ACoA accounts for ~30–35% of all intracranial aneurysms. Rupture causes subarachnoid haemorrhage presenting with a sudden 'thunderclap headache'.

facts.correctAnswer: Ductus arteriosus, foramen ovale, ductus venosus
acceptableAlternatives: foramen ovale, ductus arteriosus, ductus venosus
facts.quizQuestion: Fetal circulation contains three shunts that bypass the lungs and liver. Name all three.
facts.explanation: The three fetal shunts are: (1) the ductus venosus — diverts oxygenated umbilical venous blood past the liver into the IVC; (2) the foramen ovale — an interatrial opening that allows blood to pass from RA to LA, bypassing the lungs; (3) the ductus arteriosus — connects the pulmonary artery to the aorta, shunting blood away from the fluid-filled lungs. At birth, rising oxygen and falling prostaglandins cause all three to close functionally within hours to days.
facts.visualDescription: Fetal heart and great vessels diagram with three shunt points labeled: umbilical vein → ductus venosus → IVC, interatrial foramen ovale, and pulmonary trunk → ductus arteriosus → aorta
distractors: Ductus arteriosus, patent foramen ovale, umbilical vein
Foramen ovale, ductus arteriosus, oval fossa
Ductus venosus, ductus arteriosus, coronary sinus

variants.quizQuestion: The ductus arteriosus closes after birth and becomes a fibrous remnant. What is this remnant called?
variants.explanation: After birth, the ductus arteriosus constricts in response to increased oxygen tension and reduced prostaglandin E2 levels, becoming the fibrous ligamentum arteriosum within a few weeks. Failure to close causes patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), the most common congenital cardiac defect in premature infants.

acceptableAlternatives: foramen ovale closes to form fossa ovalis
facts.quizQuestion: After birth, the fetal interatrial opening that bypassed the lungs closes and leaves a shallow depression on the right atrial wall. What is this adult remnant called?
facts.explanation: The foramen ovale is a flap-like opening in the interatrial septum that allows oxygenated blood to pass from the right atrium directly to the left atrium in the fetus, bypassing the non-functional lungs. After birth, the increase in left atrial pressure pushes the flap closed; it fuses within months to leave the fossa ovalis — the thin oval depression in the interatrial septum. In ~25% of adults it fails to fuse (patent foramen ovale, PFO), remaining a potential route for paradoxical embolism.
facts.visualDescription: Coronal section of adult heart showing right atrial wall with oval indentation labeled fossa ovalis on the interatrial septum

variants.quizQuestion: Approximately what percentage of adults retain a patent foramen ovale (PFO) that failed to close after birth?
variants.explanation: In about 25% of the adult population the foramen ovale never fully fuses, leaving a PFO. Under most conditions it causes no symptoms, but it can allow paradoxical emboli (clots from venous to arterial circulation), especially during Valsalva manoeuvres, and has been linked to cryptogenic stroke in younger patients.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the normal systolic and diastolic pressures in the aorta (and systemic arterial system) of a healthy adult?
facts.explanation: Normal aortic (systemic arterial) pressure is approximately 120 mmHg systolic and 80 mmHg diastolic, giving a pulse pressure of ~40 mmHg and a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of roughly 93 mmHg. The left ventricle generates 120/0–5 mmHg during systole/diastole. Hypertension is defined as sustained BP ≥130/80 mmHg (ACC/AHA 2017 criteria).
facts.visualDescription: Pressure-volume diagram showing aortic systolic and diastolic pressure levels with normal range shaded

variants.quizQuestion: Using the formula MAP ≈ diastolic + 1/3 pulse pressure, what is the approximate mean arterial pressure for a blood pressure of 120/80 mmHg?
variants.explanation: Pulse pressure = 120 − 80 = 40 mmHg. MAP ≈ 80 + (40/3) ≈ 93 mmHg. MAP is the average pressure throughout the cardiac cycle and is the key driver of organ perfusion. A MAP below 60 mmHg is generally insufficient to perfuse vital organs.

acceptableAlternatives: right ventricular pressure 25/5, pulmonary arterial pressure 25/10
facts.quizQuestion: Normal right-heart pressures are far lower than left-heart pressures. What are the approximate pressures in the right ventricle and pulmonary artery?
facts.explanation: Normal right heart pressures: right ventricle ~25/0–5 mmHg, pulmonary artery ~25/10 mmHg. Normal right ventricular pressure is ~25 mmHg systolic / 0–5 mmHg diastolic. The pulmonary artery receives this output at ~25 mmHg systolic / ~10 mmHg diastolic (mean ~15 mmHg). By comparison, the right atrium operates at ~0–5 mmHg (central venous pressure). Pulmonary arterial hypertension is defined as a resting mean PA pressure >20 mmHg. These low pressures reflect the low-resistance pulmonary vascular bed.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of right heart with pressure labels: RA ~2 mmHg, RV 25/5 mmHg, PA 25/10 mmHg, arrows showing direction of flow

variants.quizQuestion: Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) approximates left atrial pressure. What is the normal PCWP range?
variants.explanation: PCWP is measured by wedging a Swan-Ganz catheter in a small pulmonary artery branch, which reflects back-pressure from the left atrium (~6–12 mmHg). A PCWP >18 mmHg suggests left heart failure and pulmonary oedema. It is used clinically to distinguish cardiogenic from non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.

facts.quizQuestion: Coronary dominance is determined by which artery supplies the posterior descending artery (PDA) and the inferior wall of the left ventricle. What is the most common pattern?
facts.explanation: In ~85% of individuals the right coronary artery (RCA) gives rise to the posterior descending artery (PDA), making the circulation right-dominant. In ~8% the left circumflex gives rise to the PDA (left-dominant), and in ~7% both contribute (co-dominant). Dominance matters clinically because RCA occlusion in a right-dominant person causes inferior MI and can impair the SA node (60% RCA supply) and AV node (85–90% RCA supply).
facts.visualDescription: Inferior view of heart showing RCA running in posterior AV groove, giving off the PDA labeled as right-dominant pattern

variants.quizQuestion: In a right-dominant coronary circulation, occlusion of the right coronary artery (RCA) typically causes infarction of which wall of the left ventricle?
variants.explanation: The RCA supplies the inferior wall of the LV via the PDA in right-dominant systems. RCA occlusion causes inferior STEMI (ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF). It also commonly impairs the AV node (supplied by the AV nodal artery, a RCA branch in 85–90% of people), causing heart block.

acceptableAlternatives: greater end-diastolic volume increases stroke volume
facts.quizQuestion: What is the Frank-Starling law of the heart, and what is its physiological basis?
facts.explanation: The Frank-Starling law states that the greater the end-diastolic volume (preload), the greater the stroke volume on the next beat — up to a physiological maximum. The mechanism is that increased stretch of cardiac muscle fibres increases the overlap of actin and myosin, optimising cross-bridge formation and generating a stronger contraction. This intrinsic mechanism allows the heart to match output to venous return without neural input.
facts.visualDescription: Frank-Starling curve graph: x-axis end-diastolic volume, y-axis stroke volume; ascending curve that plateaus, with a second higher curve for positive inotropy
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Frank-Starling law of the heart
distractors: Increased heart rate → increased stroke volume

variants.quizQuestion: In the Frank-Starling mechanism, what happens to stroke volume when the heart is overstretched beyond the optimal fibre length (on the descending limb)?
variants.explanation: Beyond the optimal sarcomere length (~2.2 µm), further stretch causes actin and myosin filaments to overlap less efficiently, reducing the force generated. This descending limb is rarely reached in the healthy heart but is important in dilated cardiomyopathy where grossly enlarged chambers lose contractile efficiency.

facts.quizQuestion: On a standard ECG, what electrical event does the P wave represent?
facts.explanation: The P wave represents the spread of the depolarization wavefront through both atria, triggering atrial contraction. It is normally <0.12 seconds in duration and <2.5 mm in amplitude. The PR interval (P wave onset to QRS onset) represents the conduction delay at the AV node and is normally 0.12–0.20 seconds. Absence of P waves suggests atrial fibrillation; peaked tall P waves (P pulmonale) suggest right atrial enlargement.
facts.visualDescription: ECG strip with single PQRST complex: P wave labeled 'atrial depolarization', QRS labeled 'ventricular depolarization', T wave labeled 'ventricular repolarization'

variants.quizQuestion: On a standard ECG, the QRS complex represents ventricular depolarization. What electrical event does the T wave represent?
variants.explanation: The T wave represents ventricular repolarization (recovery). It is normally in the same direction as the QRS. Inverted T waves can indicate ischaemia. Atrial repolarization also occurs but is buried within the QRS complex and not visible on a standard ECG.

facts.quizQuestion: Which ECG waveform represents ventricular depolarization and is normally less than 0.12 seconds in duration?
facts.explanation: The QRS complex represents depolarization of both ventricles, triggering ventricular contraction. Normal QRS duration is <0.12 s (3 small squares). A wide QRS (>0.12 s) indicates a bundle branch block, ventricular ectopic rhythm, or aberrant conduction. The Q wave (initial septal depolarization left to right), R wave (apical depolarization), and S wave (basal depolarization) each reflect specific vectors of electrical spread. The QRS complex on the ECG represents ventricular depolarization (and atrial repolarization).
facts.visualDescription: ECG trace with QRS complex highlighted in red; Q, R, S deflections individually labeled with arrows showing their respective vectors

variants.quizQuestion: A QRS duration greater than 0.12 seconds (3 small squares) on an ECG suggests what type of conduction abnormality?
variants.explanation: A wide QRS indicates that ventricular depolarization is spreading via slower cell-to-cell conduction rather than the fast His-Purkinje system. Right bundle branch block (RBBB) gives an RSR' pattern in V1; left bundle branch block (LBBB) gives a broad notched R wave in lateral leads. New LBBB with chest pain is treated as STEMI-equivalent.

facts.quizQuestion: Most venous blood from the myocardium drains via the great cardiac vein and its tributaries into which collecting structure, which then empties into which cardiac chamber?
facts.explanation: Approximately 75% of cardiac venous return drains through the great, middle, and small cardiac veins into the coronary sinus — a wide venous channel running in the posterior atrioventricular groove. The coronary sinus empties into the right atrium between the tricuspid valve and the opening of the inferior vena cava. The remaining ~25% drains via anterior cardiac veins directly into the right atrium and via the smallest cardiac veins (Thebesian veins) directly into all four chambers.
facts.visualDescription: Posterior view of heart: coronary sinus highlighted in blue running in AV groove, arrow pointing to its ostium in the right atrium

variants.quizQuestion: The smallest cardiac veins (Thebesian veins) drain directly into which cardiac chambers, bypassing the coronary sinus?
variants.explanation: Thebesian veins (venae cordis minimae) are tiny veins that open directly into all four chambers. Those draining into the left heart represent a small physiological right-to-left shunt, contributing a small amount to the normal anatomical dead-space. They are clinically significant as a potential route for paradoxical embolism.

facts.quizQuestion: The azygos system collects venous blood from the posterior thorax and abdomen. Into which great vessel does the azygos vein drain, and why is this system clinically important?
facts.explanation: The azygos vein ascends along the right side of the thoracic vertebral column and arches over the right main bronchus to drain into the superior vena cava (SVC). On the left, the hemiazygos and accessory hemiazygos veins cross to join the azygos. The azygos system forms a key collateral pathway: when the IVC is obstructed (e.g., by thrombosis, tumour, or interruption during surgery), blood from the lower body can bypass the blockage via lumbar veins → azygos → SVC → right atrium.
facts.visualDescription: Posterior thorax diagram: azygos vein running up right of spine with arch over right main bronchus into SVC; hemiazygos on left side crossing midline; arrows showing collateral flow

variants.quizQuestion: Azygos continuation of the inferior vena cava is a congenital variant in which the hepatic segment of the IVC is absent. Blood from the lower body then drains via the azygos system into which vessel?
variants.explanation: In azygos continuation, the infrarenal IVC is present but the hepatic IVC is absent; venous blood from the lower body reroutes via the azygos vein into the SVC. The hepatic veins drain directly into the right atrium. This variant is associated with asplenia/polysplenia syndromes and can complicate cardiac catheterisation or placement of IVC filters.

acceptableAlternatives: carotid body CN IX, aortic arch CN X
facts.quizQuestion: The baroreceptor reflex monitors blood pressure via stretch receptors in two main locations. Name these locations and the cranial nerves carrying the afferent signals.
facts.explanation: Baroreceptors are mechanoreceptors that detect stretch in arterial walls. The carotid sinus baroreceptors (at the bifurcation of the common carotid) send afferents via CN IX (glossopharyngeal nerve, Hering's nerve branch) to the nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS) in the medulla. The aortic arch baroreceptors send afferents via CN X (vagus nerve, aortic depressor nerve) to the same nucleus. The NTS then modulates sympathetic and parasympathetic outflow to adjust heart rate and vascular tone. Baroreceptors: carotid sinus innervated by CN IX (glossopharyngeal); aortic arch by CN X (vagus).
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of carotid bifurcation and aortic arch with baroreceptor locations marked; arrows showing CN IX and CN X pathways to the medullary NTS; efferent arrows to heart and blood vessels

variants.quizQuestion: When blood pressure rises, baroreceptor firing increases and the reflex response reduces heart rate. Which division of the autonomic nervous system is responsible for this heart-rate reduction?
variants.explanation: Increased baroreceptor firing inhibits the sympathetic cardioacceleratory centre and excites the vagal dorsal motor nucleus and nucleus ambiguus, increasing parasympathetic (vagal) tone to the SA node. This slows heart rate (negative chronotropy), helping to lower cardiac output and blood pressure back toward normal.

facts.quizQuestion: The ductus venosus shunts oxygenated umbilical blood past the fetal liver into the IVC. After birth, this vessel closes and becomes which fibrous remnant?
facts.explanation: In fetal circulation, the ductus venosus connects the left branch of the portal vein directly to the inferior vena cava, allowing ~50% of oxygenated umbilical venous blood to bypass the liver and reach the heart more quickly. When the umbilical vein is clamped at birth and portal venous pressure falls, the ductus venosus closes and fibrotises over 3–7 days to become the ligamentum venosum, a fibrous cord visible on the posterior surface of the liver.
facts.visualDescription: Fetal liver diagram: umbilical vein branching into portal circulation and ductus venosus shortcut to IVC; adult version shows same area with fibrous ligamentum venosum

variants.quizQuestion: The umbilical vein itself also persists as a fibrous remnant after birth, running in the free edge of the falciform ligament. What is this structure called?
variants.correctAnswer: Ligamentum teres hepatis (round ligament of liver)
variants.explanation: The left umbilical vein carries oxygenated blood from the placenta to the fetal liver. After birth it obliterates and becomes the ligamentum teres hepatis (round ligament of the liver), running from the umbilicus to the inferior hepatic notch in the free edge of the falciform ligament. In portal hypertension, collateral recanalization of this vessel produces caput medusae.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the normal systolic and diastolic pressures inside the left ventricle during the cardiac cycle?
facts.explanation: During systole the left ventricle generates ~120 mmHg to eject blood into the aorta. During diastole (relaxation and filling), left ventricular pressure falls to ~0–5 mmHg, allowing the mitral valve to open and the ventricle to fill passively. Left atrial pressure is also ~6–12 mmHg (similar to PCWP). The right ventricle operates at far lower pressures (~25/0–5 mmHg) because pulmonary vascular resistance is about 10× lower than systemic resistance.
facts.visualDescription: Wiggers diagram showing LV pressure curve: rising from ~5 mmHg, peak at ~120 mmHg during systole, falling back to ~5 mmHg in diastole; aortic pressure curve slightly below LV during ejection

variants.quizQuestion: On a Wiggers diagram, when the left ventricular pressure exceeds aortic pressure, which valve opens to allow ejection?
variants.explanation: The aortic valve (a semilunar valve) opens when left ventricular systolic pressure exceeds aortic diastolic pressure (~80 mmHg), initiating the ejection phase. It closes when LV pressure falls below aortic pressure at the end of systole, producing the dicrotic notch on the arterial pressure trace and the S2 heart sound.

facts.quizQuestion: The jugular (suprasternal) notch — the visible dip at the top of the sternum — corresponds to which vertebral level?
facts.explanation: The jugular notch is the concave superior border of the manubrium sterni, palpable in the midline at the base of the neck. It lies at the level of the T2–T3 intervertebral disc. Clinicians use it as an orientation landmark when counting ribs or assessing the trachea for deviation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior chest: sternal notch highlighted at top of manubrium, T2-T3 label on adjacent spine cross-section, trachea visible above

variants.question: Which palpable bony landmark on the sternum lies at the level of the T2–T3 vertebral disc?
The suprasternal notch is located at the ___ vertebral level.

facts.quizQuestion: The sternal angle (angle of Louis) is a critical clinical landmark. At which vertebral level does it lie?
facts.explanation: The sternal angle is the transverse ridge on the anterior chest wall where the manubrium meets the body of the sternum (the manubriosternal joint). It corresponds to the T4–T5 intervertebral disc. From this single palpable point, four landmarks can be identified: the 2nd rib cartilage (attaches here), the carina of the trachea, the aortic arch, and the division between the superior and inferior mediastinum.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art chest cross-section: manubriosternal junction glowing, lines radiating to rib 2, tracheal carina, aortic arch, all at same horizontal plane

variants.question: A clinician palpates the sternal angle (angle of Louis) to locate rib 2. What four structures all lie at the same horizontal plane as this landmark?
variants.correctAnswer: Rib 2 cartilage, carina, aortic arch, superior/inferior mediastinum border
distractors: Rib 4, heart apex, inferior vena cava, T6

variants.question: The sternal angle marks the junction of the manubrium and sternal body and lies at the ___ vertebral level.

facts.quizQuestion: The xiphoid process — the inferior tip of the sternum — corresponds to which vertebral level?
facts.explanation: The xiphoid process is the small cartilaginous/bony tip at the inferior end of the sternum, corresponding to the T9 vertebral level. It is an important CPR landmark: during chest compressions, the heel of the hand is placed on the lower half of the sternum, above the xiphoid, to avoid lacerating the liver or diaphragm with a displaced xiphoid.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art sternum from front: xiphoid process at lower tip highlighted red, spine silhouette alongside with T9 labeled
