variants.question: During CPR, why is it important to identify the xiphoid process before placing your hands for chest compressions?
variants.correctAnswer: To avoid pressing on the xiphoid, which can lacerate the liver or diaphragm
distractors: To find the correct rib for needle decompression
To locate the cardiac apex for auscultation
To identify the costal margin for liver palpation

variants.question: The xiphoid process at the inferior tip of the sternum lies at the ___ vertebral level.

facts.quizQuestion: The anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) is a prominent bony landmark on the hip. What major ligament attaches to it medially?
facts.explanation: The ASIS is the anterior projection of the iliac crest — the bony bump palpable at the front of the hip. The inguinal ligament runs from the ASIS to the pubic tubercle, forming the inferior boundary of the inguinal triangle. The ASIS is also used to locate McBurney's point (appendicitis), the femoral pulse (midinguinal point), and as a bony landmark for leg length measurement.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art pelvis anterior view: ASIS highlighted on right ilium, dashed line of inguinal ligament running to pubic tubercle, femoral vessels passing beneath

variants.question: The inguinal ligament runs between which two bony landmarks?
The ___ ligament attaches medially to the anterior superior iliac spine and forms the floor of the inguinal canal.

facts.quizQuestion: The ischial tuberosity — the 'sit bones' palpable when the hip is flexed — is the origin of which muscle group?
facts.explanation: The ischial tuberosity is the posteroinferior projection of the ischium, easily palpated through the gluteal fold when the hip is flexed (the gluteus maximus slides superiorly, exposing it). It is the common origin for all three hamstring muscles: biceps femoris (long head), semitendinosus, and semimembranosus. Avulsion fractures of the ischial tuberosity can occur in sprinters during explosive hamstring contraction.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior pelvis: ischial tuberosity highlighted, three hamstring muscles (labeled) originating from it and descending down the posterior thigh

variants.question: A sprinter feels a sharp posterior thigh pain and 'pop' at the hip after an explosive start. Imaging shows an avulsion fracture of the ischial tuberosity. Which muscle group avulsed it?
The ischial tuberosity is the origin of the ___ muscle group, which flex the knee and extend the hip.

acceptableAlternatives: halfway between ASIS and pubic symphysis
facts.quizQuestion: Where is the femoral pulse palpated on the surface of the thigh?
facts.explanation: The femoral pulse is palpated just below the inguinal ligament at the midinguinal point — the midpoint between the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) and the pubic symphysis. The femoral artery, vein, and nerve pass under the inguinal ligament in the femoral triangle at this location (from lateral to medial: nerve, artery, vein — remembered as NAV). This is also the site for femoral artery access in cardiac catheterization.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art groin region: inguinal ligament as dashed line from ASIS to pubic tubercle, midpoint marked with pulsating dot showing femoral artery, NAV arrangement labeled
distractors: Midpoint of inguinal ligament between ASIS and pubic tubercle
At the midpoint of the thigh

variants.question: Using the mnemonic NAV, what is the lateral-to-medial arrangement of structures passing under the inguinal ligament at the femoral pulse point?
The femoral pulse is palpated just below the inguinal ligament at the ___ — halfway between the ASIS and pubic symphysis.

acceptableAlternatives: dorsum of foot between 1st and 2nd metatarsal
facts.quizQuestion: Where is the dorsalis pedis pulse palpated on the foot?
facts.explanation: The dorsalis pedis artery is the continuation of the anterior tibial artery onto the dorsum of the foot. Its pulse is palpated on the dorsal surface of the foot, in the groove between the tendons of extensor hallucis longus and extensor digitorum longus — which corresponds to the space between the 1st and 2nd metatarsal bones. It is assessed routinely in peripheral vascular disease and after lower limb surgery or trauma.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art dorsal foot view: dorsalis pedis artery highlighted between 1st and 2nd metatarsals, pulsating dot at palpation site, extensor tendons labeled alongside

variants.question: The dorsalis pedis artery — assessed in peripheral vascular disease — is the continuation of which artery onto the dorsum of the foot?
The dorsalis pedis pulse is palpated on the dorsum of the foot between the ___ and ___ metatarsals.

acceptableAlternatives: lateral wrist between FCR and radial styloid
facts.quizQuestion: Where is the radial pulse palpated at the wrist?
facts.explanation: The radial artery runs along the lateral (radial) aspect of the forearm and is palpated at the wrist between the tendon of flexor carpi radialis (FCR) medially and the distal radius laterally. It is the most commonly used site for pulse measurement. The artery then passes through the anatomical snuffbox before entering the palm to form the deep palmar arch.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art wrist cross-section: radial artery highlighted between FCR tendon and radius, fingers pressing over pulsating vessel
distractors: Between FCR and flexor carpi ulnaris

variants.question: After passing through the anatomical snuffbox, the radial artery enters the palm and forms which arterial arch?
The radial pulse is palpated at the wrist between the flexor carpi radialis tendon and the ___.

facts.correctAnswer: 1/3 of the way from the ASIS to the umbilicus
acceptableAlternatives: one third from ASIS to umbilicus
one-third distance from anterior superior iliac spine to navel
facts.quizQuestion: McBurney's point is the classic surface landmark for appendicitis tenderness. Where is it located?
facts.explanation: McBurney's point lies at the junction of the outer one-third and inner two-thirds of the line drawn from the right anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the umbilicus — in other words, 1/3 of the distance from the ASIS. Direct tenderness at this point (McBurney's sign) is highly suggestive of acute appendicitis. The appendix tip typically overlies this point when the base is at the ileocaecal junction.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art abdomen anterior: dashed line from right ASIS to umbilicus, red dot at 1/3 from ASIS labeled McBurney's point, appendix outline beneath

variants.question: Rebound tenderness at McBurney's point (pain on sudden release of pressure worse than on pressing) suggests peritoneal irritation. In the context of right iliac fossa pain, what diagnosis does this most suggest?
McBurney's point is located ___ of the way from the right ASIS to the umbilicus.

facts.quizQuestion: Murphy's sign is elicited by palpating the right upper quadrant deeply during inspiration. Arrest of inspiration due to pain constitutes a positive sign. What condition does it indicate?
facts.explanation: Murphy's sign is tested by hooking the fingers under the right costal margin in the midclavicular line (over the gallbladder fossa) and asking the patient to breathe in deeply. As the diaphragm descends, the inflamed gallbladder is pushed onto the examiner's fingers, causing pain that halts inspiration — a positive sign. It has high sensitivity for acute cholecystitis. The left side is tested as a control (negative). A positive Murphy's ultrasound sign can also be elicited sonographically.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art clinical exam: examiner's hand pressing under right costal margin, patient silhouette inhaling, gallbladder descending into hand with pain indicator

variants.question: Which clinical sign involves arrest of inspiration during deep RUQ palpation, indicating an inflamed gallbladder?
Murphy's sign, elicited by palpating under the right costal margin during inspiration, is positive in acute ___.

facts.correctAnswer: Gluteus medius weakness (superior gluteal nerve palsy)
facts.quizQuestion: The Trendelenburg test: a patient stands on one leg and the pelvis drops on the contralateral (opposite) side. What does this indicate?
facts.explanation: Normally when standing on one leg, the gluteus medius on the stance-leg side contracts to keep the pelvis level (or raise the contralateral side slightly). A positive Trendelenburg — where the unsupported side drops — means the ipsilateral gluteus medius is failing. This muscle is innervated by the superior gluteal nerve (L4–S1). Causes include superior gluteal nerve damage, hip pathology (e.g., hip OA), or proximal femoral fracture. The patient may also show a Trendelenburg gait (lurching to the affected side while walking to compensate).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art stick figure standing on right leg: left pelvis side drooping down, right hip gluteus medius labeled with X, superior gluteal nerve pathway highlighted

variants.question: The superior gluteal nerve (L4–S1) innervates gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, and tensor fasciae latae. What is the nerve root level of this nerve?
A positive Trendelenburg sign indicates weakness of ___ on the side that is bearing weight.

facts.quizQuestion: Phalen's test: the patient holds the wrists in full flexion for 60 seconds. Tingling or numbness in the thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger constitutes a positive test. What does this diagnose?
facts.explanation: Phalen's test (wrist flexion test) reproduces symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome by maximally narrowing the carpal tunnel, compressing the median nerve. The median nerve supplies sensation to the lateral 3.5 fingers (thumb, index, middle, and lateral half of ring finger) — tingling in this distribution after 60 seconds is positive. The thenar muscles (abductor pollicis brevis, opponens pollicis, flexor pollicis brevis) may waste if chronic. Tinel's sign (tapping over the flexor retinaculum) is a complementary test.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art hands pressed back-to-back with wrists maximally flexed, median nerve highlighted in carpal tunnel, tingling sensation radiating to thumb and first two fingers

variants.question: In carpal tunnel syndrome, which specific muscle group in the hand wastes with chronic median nerve compression?
variants.correctAnswer: Thenar muscles (abductor pollicis brevis, opponens pollicis, flexor pollicis brevis)

variants.question: Phalen's test provokes symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome by compressing the ___ nerve under the flexor retinaculum.

facts.quizQuestion: Tinel's sign: tapping over a nerve produces tingling that radiates distally along its distribution. What does a positive Tinel's sign indicate?
facts.explanation: Tinel's sign is a clinical test for nerve compression or regeneration. When a compressed or regenerating nerve is percussed, mechanically sensitive axons depolarize and produce paraesthesia (tingling) in the distribution of that nerve distally — this is a positive Tinel's sign. It is used at the wrist (carpal tunnel — median nerve), elbow (cubital tunnel — ulnar nerve), and along any compressed peripheral nerve. In nerve regeneration after injury, the advancing front of regenerating axons produces Tinel's sign, and its distal progression over weeks indicates active recovery.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art wrist: finger tapping over carpal tunnel area, lightning bolt symbol radiating distally to fingertips along median nerve pathway

variants.question: After a radial nerve injury at the arm, a physician checks Tinel's sign weekly by tapping along the nerve's course. The point of tingling moves progressively more distally each week. What does this indicate?
Tinel's sign is elicited by ___ over a nerve, producing distal tingling in its distribution.

facts.quizQuestion: Which clinical test for ACL integrity is performed with the knee flexed to 20–30° and is considered the most sensitive test for ACL rupture?
facts.explanation: The Lachman test is the most sensitive clinical test for ACL rupture (sensitivity ~85%). With the knee at 20–30° flexion, the examiner stabilizes the femur with one hand and pulls the tibia anteriorly with the other. Excessive anterior tibial translation with a soft or absent endpoint is a positive result, indicating ACL disruption. At this angle of flexion, the posterior capsule and hamstrings do not mask the result (unlike the anterior drawer test at 90°).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lateral knee view: knee at 30° flexion, arrow showing anterior tibial translation, examiner hands stabilizing femur and pulling tibia, ACL labeled as torn

variants.question: Why is the Lachman test more sensitive for ACL rupture than the anterior drawer test (which uses 90° knee flexion)?
variants.correctAnswer: At 20–30° flexion, the posterior capsule and hamstrings don't mask tibial translation
distractors: The ACL is under more tension at 20–30° flexion
The PCL cannot confound the result at low flexion
The knee joint is more unstable at low flexion angles
The examiner can apply more force at low flexion

variants.question: The Lachman test is performed with the knee at ___ degrees of flexion and is the most sensitive test for ACL rupture.

facts.quizQuestion: The anterior drawer test is performed with the knee at 90° flexion. The tibia is pulled forward. Excessive forward translation indicates rupture of which ligament?
facts.explanation: The anterior drawer test assesses the integrity of the ACL. With the patient supine, hip at 45°, knee at 90°, and foot flat (examiner sits on the foot to stabilize), the tibia is pulled anteriorly. Excessive translation (>5–8mm compared to the other side) or a soft endpoint indicates ACL rupture. This test has lower sensitivity than the Lachman test because at 90° flexion, the posterior capsule and hamstrings may provide a false-negative result by restraining tibial translation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lateral knee at 90° flexion: anterior arrow on tibia, ACL ligament shown torn or absent, posterior capsule labeled as secondary restraint

variants.question: The classic mechanism for an ACL tear in sport is a non-contact deceleration with the knee near extension and the foot planted. Why does this injure the ACL specifically?
variants.correctAnswer: The ACL resists anterior tibial translation; deceleration with foot planted creates this force
distractors: The ACL resists valgus stress, which peaks during deceleration
The ACL resists hyperflexion, which occurs during this mechanism
The ACL is stressed by tibial rotation alone
The ACL is weakest when the knee is fully extended

variants.question: The anterior drawer test, performed with the knee at 90° flexion, tests the integrity of the ___ ligament.

facts.quizQuestion: The McMurray test: the knee is taken from full flexion and progressively extended while applying rotational stress. A palpable click or pain at the joint line is a positive test. What does it diagnose?
facts.explanation: The McMurray test is used to detect meniscal tears. With the patient supine, the examiner fully flexes the knee, then slowly extends it while applying either internal rotation (testing lateral meniscus) or external rotation (testing medial meniscus) with axial load. A palpable or audible click, clunk, or pain along the joint line at the level of the meniscus constitutes a positive result. The medial meniscus is torn more frequently than the lateral due to its attachment to the MCL.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art knee from lateral view: knee moving from full flexion toward extension, examiner's hands applying rotation, click symbol at medial joint line, meniscus outline highlighted

variants.question: During McMurray testing, external tibial rotation stresses the medial meniscus while internal rotation stresses the lateral meniscus. Why is the medial meniscus more commonly torn?
variants.correctAnswer: It is attached to the MCL, making it less mobile and more vulnerable to shear forces
distractors: It is thicker and thus under greater mechanical load
It lacks a blood supply throughout, making it weaker
It is compressed more during weight-bearing
The lateral meniscus is protected by the popliteus tendon

variants.question: The McMurray test detects ___ tears by reproducing a click or pain at the joint line during rotated knee extension.

facts.quizQuestion: Babinski's sign: stroking the lateral plantar surface of the foot causes extension of the great toe and fanning of other toes. In adults, what does this indicate?
facts.explanation: Babinski's sign is elicited by firmly stroking the lateral plantar surface of the foot from heel to ball, then across the ball to the great toe. In healthy adults, the response is plantar flexion of all toes (downgoing). An upgoing great toe with fanning of the others (positive Babinski) indicates disruption of the corticospinal (pyramidal) tract — an upper motor neuron lesion. It is NORMAL in infants under 2 years old because myelination is incomplete. Causes in adults include stroke, multiple sclerosis, spinal cord injury, ALS, and tumors.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art foot sole: key sliding laterally from heel toward toes, great toe extending upward with arrows showing fan pattern of lateral toes, red alert symbol

variants.question: Babinski's sign is normal in infants under 2 years old. Why?
variants.correctAnswer: Myelination of the corticospinal tract is incomplete, so the reflex arc is not yet fully inhibited
distractors: Infants have immature plantar skin receptors
Infants have a different nerve supply to the sole
The reflex is mediated by a different pathway in infants
Infant tendons are too lax to show the normal downgoing response

variants.question: A positive Babinski sign in an adult — upgoing great toe on stroking the lateral sole — indicates a ___ motor neuron lesion.

facts.quizQuestion: Romberg's test: the patient stands with feet together and eyes closed. Falling or significant sway is a positive result. What does this indicate?
facts.explanation: Romberg's test assesses proprioception. Standing requires three inputs: vision, vestibular function, and proprioception (dorsal columns). Closing the eyes removes visual input — patients with proprioceptive loss (dorsal column dysfunction) can compensate while eyes are open but lose balance when vision is removed. A positive Romberg indicates dorsal column pathology (e.g., subacute combined degeneration from B12 deficiency, tabes dorsalis, Friedrich's ataxia). It does NOT indicate cerebellar disease — cerebellar patients are unsteady whether eyes open or closed.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art standing figure with feet together, eyes closed, wobbling, dorsal spinal column highlighted, proprioceptive pathway shown ascending to brain

variants.question: A patient with B12 deficiency develops a positive Romberg's sign. What spinal cord tract is affected, and what is this condition called?
variants.correctAnswer: Dorsal columns; subacute combined degeneration of the cord

variants.question: A positive Romberg test (falling with eyes closed, stable with eyes open) indicates ___ loss due to dorsal column dysfunction.

facts.quizQuestion: Rotator cuff tears most commonly involve which specific muscle?
facts.explanation: The supraspinatus is the most commonly torn rotator cuff muscle, accounting for roughly 90% of rotator cuff tears. It originates from the supraspinous fossa of the scapula and inserts on the superior facet of the greater tubercle of the humerus. It initiates abduction (first 0–15°) and is mechanically stressed where its tendon passes under the coracoacromial arch. The characteristic finding is a painful arc between 60–120° of abduction, when the tendon is most compressed under the acromion.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art shoulder cross-section: supraspinatus muscle and tendon highlighted, painful arc zone marked at 60-120° during shoulder abduction, coracoacromial arch above

variants.question: A supraspinatus tear produces a 'painful arc' of shoulder abduction. Between which degrees of abduction is pain maximal?
The ___ muscle is most commonly torn in rotator cuff injuries and produces a painful arc between 60–120° of abduction.

acceptableAlternatives: median nerve entrapment at the wrist
median nerve compression at carpal tunnel
facts.quizQuestion: Carpal tunnel syndrome causes tingling in the lateral 3.5 fingers and thenar wasting. What is the underlying anatomical pathology?
facts.explanation: The carpal tunnel is formed by the concave carpal bones posteriorly and the flexor retinaculum (transverse carpal ligament) anteriorly. Nine flexor tendons and the median nerve pass through this rigid tunnel. When tunnel pressure rises (from tenosynovitis, fluid retention in pregnancy, hypothyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis, or repetitive wrist flexion), the median nerve is compressed. This causes sensory loss in the lateral 3.5 fingers and, in chronic cases, wasting of the thenar muscles (abductor pollicis brevis, opponens pollicis, and superficial head of flexor pollicis brevis — all median-innervated). Carpal tunnel: median nerve is compressed under the flexor retinaculum (transverse carpal ligament).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art wrist cross-section: carpal tunnel bounded by carpal bones and flexor retinaculum, median nerve compressed centrally, nine flexor tendons surrounding it
distractors: Ulnar nerve compressed at Guyon's canal
Radial nerve compressed at the wrist
Median nerve compressed at the elbow

variants.question: Which three thenar muscles waste in chronic carpal tunnel syndrome due to median nerve compression?
variants.correctAnswer: Abductor pollicis brevis, opponens pollicis, flexor pollicis brevis (superficial head)
distractors: Adductor pollicis, abductor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis
All four thenar muscles including adductor pollicis
Flexor pollicis longus, abductor pollicis longus, opponens pollicis
First dorsal interosseous, adductor pollicis, flexor pollicis brevis

variants.question: The carpal tunnel is roofed by the flexor ___, the rigid structure whose lack of expandability causes median nerve compression.

facts.quizQuestion: Sciatica presents with pain radiating from the buttock down the posterior thigh and leg. Which nerve roots contribute to the sciatic nerve?
facts.explanation: The sciatic nerve is the largest nerve in the body, formed from the anterior rami of L4, L5, S1, S2, and S3. It exits the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen, usually below piriformis, and divides into the tibial nerve and common fibular (peroneal) nerve at the apex of the popliteal fossa. Most sciatica is caused by a prolapsed lumbar disc compressing a nerve root — most commonly L4/L5 (L5 root) or L5/S1 (S1 root). True sciatic nerve damage from piriformis syndrome is rare.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior pelvis and thigh: sciatic nerve highlighted from lumbar plexus (L4-S3) through greater sciatic foramen, descending to posterior thigh, branching to tibial and common fibular

variants.question: A patient with sciatica has loss of ankle dorsiflexion (foot drop) and numbness over the dorsum of the foot. Which nerve root is most likely compressed?
variants.correctAnswer: L4–L5 disc prolapse compressing L5 root
distractors: L3–L4 disc prolapse compressing L4 root
L5–S1 disc prolapse compressing S1 root
S1–S2 disc prolapse compressing S2 root
L2–L3 disc prolapse compressing L3 root

variants.question: The sciatic nerve is formed from nerve roots ___, the largest nerve in the body.

facts.correctAnswer: Radial nerve in spiral groove (Saturday night palsy)
acceptableAlternatives: radial nerve compression at spiral groove
facts.quizQuestion: Saturday night palsy causes wrist drop and loss of finger/thumb extension. What is the anatomical basis?
facts.explanation: Radial nerve compression in the spiral groove of the humerus causes Saturday night palsy (wrist drop). Saturday night palsy (radial nerve palsy from prolonged arm compression) occurs when the radial nerve is compressed against the spiral groove of the humerus — classically while someone sleeps deeply (due to alcohol intoxication) with their arm draped over a chair or bed edge. The radial nerve supplies all wrist and finger extensors. The result is wrist drop (inability to extend the wrist) and inability to extend the fingers and thumb at the MCP joints. Sensation over the dorsal hand (dorsal web space of thumb) is also impaired. Supinator, brachioradialis, and long head of triceps are typically spared (they branch above the spiral groove). Radial nerve palsy from compression against the humerus in the spiral groove → wrist drop.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art arm hanging limply over chair back at night, radial nerve highlighted at spiral groove of humerus, wrist drooping with fingers curled

variants.question: In Saturday night palsy, triceps function is typically preserved despite wrist drop. Why?
variants.correctAnswer: The branch to triceps leaves the radial nerve above the spiral groove where compression occurs
distractors: Triceps is innervated by the ulnar nerve
The spiral groove compresses only motor fibers below the elbow
Triceps has dual innervation from the axillary nerve
The spiral groove is distal to where the radial nerve leaves the axilla

variants.question: Saturday night palsy involves compression of the ___ nerve at the spiral groove of the humerus, causing wrist drop.

facts.quizQuestion: Erb-Duchenne palsy produces a 'waiter's tip' arm position. Which nerve roots are damaged?
facts.explanation: Erb-Duchenne palsy results from traction injury to the upper trunk of the brachial plexus (C5–C6), typically during difficult delivery (shoulder dystocia) when the head is forcefully laterally flexed away from the shoulder, or in adults from falls on the shoulder/neck. The resulting 'waiter's tip' position shows: arm adducted and internally rotated (deltoid/supraspinatus lost), forearm extended and pronated (biceps/brachialis lost), wrist flexed. C5–C6 supply the deltoid (via axillary nerve), supraspinatus (suprascapular nerve), biceps (musculocutaneous), brachialis, and brachioradialis.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art infant or adult arm: limb hanging with shoulder adducted and internally rotated, elbow extended, wrist flexed in classic waiter's tip posture, C5-C6 nerve roots highlighted at neck

variants.question: Erb-Duchenne palsy commonly follows shoulder dystocia in neonates. The classic mechanism is lateral neck flexion away from the shoulder. Which point on the brachial plexus is the lesion site?
variants.correctAnswer: Erb's point — junction of C5 and C6 roots forming the upper trunk, just above the clavicle
distractors: The posterior cord at the axilla
The medial cord at the clavicle
The lateral cord below the clavicle
The C5–C6 roots at the intervertebral foramina

variants.question: Erb-Duchenne palsy (waiter's tip position) results from injury to ___ nerve roots of the brachial plexus.

facts.quizQuestion: Klumpke palsy produces a 'claw hand' deformity. Which nerve roots are damaged?
facts.explanation: Klumpke palsy results from injury to the lower trunk of the brachial plexus (C8–T1), typically caused by forceful arm abduction (e.g., grabbing a branch during a fall, birth injury with arm traction). C8–T1 supply the intrinsic hand muscles via the ulnar nerve (interossei, hypothenar, medial two lumbricals) and median nerve (lateral two lumbricals, thenar). Loss of intrinsic muscles causes hyperextension at the MCP joints and flexion at the IPJ joints — the 'claw hand.' If T1 is also damaged, Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) may occur ipsilaterally.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art hand showing claw deformity: MCPs hyperextended, IP joints flexed, hypothenar and interosseous wasting, C8-T1 nerve roots labeled at cervical spine

variants.question: A patient with Klumpke palsy (C8–T1 injury) also has ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis on the same side. What additional structure is involved?
variants.correctAnswer: T1 sympathetic fibers (Horner's syndrome from disruption of the sympathetic chain)
distractors: Facial nerve on the same side
Vagus nerve at the thoracic inlet

variants.question: Klumpke palsy causing claw hand results from injury to ___ nerve roots, affecting intrinsic hand muscles.

facts.correctAnswer: Supraspinatus: from supraspinous fossa to greater tubercle
acceptableAlternatives: scapula supraspinous fossa to humerus greater tubercle superior facet
facts.quizQuestion: What is the origin and insertion of the supraspinatus muscle?
facts.explanation: Supraspinatus (SITS) runs from the supraspinous fossa to insert on the greater tubercle of the humerus. Supraspinatus originates from the supraspinous fossa of the scapula (above the scapular spine) and inserts onto the superior facet of the greater tubercle of the humerus. It is innervated by the suprascapular nerve (C5–C6) and initiates shoulder abduction from 0–15° before the deltoid takes over. Its tendon passes under the coracoacromial arch between the acromion and humeral head — the area of impingement in rotator cuff pathology. Supraspinatus origin: supraspinous fossa of scapula. Insertion: superior facet of the greater tubercle of the humerus.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior scapula view: supraspinous fossa highlighted above spine of scapula, dotted line to insertion on superior greater tubercle of humerus, suprascapular nerve labeled
distractors: Infraspinous fossa to middle facet of greater tubercle
Spine of scapula to greater tubercle
Infraspinous fossa to greater tubercle superior facet

distractors: All four insert on the greater tubercle

variants.question: The supraspinatus originates from the ___ fossa of the scapula and is innervated by the suprascapular nerve (C5–C6).

facts.quizQuestion: Trendelenburg gait results from weakness of which nerve and its muscles?
facts.explanation: The superior gluteal nerve (L4–S1) innervates gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, and tensor fasciae latae — the hip abductors that stabilize the pelvis during single-leg stance. When this nerve is damaged (e.g., from a poorly placed gluteal injection, hip surgery, or pelvic fracture), patients develop a Trendelenburg gait: the pelvis drops on the unsupported side at each step, and the patient lurches the trunk toward the affected (weak) side to shift the centre of gravity and compensate.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art walking figure: pelvis tilting down on unsupported right side, left leg bearing weight, gluteus medius on left side marked with X, superior gluteal nerve highlighted
distractors: Posterior cutaneous nerve of the thigh

variants.question: Intramuscular injections to the gluteal region risk injuring the superior gluteal nerve. Where should gluteal injections be given to avoid this nerve?
variants.correctAnswer: Upper outer quadrant of the gluteus (ventrogluteal or upper-outer gluteal), away from the nerve's path through the greater sciatic notch
distractors: Lower inner quadrant, away from the sciatic nerve
Just below the posterior superior iliac spine

variants.question: The superior gluteal nerve (L4–S1) innervates gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, and ___, which together abduct the hip and stabilize the pelvis.

facts.quizQuestion: In carpal tunnel syndrome, which rigid roof structure of the carpal tunnel is divided during surgical decompression?
facts.explanation: The carpal tunnel is bounded posteriorly and laterally by the concave carpal bones and anteriorly (as its roof) by the flexor retinaculum (transverse carpal ligament). During carpal tunnel release surgery, the flexor retinaculum is divided longitudinally to decompress the median nerve. The procedure can be performed open (through a palmar incision) or endoscopically. Anatomical variations in the median nerve (bifid nerve, persistent median artery) must be identified to avoid iatrogenic damage.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art wrist cross-section: carpal tunnel with flexor retinaculum roof highlighted, median nerve and nine flexor tendons inside, surgical blade cutting through retinaculum

variants.question: Nine structures pass through the carpal tunnel alongside the median nerve. What are they?
variants.correctAnswer: 4 tendons of FDS, 4 tendons of FDP, and 1 tendon of FPL (nine flexor tendons total)
distractors: 4 FDS, 4 FDP, and the ulnar nerve
4 FDS, 4 FDP, and the median artery
4 FDS, 3 FDP, 1 FPL, and 1 palmar artery

variants.question: Carpal tunnel release surgery divides the ___ retinaculum to decompress the median nerve.

facts.correctAnswer: Anterior tibial translation with a soft endpoint
facts.quizQuestion: What finding on the Lachman test constitutes a positive result indicating ACL rupture?
facts.explanation: A positive Lachman test is characterized by: (1) excessive anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur (more than the unaffected side, typically >5mm), AND (2) a soft or absent endpoint — instead of the normal firm 'hard stop' as the intact ACL becomes taut, the examiner feels little or no resistance to further translation. The combination of increased translation AND a soft endpoint differentiates true ACL rupture from laxity or a partial tear. Grade I = firm endpoint, minimal laxity; Grade II = firm endpoint, moderate laxity; Grade III = soft endpoint, marked laxity (complete tear).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art knee Lachman test: tibia translating forward from femur, soft endpoint shown with dashed terminus (no firm stop), ACL absent from joint interior

variants.question: Lachman grading: Grade III laxity means a soft endpoint and marked tibial translation — indicating complete ACL rupture. What does Grade I mean?
variants.correctAnswer: Firm endpoint with minimal tibial translation (ACL likely intact or partial tear only)

variants.question: A positive Lachman test shows excessive anterior tibial translation combined with a ___ endpoint, distinguishing complete ACL rupture from laxity.

facts.quizQuestion: A 68-year-old presents after a sudden onset of left-sided weakness. Examination reveals right-sided hyperreflexia, spasticity, and a positive Babinski sign. What class of lesion does this constellation suggest?
facts.explanation: The triad of hyperreflexia, spasticity, and positive Babinski sign is the hallmark of an upper motor neuron (UMN) lesion — specifically damage to the corticospinal (pyramidal) tract. In the context of sudden onset, stroke (ischaemic or haemorrhagic) is the most common cause. Note the contralateral signs: left-sided weakness with right-sided examination findings indicates the lesion is above the decussation in the medulla, i.e., in the right cerebral hemisphere or right brainstem above the pyramidal decussation. UMN signs contrast with LMN signs (flaccid paralysis, hyporeflexia, fasciculations, muscle atrophy).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art brain cross-section: right hemisphere infarct highlighted, descending corticospinal tract shown decussating at medulla, left body affected on clinical diagram

variants.question: UMN lesion signs include spasticity, hyperreflexia, and Babinski. LMN lesion signs include flaccid weakness, hyporeflexia, and fasciculations. Which pattern would you expect from an S1 nerve root compression causing foot drop?
variants.correctAnswer: LMN signs — hyporeflexia (loss of ankle reflex), flaccid weakness, possible fasciculations
distractors: UMN signs — spasticity with preserved reflexes
Mixed UMN and LMN signs equally
Neither — nerve root compression causes only sensory loss

variants.question: Hyperreflexia, spasticity, and positive Babinski sign together indicate a ___ motor neuron lesion.

facts.correctAnswer: Lateral plantar surface, stroked from heel toward toes
acceptableAlternatives: outer sole from heel to ball
lateral sole of foot heel to toe
lateral plantar aspect heel to metatarsal heads
facts.quizQuestion: How is Babinski's sign correctly elicited — what is the exact technique?
facts.explanation: Babinski's sign is correctly elicited using a blunt instrument (e.g., a key or the handle of a tendon hammer) to firmly stroke the lateral plantar surface of the foot, beginning at the heel, moving toward the little toe, and then curving medially across the metatarsal heads toward the great toe. A key point: the stimulus must be on the lateral sole (not the medial sole or dorsum). Stroking too medially stimulates a different reflex. The normal adult response is plantar flexion of all toes. An abnormal (positive) response is extension of the great toe with fanning of the lateral toes.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art plantar foot: blue arrow starting at heel, curving laterally along outer edge to little toe, then sweeping medially across metatarsal heads to great toe, illustrating correct Babinski stroke path
distractors: Medial plantar surface from heel to great toe
Dorsum of the foot from ankle to toes
Lateral plantar surface from toes to heel
Central plantar surface in a straight line
Scratching the dorsum of the great toe

variants.question: You stroke the lateral sole and the great toe flexes downward with all toes curling — the normal adult response. What is the name of this normal plantar reflex?
variants.correctAnswer: Plantar flexion response (normal flexor plantar reflex)

variants.question: Babinski's sign is elicited by stroking the ___ plantar surface from heel toward toes, provoking great toe extension in UMN lesions.

facts.quizQuestion: Acute compartment syndrome most commonly affects which compartment?
facts.explanation: The anterior compartment of the leg is the most frequent site of acute compartment syndrome. It contains tibialis anterior, extensor digitorum longus, extensor hallucis longus, and the deep peroneal nerve — tight unyielding fascia means even modest pressure rises rapidly reach the threshold for ischaemia. Treatment is emergency four-compartment fasciotomy.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of lower leg: four compartments labelled, anterior compartment highlighted red with pressure arrows, deep peroneal nerve glowing inside

variants.question: A patient with a tibial fracture develops the '6 P's' — pain, pressure, pulselessness, paralysis, paresthesia, and pallor. What is the emergency treatment?
variants.correctAnswer: Fasciotomy (urgent surgical release of compartment fascia)

variants.question: The ___ compartment of the leg is most commonly affected by acute compartment syndrome.

facts.quizQuestion: Anterior shoulder dislocation — which accounts for 95% of all shoulder dislocations — puts which nerve most at risk?
facts.explanation: In anterior dislocation the humeral head translates anteriorly and inferiorly, placing direct traction on the axillary nerve as it winds around the surgical neck of the humerus. The axillary nerve innervates deltoid and teres minor and supplies sensation to the 'regimental badge' area (lateral upper arm). Clinical check: test deltoid contraction and lateral shoulder sensation immediately after reduction.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art shoulder girdle: humeral head displaced anteriorly, axillary nerve highlighted orange at surgical neck, 'regimental badge' sensory patch outlined

variants.question: After reduction of an anterior shoulder dislocation, the patient cannot abduct the shoulder and has numbness over the lateral arm. Which nerve is injured?
Anterior shoulder dislocation accounts for approximately ___% of all glenohumeral dislocations.

facts.quizQuestion: Hip fractures are classified as intracapsular or extracapsular. Which type carries the higher risk of avascular necrosis of the femoral head?
facts.explanation: The blood supply to the femoral head (primarily retinacular branches of the medial femoral circumflex artery running along the femoral neck) lies within the joint capsule. Intracapsular fractures tear these vessels, leaving only the small artery of the ligamentum teres. Extracapsular fractures (intertrochanteric, subtrochanteric) spare capsular vessels and rarely cause AVN. The Garden classification (I–IV) grades intracapsular fracture displacement; grades III–IV typically require arthroplasty.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art proximal femur: joint capsule outlined, retinacular vessels running up femoral neck highlighted red, intracapsular fracture line severing them, femoral head shaded grey to indicate ischaemia risk
facts.sourceName: Geeky Medics — Intracapsular vs Extracapsular Hip Fractures
distractors: Stress fractures of the femoral neck

variants.question: The Garden classification is used to grade which type of hip fracture, and why does grade IV carry the highest risk of avascular necrosis?
variants.correctAnswer: Femoral neck (intracapsular) fractures; Grade IV is fully displaced, completely disrupting retinacular blood vessels
distractors: Intertrochanteric fractures; displacement tears the ilio-femoral ligament
Subtrochanteric fractures; comminution blocks fixation devices
Pubic rami fractures; proximity to obturator vessels

variants.question: The ___ artery provides the main blood supply to the femoral head via retinacular branches running along the femoral neck.

facts.quizQuestion: Forced inversion of the ankle most commonly tears which ligament?
facts.explanation: The anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) runs from the anterior border of the lateral malleolus to the lateral talar neck. It is the weakest of the lateral ankle ligaments and resists inversion and plantar-flexion. It is injured first in approximately 66–85% of lateral ankle sprains. With greater force the calcaneofibular ligament (CFL) tears next, then the posterior talofibular ligament (PTFL).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lateral ankle: fibula, talus, calcaneus visible; ATFL highlighted red, CFL and PTFL shown in grey; inversion arrow applied to foot

variants.question: A footballer rolls their ankle inward. On examination there is tenderness at the anterior inferior fibula and talar neck. Which structure is most likely torn?
The ATFL connects the anterior border of the ___ to the neck of the talus.

facts.quizQuestion: Female athletes tear their ACL at what rate compared with males playing the same sports?
facts.explanation: Female ACL injury incidence is estimated at 2–4 times higher than males in matched sports. Contributing factors include a narrower intercondylar notch, wider Q-angle, smaller ACL cross-sectional area, greater posterior tibial slope, and neuromuscular differences — females land with greater knee valgus and lower flexion angles. The classic mechanism is a non-contact pivot-shift: the knee is slightly flexed, loaded in valgus, with tibial internal rotation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art knee joint: ACL fibres highlighted, intercondylar notch narrow, valgus collapse arrow on knee, female silhouette landing
facts.sourceName: PMC — Non-contact ACL Injuries: Mechanisms and Risk Factors
distractors: Equal rates in males and females
Males have higher ACL injury rates

variants.question: A female footballer hears a 'pop' while cutting and pivoting without contact, then has immediate swelling. What is the most likely injury and the classic mechanism?
variants.correctAnswer: ACL tear; non-contact pivot-shift with valgus loading and tibial internal rotation
distractors: Medial meniscus tear; direct valgus blow
PCL tear; posterior tibial force with knee flexed

variants.question: The ACL runs from the ___ tibial plateau to attach on the medial wall of the lateral femoral condyle.

facts.quizQuestion: Meniscal tears are twice as common on which side of the knee?
facts.explanation: Medial meniscus tears outnumber lateral tears approximately 2:1. The medial meniscus is more firmly attached to the medial capsule and medial collateral ligament, giving it less mobility to escape shear forces. The McMurray test (rotation with compression of the flexed knee) produces a palpable click or pain over the joint line. A bucket-handle tear can produce a 'locked knee' — inability to fully extend due to the torn flap displacing into the intercondylar notch.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art superior knee cross-section: medial and lateral menisci visible, medial meniscus highlighted red with tear line, McMurray test examiner silhouette
distractors: Neither — discoid meniscus tears are most common

variants.question: A patient cannot fully extend their knee and has joint-line tenderness. Imaging shows a bucket-handle meniscal tear. What anatomical structure displaced to block extension?
variants.correctAnswer: Torn meniscal flap displaced into the intercondylar notch
distractors: Osteochondral loose body from the patellar groove

variants.correctAnswer: External tibial rotation with valgus stress and knee extension from full flexion

facts.quizQuestion: Tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis) is primarily a degenerative condition of which tendon at the lateral epicondyle?
facts.explanation: Lateral epicondylitis is an overuse tendinopathy of the common extensor tendon, with the extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) almost always the primary site of pathology. ECRB originates from the lateral epicondyle and inserts at the base of the 3rd metacarpal, acting as the prime wrist dorsiflexor. Histology shows angiofibroblastic degeneration (fibroblasts, vascular hyperplasia, disorganised collagen) rather than true inflammation — hence the preferred term 'tendinopathy' over 'itis'.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lateral elbow: lateral epicondyle highlighted, ECRB tendon origin shown in red with degenerative fibres, forearm extensors labelled

variants.question: A tennis player has pain at the lateral epicondyle that worsens with resisted wrist extension. What structure is affected and what does biopsy typically show?
variants.correctAnswer: ECRB tendon; angiofibroblastic degeneration with fibroblasts and vascular hyperplasia — not inflammatory cells
distractors: Extensor digitorum communis; acute inflammatory infiltrate with neutrophils

acceptableAlternatives: common flexor tendon at medial epicondyle

facts.quizQuestion: Golfer's elbow (medial epicondylitis) involves degeneration of which tendon origin?
facts.explanation: Medial epicondylitis affects the common flexor tendon origin at the medial epicondyle — the shared origin of flexor carpi radialis, flexor carpi ulnaris, palmaris longus, flexor digitorum superficialis, and pronator teres. Overuse (gripping, throwing, wrist flexion) causes degenerative micro-tears. Pain is reproduced by resisted wrist flexion and pronation. The ulnar nerve (in the cubital tunnel just posterior) may also be irritated, causing ring/little-finger tingling. Common flexor origin at the medial epicondyle of the humerus includes flexor carpi radialis and pronator teres.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art medial elbow: medial epicondyle highlighted, common flexor tendon origin shown in red, ulnar nerve in cubital tunnel visible posteriorly
distractors: Brachioradialis origin at lateral supracondylar ridge

variants.question: A golfer has medial elbow pain worsened by resisted wrist flexion and tingling in the ring and little fingers. What two structures are likely affected?
variants.correctAnswer: Common flexor tendon (medial epicondylitis) and ulnar nerve in the cubital tunnel
distractors: ECRB tendon and radial nerve in radial tunnel

variants.question: The common flexor tendon of the medial epicondyle is the shared origin of flexor carpi radialis, flexor carpi ulnaris, palmaris longus, FDS, and ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Winging of the scapula — the medial border lifting off the chest wall — results from injury to which nerve?
facts.explanation: The long thoracic nerve (C5–C7) arises from the brachial plexus roots, runs along the mid-axillary line on the chest wall, and innervates serratus anterior. Serratus anterior protracts the scapula and holds it flush against the thorax. When paralysed, the rhomboids and trapezius pull the medial scapular border posteriorly, producing the characteristic 'wing'. Causes include mastectomy, chest-drain insertion, first-rib resection, and heavy backpack straps.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior thorax: scapula with medial border lifted (winged) highlighted; long thoracic nerve tracing from C5-C7 to serratus anterior muscle on lateral chest wall

facts.quizQuestion: Foot drop — inability to dorsiflex the foot — typically results from injury to which nerve at which anatomical site?
facts.explanation: The common fibular nerve winds around the fibular neck, just below the lateral knee, where it is superficial and easily compressed or injured. Damage here interrupts the deep fibular branch (tibialis anterior → dorsiflexion) and the superficial fibular branch (peroneus longus/brevis → eversion). The patient walks with a high-stepping 'steppage' gait to clear the dropped foot. Common causes: tight plaster cast, prolonged leg-crossing, or fibular neck fracture. Foot drop: common fibular (peroneal) nerve injured at the fibular neck → inability to dorsiflex.

facts.quizQuestion: Wrist drop (loss of wrist and finger extension) occurs after injury to the radial nerve — most often damaged at which anatomical site?
facts.explanation: The radial nerve runs in the spiral groove (radial groove) on the posterior humeral shaft, tightly bound to bone. A mid-shaft humeral fracture — or compression ('Saturday night palsy') with the arm draped over a chair — damages the nerve here. Triceps is typically spared (branch leaves before the groove), but brachioradialis, wrist extensors, and finger extensors are lost. Sensory loss covers the dorsal forearm and dorsal hand.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior humerus: spiral groove highlighted, radial nerve winding through it in orange, mid-shaft fracture line, hand shown in characteristic wrist-drop position

variants.question: A patient wakes with wrist drop after falling asleep drunk with their arm over a chair back. Triceps is intact. Which nerve and site are affected?

facts.correctAnswer: Loss of ulnar intrinsics → claw hand (4th/5th digits)
facts.quizQuestion: Ulnar nerve injury causes 'claw hand'. What is the anatomical mechanism?
facts.explanation: The ulnar nerve innervates the 3rd and 4th lumbricals plus all interossei. These intrinsic muscles normally flex the MCP joints and extend the IP joints. Without them, the long finger extensors hyperextend MCPs and the long flexors (FDP, innervated by ulnar nerve) flex the IPs — producing clawing of the 4th and 5th digits. The 2nd and 3rd digits are less affected because their lumbricals are median-innervated. Paradoxically, a distal (low) ulnar palsy causes worse clawing than a proximal one because FDP (which drives IP flexion) is also lost in high lesions. Ulnar nerve injury: loss of lumbricals and interossei → MCP hyperextension, IP flexion of 4th and 5th digits.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art hand: 4th and 5th digits clawed — MCP joints hyperextended, PIP and DIP flexed; ulnar nerve tract highlighted, lumbrical muscles absent

Loss of extensor digitorum → all fingers dropped
Loss of flexor digitorum profundus → inability to flex DIP joints only

variants.question: A low ulnar nerve palsy produces more pronounced clawing than a high ulnar palsy. Why?

distractors: High palsy spares the interossei at the wrist level

facts.correctAnswer: Loss of thenar muscles → ape hand
facts.quizQuestion: Median nerve injury causes 'ape hand'. What is the anatomical basis of this deformity?
facts.explanation: The recurrent motor branch of the median nerve (arising just distal to the carpal tunnel) innervates the thenar eminence: opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis (APB), and flexor pollicis brevis (superficial head). These muscles rotate and abduct the thumb into opposition. Without them, adductor pollicis (ulnar nerve) and the thumb extensors pull the thumb flat into the plane of the palm — mimicking a primate hand where the thumb is not opposable. Median nerve injury: loss of thenar muscles (opponens pollicis, APB, FPB) → thumb lies flat in plane of palm.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art hand: thumb adducted and lying flat against the index finger, thenar eminence wasted; median nerve recurrent branch highlighted, APB and opponens absent

variants.question: The best single clinical test for median nerve motor dysfunction at the wrist is weakness of ___.

facts.correctAnswer: Head tilts toward affected side; chin away
acceptableAlternatives: tilt to same side, rotation to opposite
Head tilts to the affected (contracted) side; chin rotates to the opposite side
facts.quizQuestion: In torticollis from unilateral SCM contracture, how does the head posture change?
facts.explanation: Sternocleidomastoid (SCM) has two actions: it tilts the head toward the same side (ipsilateral lateral flexion) and rotates the chin toward the opposite side (contralateral rotation). A contracted right SCM therefore tilts the head toward the right and rotates the chin toward the left. The palpable tight band or mass confirms the affected side. Most congenital cases respond to physiotherapy stretching within the first year of life. Head tilt with chin rotation to opposite side is the classic posture in CN IV (trochlear) palsy.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art neck and head: right SCM contracted and thickened (highlighted red), head tilted right, chin pointing left, sternum and clavicle attachment visible

variants.correctAnswer: Congenital muscular torticollis (right SCM); passive stretching physiotherapy

variants.correctAnswer: Contralaterally (to the opposite side); ipsilaterally (to the same side)

facts.correctAnswer: Plantar fasciitis: pain first step after rest
facts.quizQuestion: Plantar fasciitis produces heel pain classically worst at which time?
facts.explanation: During sleep the plantar fascia shortens in slight foot flexion. The first weight-bearing steps abruptly stretch the tight, degenerated fascia from its origin at the medial calcaneal tubercle, tearing apart already-damaged fibres — causing intense 'first-step pain'. Pain typically eases with further walking as the tissue warms up and lengthens, then may return after prolonged activity. Despite the '-itis' name, biopsy shows degenerative fibres without inflammatory infiltrate. Classic presentation: sharp heel pain on the first step in the morning or after prolonged rest.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art foot: plantar fascia running from calcaneal tubercle to toes highlighted red, 'morning step' arrow, heel bone labeled
distractors: Only during the first week after injury

variants.question: A runner has sharp heel pain on the first steps out of bed each morning, improving with walking. Tenderness is at the medial calcaneal tubercle. What is the diagnosis and its anatomical origin?
variants.correctAnswer: Plantar fasciitis — degeneration of plantar fascia at its medial calcaneal tubercle origin
distractors: Achilles tendinopathy — degeneration of Achilles at calcaneal insertion
Calcaneal stress fracture — trabecular failure of calcaneus body
Tarsal tunnel syndrome — tibial nerve compression under flexor retinaculum

variants.question: The plantar fascia originates at the ___ of the calcaneus.

facts.quizQuestion: In trigger finger (stenosing tenosynovitis), a nodule on the flexor tendon catches on which structure?
facts.explanation: The A1 pulley sits at the base of each finger, overlying the metacarpal head. In stenosing tenosynovitis, repetitive mechanical stress causes reactive thickening of the flexor tendon sheath and a palpable nodule on the tendon surface. As the finger flexes, the nodule passes through the A1 pulley; on extension it catches, causing a painful snap ('trigger'). In locked trigger finger the digit remains fixed in flexion. Injection into the tendon sheath or surgical A1 pulley release are effective treatments.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art sagittal finger cross-section: flexor tendon with nodule trying to pass through A1 pulley at metacarpal head; snapping arrow and A1 label
distractors: A2 pulley over the proximal phalanx
A4 pulley at the middle phalanx

variants.question: Treatment options for trigger finger include corticosteroid injection into which space?
variants.correctAnswer: The flexor tendon sheath (at the A1 pulley level)
distractors: The palmar bursa (radial and ulnar bursae)

variants.question: The A1 pulley lies at the level of the ___ head.

facts.correctAnswer: EPB and APL in anatomical snuffbox (lateral border)
acceptableAlternatives: abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis
facts.quizQuestion: De Quervain's tenosynovitis affects which two tendons in the first dorsal compartment of the wrist?
facts.explanation: De Quervain's tenosynovitis is stenosing tenosynovitis of the first dorsal wrist compartment, which contains the abductor pollicis longus (APL) and extensor pollicis brevis (EPB). These tendons are compressed as they pass under the extensor retinaculum. Pain is elicited by the Finkelstein test: the thumb is tucked into the fist and the wrist is ulnar-deviated, maximally stretching and impinging the two tendons. Common in new mothers ('new-mother's wrist') from repetitive lifting. Lateral border of the anatomical snuffbox: extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) and abductor pollicis longus (APL).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art dorsal wrist: first extensor compartment highlighted, APL and EPB tendons labelled in red, anatomical snuffbox outline, Finkelstein test position shown
distractors: Extensor pollicis longus (EPL) and extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB)
Extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis (2nd compartment)
Extensor indicis and extensor digitorum (4th compartment)

variants.question: The Finkelstein test is positive in De Quervain's. How is it performed?
variants.correctAnswer: Thumb enclosed in fist, wrist ulnar-deviated — reproduces pain at the radial styloid/first dorsal compartment
distractors: Resisted wrist extension at the lateral epicondyle
Passive wrist flexion held for 60 seconds (Phalen's)
Tapping over the volar carpal ligament (Tinel's)

facts.correctAnswer: Palmar fascia fibrosis: ring then little finger
acceptableAlternatives: Progressive fibrosis and contracture of the palmar fascia — typically ring then little finger
facts.quizQuestion: Dupuytren's contracture is caused by myofibroblastic proliferation in which structure, most often affecting which digits?
facts.explanation: Dupuytren's disease is a benign, progressive fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia. Myofibroblasts produce excess type III collagen, forming hard nodules then longitudinal cords that tether the MCP and PIP joints into flexion. The ring finger is most often involved first, followed by the little finger, then occasionally middle and index. Risk factors include Northern European ancestry, male sex, diabetes, and alcohol use. Treatment (injection of collagenase, needle aponeurotomy, or surgery) cuts or dissolves the cords. Dupuytren contracture: fibrosis of palmar fascia typically affects ring finger first, then little finger.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art palm: thick cord of fibrotic palmar fascia pulling ring and little fingers into flexion, palmar nodule visible proximal to ring finger MCP joint
distractors: Degenerative arthritis of the IP joints — index and middle first
Extensor tendon adhesions on the dorsum — all digits equally
Palmar plate calcification — little finger only
Ulnar nerve entrapment causing flexor tendon shortening

facts.quizQuestion: What is the primary biomechanical function of the anterior cruciate ligament (ACL)?
facts.explanation: The ACL runs from the anterior intercondylar area of the tibia to the posterolateral wall of the intercondylar notch (medial surface of the lateral femoral condyle). Its primary role is resisting anterior tibial translation on the femur, especially at 30–90° of knee flexion. The Lachman test (anterior tibial drawer at 20–30° flexion) and anterior drawer test (at 90°) assess ACL integrity. A positive pivot-shift test confirms rotational instability. The anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) prevents anterior translation of the tibia on the femur.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art sagittal knee: ACL fibres running from anterior tibia to posterior femur, anterior tibial translation arrow blocked by taut ACL, Lachman test examiner silhouette
facts.sourceName: StatPearls — Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb, Knee Anterior Cruciate Ligament
distractors: Prevents posterior translation of the tibia on the femur
Resists valgus stress on the knee
Resists varus stress on the knee

variants.question: The Lachman test assesses ACL integrity. How is it performed and what constitutes a positive result?
variants.correctAnswer: Anterior drawer on the tibia with knee at 20–30° flexion; soft endpoint or >5 mm anterior translation is positive
distractors: Valgus stress at 30° flexion; medial joint line opening
Posterior drawer at 90° flexion; posterior sag of tibia

variants.question: The ACL attaches on the tibia at the ___ intercondylar area and on the femur at the medial surface of the lateral condyle.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the primary function of the PCL, and what is the classic injury mechanism?
facts.explanation: The posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) is the strongest knee ligament — approximately twice as strong as the ACL — and its primary role is preventing posterior tibial translation on the femur. The classic mechanism is a 'dashboard injury': in a motor vehicle collision, a flexed knee strikes the dashboard, driving the tibia posteriorly. The posterior sag sign (tibia sags posteriorly at 90° compared with the normal leg) and posterior drawer test confirm PCL injury. The posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) prevents posterior translation of the tibia on the femur.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art sagittal knee: PCL fibres highlighted, posterior tibial translation arrow blocked; inset MVC scene with knee hitting dashboard
facts.sourceName: StatPearls — Posterior Cruciate Ligament Knee Injuries
distractors: Prevents anterior translation; mechanism is a pivot-shift landing
Resists varus stress; mechanism is contact blow to medial knee
Resists valgus stress; mechanism is lateral impact in sport
Prevents hyperextension; mechanism is a forced extension kick

variants.question: A patient in a car crash has knee pain, a posterior sag sign at 90° flexion, and a positive posterior drawer. Which ligament is injured?
The PCL is approximately ___ times as strong as the ACL.

facts.quizQuestion: The MCL resists valgus stress and is the most commonly injured knee ligament. What mechanism typically causes an MCL tear?
facts.explanation: The MCL (tibial collateral ligament) runs from the medial femoral epicondyle to the medial tibial metaphysis and provides approximately 78% of valgus restraint. MCL tears account for about 40% of all knee ligament injuries. The mechanism is a valgus force on the slightly bent knee — a direct blow to the lateral knee (contact), or a combined valgus–external rotation load (skiing). Most isolated MCL injuries heal without surgery; concomitant ACL or medial meniscus tears ('unhappy triad') require more complex management.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior knee: MCL on medial side highlighted red, valgus stress arrow from lateral, superficial and deep MCL layers shown, fibular head on lateral side
facts.sourceName: StatPearls — Medial Collateral Ligament Knee Injury

variants.question: The 'unhappy triad' of knee injury involves which three structures?

acceptableAlternatives: prevents varus angulation of the knee
facts.quizQuestion: What is the primary function of the lateral collateral ligament (LCL)?
facts.explanation: The LCL (fibular collateral ligament) runs from the lateral femoral epicondyle to the fibular head, resisting varus (bow-legged) stress at the knee. LCL injuries are less common than MCL injuries because direct varus loads are rare in everyday sport. The LCL is part of the posterolateral corner complex (with popliteus, popliteofibular ligament) — combined injuries cause posterolateral rotatory instability. Isolated LCL tears are tested with varus stress at both 0° and 30° flexion.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lateral knee: LCL cord running from lateral femoral epicondyle to fibular head highlighted; varus stress arrow applied to medial knee
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Lateral collateral ligament of knee joint

variants.question: A varus stress test at 30° of knee flexion shows lateral joint line opening. Which structure is disrupted?

facts.quizQuestion: Rotator cuff impingement syndrome: which tendon is most commonly involved and where is it impinged?
facts.explanation: Supraspinatus passes through the subacromial space between the humeral head below and the coracoacromial arch (acromion + coracoacromial ligament + coracoid) above. Repeated overhead activity or decreased subacromial space traps it, causing pain at 60–120° of abduction (the 'painful arc'). Two provocative tests: the Neer test (arm passively forward-flexed, impinges tendon against anterior acromion) and the Hawkins–Kennedy test (90° flexion, internal rotation — drives supraspinatus under coracoacromial ligament). Rotator cuff impingement: supraspinatus tendon (and subacromial bursa) compressed under the coracoacromial arch.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art coronal shoulder: supraspinatus tendon highlighted red between humeral head and acromion; Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy test positions inset
facts.sourceName: PMC — Tests for diagnosing subacromial impingement syndrome
distractors: Infraspinatus at the posterior glenoid rim
Biceps long head at the coracoid process

variants.question: The Hawkins–Kennedy test for shoulder impingement: the arm is positioned at 90° flexion and internally rotated. What is impinged, and against what?
variants.correctAnswer: Supraspinatus tendon is driven against the coracoacromial ligament and coracoid process
distractors: Infraspinatus against the posterior glenoid labrum

variants.question: The 'painful arc' in shoulder impingement typically occurs between ___ and ___ degrees of shoulder abduction.

facts.correctAnswer: Restricted active and passive ROM, especially external rotation
acceptableAlternatives: loss of both active and passive ROM
restricted passive and active range of motion
facts.quizQuestion: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) is characterised by which hallmark finding on examination?
facts.explanation: Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis): significant restriction of both active and passive range of motion. Frozen shoulder is defined by significant restriction of both active AND passive glenohumeral ROM — especially external rotation — in the absence of another identifiable intra-articular cause. Loss of passive ROM distinguishes it from rotator cuff tears (where passive ROM is preserved). Pathology is fibroblastic proliferation and contracture of the GH joint capsule with capsular thickening, loss of axillary fold, and reduced synovial volume. The condition progresses through three phases: freezing (pain-dominated, 2–9 months), frozen (stiffness), and thawing (gradual recovery). Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis): restricted active AND passive range of motion, especially external rotation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art shoulder: glenohumeral capsule thickened and contracted highlighted; range-of-motion dial showing severely limited external rotation; three clinical phases labelled
distractors: Restricted active ROM only, with preserved passive ROM (like rotator cuff tear)
Full ROM with pain at end range only
Restricted passive ROM with normal active ROM

variants.question: How does examination differentiate adhesive capsulitis from a rotator cuff tear?
variants.correctAnswer: Adhesive capsulitis restricts passive ROM; rotator cuff tears preserve passive ROM but limit active ROM due to weakness

Capsulitis is bilateral by definition; cuff tears are unilateral

facts.correctAnswer: Brachial plexus and subclavian vessels compressed

facts.quizQuestion: Thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS) results from compression of which structures between which anatomical boundaries?
facts.explanation: The thoracic outlet has three compression zones: (1) interscalene triangle (anterior scalene, middle scalene, first rib — contains brachial plexus trunks and subclavian artery); (2) costoclavicular space (clavicle and first rib — all structures); (3) subcoracoid space (pectoralis minor). Neurogenic TOS (brachial plexus compression — most common, >90%) causes arm/hand pain, numbness, and weakness. Vascular TOS (arterial or venous compression) causes ischaemia or thrombosis. Risk factors include a cervical rib and scalene hypertrophy. Thoracic outlet syndrome: brachial plexus and subclavian vessels compressed between anterior and middle scalene muscles.

facts.quizQuestion: Piriformis syndrome causes buttock pain and sciatica by what mechanism?
facts.explanation: The piriformis muscle originates from the anterior sacrum, exits through the greater sciatic notch, and inserts on the greater trochanter, acting as a hip external rotator (and internal rotator when hip is >60° flexed). The sciatic nerve normally exits the greater sciatic notch anterior to (below) piriformis. In piriformis syndrome, spasm, hypertrophy, or anatomical variation causes sciatic nerve compression — producing buttock pain and sciatica mimicking lumbar disc herniation, but without spine pathology. Piriformis syndrome: sciatic nerve compressed by or through the piriformis muscle → sciatica-like symptoms.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art gluteal region: piriformis muscle outlined, sciatic nerve exiting below it, compression at greater sciatic notch highlighted; hip flexion angle annotation

variants.correctAnswer: Piriformis syndrome — sciatic nerve compressed by piriformis muscle

facts.correctAnswer: Phalen's, Tinel's, nocturnal paraesthesia, thenar wasting
acceptableAlternatives: Phalen and Tinel positive with thenar wasting
facts.quizQuestion: What are the four key clinical features of carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS)?
facts.explanation: CTS involves compression of the median nerve under the flexor retinaculum in the carpal tunnel. Classic presentation: (1) Tinel's sign — tingling on tapping the carpal tunnel (parasympathetic nerve regeneration marker); (2) Phalen's test — tingling reproduced after 60 seconds of wrist flexion (compresses tunnel); (3) nocturnal paraesthesia — lying flat reduces venous drainage, increasing tunnel pressure; (4) thenar wasting (abductor pollicis brevis) — indicates chronic motor fibre damage. Nerve conduction studies confirm diagnosis. Carpal tunnel syndrome features: positive Phalen's test, positive Tinel's sign, nocturnal paraesthesia, and thenar muscle wasting.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art wrist cross-section: carpal tunnel with median nerve, 9 flexor tendons and flexor retinaculum; thenar eminence atrophied; Phalen's position inset
distractors: Positive McMurray, locked wrist, nocturnal pain, hypothenar wasting
Weak grip, wrist drop, positive Finkelstein, thenar wasting
Positive Lachman, hyperflexion deformity, night pain, intrinsic wasting

variants.question: Thenar wasting in carpal tunnel syndrome most specifically indicates denervation of which muscle?
variants.correctAnswer: Abductor pollicis brevis (APB) — best isolated indicator of median nerve motor dysfunction
distractors: Opponens pollicis — shared innervation makes it less specific
Adductor pollicis — innervated by ulnar nerve, not median
Flexor pollicis brevis — often has dual innervation

variants.question: The carpal tunnel contains the median nerve and ___ flexor tendons.

acceptableAlternatives: L4-L5 is most common; L5 root affected
L4–L5 disc level; the exiting L5 nerve root is typically compressed
facts.quizQuestion: Lumbar disc herniation most commonly occurs at which level, and which nerve root is usually affected?
facts.explanation: L4–L5 and L5–S1 together account for approximately 95% of lumbar disc herniations, with L4–L5 the single most common level. A posterolateral herniation at L4–L5 compresses the traversing L5 nerve root in the lateral recess. L5 compression causes: pain radiating to lateral calf and dorsum of foot, weakness of foot dorsiflexion (tibialis anterior) and great toe extension (EHL), and sensory loss on the dorsum of the foot and web space between 1st and 2nd toes. Reflex changes are subtle (medial hamstring). The L5–S1 disc compresses S1, causing posterior leg pain, weak plantar flexion, and loss of ankle jerk. Most common lumbar herniation level is L4–L5, typically compressing the L5 nerve root.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lumbar cross-section L4-L5: herniated disc posterolateral pressing on L5 nerve root, dermatome chart showing dorsal foot, EHL and tibialis anterior labelled
distractors: L1–L2 disc; femoral nerve root compressed
L3–L4 disc; L4 root — weak knee extension, absent knee jerk
L5–S1 disc; S1 root — absent knee jerk
L2–L3 disc; obturator nerve root affected

variants.question: A patient with L4–L5 disc herniation and L5 radiculopathy cannot walk on their heels. Which muscle is weakened and which nerve root level is affected?
variants.correctAnswer: Tibialis anterior (foot dorsiflexion); L5 nerve root

variants.question: L5 and S1 disc herniations together account for approximately ___% of lumbar disc herniations.

acceptableAlternatives: leg pain with walking, relieved by bending forward
facts.quizQuestion: Lumbar spinal stenosis causes which characteristic symptom pattern?
facts.explanation: Lumbar spinal stenosis narrows the central canal, lateral recesses, or foramina, compressing nerve roots. Extension (standing, walking) further reduces the spinal canal diameter while flexion (sitting, leaning on a shopping trolley — 'shopping cart sign') increases it. This causes neurogenic claudication: bilateral leg pain, heaviness, numbness, or weakness that appears with walking and is relieved within seconds to minutes by sitting or bending forward. Differentiated from vascular claudication by relief with posture change rather than just stopping walking, and by preserved pedal pulses. Lumbar spinal stenosis: bilateral leg pain/heaviness that is relieved by lumbar flexion (leaning forward/sitting).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lumbar spine: central canal narrowed by ligamentum flavum hypertrophy and disc bulge; extension narrowing vs flexion opening arrows; elderly patient leaning on walking frame
facts.sourceName: StatPearls — Spinal Stenosis and Neurogenic Claudication
distractors: Unilateral sharp shooting pain down the posterior leg, relieved by lying flat
Central back pain worsened by flexion and relieved by extension
Bilateral leg oedema with absent pulses — vascular claudication
Saddle anaesthesia and urinary retention — cauda equina syndrome

variants.question: An elderly patient can only walk 200 m before bilateral leg pain forces them to stop. Sitting briefly relieves symptoms. Pedal pulses are normal. What is the diagnosis and what relieves it?
variants.correctAnswer: Lumbar spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication; lumbar flexion (sitting/leaning forward) relieves it by increasing canal diameter
distractors: Peripheral arterial disease; rest alone relieves it regardless of posture
Bilateral L4/L5 disc herniations; supine straight-leg raise is positive

facts.quizQuestion: Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) is at the lateral epicondyle. Its medial counterpart, affecting the common flexor tendon, is called what?
facts.explanation: Medial epicondylitis ('golfer's elbow') mirrors tennis elbow on the medial side: the common flexor tendon origin (FCR, palmaris longus, FDS, FCU, pronator teres) undergoes degenerative tendinopathy from overuse. Pain is at the medial epicondyle and worsens with resisted wrist flexion and pronation. Because the ulnar nerve lies just posterior in the cubital tunnel, co-existing ulnar neuritis with ring/little-finger tingling is common (~50% of cases). Unlike tennis elbow (ECRB/extensor), golfer's elbow involves flexor/pronator tendons.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art medial elbow: medial epicondyle highlighted yellow, common flexor tendon attachment shown in red, ulnar nerve in cubital tunnel posterior to it
facts.sourceName: StatPearls — Lateral Epicondylitis (Tennis Elbow) [medial variant cross-reference]

variants.question: A golfer has medial elbow pain. Which resisted movement reproduces the pain, and which co-existing nerve can be involved?
variants.correctAnswer: Resisted wrist flexion/pronation reproduces pain; ulnar nerve (in cubital tunnel) may cause ring/little-finger tingling
distractors: Resisted wrist extension reproduces pain; radial nerve causes posterior forearm tingling
Resisted elbow extension; median nerve causes thenar symptoms
Resisted supination; musculocutaneous nerve causes anterior forearm tingling

variants.question: Both lateral epicondylitis and medial epicondylitis are histologically degenerative (___ degeneration) rather than truly inflammatory conditions.

facts.quizQuestion: Which enzyme in saliva begins the chemical digestion of starch in the mouth?
facts.explanation: Salivary amylase (also called ptyalin) breaks starch into maltose at the slightly neutral pH of saliva (6.8–7). Its activity stops once food reaches the acidic stomach, where the low pH denatures it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art close-up of a mouth with saliva droplets, small starch granule molecules breaking into maltose fragments

variants.quizQuestion: What is the pH range at which salivary amylase is most active?
variants.explanation: Salivary amylase functions optimally in the near-neutral pH of the oral cavity. Stomach acid quickly denatures it, ending its starch-digesting activity.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the term for the wave-like muscular contractions that propel food through the esophagus and intestines?
facts.explanation: Peristalsis is the coordinated contraction and relaxation of smooth muscle in the GI tract wall. It is forceful enough to move food even when a person is lying down or upside down, proving that gravity is not required for swallowing.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of esophagus with visible wave of muscle contraction squeezing a bolus of food downward

variants.quizQuestion: Peristalsis is the movement mechanism of the esophagus — does it require gravity to move food to the stomach?
variants.explanation: Peristaltic muscle contractions are strong enough to move food against gravity, which is why astronauts in zero-g can still swallow normally.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how long is the adult esophagus?
facts.explanation: The esophagus is a muscular tube approximately 25 cm long that connects the pharynx to the stomach. It passes through the diaphragm at the T10 vertebral level via the esophageal hiatus.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art sagittal torso diagram with esophagus highlighted in blue, length marked with a small ruler from pharynx to stomach

variants.quizQuestion: At which vertebral level does the esophagus pierce the diaphragm?
variants.explanation: The esophagus passes through the diaphragm at the T10 level, slightly above the aortic hiatus at T12.

facts.quizQuestion: The stomach is divided into how many anatomical regions?
facts.explanation: The stomach has four regions: the cardia (where the esophagus joins), the fundus (upper dome), the body (largest part), and the pylorus (outlet leading to the duodenum). Each region has distinct functions and cell types.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art stomach diagram with four regions color-coded and labeled: cardia, fundus, body, pylorus
