variants.explanation: Type IV is 'delayed' because the T-cell response takes 48–72 hours (vs. minutes for Type I). Sensitised CD4+ Th1 cells re-encounter antigen presented by APCs and release cytokines (IFN-γ, TNF) that activate macrophages, causing granulomatous inflammation. Examples: PPD skin test, nickel/poison ivy contact dermatitis, coeliac disease, type 1 diabetes (beta cell destruction).

variants.quizQuestion: Type III hypersensitivity is caused by immune complex deposition. What is the mechanism and a key clinical example?
variants.correctAnswer: Antigen-antibody complexes deposit in vessel walls/tissues → complement activation → neutrophil infiltration → tissue damage. Example: serum sickness, post-streptococcal GN
variants.explanation: In Type III, soluble antigen-antibody (IgG/IgM) complexes form in excess and deposit in blood vessel walls, joints, and glomeruli. Complement is activated (C3a, C5a attract neutrophils) causing local inflammation. Examples: serum sickness (drug reaction to foreign proteins), systemic lupus erythematosus, post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, Arthus reaction.

acceptableAlternatives: MHC II on APCs recognised by CD4+
facts.quizQuestion: While MHC class I molecules are expressed on all nucleated cells and present intracellular peptides to CD8+ cytotoxic T cells, which MHC class is restricted to antigen-presenting cells and presents extracellular/phagocytosed antigens to CD4+ T helper cells?
facts.explanation: MHC class II molecules are expressed only on professional antigen-presenting cells (APCs): dendritic cells, macrophages, and B cells (in humans, encoded by HLA-DP, HLA-DQ, HLA-DR). They present peptides derived from exogenous antigens (phagocytosed bacteria, extracellular proteins) processed in endosomes. CD4+ T helper cells recognise antigen in the context of MHC II (the 'CD4 restricts to MHC II' rule). MHC class I (HLA-A, B, C) presents endogenous (cytosolic) peptides — viral proteins, tumour antigens — to CD8+ cytotoxic T cells. Memory rule: CD4 x MHC II = 4 x 2 = 8 → no, use 'CD4 with class II' and 'CD8 with class I' or the rule: 4 ÷ 2 = 2 (CD4/MHC II), 8 ÷ 2 = 4 (CD8/MHC I). MHC class II: found only on antigen-presenting cells (APCs); recognised by CD4+ T helper cells. MHC class II is found only on antigen-presenting cells (APCs) and recognised by CD4+ T helper cells.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art side-by-side: left shows nucleated cell with MHC-I presenting to CD8+ cytotoxic T cell (skull on T cell); right shows macrophage with MHC-II presenting to CD4+ helper T cell (helper hat)
distractors: MHC class I on all cells, CD4+
MHC class II on all nucleated cells, CD8+
MHC class I on APCs only, CD4+

variants.quizQuestion: Which cells express MHC class I molecules, and which T cell subset recognises peptides presented on MHC class I?
variants.correctAnswer: All nucleated cells express MHC class I; CD8+ cytotoxic T cells recognise it
variants.explanation: MHC class I is expressed on virtually all nucleated cells (not red blood cells or platelets). It presents endogenous peptides — from the cytosol (viral proteins, tumour antigens, normal self-proteins) — allowing CD8+ CTLs to survey every cell for intracellular infection or malignancy.

facts.quizQuestion: Haemolytic disease of the newborn (Rh incompatibility) and autoimmune haemolytic anaemia are examples of which hypersensitivity type, where IgG or IgM targets cell-surface antigens for destruction?
facts.explanation: Type II (cytotoxic) hypersensitivity: IgG or IgM antibodies bind directly to antigens on cell surfaces or extracellular matrix. Consequences include: (1) Complement activation (MAC formation → cell lysis), (2) Antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) via NK cells, (3) Opsonisation for phagocytosis. Clinical examples: haemolytic disease of the newborn (maternal anti-Rh IgG crosses placenta, destroys foetal RBCs), autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM antibodies), myasthenia gravis (anti-AChR blocks acetylcholine binding), Graves disease (anti-TSH receptor stimulates thyroid), pemphigus vulgaris (anti-desmoglein causes skin blistering). Type II: IgG/IgM binds cell-surface antigens → complement activation or ADCC → cell destruction. Examples: Rh incompatibility, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia. Type II: IgG/IgM antibodies bind cell-surface antigens → complement activation or ADCC.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art red blood cell with IgG antibodies coating its surface, complement proteins and NK cell attacking it, newborn jaundice in background

variants.quizQuestion: In myasthenia gravis, autoantibodies cause muscle weakness by targeting which receptor?
variants.correctAnswer: Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) at the neuromuscular junction
variants.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is a Type II hypersensitivity where IgG antibodies block or destroy nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction, preventing muscle activation. Hallmarks: fatigable ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia worsening with use. Treatment includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (pyridostigmine) and immunosuppression.

facts.quizQuestion: The tuberculin skin test (PPD) produces induration at 48–72 hours. The Mantoux reaction is the classic example of which hypersensitivity type — the only type not mediated by antibodies?
facts.explanation: Type IV (delayed-type hypersensitivity, DTH) is the only hypersensitivity reaction mediated entirely by T cells (not antibodies). Sensitised CD4+ Th1 cells, upon re-encountering antigen presented via MHC II, release IFN-γ and TNF-α, activating macrophages and recruiting monocytes. The reaction peaks at 48–72 hours (vs. minutes for Type I). The tuberculin (PPD) skin test exploits this — a positive result (induration ≥10–15mm) indicates prior sensitisation to M. tuberculosis. Other examples: contact dermatitis (nickel, poison ivy), graft rejection, coeliac disease, and the pathology in tuberculosis granuloma formation. Type IV (delayed-type) hypersensitivity is T-cell mediated with reactions occurring 48–72 hours after exposure. Type IV (delayed-type) hypersensitivity is T-cell mediated with reactions at 48–72 hours.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art forearm with indurated PPD skin test wheal, Th1 lymphocytes and macrophages shown in dermis cross-section, 48-hour timer in corner

variants.quizQuestion: Contact dermatitis to nickel or poison ivy is mediated by which T cell subset?
variants.correctAnswer: CD4+ Th1 cells (with CD8+ CTL contribution in the effector phase)
variants.explanation: The sensitisation phase involves dendritic cells presenting hapten-modified self proteins to naive T cells in lymph nodes. The elicitation phase on re-exposure involves CD4+ Th1 cells releasing IFN-γ (recruiting macrophages) and CD8+ cytotoxic T cells directly killing keratinocytes. Together they produce the eczematous rash of allergic contact dermatitis.

acceptableAlternatives: open-angle is painless chronic, closed-angle is acute painful
facts.quizQuestion: Glaucoma involves raised intraocular pressure damaging the optic nerve. Distinguish open-angle from closed-angle glaucoma in terms of onset, symptoms, and urgency.
facts.explanation: Open-angle glaucoma (90% of cases): The trabecular meshwork is partially blocked, drainage is impaired but the iridocorneal angle remains open. IOP rises gradually. The condition is chronic, painless, and peripheral ('tunnel') vision is lost insidiously — patients are often unaware until significant damage has occurred. Closed-angle (angle-closure) glaucoma: The iris physically blocks the trabecular meshwork (angle closes). IOP rises acutely and severely. Symptoms: sudden severe eye pain, headache, blurred vision, halos around lights, nausea/vomiting, red eye, mid-dilated fixed pupil. This is an ophthalmic EMERGENCY requiring immediate treatment (IV acetazolamide, pilocarpine, laser iridotomy) to prevent permanent blindness within hours. Glaucoma: open-angle type is chronic, painless with progressive peripheral vision loss; angle-closure type is acute with pain. Open-angle: chronic, painless, progressive peripheral loss. Angle-closure: acute, painful, emergency.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art eye cross-section split: left open-angle with partially obstructed trabecular meshwork, gradual vision tunnel; right closed-angle with iris blocking drainage angle, urgent red eye with pain
distractors: Open-angle is acute, closed-angle is chronic
Both cause central vision loss first
Closed-angle is treated with beta-blockers alone

variants.quizQuestion: A patient presents with sudden severe eye pain, halos around lights, a hard red eye, nausea, and a mid-dilated non-reactive pupil. What is the diagnosis?
variants.explanation: This is a classic presentation of acute angle-closure glaucoma — an ophthalmological emergency. The pupil dilates (from a dim room, stress, or mydriatic drops), the peripheral iris contacts the trabecular meshwork, blocking aqueous outflow. IOP can reach 60–80 mmHg (normal ~10–21). Immediate treatment prevents permanent optic nerve damage.

facts.correctAnswer: Cataracts: lens opacity, painless vision loss
acceptableAlternatives: Cataracts — lens opacity causing painless progressive vision loss
facts.quizQuestion: Which condition involves opacity of the crystalline lens, producing painless, gradual vision blurring and glare, and is the leading cause of reversible blindness worldwide?
facts.explanation: Cataracts are opacities within the crystalline lens caused by protein denaturation and aggregation. The lens is normally transparent; opacity scatters incoming light, reducing visual acuity. Symptoms: blurred vision (often worse at distance), glare and halos (especially driving at night), 'second sight' (temporary near-vision improvement as the denser nucleus acts as a magnifier), and faded colour perception. Risk factors: age (most common — senile cataract), UV exposure, smoking, diabetes, corticosteroids, trauma, congenital (TORCH infections, galactosaemia). Treatment: surgical lens removal with intraocular lens (IOL) implantation — one of the most commonly performed and successful surgeries worldwide. Cataracts cause ~51% of all global blindness (WHO). Cataracts: progressive lens opacity causing painless vision loss.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art eye cross-section with clouded whitish lens visible through pupil, visual simulation showing blurred hazy image through cataractous lens vs clear lens

variants.quizQuestion: Cataracts are the leading cause of reversible blindness. What percentage of global blindness do they cause according to the WHO?
variants.explanation: The WHO estimates cataracts account for approximately 51% of world blindness — about 20 million people. Because cataract surgery is inexpensive and highly effective, most of this blindness is preventable. The remaining major causes are glaucoma (8%), age-related macular degeneration (5%), corneal opacification (4%), and diabetic retinopathy (1%).

acceptableAlternatives: Meniere's disease — endolymphatic hydrops causing episodic vertigo, sensorineural hearing loss
facts.quizQuestion: Which inner ear disorder is caused by excess endolymph (endolymphatic hydrops) and presents with recurrent episodes of severe vertigo, fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and a sensation of ear fullness?
facts.explanation: Meniere's disease (endolymphatic hydrops) results from abnormal accumulation of endolymph in the membranous labyrinth of the inner ear, distending the scala media and damaging hair cells. Episodes typically last 20 minutes to several hours. The four hallmark symptoms form a tetrad: (1) episodic vertigo with nausea/vomiting, (2) fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (eventually permanent), (3) tinnitus (roaring, low-pitched), (4) aural fullness/pressure. Diagnosis is clinical. Management: low-salt diet and diuretics to reduce endolymph volume, betahistine, vestibular rehabilitation; for severe cases intratympanic gentamicin or endolymphatic sac decompression surgery. Menière disease: endolymphatic hydrops causing episodic vertigo, sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cochlear cross-section with distended endolymph-filled scala media labelled, figure experiencing room-spinning vertigo, tinnitus waves near ear

variants.quizQuestion: Meniere's disease is managed partly with a low-sodium diet. What is the physiological rationale?
variants.correctAnswer: Reducing dietary sodium decreases endolymph volume by lowering osmotic pressure and fluid retention in the labyrinth
variants.explanation: Sodium intake influences systemic fluid and electrolyte balance. A low-sodium diet combined with diuretics reduces circulating fluid volume, which in turn reduces endolymph overproduction or impairs reabsorption in the endolymphatic sac, decreasing distension of the membranous labyrinth.

facts.quizQuestion: Which chronic autoimmune skin disease is characterised by well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scales, the Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale is removed), and the Koebner phenomenon?
facts.explanation: Psoriasis is a T-cell mediated autoimmune condition causing keratinocyte hyperproliferation — skin cells turn over in 3–5 days instead of the normal 28 days, producing the thick silvery-white scale. Key signs: Auspitz sign — removal of scale reveals pinpoint bleeding from dilated capillaries; Koebner (isomorphic) phenomenon — new lesions appear at sites of skin trauma (scratches, surgical scars). Common sites: extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), scalp, sacrum. Associated with psoriatic arthritis (seronegative, asymmetric), cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and depression. Treatment: emollients, topical corticosteroids/vitamin D analogues, phototherapy (UVB), systemics (methotrexate, ciclosporin), biologics (anti-TNF, anti-IL-17, anti-IL-23).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art elbow with well-defined silvery-white plaque, Auspitz sign close-up showing pinpoint bleeding, Koebner lesion forming on scratch line on forearm

variants.quizQuestion: The Koebner phenomenon is seen in psoriasis. What does it describe?
variants.correctAnswer: New psoriatic lesions appearing at sites of skin trauma or injury
variants.explanation: The Koebner (isomorphic) response describes the development of new psoriatic plaques along lines of trauma — scratches, surgical incisions, sunburn, or even tattoo sites. It reflects the pro-inflammatory state of psoriatic skin making it highly reactive to any mechanical disruption.

facts.quizQuestion: Which autoimmune skin condition is caused by destruction of melanocytes, producing sharply demarcated, chalky-white depigmented patches that fluoresce brightly under Wood's lamp?
facts.explanation: Vitiligo results from autoimmune-mediated destruction (CD8+ cytotoxic T cells targeting melanocyte-specific antigens such as TYRP1, TYRP2, and melanin biosynthesis enzymes) leading to complete loss of melanin pigment in affected areas. Patches are typically well-circumscribed, chalk-white, and may be surrounded by hyperpigmented borders. Under Wood's lamp (UVA 365nm), depigmented patches fluoresce bright white-blue, aiding diagnosis in fair-skinned patients. Vitiligo has segmental and non-segmental (generalised) types. It is associated with other autoimmune conditions: Hashimoto thyroiditis, Addison disease, type 1 diabetes, pernicious anaemia. Treatment: topical calcineurin inhibitors, narrow-band UVB phototherapy, JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib — FDA approved 2022 for repigmentation).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art dark-skinned arm with stark white depigmented patches, Wood's lamp shining UV light and patches glowing bright white, melanocyte shown being attacked by T cells

variants.quizQuestion: Vitiligo is associated with several other autoimmune diseases. Name three that are commonly associated.
variants.correctAnswer: Hashimoto thyroiditis, Addison disease, and type 1 diabetes mellitus
variants.explanation: The autoimmune diathesis underlying vitiligo predisposes to other organ-specific autoimmune diseases. Hashimoto thyroiditis is most common; Addison disease (adrenal), type 1 DM (pancreatic beta cells), and pernicious anaemia (gastric parietal cells) are also recognised associations. Screening for thyroid function is recommended in all vitiligo patients.

acceptableAlternatives: ABCDE: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution
facts.quizQuestion: What are the five ABCDE clinical warning signs used to identify a suspicious pigmented lesion that may be malignant melanoma?
facts.explanation: The ABCDE criteria for melanoma recognition: A — Asymmetry (one half doesn't mirror the other); B — Border irregularity (ragged, notched, or blurred edges); C — Colour variation (multiple colours within one lesion: black, brown, tan, red, white, blue); D — Diameter greater than 6mm (size of a pencil eraser, though melanomas can be smaller); E — Evolution (any change in size, shape, colour, or appearance over weeks to months — this is arguably the most important criterion). Melanoma arises from malignant transformation of melanocytes. Despite representing only ~1% of skin cancers, melanoma causes ~75% of skin cancer deaths due to its metastatic potential. Key risk factors: fair skin, UV exposure, family history, dysplastic naevus syndrome, BRAF mutation (V600E in ~50%, targetable by vemurafenib). Treatment: wide local excision with sentinel node biopsy; metastatic disease: BRAF inhibitors + MEK inhibitors, immunotherapy (anti-PD1: nivolumab, pembrolizumab). Melanoma warning signs: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter >6mm, Evolution.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art skin lesion with all five ABCDE features labelled: asymmetric irregular-bordered multi-coloured lesion over 6mm, before/after evolution comparison
distractors: ABCDE: Age, Bleeding, Colour, Depth, Extension
ABCDE: Asymmetry, Bleeding, Crust, Diameter, Erythema
ABCD only — Evolution is not part of the criteria

variants.quizQuestion: Approximately 50% of melanomas carry a BRAF mutation (V600E). What class of drug specifically targets this mutation?
variants.explanation: The V600E BRAF mutation constitutively activates the MAP kinase signalling pathway, driving uncontrolled melanocyte proliferation. BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib) block this. They are often combined with MEK inhibitors (trametinib, cobimetinib) to delay resistance. This combination produces dramatic initial responses but resistance typically develops within ~12 months.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle type is voluntary, striated, multinucleate, and attached to bone?
facts.explanation: Skeletal muscle is the only voluntary muscle type. Its multinucleate cells (muscle fibers) are formed by the fusion of many precursor cells. The ~600 skeletal muscles in the body move the skeleton and maintain posture.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section of striated skeletal muscle fiber showing multiple peripheral nuclei and banded pattern

variants.quizQuestion: Which of the three muscle types has multinucleate cells?
variants.explanation: Skeletal muscle fibers are multinucleate because they form by the fusion of many myoblasts. Cardiac and smooth muscle cells each have a single nucleus.

variants.quizQuestion: Approximately how many skeletal muscles are in the human body?
variants.explanation: The human body contains approximately 600 named skeletal muscles. They make up roughly 40% of body mass in a typical adult.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle type is striated and involuntary, contains intercalated discs, and has uninucleate branched cells?
facts.explanation: Cardiac muscle is unique: it looks striated like skeletal muscle but is involuntary like smooth muscle. Intercalated discs connect adjacent cardiomyocytes and contain gap junctions that allow electrical impulses to spread rapidly, making the heart beat as a coordinated unit.
facts.visualDescription: Cardiac muscle cells with visible branching and dark intercalated disc lines between cells

variants.quizQuestion: What structures in cardiac muscle allow electrical coupling between adjacent cells?
variants.explanation: Intercalated discs are specialized junctions between cardiomyocytes. They contain gap junctions (electrical synapses) that let action potentials pass directly from cell to cell, and desmosomes that mechanically link the cells during contraction.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle type is non-striated, involuntary, spindle-shaped, and found in the walls of hollow organs such as the intestines and bladder?
facts.explanation: Smooth muscle lacks the sarcomeric banding pattern of skeletal and cardiac muscle — hence 'smooth.' It lines the digestive tract, blood vessels, bladder, and uterus, working without conscious control to move substances through the body.
facts.visualDescription: Spindle-shaped smooth muscle cells arranged in parallel layers without visible striations

variants.quizQuestion: Which muscle type controls peristalsis in the digestive tract?
variants.explanation: Peristaltic waves that push food through the gut are produced by smooth muscle in the muscularis layer of the gastrointestinal wall. This is entirely involuntary — you cannot consciously stop your intestines from contracting.

facts.quizQuestion: Which skeletal muscle fiber type is fatigue-resistant, aerobic, appears dark red due to high myoglobin content, and is used for posture and endurance activities?
facts.explanation: Type I fibers are the workhorses of sustained activity. Their abundant mitochondria and myoglobin (the oxygen-binding protein that gives them a dark red color) make them ideal for long-duration, low-intensity work like maintaining posture and marathon running.
facts.visualDescription: Dark red slow-twitch muscle fiber cross-section with dense mitochondria visible

variants.quizQuestion: Which protein gives Type I muscle fibers their characteristic dark red color?
variants.explanation: Myoglobin is an oxygen-binding protein similar to hemoglobin. High concentrations in Type I fibers store oxygen for aerobic respiration, giving slow-twitch muscle its deep red appearance — think dark meat in poultry.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle fiber type fatigues quickly, relies on anaerobic glycolysis, has low myoglobin content, and is recruited for sprinting and power movements?
facts.explanation: Type IIx fibers generate force rapidly but exhaust their glycogen stores quickly and produce lactic acid. Their low myoglobin content makes them pale. Sprinters have a high proportion of these fibers, while endurance athletes tend to have more Type I fibers.
facts.visualDescription: Pale fast-twitch fiber cross-section showing sparse mitochondria compared to slow-twitch fiber

variants.quizQuestion: A sprinter's muscle biopsy would show a high proportion of which fiber type?
variants.explanation: Elite sprinters have a high percentage of Type IIx fast-glycolytic fibers, which produce explosive force over short durations. Marathon runners, by contrast, tend to have predominantly Type I slow-oxidative fibers.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the functional unit of a skeletal muscle fiber, defined as the segment between two Z-lines?
facts.explanation: The sarcomere is the repeating contractile unit of striated muscle. Each sarcomere runs from one Z-line to the next and contains interdigitating thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments. When thousands of sarcomeres shorten simultaneously, the entire muscle contracts.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of a single sarcomere showing Z-lines at each end, A-band in the middle, and I-band near the Z-lines

variants.quizQuestion: The boundaries of a sarcomere are defined by which structural landmark?
variants.explanation: Z-lines (also called Z-discs) are protein structures that anchor thin actin filaments and mark the ends of each sarcomere. The distance between Z-lines shortens during contraction as actin and myosin filaments slide past each other.

facts.quizQuestion: In the sliding filament model, what molecular event exposes the myosin-binding sites on actin to initiate contraction?
facts.explanation: At rest, tropomyosin physically blocks actin's myosin-binding sites. When Ca²⁺ is released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, it binds to troponin — a regulatory protein complex on the thin filament. This causes a conformational shift that moves tropomyosin aside, exposing actin so myosin heads can attach and execute the power stroke. Ca²⁺ binds troponin C, causing tropomyosin to shift and expose myosin-binding sites on actin, enabling cross-bridge formation.
facts.visualDescription: Thin filament diagram showing troponin complex, tropomyosin strand, and actin monomers with binding sites exposed after Ca2+ binding

variants.quizQuestion: Which intracellular organelle releases calcium to trigger skeletal muscle contraction?
variants.explanation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a specialized form of endoplasmic reticulum that wraps around each myofibril. When an action potential travels down the T-tubules, it triggers voltage-gated channels to release a flood of Ca²⁺ from the sarcoplasmic reticulum into the cytoplasm, initiating contraction.

facts.quizQuestion: What neurotransmitter does a motor neuron release at the neuromuscular junction to depolarize the motor end plate?
facts.explanation: When a motor neuron action potential reaches the axon terminal, synaptic vesicles fuse with the membrane and release acetylcholine into the synaptic cleft. ACh binds nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate, causing depolarization that propagates as an action potential along the muscle fiber's sarcolemma and down T-tubules.
facts.visualDescription: Neuromuscular junction diagram showing motor neuron terminal, synaptic cleft, and muscle fiber motor end plate

variants.quizQuestion: A motor unit consists of one motor neuron and what other component?
variants.correctAnswer: All the muscle fibers it innervates
variants.explanation: A motor unit is the functional unit of muscle control — one alpha motor neuron and every muscle fiber it contacts. Small motor units (few fibers) allow fine control; large motor units (many fibers) generate greater but less precise force.

facts.quizQuestion: Which facial muscle encircles the eye and is responsible for closing the eyelids?
facts.explanation: The orbicularis oculi is a sphincter muscle arranged in concentric rings around the orbital opening. Its orbital portion forcefully closes the eye (as in a tight wink), while its palpebral portion gently closes the eyelids during blinking. It is innervated by CN VII (facial nerve).
facts.visualDescription: Front view of face with orbicularis oculi highlighted as a ring-shaped muscle around both eyes

variants.quizQuestion: Damage to CN VII would impair the ability to close which facial structure?
variants.explanation: Bell's palsy (CN VII paralysis) causes inability to close the eye on the affected side because the orbicularis oculi, which closes the eyelids, is innervated by CN VII. This can lead to corneal damage from drying if untreated.

facts.quizQuestion: Which facial muscle draws the corner of the mouth upward and laterally, producing a smile?
facts.explanation: The zygomaticus major runs from the zygomatic bone (cheekbone) to the corner of the mouth. Its contraction lifts the mouth corners into a smile. Along with the orbicularis oculi, it contributes to a genuine (Duchenne) smile.
facts.visualDescription: Lateral face diagram showing zygomaticus major running diagonally from cheekbone to mouth corner

variants.quizQuestion: All muscles of facial expression are innervated by which cranial nerve?
variants.explanation: The facial nerve (CN VII) innervates all muscles of facial expression, including the orbicularis oculi, zygomaticus major, frontalis, orbicularis oris, buccinator, and platysma. This is why Bell's palsy — a CN VII lesion — causes one-sided facial drooping affecting all these muscles simultaneously.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle compresses the cheek to keep food between the teeth during chewing and is also used when blowing a trumpet?
facts.explanation: The buccinator forms the muscular wall of the cheek. During chewing it presses the cheek against the teeth to keep food on the occlusal surface. It is also the primary muscle used to blow out air — hence its name from Latin 'buccina' (trumpet). Despite assisting mastication, it is innervated by CN VII, not CN V3.
facts.visualDescription: Buccinator muscle highlighted on the lateral cheek wall, deep to the skin and superficial to the oral mucosa

variants.quizQuestion: The buccinator muscle is innervated by which cranial nerve?
variants.explanation: Although the buccinator assists in chewing, it is innervated by CN VII (facial nerve), not CN V3 like the true muscles of mastication. This distinction matters clinically — a CN VII lesion can impair chewing efficiency even though the jaw-closing muscles are intact.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle of mastication is considered the strongest muscle in the body by bite force, and elevates (closes) the mandible?
facts.explanation: The masseter is a thick, powerful muscle running from the zygomatic arch to the angle of the mandible. It is the primary jaw-closer and generates the greatest bite force of any muscle. Like all muscles of mastication, it is innervated by CN V3 (the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve).
facts.visualDescription: Lateral skull view with masseter muscle highlighted as a broad rectangular muscle over the jaw angle

variants.quizQuestion: The muscles of mastication are all innervated by which cranial nerve branch?
variants.explanation: All four muscles of mastication — masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid, and lateral pterygoid — are innervated by CN V3, the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve. This branch also carries sensory information from the lower jaw and teeth.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle of mastication depresses and protrudes the mandible (opens the jaw), making it unique among the four muscles of mastication?
facts.explanation: While the masseter, temporalis, and medial pterygoid all elevate (close) the jaw, the lateral pterygoid is the exception — it opens the jaw by depressing and protracting the mandible. It also guides lateral grinding movements by acting unilaterally.
facts.visualDescription: Medial view of mandible showing lateral pterygoid muscle from greater wing of sphenoid to the mandibular condyle

variants.quizQuestion: Which two muscles of mastication elevate the mandible AND can retract it?
variants.explanation: The temporalis, a fan-shaped muscle filling the temporal fossa, both elevates the mandible (via its anterior fibers) and retracts the protruded jaw (via its posterior fibers). The masseter primarily elevates without significant retraction.

facts.quizQuestion: Which neck muscle originates from the sternum and clavicle, inserts on the mastoid process, and when acting unilaterally causes ipsilateral lateral flexion and contralateral rotation of the head?
facts.explanation: The sternocleidomastoid (SCM) is the strap-like muscle visible on the side of the neck. Acting on one side, it tilts the head toward the same side and rotates the face to the opposite side. Acting bilaterally, it flexes the neck (chin toward chest). It is innervated by CN XI (spinal accessory nerve).
facts.visualDescription: Neck with prominent sternocleidomastoid muscle highlighted running diagonally from behind the ear to the sternoclavicular joint

variants.quizQuestion: The sternocleidomastoid and trapezius are both innervated by which cranial nerve?
variants.explanation: CN XI (spinal accessory nerve) innervates two muscles: the sternocleidomastoid and the trapezius. Because both are supplied by the same nerve, a CN XI lesion causes weakness of both ipsilateral head rotation and ipsilateral shoulder elevation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which group of neck muscles originates from the cervical transverse processes, inserts on ribs 1–2, and acts as accessory muscles of inspiration by elevating the upper ribs?
facts.explanation: The scalenes (anterior, middle, and posterior) run from the transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae to the first two ribs. Their primary role is lateral neck flexion, but they are also important accessory inspiratory muscles — they lift the upper rib cage, increasing thoracic volume during deep or labored breathing.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior neck dissection showing three scalene muscles as columns between the cervical spine and the first two ribs

variants.quizQuestion: The scalene muscles are innervated by which nerve network?
variants.explanation: The scalene muscles are innervated by branches of the cervical plexus (ventral rami of C3-C8). The brachial plexus travels between the anterior and middle scalenes — this space (the scalene triangle) is clinically significant in thoracic outlet syndrome.

facts.quizQuestion: Which anterior chest muscle originates from the clavicle, sternum, and ribs 1–6, inserts on the greater tubercle of the humerus, and performs flexion, adduction, and medial rotation of the arm?
facts.explanation: The pectoralis major is the large fan-shaped chest muscle used for pushing movements. Its clavicular head flexes the arm, while its sternocostal head adducts and medially rotates it. It is innervated by both the medial and lateral pectoral nerves from the brachial plexus.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior chest with large fan-shaped pectoralis major highlighted from sternum and clavicle converging to the humerus

variants.quizQuestion: What movement does the pectoralis major perform when you do a push-up or bench press?
variants.correctAnswer: Horizontal adduction (and medial rotation) of the arm
variants.explanation: In a push-up or bench press, the pectoralis major horizontally adducts and medially rotates the humerus, pressing the arms forward and inward. The triceps extend the elbow simultaneously. Both movements are essential to the pushing pattern.

facts.quizQuestion: Which abdominal muscle runs from the pubic crest to the xiphoid process and ribs 5–7, is responsible for trunk flexion (sit-ups), and is divided into segments by tendinous intersections?
facts.explanation: The rectus abdominis is the paired strap muscle visible as the 'six-pack' when well developed. It runs vertically in the anterior abdominal wall, enclosed in the rectus sheath. It flexes the lumbar spine (e.g., in sit-ups) and compresses abdominal contents, increasing intra-abdominal pressure.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior abdominal wall showing rectus abdominis as segmented vertical muscles either side of the linea alba

variants.quizQuestion: The rectus abdominis is enclosed in a fibrous sheath formed by contributions from which muscles?
variants.correctAnswer: External oblique, internal oblique, and transversus abdominis
variants.explanation: The aponeuroses (flat tendon sheets) of the three lateral abdominal muscles split and interweave to form the rectus sheath that surrounds the rectus abdominis. This arrangement transfers force across the midline (linea alba) and provides structural support to the anterior abdomen.

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the deepest abdominal muscle, running horizontally from the lower ribs and iliac crest to the linea alba, and acts primarily to compress the abdomen?
facts.explanation: The transversus abdominis (TA) is the innermost layer of the abdominal wall. Its horizontal fibers act like a corset, compressing abdominal contents and increasing intra-abdominal pressure. It is a key stabilizer of the lumbar spine and is targeted specifically in core rehabilitation programs.
facts.visualDescription: Lateral cross-section of abdomen showing external oblique, internal oblique, and transversus abdominis as three distinct layers with innermost having horizontal fibers

variants.quizQuestion: When turning your torso to the right, which abdominal muscle on the LEFT side is the primary mover?
variants.explanation: The external oblique performs contralateral rotation — the left external oblique rotates the trunk to the right. The internal oblique does ipsilateral rotation — the right internal oblique also rotates right. Both muscles work together for most rotational movements.

facts.quizQuestion: Which abdominal muscle originates from ribs 5–12 and performs contralateral trunk rotation and lateral flexion?
facts.explanation: The external oblique is the most superficial of the three lateral abdominal muscles. Its fibers run 'hands in pockets' direction — obliquely downward and medially. It rotates the trunk to the opposite side (right external oblique → left rotation) and is innervated by intercostal nerves T7–T12.
facts.visualDescription: Lateral abdominal wall with external oblique fibers running diagonally downward and medially, like fingers pointing into pockets

variants.quizQuestion: Which abdominal muscle performs IPSILATERAL trunk rotation?
variants.explanation: Internal oblique fibers run perpendicular to the external oblique — upward and medially. This means they rotate the trunk to the same side as their location (ipsilateral rotation). Paired obliques work together for lateral flexion and are antagonists to each other in rotation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which dome-shaped muscle is the primary muscle of inspiration and is innervated by the phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5)?
facts.explanation: The diaphragm is the musculotendinous partition between the thoracic and abdominal cavities. When it contracts, its dome flattens downward, increasing thoracic volume and lowering intrathoracic pressure, causing air to flow into the lungs. The mnemonic 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive' reminds students of its phrenic nerve supply.
facts.visualDescription: Inferior view of diaphragm as a dome-shaped muscle with central tendon, showing openings for aorta, esophagus, and inferior vena cava

variants.quizQuestion: Complete the mnemonic: 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the ___ alive'
variants.explanation: This classic anatomy mnemonic reminds students that the phrenic nerve, which arises from cervical spinal levels C3, C4, and C5, innervates the diaphragm. A cervical spinal cord injury above C3 can therefore cause respiratory failure by denervating the diaphragm.

facts.quizQuestion: Which large, diamond-shaped posterior muscle originates from the occiput and C7–T12 spinous processes, inserts on the clavicle, acromion, and spine of scapula, and elevates, retracts, and depresses the scapula?
facts.explanation: The trapezius has three functional parts: the upper fibers elevate the scapula (shrugging), middle fibers retract it, and lower fibers depress it. All three parts share CN XI (spinal accessory nerve) innervation — the same nerve that supplies the sternocleidomastoid.
facts.visualDescription: Posterior view showing large diamond/kite-shaped trapezius muscle spanning from skull base to mid-thoracic spine and laterally to both shoulders

variants.quizQuestion: Shoulder shrugging is primarily performed by which portion of the trapezius?
variants.explanation: The upper trapezius fibers run from the skull and upper cervical vertebrae to the lateral clavicle. Their near-vertical orientation makes them ideal for elevating the shoulder girdle (shrugging). They work with the levator scapulae for this movement.

facts.quizQuestion: Which large posterior muscle originates from T7–L5 spinous processes and the iliac crest, inserts on the intertubercular groove of the humerus, and performs extension, adduction, and medial rotation of the arm?
facts.explanation: The latissimus dorsi ('broadest muscle of the back') is the primary pulling muscle. It is the main muscle used in pull-ups, rowing, and swimming strokes. Innervated by the thoracodorsal nerve, it works synergistically with the pectoralis major to adduct and medially rotate the arm.
facts.visualDescription: Posterior view with broad latissimus dorsi highlighted from lower spine and hip to the armpit, tapering to its humeral insertion

variants.quizQuestion: The latissimus dorsi is innervated by which nerve?
variants.explanation: The thoracodorsal nerve (C6–C8) branches from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus to innervate the latissimus dorsi. Damage during axillary lymph node dissection (common in breast cancer surgery) can weaken this muscle, impairing pulling and swimming movements.

facts.quizQuestion: Which group of muscles running along the vertebral column originates from the iliac crest and sacrum, and extends and laterally flexes the vertebral column?
facts.explanation: The erector spinae is a column of three parallel muscle groups (iliocostalis, longissimus, and spinalis) running the length of the back. Acting bilaterally, they extend the spine (standing up from a forward bend); acting unilaterally, they laterally flex it. They are innervated by posterior rami of spinal nerves at each level.
facts.visualDescription: Posterior back dissection showing three parallel columns of erector spinae muscles flanking the vertebral spinous processes

variants.quizQuestion: The erector spinae group is innervated by which branch of spinal nerves?
variants.explanation: Each spinal nerve divides into an anterior ramus (supplying limbs and anterior body wall) and a posterior ramus (supplying the back muscles and skin). The erector spinae and other deep back muscles are innervated by these posterior (dorsal) rami at each segmental level.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscles originating from C7–T5 spinous processes insert on the medial border of the scapula and perform scapular retraction and downward rotation?
facts.explanation: The rhomboids (major and minor) pull the scapula toward the midline (retraction) and rotate its inferior angle medially (downward rotation). They lie deep to the trapezius. Weakness of the rhomboids contributes to scapular winging and rounded shoulder posture.
facts.visualDescription: Posterior upper back with rhomboid major and minor highlighted as parallel bands between the thoracic spine and the medial scapular border

variants.quizQuestion: Which nerve innervates the rhomboid muscles?
variants.explanation: The dorsal scapular nerve (C5) innervates both the rhomboid major, rhomboid minor, and the levator scapulae. It arises directly from the C5 nerve root and travels deep to the middle scalene muscle to reach the posterior scapular region.

facts.quizQuestion: Which scalp muscle raises the eyebrows and creates horizontal wrinkles on the forehead?
facts.explanation: The frontalis is the muscular layer of the forehead, technically part of the epicranius muscle. It has no bony origin — instead, it connects to the galea aponeurotica superiorly and to the skin of the eyebrows inferiorly. Contraction lifts the eyebrows and forms the characteristic surprise or worry expression.
facts.visualDescription: Frontal face view with frontalis highlighted as a broad flat muscle covering the forehead above the eyebrows

variants.quizQuestion: In an upper motor neuron (UMN) facial palsy, which portion of the face is relatively SPARED compared to a lower motor neuron (LMN) palsy?
variants.explanation: The forehead muscles, including frontalis, receive bilateral cortical innervation. In UMN lesions (e.g., stroke), the forehead is spared because the contralateral hemisphere compensates. In LMN lesions (Bell's palsy, CN VII damage), the entire ipsilateral face is paralyzed including the forehead — a key clinical distinction.

facts.quizQuestion: Which thin, sheet-like muscle covering the anterior neck depresses the mandible and tenses the skin of the neck, and is a muscle of facial expression innervated by CN VII?
facts.explanation: The platysma is a broad, thin muscle in the subcutaneous tissue of the neck. It originates from the fascia of the deltoid and pectoralis major, sweeps upward over the clavicle and lower face, and inserts into the mandible and skin of the lower face. Despite being in the neck, it is a muscle of facial expression (CN VII), not a neck muscle in the strict anatomical sense.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior neck showing thin sheet-like platysma muscle covering the neck from the upper chest to the lower jaw

variants.quizQuestion: Despite being located on the neck, the platysma is innervated by which nerve, classifying it as a muscle of facial expression?
variants.explanation: All muscles of facial expression, including the platysma, are innervated by CN VII regardless of their anatomical location. The platysma visibly tenses and the skin of the neck ridges during maximum effort or extreme fear — visible in athletes during maximal exertion.

facts.quizQuestion: Which skeletal muscle fiber type is intermediate between Type I and Type IIx, combining moderate speed and moderate fatigue resistance, and is characterized as fast oxidative-glycolytic?
facts.explanation: Type IIa fibers (fast oxidative-glycolytic) are the 'middle ground' fiber type. They contract faster than Type I fibers but are more fatigue-resistant than Type IIx fibers. They use both aerobic (oxidative) and anaerobic (glycolytic) pathways. They are prominent in activities requiring sustained power output, such as 400m running or cycling sprints. Type IIa (Fast Oxidative-Glycolytic) fibers are intermediate between slow Type I and fast Type IIb fibers.
facts.visualDescription: Three fiber type comparison diagram showing Type I (dark red), Type IIa (pink/intermediate), and Type IIx (pale white) in cross-section

variants.quizQuestion: Which muscle fiber type would be predominantly recruited during a 400-meter race — an activity requiring both speed and endurance?
variants.explanation: A 400m race is too fast and intense for Type I fibers alone, but too long for Type IIx fibers to sustain without severe fatigue. Type IIa fibers are ideal: they generate speed above walking pace while sustaining output through both aerobic and glycolytic pathways for roughly 45–60 seconds.

facts.quizQuestion: The deltoid muscle — responsible for raising the arm to the side — is innervated by which nerve?
facts.explanation: The axillary nerve (C5-C6) innervates both the deltoid and teres minor. It wraps around the surgical neck of the humerus, which is why fractures there can cause deltoid paralysis and loss of arm abduction. The three parts of the deltoid perform different actions: anterior flexes and medially rotates, middle abducts, and posterior extends and laterally rotates.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior shoulder view showing the fan-shaped deltoid muscle highlighted in blue, with axillary nerve traced around surgical neck of humerus

variants.question: The axillary nerve (C5-C6) is at risk in fractures of the surgical neck of the humerus. Which large superficial shoulder muscle does it innervate?
The anterior deltoid performs flexion and ___ rotation of the arm.

facts.quizQuestion: Which rotator cuff muscle is responsible for initiating the first 15 degrees of arm abduction before the deltoid takes over?
facts.explanation: The supraspinatus initiates the first 15° of arm abduction. It runs through the subacromial space and is the most commonly torn rotator cuff muscle — typically from impingement or degeneration. The suprascapular nerve (C5-C6) innervates it. The 'empty can test' (arm extended, internally rotated, depressed against resistance) specifically tests supraspinatus integrity.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior shoulder showing supraspinatus emerging from supraspinous fossa, passing under acromion to greater tubercle, highlighted in yellow

variants.question: The 'empty can test' is used clinically to assess which rotator cuff muscle — the one most commonly torn in shoulder impingement?
True or false: The supraspinatus is innervated by the axillary nerve.

facts.quizQuestion: What mnemonic names the four muscles of the rotator cuff — the group that stabilizes the glenohumeral joint?
facts.explanation: SITS: Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis. These four muscles form a cuff around the glenohumeral joint, holding the humeral head in the glenoid fossa. Three (SIT) attach to the greater tubercle; Subscapularis attaches to the lesser tubercle. Collectively they compress the joint and counteract the superior pull of the deltoid during abduction.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior scapula view showing all four rotator cuff muscles color-coded: supraspinatus (yellow), infraspinatus (blue), teres minor (green), subscapularis (red, anterior face)

variants.question: The SITS mnemonic names the four rotator cuff muscles. Which muscle is represented by the 'S' that attaches to the lesser tubercle (not the greater)?
The rotator cuff SITS muscles are: Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, and ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Both the infraspinatus and teres minor attach to the greater tubercle of the humerus. What single primary action do they share?
facts.explanation: Both infraspinatus (from the infraspinous fossa) and teres minor (from the lateral border of the scapula) perform lateral rotation of the arm. Both are innervated by the suprascapular nerve (infraspinatus) and axillary nerve (teres minor). Lateral rotation is tested with the 'external rotation lag sign' — weakness suggests a combined infraspinatus/teres minor tear.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior view of right shoulder showing infraspinatus and teres minor side by side converging on the greater tubercle, arrow indicating lateral rotation direction

variants.question: Testing 'external rotation lag' assesses the integrity of which two rotator cuff muscles that share this action?

facts.quizQuestion: Subscapularis arises from the anterior surface of the scapula and inserts on the lesser tubercle of the humerus. What is its primary action — the opposite of infraspinatus?
facts.explanation: Subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle that medially (internally) rotates the arm. It originates from the subscapular fossa on the anterior (costal) surface of the scapula and is innervated by the upper and lower subscapular nerves (C5-C7). It is the largest and strongest rotator cuff muscle and is tested with the 'lift-off test' (arm behind back, push away from body).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior scapula view showing subscapularis filling the subscapular fossa, tendon crossing to lesser tubercle on anterior humerus, arrow showing internal rotation

variants.question: The 'lift-off test' (pressing the dorsum of the hand away from the lower back) specifically tests which rotator cuff muscle?
True or false: Subscapularis inserts on the greater tubercle of the humerus.

facts.quizQuestion: Injury to which nerve causes 'winged scapula' by paralyzing the serratus anterior — leaving the medial border of the scapula protruding from the back?
facts.explanation: The long thoracic nerve (C5-C7) runs superficially along the lateral chest wall to reach serratus anterior. Because of its long, exposed course it is vulnerable to traction injuries (e.g., carrying a heavy backpack), stab wounds, or radical mastectomy. Serratus anterior normally holds the scapula flat against the thorax and protracts it; paralysis allows the medial border to 'wing' outward, impairing shoulder elevation above 90°.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior view of patient with winged scapula — right medial scapular border lifting away from rib cage, long thoracic nerve path traced in red along lateral chest

variants.question: Serratus anterior protracts the scapula and holds it against the chest wall. Paralysis of this muscle causes 'winged scapula' — what nerve was injured?
Serratus anterior attaches to ribs 1-8 and inserts on the ___ border of the scapula (anterior surface).

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle arises from the inferior angle of the scapula and performs adduction, medial rotation, and extension of the arm — making it functionally similar to latissimus dorsi despite not being part of the rotator cuff?
facts.explanation: Teres major originates from the inferior angle of the scapula and inserts into the intertubercular groove of the humerus (same groove as latissimus dorsi). It is innervated by the lower subscapular nerve (C5-C7). Because it performs the same actions as latissimus dorsi — adduction, medial rotation, extension — it is sometimes called 'lat's little helper.' It is NOT part of the rotator cuff (SITS).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior shoulder showing teres major as a thick band running from inferior scapular angle diagonally to the intertubercular groove, distinct from the smaller teres minor above it

variants.question: True or false: Teres major is one of the four rotator cuff muscles.

facts.quizQuestion: Biceps brachii and brachialis — the two main flexors of the forearm — are both innervated by which nerve?
facts.explanation: The musculocutaneous nerve (C5-C6) pierces coracobrachialis and runs between biceps brachii and brachialis, innervating all three anterior arm muscles. After supplying motor branches it continues as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm. Injury (e.g., from prolonged use of a crutch or anterior shoulder dislocation) causes weakness of elbow flexion and supination, plus sensory loss over the lateral forearm.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of arm showing musculocutaneous nerve threading between biceps brachii (anterior) and brachialis (posterior/deep), with C5-C6 root labels

variants.question: The musculocutaneous nerve (C5-C6) innervates three anterior arm muscles. Which two are the primary forearm flexors?

facts.quizQuestion: Biceps brachii is the most powerful supinator of the forearm. It achieves maximum supination force in which elbow position — flexed at 90° or fully extended?
facts.explanation: Biceps brachii is the most powerful supinator when the elbow is flexed at 90°. Biceps is most powerful at supination when the elbow is flexed at 90°, because the muscle's line of pull is then perpendicular to the radius (maximum moment arm). This is why a screwdriver is most effective when the elbow is bent. Brachialis, by contrast, flexes the forearm regardless of pronation/supination position and is considered the 'workhorse' flexor. Biceps also contributes weak shoulder flexion via its long and short heads.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art arm at 90 degree elbow flexion showing biceps brachii tendon wrapping around radial tuberosity, arrow indicating clockwise supination rotation, palm turning upward

variants.question: The long head of biceps brachii originates from the ___ tubercle of the scapula.
True or false: Brachialis is the primary forearm flexor regardless of whether the forearm is pronated or supinated.

facts.quizQuestion: Biceps brachii inserts via two structures — a tendon onto bone, and a flat fibrous sheet into the forearm fascia. Name both insertion points.
facts.explanation: Biceps inserts via its tendon onto the radial tuberosity (enabling supination) and via the bicipital aponeurosis — a flat sheet that blends into the deep fascia of the forearm medially. The aponeurosis overlies the brachial artery and median nerve at the elbow crease, which is why these structures can be compressed in distal biceps ruptures or tight casting. Biceps brachii inserts via a tendon onto the radial tuberosity and via the bicipital aponeurosis into the forearm fascia.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior elbow showing bifurcated biceps insertion: tendon to radial tuberosity on lateral side, flat aponeurosis sweeping medially over forearm fascia

variants.question: The bicipital aponeurosis fans out medially from the biceps tendon at the elbow. Which two neurovascular structures lie immediately beneath it?

facts.quizQuestion: Triceps brachii — the sole extensor of the forearm — is innervated by which nerve, which also supplies brachioradialis?
facts.explanation: The radial nerve (C6-C8) innervates triceps brachii, brachioradialis, and all posterior compartment muscles of the forearm. It runs in the radial groove (spiral groove) of the humerus, making it vulnerable to mid-shaft humeral fractures — causing 'Saturday night palsy' (wrist drop) when compressed against a hard surface. Triceps' three heads all converge on the olecranon; only the long head contributes to shoulder extension.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior arm cross-section showing radial nerve in the radial (spiral) groove between medial and lateral heads of triceps, with wrist drop illustration inset

variants.question: A mid-shaft humeral fracture damages the radial nerve in the spiral groove. Which clinical sign — inability to extend the wrist — would result?
The three heads of triceps brachii all converge on the ___ of the ulna.

facts.quizQuestion: Which anterior arm muscle originates from the coracoid process, inserts on the mid-medial shaft of the humerus, and performs flexion and adduction of the arm — and is also pierced by the musculocutaneous nerve?
facts.explanation: Coracobrachialis originates from the coracoid process of the scapula (sharing it with the short head of biceps) and inserts on the medial shaft of the humerus at mid-level. It is innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve (C5-C7), which characteristically pierces through it before continuing to innervate biceps and brachialis. This anatomical relationship means that coracobrachialis compression can trap the musculocutaneous nerve.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior arm showing coracobrachialis as a slender muscle alongside short head of biceps from coracoid process, with musculocutaneous nerve piercing through it

variants.question: True or false: The musculocutaneous nerve pierces through coracobrachialis before innervating biceps brachii.

facts.quizQuestion: Which forearm muscle originates from the lateral supracondylar ridge of the humerus, inserts on the styloid process of the radius, and is the strongest flexor of the forearm when in the mid-prone (thumb-up) position?
facts.explanation: Brachioradialis is anatomically a forearm muscle (it crosses the elbow anteriorly) but is innervated by the radial nerve (C5-C6), making it unique — a 'forearm flexor innervated by an extensor nerve.' It is most effective at elbow flexion when the forearm is in the neutral (mid-prone, thumb-up) position, as in a handshake or hammer grip. It forms the lateral boundary of the antecubital fossa.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lateral forearm showing brachioradialis as the most prominent anterior muscle from the lateral epicondyle region down to the radial styloid, forearm in mid-prone position

variants.question: Brachioradialis is innervated by the radial nerve despite being a forearm flexor. Which anatomical feature makes it the boundary of the antecubital fossa on the lateral side?
variants.correctAnswer: Its origin from the lateral supracondylar ridge
distractors: Its origin from the medial epicondyle
Its attachment to the coronoid process

facts.quizQuestion: Pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis — two superficial forearm flexors from the medial epicondyle — are innervated by which nerve?
facts.explanation: The median nerve (C6-C7) innervates pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis, along with most other anterior forearm muscles. Pronator teres performs pronation and forearm flexion, while FCR flexes and radially deviates (abducts) the wrist. Injury to the median nerve above the elbow causes loss of pronation, wrist flexion, and finger flexion (the 'hand of benediction' at rest, with inability to make the 'OK' sign).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior forearm showing median nerve entering between two heads of pronator teres, with color-coded branches to pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis

variants.question: The median nerve passes between the two heads of pronator teres at the elbow. What action does pronator teres perform besides forearm flexion?

facts.quizQuestion: Flexor carpi ulnaris is the only forearm flexor not innervated by the median nerve. Which nerve supplies it — the same one that passes behind the medial epicondyle ('funny bone')?
facts.explanation: Flexor carpi ulnaris is innervated by the ulnar nerve (C7-T1). The ulnar nerve passes posterior to the medial epicondyle in the cubital tunnel — striking this area causes the sharp 'funny bone' sensation. FCU performs wrist flexion and ulnar deviation (adduction). Cubital tunnel syndrome (ulnar nerve compression at the elbow) causes weak FCU, hypothenar wasting, 'claw hand,' and numbness in digits 4-5.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art medial elbow showing ulnar nerve passing behind medial epicondyle in cubital tunnel, branching to innervate flexor carpi ulnaris, digits 4-5 highlighted for sensory distribution

variants.question: Cubital tunnel syndrome compresses the ulnar nerve at the medial epicondyle. Which wrist deviation does this weaken — radial or ulnar?
Flexor carpi ulnaris inserts on the pisiform, hamate, and base of metacarpal ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which forearm flexor inserts on the middle phalanges of digits 2-5 and is therefore responsible for flexing the PIP (proximal interphalangeal) joints?
facts.explanation: Flexor digitorum superficialis (FDS) inserts on the middle phalanges of digits 2-5, flexing the PIP joints and the wrist. It is innervated by the median nerve (C7-T1). At the finger, the FDS tendon splits to allow the deeper FDP tendon to pass through — this splitting is called Camper's chiasm. FDS is tested by holding adjacent fingers extended (to inactivate FDP) and asking the patient to flex the target finger at the PIP joint.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art sagittal finger view showing FDS tendon splitting at Camper's chiasm into two slips inserting on middle phalanx, with FDP passing through underneath to reach distal phalanx

variants.question: True or false: Flexor digitorum superficialis inserts on the distal phalanges.
The FDS tendon splits at Camper's chiasm to allow the ___ tendon to pass through to the distal phalanx.

facts.quizQuestion: Which forearm flexor is the ONLY muscle capable of flexing the DIP (distal interphalangeal) joints of digits 2-5?
facts.explanation: Flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) is uniquely able to flex the DIP joints because it is the only tendon that inserts on the distal phalanges. It has a dual nerve supply: the median nerve (via anterior interosseous branch) innervates the radial half (digits 2-3), while the ulnar nerve innervates the ulnar half (digits 4-5). FDP is tested by holding the PIP joint extended and asking the patient to flex the fingertip. The lumbricals arise from FDP tendons.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lateral finger showing FDP tendon running through Camper's chiasm of FDS, continuing to insert on distal phalanx — only tendon reaching the fingertip

variants.question: FDP to digits 2-3 is supplied by the median nerve's anterior interosseous branch, while FDP to digits 4-5 is supplied by which nerve?
The lumbricals of the hand arise from which forearm muscle's tendons?

acceptableAlternatives: anterior interosseous branch of median nerve
facts.quizQuestion: Flexor pollicis longus — the only muscle that flexes the thumb IP joint — is innervated by which branch of the median nerve?
facts.explanation: Flexor pollicis longus (FPL) is innervated by the anterior interosseous nerve (AIN), a purely motor branch of the median nerve arising in the forearm. The AIN also innervates FDP to digits 2-3 and pronator quadratus. AIN syndrome (compression or palsy) causes inability to flex the thumb IP joint or the index DIP joint, resulting in an inability to make the 'OK' sign (Kiloh-Nevin sign). FPL originates from the anterior radius and inserts on the distal phalanx of the thumb. Anterior interosseous nerve is a branch of the median nerve innervating FPL.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior forearm showing flexor pollicis longus running from radius to thumb, with anterior interosseous nerve branching from median nerve to supply it

variants.question: Anterior interosseous nerve palsy causes inability to flex the thumb IP joint and the index DIP joint. What clinical sign demonstrates this — the inability to form a circle with thumb and index finger?
variants.correctAnswer: Kiloh-Nevin sign (inability to make OK sign)

facts.quizQuestion: Most superficial forearm flexors — pronator teres, flexor carpi radialis, palmaris longus, and flexor carpi ulnaris — share a common origin from which bony landmark?
facts.explanation: The common flexor origin is the medial epicondyle of the humerus. Overuse inflammation of this origin causes 'golfer's elbow' (medial epicondylitis), producing pain and tenderness at the medial epicondyle worsened by resisted wrist flexion or pronation. This contrasts with 'tennis elbow' (lateral epicondylitis), which involves the common extensor origin at the lateral epicondyle.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior elbow showing the medial epicondyle with four forearm flexor muscles fanning out from it labeled: pronator teres, FCR, palmaris longus, FCU
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Medial epicondyle of the humerus

variants.question: Inflammation of the common flexor origin at the medial epicondyle is the clinical condition known as what?
True or false: 'Tennis elbow' involves the medial epicondyle, while 'golfer's elbow' involves the lateral epicondyle.

facts.quizQuestion: Extensor digitorum, extensor carpi ulnaris, and supinator of the deep posterior forearm are all innervated by which nerve — the deep branch of the radial nerve?
facts.explanation: The posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) is the deep motor branch of the radial nerve that enters the posterior forearm by passing through the supinator muscle (via the radial tunnel). It innervates most posterior forearm muscles including extensor digitorum, ECU, extensor digiti minimi, extensor pollicis longus/brevis, abductor pollicis longus, and extensor indicis. PIN compression ('radial tunnel syndrome') causes weakness of finger and wrist extension without sensory loss. Posterior interosseous nerve is the deep branch of the radial nerve (C7–C8).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior forearm cross-section showing posterior interosseous nerve emerging from supinator muscle to branch into extensor compartment muscles

variants.question: The posterior interosseous nerve passes through which posterior forearm muscle before branching to the extensor compartment?

facts.quizQuestion: Which posterior forearm muscle originates from the lateral epicondyle and extends both the MCP joints and IP joints of digits 2-5 via extensor expansions?
facts.explanation: Extensor digitorum (ED) originates from the lateral epicondyle (common extensor origin) and inserts via dorsal extensor expansions (dorsal hoods) onto the dorsum of digits 2-5. It extends the MCP, PIP, and DIP joints and assists in wrist extension. The extensor expansions are complex fibrous sheets that also receive insertions from lumbricals and interossei. 'Tennis elbow' often involves ED's origin at the lateral epicondyle.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art dorsal forearm and hand showing extensor digitorum tendons fanning out to digits 2-5, forming extensor hood expansions over the knuckles

variants.question: True or false: Extensor digitorum is innervated by the posterior interosseous nerve.

facts.quizQuestion: Extensor carpi ulnaris originates from the lateral epicondyle and inserts on the base of metacarpal 5. What two combined wrist actions does it perform?
facts.explanation: Extensor carpi ulnaris (ECU) extends and adducts (ulnar deviation) the wrist. It is innervated by the posterior interosseous nerve. Together with flexor carpi ulnaris, it produces pure ulnar deviation when both contract simultaneously. ECU tendon instability (subluxation out of its groove at the ulnar head) is common in racket sports and produces a painful snap with forearm rotation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art dorsal forearm showing ECU running from lateral epicondyle along the ulnar border to base of metacarpal 5, wrist in slight ulnar deviation

variants.question: Extensor carpi ulnaris and flexor carpi ulnaris, when contracting simultaneously, produce which pure wrist motion?

facts.quizQuestion: Which deep posterior forearm muscle, originating from the lateral epicondyle and supinator crest of the ulna, supinates the forearm at all elbow positions — and also wraps around the posterior interosseous nerve?
facts.explanation: Supinator originates from the lateral epicondyle and supinator crest of the ulna, wrapping around the proximal radius to insert on its proximal lateral surface. It supinates the forearm regardless of elbow position (unlike biceps, which supinates most effectively at 90°). The posterior interosseous nerve (deep radial) passes through the radial tunnel between supinator's superficial and deep heads — compression here causes radial tunnel syndrome.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art proximal forearm cross-section showing supinator wrapping around the radius, with posterior interosseous nerve threading between its two heads in the radial tunnel

variants.question: Supinator supinates at all elbow positions, whereas biceps is the most powerful supinator only in which elbow position?

facts.quizQuestion: Extensor digitorum, extensor carpi ulnaris, extensor carpi radialis brevis, and extensor digiti minimi all share a common origin from which bony landmark — the site of 'tennis elbow'?
facts.explanation: The lateral epicondyle is the common extensor origin for most superficial posterior forearm muscles. Lateral epicondylitis ('tennis elbow') is caused by repetitive microtrauma to these tendons, especially extensor carpi radialis brevis. It causes pain at the lateral epicondyle worsened by resisted wrist extension and grip. This contrasts with 'golfer's elbow' at the medial epicondyle (common flexor origin).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lateral elbow showing the lateral epicondyle with four extensor muscles fanning out from it, 'tennis elbow' pain point marked with a red X
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Lateral epicondyle of the humerus

variants.question: Tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis) most commonly involves microtears in which specific extensor tendon at the lateral epicondyle?

facts.quizQuestion: Which pair of posterior forearm muscles perform wrist extension combined with radial deviation (abduction), inserting on the bases of metacarpals 2 and 3 respectively?
facts.explanation: Extensor carpi radialis longus (ECRL) inserts on base of MC2, and extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) inserts on base of MC3. Both perform wrist extension and radial deviation. ECRL originates from the lateral supracondylar ridge (radial nerve, C5-C6), while ECRB originates from the lateral epicondyle (posterior interosseous nerve). When ECRL+ECRB contract with ECU, pure wrist extension occurs without deviation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art dorsal forearm showing ECRL and ECRB running side by side down the radial side, terminating at MC2 and MC3 base, wrist in slight radial deviation
facts.sourceName: Wikipedia — Extensor carpi radialis longus muscle

variants.question: Extensor carpi radialis longus inserts on the base of metacarpal ___, while extensor carpi radialis brevis inserts on metacarpal ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Both supraspinatus and infraspinatus — two of the four rotator cuff muscles — are innervated by the same nerve. Which nerve passes through the suprascapular notch to reach them?
facts.explanation: The suprascapular nerve (C5-C6) passes through the suprascapular notch (under the superior transverse scapular ligament) to innervate supraspinatus, then continues around the spine of the scapula through the spinoglenoid notch to reach infraspinatus. Entrapment at the suprascapular notch affects both muscles; entrapment at the spinoglenoid notch affects only infraspinatus. Suprascapular nerve palsy causes posterosuperior shoulder pain, supraspinatus wasting, and weak abduction/external rotation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior scapula view showing suprascapular nerve passing through suprascapular notch to supraspinatus, then arcing around spine of scapula through spinoglenoid notch to infraspinatus

variants.question: Entrapment of the suprascapular nerve specifically at the spinoglenoid notch (rather than the suprascapular notch) would spare which muscle?

facts.quizQuestion: Teres major is innervated by which nerve — the same one that supplies the lower portion of subscapularis?
facts.explanation: Teres major is innervated by the lower subscapular nerve (C5-C7), which arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus. Subscapularis receives dual innervation from both the upper subscapular nerve (C5-C6) and lower subscapular nerve (C5-C7). The lower subscapular nerve is a branch of the posterior cord alongside the thoracodorsal and axillary nerves — all supplying posterior and medial rotator group muscles.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior cord of brachial plexus branching into upper subscapular, lower subscapular, thoracodorsal, axillary, and radial nerves, with lower subscapular nerve traced to teres major

variants.question: The lower subscapular nerve is a branch of which cord of the brachial plexus — the same cord that gives rise to the axillary and radial nerves?

facts.quizQuestion: The iliopsoas — formed by psoas major (T12-L5) and iliacus (iliac fossa) — inserts on which bony landmark of the femur?
facts.explanation: Both psoas major and iliacus converge into a single tendon that inserts on the lesser trochanter of the femur. As the primary hip flexor, iliopsoas is the strongest hip flexor in the body and is essential for walking, running, and climbing stairs. It is innervated by the femoral nerve (L2-L4).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior pelvis cross-section showing psoas major descending from lumbar spine and iliacus from iliac fossa, both converging to insert on the lesser trochanter of the femur

variants.quizQuestion: Which single muscle group, formed by psoas major and iliacus, is considered the primary and strongest hip flexor?
variants.explanation: Iliopsoas is the combined name for psoas major (origin: T12-L5 vertebral bodies and transverse processes) and iliacus (origin: iliac fossa). Together they insert on the lesser trochanter and generate more hip flexion force than any other muscle.

variants.quizQuestion: Psoas major originates from the T12-L5 vertebral bodies and transverse processes. Which nerve innervates the entire iliopsoas complex?
variants.explanation: The femoral nerve, carrying fibers from L2 to L4, innervates iliopsoas. Femoral nerve injury — from trauma, retroperitoneal hematoma, or surgical positioning — causes weakness of hip flexion and knee extension.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle is the largest muscle in the human body by mass, originating from the ilium, sacrum, and coccyx, and producing hip extension and lateral rotation?
facts.explanation: Gluteus maximus is the largest muscle in the body by mass. It originates from the posterior ilium, sacrum, and coccyx, and inserts via the iliotibial tract and the gluteal tuberosity of the femur. It is a powerful hip extensor and lateral rotator, critical for climbing stairs, rising from a chair, and sprinting. Innervation: inferior gluteal nerve (L5-S2).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior view of pelvis and thigh showing the broad gluteus maximus muscle with origin on sacrum/ilium and insertion on iliotibial tract and femoral gluteal tuberosity

variants.quizQuestion: Gluteus maximus inserts on two structures. One is the gluteal tuberosity of the femur — what is the other?
variants.explanation: Gluteus maximus has a dual insertion: the gluteal tuberosity of the femur (deep fibers) and the iliotibial tract (superficial fibers). The IT band portion transmits force to the tibia and helps stabilize the knee in extension during standing.

facts.quizQuestion: Which hip abductor muscle, innervated by the superior gluteal nerve (L4-S1), stabilizes the pelvis during walking — and whose paralysis causes Trendelenburg gait?
facts.explanation: Gluteus medius originates from the outer ilium between the anterior and posterior gluteal lines and inserts on the greater trochanter. During the stance phase of gait, it prevents the opposite hip from dropping. Loss of gluteus medius function (e.g., from superior gluteal nerve injury) causes a characteristic Trendelenburg gait, where the pelvis tilts toward the unsupported side. Gluteus minimus (same nerve, same insertion) assists with abduction and adds medial rotation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lateral hip view showing gluteus medius fanning from outer ilium to greater trochanter, with walking figure showing Trendelenburg pelvic drop on the opposite side

variants.quizQuestion: Both gluteus medius and gluteus minimus insert on the greater trochanter. What is the primary action they share?
variants.explanation: Gluteus medius and minimus are the primary hip abductors, both originating from the outer ilium and inserting on the greater trochanter. Both are innervated by the superior gluteal nerve (L4-S1). Gluteus minimus also contributes medial rotation of the hip.

facts.quizQuestion: Which lateral hip rotator originates from the anterior sacrum, inserts on the greater trochanter, and is clinically significant because the sciatic nerve passes deep to it — giving rise to piriformis syndrome?
facts.explanation: Piriformis is a lateral rotator of the hip, originating from the anterior surface of the sacrum and inserting on the greater trochanter. The sciatic nerve (L4-S3) normally exits the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen deep to piriformis. In piriformis syndrome, spasm or hypertrophy of the muscle compresses the sciatic nerve, causing pain and paresthesia down the leg that mimics disc herniation. Innervation: nerve to piriformis (S1-S2).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art posterior pelvis cross-section showing piriformis running from sacrum to greater trochanter, with sciatic nerve passing deep to the muscle belly highlighted in yellow

variants.quizQuestion: The sciatic nerve — the largest nerve in the body — passes deep to which hip muscle as it exits the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen?
variants.explanation: In most people, the sciatic nerve exits the pelvis via the greater sciatic foramen, deep to piriformis. In about 15% of people the nerve passes through or above piriformis, increasing susceptibility to piriformis syndrome.

facts.quizQuestion: Of the four quadriceps muscles, which one is unique in crossing two joints — originating from the AIIS and contributing to both knee extension and hip flexion?
facts.explanation: Rectus femoris originates from the anterior inferior iliac spine (AIIS) and crosses both the hip (flexion) and knee (extension) joints before inserting on the tibial tuberosity via the patellar ligament. The other three quads (vastus lateralis, medialis, intermedius) originate on the femur and cross only the knee. All four are innervated by the femoral nerve (L2-L4).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior thigh showing four quadriceps muscles with rectus femoris highlighted in a different color, its origin at AIIS labeled, and the dual-joint crossing illustrated

variants.quizQuestion: All four quadriceps muscles insert on the tibial tuberosity via the patellar ligament. Which of the four originates from the anterior inferior iliac spine (AIIS)?
