facts.wowFactor: The uterus grows from ~7.5 cm to ~30 cm during pregnancy, with the myometrium fibers stretching and hypertrophying — then returning to near-normal size within weeks after delivery.
facts.visualDescription: Uterus cross-section with the myometrium highlighted as the thick middle muscle layer, with contraction wave arrows indicating labor contractions

variants.question: Labor contractions are generated by the ___, the thick smooth muscle layer of the uterus.

facts.quizQuestion: What lower cylindrical portion of the uterus connects to the vagina, produces mucus that changes with the menstrual cycle, and is sampled in a Pap smear?
facts.explanation: The cervix is the lower, narrow portion of the uterus that projects into the vaginal vault. It has an internal os (opening into the uterus) and external os (opening into the vagina). Cervical glands produce mucus that is thin and slippery around ovulation (facilitating sperm entry) and thick and impenetrable during the rest of the cycle (blocking pathogens and sperm). The Pap smear samples cells from the cervical transformation zone.
facts.statement: The cervix connects the uterus to the vagina, produces cycle-dependent mucus, and is the site of Pap smear sampling.
facts.wowFactor: Cervical mucus at ovulation forms microscopic channels aligned with the direction of sperm travel — essentially rolling out a red carpet for sperm at exactly the right moment.
facts.visualDescription: Sagittal uterus diagram with the cervix highlighted, showing internal and external os, with a Pap smear spatula sampling the transformation zone

variants.question: True or false: Cervical mucus is thin and watery at ovulation, facilitating sperm entry.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate normal pH of the vagina, maintained by Lactobacillus bacteria to protect against pathogens?
facts.explanation: The vaginal microbiome is dominated by Lactobacillus species, which ferment glycogen shed from vaginal epithelial cells to produce lactic acid. This maintains a vaginal pH of approximately 3.8–4.5 — strongly acidic — which inhibits growth of pathogens such as Candida, Gardnerella, and sexually transmitted organisms. The acidic environment is also hostile to sperm, which is why cervical mucus (pH ~7) is critical for sperm survival.
facts.statement: The normal vaginal pH is ~3.8–4.5, maintained by Lactobacillus bacteria producing lactic acid, which protects against infection.
facts.wowFactor: Sperm cannot survive in the acidic vagina for long — alkaline seminal fluid temporarily raises vaginal pH at ejaculation, giving sperm a brief window to escape into the cervical mucus.
facts.visualDescription: pH scale from 0–14 with a marker at 3.8–4.5 labeled 'vagina', Lactobacillus bacteria icons surrounding the vaginal canal diagram

variants.question: The acidic vaginal environment (pH ~3.8–4.5) is maintained by ___ bacteria that produce lactic acid.

facts.quizQuestion: What erectile organ of the vulva contains approximately 8,000 nerve endings — more than any other human structure of its size — and has no reproductive function other than pleasure?
facts.explanation: The clitoris is an erectile organ homologous to the penis, extending internally as two crura and two vestibular bulbs. Its external visible portion (glans clitoris) contains approximately 8,000 afferent nerve endings — the densest concentration of sensory nerves in the human body. Unlike the penis, the clitoris has no role in urination or sperm delivery; its sole function is sexual sensation.
facts.statement: The clitoris is an erectile organ of the vulva with ~8,000 nerve endings, making it the most densely innervated structure of its size in the human body.
facts.wowFactor: The full internal clitoris — including crura and vestibular bulbs — is roughly 9–11 cm long, most of it hidden under the pubic skin. The external glans is just the tip.
facts.visualDescription: External and internal anatomy diagram of the clitoris, showing the visible glans at the top and the internal crura extending downward around the vaginal opening like an inverted wishbone

variants.question: True or false: The clitoris contains approximately 8,000 nerve endings and is an erectile structure homologous to the penis.

facts.quizQuestion: At what stage of meiosis are primary oocytes arrested from fetal life until puberty?
facts.explanation: Primary oocytes begin meiosis I during fetal development but arrest in the diplotene stage of prophase I before birth. They remain arrested for years or decades — some oocytes may be arrested for up to 50 years if ovulated late in reproductive life. This prolonged arrest is thought to contribute to the increased risk of chromosomal nondisjunction errors (such as trisomy 21) with advancing maternal age.
facts.statement: Primary oocytes are arrested at prophase I of meiosis from fetal development until puberty, when LH surge triggers completion.
facts.wowFactor: Some oocytes are arrested in prophase I for up to 50 years — the longest cell-cycle arrest known in human biology — which is why older mothers have higher rates of chromosomal abnormalities in eggs.
facts.visualDescription: Timeline diagram showing a primary oocyte frozen at prophase I from fetal life through childhood to puberty, when the LH surge unlocks meiosis resumption

variants.question: Primary oocytes are arrested at ___ of meiosis from fetal life until puberty.

facts.quizQuestion: At what stage of meiosis is the secondary oocyte (the egg that is ovulated) arrested, completing division only if fertilization occurs?
facts.explanation: At ovulation, the oocyte released is technically a secondary oocyte arrested at metaphase II of meiosis — not yet a mature ovum. It remains arrested in this state. If a sperm penetrates the secondary oocyte, fertilization triggers completion of meiosis II: the second polar body is extruded and the haploid female pronucleus is formed. If no sperm arrives, the oocyte degenerates.
facts.statement: The ovulated oocyte is a secondary oocyte arrested at metaphase II; meiosis II is completed only upon fertilization.
facts.wowFactor: Technically speaking, a human egg is never fully formed until the moment a sperm enters it — the final meiotic division completes only in response to sperm penetration.
facts.visualDescription: Ovulated secondary oocyte depicted with chromosomes aligned on the metaphase II plate, a 'pause' symbol on it, with a sperm cell approaching to trigger resumption

variants.question: True or false: The ovulated oocyte is arrested at metaphase II and only completes meiosis if fertilization occurs.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many oocytes remain in the ovaries at puberty, down from 1–2 million at birth?
facts.explanation: A female fetus has approximately 6–7 million germ cells at peak (around 5 months gestation), which atresia reduces to 1–2 million oocytes by birth and approximately 400,000 by puberty. Of these, only about 400–500 oocytes will ever be ovulated in a lifetime; the rest undergo atresia. The continuous loss of oocytes (regardless of ovulation) eventually leads to menopause when the supply is effectively exhausted.
facts.statement: Approximately 400,000 oocytes remain at puberty, down from 1–2 million at birth; only ~400 will ever be ovulated.
facts.wowFactor: The vast majority of oocytes — over 99.9% — are never ovulated. They silently self-destruct through a process called atresia, which continues throughout the reproductive years.
facts.visualDescription: Declining bar chart: 7 million (fetal peak), 1–2 million (birth), 400,000 (puberty), 400 (ovulated in lifetime), 0 (menopause)

variants.question: Of the ~400,000 oocytes present at puberty, approximately how many will be ovulated in a woman's lifetime?

facts.quizQuestion: On approximately what day of a 28-day menstrual cycle does ovulation occur, triggered by a surge in LH?
facts.explanation: In a textbook 28-day cycle, ovulation occurs on approximately day 14. A surge in LH (luteinizing hormone) from the anterior pituitary — driven by positive feedback from peak estrogen levels — triggers final maturation of the Graafian follicle and its rupture ~36–40 hours later. Day 14 marks the boundary between the follicular phase (days 1–13) and the luteal phase (days 15–28).
facts.statement: Ovulation occurs on approximately day 14 of a 28-day cycle, triggered by the LH surge caused by peak estrogen levels.
facts.wowFactor: The LH surge is so reliably detectable in urine that home ovulation predictor kits test for it — a positive test means ovulation is about 36 hours away.
facts.visualDescription: 28-day hormone cycle graph showing estrogen peak on days 12–13, LH surge spike on day 13, and ovulation marked on day 14 with a follicle-burst icon

variants.question: Ovulation in a 28-day cycle is triggered by a surge in ___ on approximately day 14.

facts.quizQuestion: What phase of the menstrual cycle (days 1–13) is characterized by FSH-driven follicle growth and rising estrogen levels?
facts.explanation: The follicular phase runs concurrently with and after menstruation (days 1–13). FSH from the anterior pituitary stimulates several ovarian follicles to grow; the dominant follicle matures while others undergo atresia. The growing follicle secretes increasing amounts of estrogen, which thickens the endometrium (proliferative phase) and eventually causes the LH surge that triggers ovulation on day 14.
facts.statement: The follicular phase (days 1–13) is driven by FSH, which stimulates follicle development and rising estrogen that thickens the endometrium.
facts.wowFactor: During the follicular phase, estrogen shifts from suppressing LH to stimulating it — a rare positive feedback loop in endocrinology — which causes the LH surge that triggers ovulation.
facts.visualDescription: Uterine lining cross-section on day 5 and day 13 side by side, showing thin endometrium at start and thickened proliferative endometrium by day 13, with a growing follicle in the ovary above

variants.question: True or false: FSH drives follicle growth and rising estrogen during the follicular phase of the menstrual cycle.

facts.quizQuestion: What phase of the menstrual cycle (days 15–28) is dominated by progesterone from the corpus luteum maintaining the endometrium for potential implantation?
facts.explanation: The luteal phase follows ovulation (days 15–28). The corpus luteum — formed from the ruptured follicle — secretes large amounts of progesterone and some estrogen. Progesterone converts the estrogen-primed endometrium into a secretory state, thickening the uterine lining with glycogen-rich glands to support a potential implanting embryo. If no implantation occurs, the corpus luteum degenerates, progesterone drops, and menstruation begins.
facts.statement: The luteal phase (days 15–28) is dominated by progesterone from the corpus luteum, which maintains the secretory endometrium for potential implantation.
facts.wowFactor: The luteal phase is almost always exactly 14 days long — it is the follicular phase that varies in length between individuals, not the luteal phase.
facts.visualDescription: Hormone chart showing progesterone peak during days 15–25, corpus luteum icon in ovary, and secretory endometrium with glandular pockets in uterus cross-section

variants.question: The luteal phase of the menstrual cycle is dominated by ___ secreted by the corpus luteum.

facts.quizQuestion: What anterior pituitary hormone stimulates ovarian follicle growth in females and spermatogenesis in males?
facts.explanation: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is a gonadotropin released by the anterior pituitary. In females, FSH stimulates the growth and maturation of ovarian follicles and promotes estrogen secretion by granulosa cells. In males, FSH acts on Sertoli cells to support spermatogenesis. FSH release is controlled by GnRH from the hypothalamus and negatively inhibited by inhibin.
facts.statement: FSH is an anterior pituitary hormone that stimulates follicle growth in females and supports spermatogenesis via Sertoli cells in males.
facts.wowFactor: FSH levels naturally spike after menopause as the pituitary desperately tries to stimulate ovaries that no longer respond — measuring elevated FSH is one way to confirm menopause.
facts.visualDescription: Hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis diagram with FSH arrow pointing from pituitary to both an ovary (follicle development) and a testis (spermatogenesis)

variants.question: True or false: FSH stimulates follicle growth in females and supports spermatogenesis in males.

facts.quizQuestion: What pituitary hormone surges mid-cycle to trigger ovulation in females, and stimulates Leydig cells to produce testosterone in males?
facts.explanation: Luteinizing hormone (LH) is a gonadotropin from the anterior pituitary. In females, the mid-cycle LH surge (caused by peak estrogen positive feedback) triggers final maturation and rupture of the Graafian follicle (ovulation) and stimulates the formation of the corpus luteum. In males, LH acts tonically on Leydig cells to stimulate testosterone synthesis.
facts.statement: LH triggers ovulation via the mid-cycle LH surge in females and stimulates testosterone production by Leydig cells in males.
facts.wowFactor: The LH surge is one of the largest and fastest hormonal changes in human physiology — plasma LH can increase tenfold in under 24 hours.
facts.visualDescription: Hormone cycle graph with a dramatic LH spike on day 13–14, labeled 'LH surge', with ovulation arrow immediately following on day 14

variants.question: In males, LH acts on ___ cells to stimulate testosterone production.

facts.quizQuestion: What hormone produced by the embryo after implantation rescues the corpus luteum, maintains progesterone production, and is the basis of pregnancy tests?
facts.explanation: Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) is secreted by the trophoblast of the implanting embryo beginning around day 8–10 post-fertilization. Because it is structurally similar to LH, hCG binds LH receptors on the corpus luteum and prevents its degeneration — maintaining progesterone secretion through the first trimester until the placenta takes over. Urinary hCG is what home pregnancy tests detect.
facts.statement: hCG is secreted by the embryo after implantation to rescue the corpus luteum; it is structurally similar to LH and is detected by pregnancy tests.
facts.wowFactor: hCG levels double approximately every 48–72 hours in early healthy pregnancies — doctors track the doubling time to confirm normal embryo development.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing implanting embryo secreting hCG, arrow pointing to corpus luteum, arrow pointing to progesterone, and a pregnancy test stick showing a positive result

variants.question: True or false: hCG is produced by the embryo after implantation and is the hormone detected by home pregnancy tests.
hCG maintains early pregnancy by preventing degeneration of which structure?

facts.quizQuestion: What steroid hormone, produced primarily by ovarian granulosa cells, stimulates endometrial proliferation and drives female secondary sexual characteristics?
facts.explanation: Estrogen (principally estradiol in reproductive-age women) is secreted by granulosa cells of growing follicles and, after ovulation, by the corpus luteum. It stimulates proliferation of the endometrium during the follicular phase, promotes development of female secondary sexual characteristics (breast development, fat distribution, bone growth), and at peak levels triggers the LH surge via positive feedback on the pituitary.
facts.statement: Estrogen, primarily from ovarian granulosa cells, proliferates the endometrium, drives female secondary sexual characteristics, and triggers the LH surge at peak levels.
facts.wowFactor: Estrogen actually strengthens bones by inhibiting osteoclasts — the bone-density loss of menopause is largely because estrogen disappears, unleashing bone resorption.
facts.visualDescription: Estradiol molecular structure next to icons of its key effects: growing uterine lining, breast development, and an LH surge arrow on a hormone graph

variants.question: The endometrial proliferative phase is driven by rising ___ secreted by the growing ovarian follicle.

facts.quizQuestion: What steroid hormone, secreted by the corpus luteum and later the placenta, maintains the secretory endometrium and suppresses uterine contractions during pregnancy?
facts.explanation: Progesterone ('pro-gestational hormone') is produced by the corpus luteum after ovulation and by the placenta from ~10 weeks gestation onward. It converts the endometrium to its secretory state, thickening glands with glycogen to nourish a potential embryo. During pregnancy it suppresses uterine smooth muscle contractions and immune responses to the fetus. A fall in progesterone at term contributes to labor onset.
facts.statement: Progesterone from the corpus luteum maintains the secretory endometrium and suppresses uterine contractions during pregnancy; the placenta takes over production after the first trimester.
facts.wowFactor: Progesterone actively suppresses the mother's immune system during pregnancy — without it the immune system would recognize the embryo as foreign tissue and reject it.
facts.visualDescription: Hormone bar chart comparing progesterone level (high) vs. estrogen level (moderate) during weeks 10–40 of pregnancy, with placenta illustration taking over from corpus luteum

variants.question: True or false: Progesterone maintains the secretory endometrium and suppresses uterine contractions during pregnancy.

facts.quizQuestion: During which days of a 28-day menstrual cycle does the menstrual phase occur, when the functional endometrial layer is shed?
facts.explanation: The menstrual phase (days 1–5) marks the beginning of the cycle. With progesterone and estrogen levels having fallen at the end of the luteal phase, the spiral arteries of the endometrium contract and the functional layer (but not the basal layer) is shed as menstrual blood over ~3–7 days. Day 1 is conventionally the first day of visible menstrual bleeding.
facts.statement: The menstrual phase (days 1–5) involves shedding of the functional endometrial layer due to withdrawal of progesterone and estrogen.
facts.wowFactor: Menstrual blood does not clot like a wound because the endometrium produces plasminogen activators that actively digest fibrin — the bleeding naturally self-limits as the basal layer repairs.
facts.visualDescription: Uterus diagram during menstrual phase showing the functional endometrial layer peeling away, with downward arrows indicating shedding, and calendar days 1–5 highlighted

variants.question: The menstrual phase of the cycle occurs on approximately days ___ of a 28-day cycle.

facts.quizQuestion: What paired glands at the vaginal opening secrete mucus for lubrication, homologous to the bulbourethral glands in males?
facts.explanation: Bartholin's glands (greater vestibular glands) are located at the 4 and 8 o'clock positions at the vaginal introitus. They secrete a small amount of mucus during sexual arousal to lubricate the vestibule. They are homologous to the bulbourethral (Cowper's) glands in males. Bartholin's cysts occur when the duct becomes blocked; Bartholin's abscesses are painful infections requiring drainage.
facts.statement: Bartholin's glands (greater vestibular glands) are paired glands at the vaginal opening that produce lubricating mucus; they are homologous to the male bulbourethral glands.
facts.wowFactor: Bartholin's glands produce only a few drops of mucus — they contribute far less to vaginal lubrication than transudate fluid that seeps through the vaginal walls during arousal.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of the vulva/vestibule with two Bartholin's glands highlighted at the 4 and 8 o'clock positions flanking the vaginal opening, with small duct openings indicated

variants.question: Bartholin's glands in females are homologous to the ___ glands in males.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the normal anatomical position of the uterus relative to the cervix — tilted forward toward the bladder?
facts.explanation: In most women the uterus is in an anteverted (tilted forward toward the bladder) and anteflexed (bent forward at the junction of uterine body and cervix) position. This places the uterine fundus just behind the bladder. A retroverted or retroflexed uterus — where the body tilts backward toward the rectum — is a normal variant in about 20–25% of women and is not usually pathological.
facts.statement: The normal uterine position is anteflexed (and anteverted) — tilted and bent forward toward the bladder.
facts.wowFactor: About 20–25% of women have a naturally retroverted uterus (tilted backward) with no symptoms — it is a normal anatomical variant, not a pathology.
facts.visualDescription: Sagittal pelvis diagram comparing an anteverted/anteflexed uterus (tilted forward) and a retroverted/retroflexed uterus (tilted backward), with bladder and rectum labeled for reference

variants.question: True or false: The normal position of the uterus is anteverted and anteflexed — tilted forward toward the bladder.

facts.quizQuestion: What small non-functional cell is extruded when a primary oocyte completes Meiosis I, preserving most cytoplasm in the secondary oocyte?
facts.explanation: When the primary oocyte resumes and completes Meiosis I (triggered by the LH surge just before ovulation), it divides asymmetrically: the large secondary oocyte retains almost all the cytoplasm (and thus the stored nutrients for a potential embryo), while a small, essentially cytoplasm-free first polar body is extruded. This asymmetric division is characteristic of oogenesis and contrasts sharply with the equal divisions of spermatogenesis.
facts.statement: Meiosis I in the primary oocyte produces a large secondary oocyte and a small, non-functional first polar body that receives minimal cytoplasm.
facts.wowFactor: The polar body strategy is oogenesis's way of halving the chromosome number while keeping nearly all the cytoplasm in one cell — without this trick, the egg would have too little nutrient stores to sustain early embryo development.
facts.visualDescription: Oogenesis diagram: large primary oocyte divides asymmetrically — most of the cell becomes the secondary oocyte (large), a tiny first polar body pinches off at the side

variants.question: Why does oogenesis produce polar bodies instead of four equal daughter cells like spermatogenesis?
variants.correctAnswer: To preserve cytoplasm (nutrients) in the egg

variants.question: In oogenesis, Meiosis I produces a large secondary oocyte and a small non-functional ___ that receives almost no cytoplasm.

facts.quizQuestion: Which structure is the first section of the upper airway, responsible for warming, humidifying, and filtering incoming air?
facts.explanation: The nasal cavity is the entry point of the respiratory tract. Its rich blood supply warms air to body temperature, mucous membranes humidify it, and hairs plus cilia trap particles before air reaches the delicate lungs.
facts.visualDescription: Sagittal head cross-section with nasal cavity highlighted, arrows showing air path and turbinate bones labeled

variants.quizQuestion: Air entering the nose is warmed, humidified, and filtered by which structure before reaching the pharynx?
variants.explanation: The nasal cavity's turbinate bones increase surface area to maximise warming and humidification, while mucus and cilia filter out dust and pathogens.

facts.quizQuestion: How many anatomical parts does the pharynx consist of?
facts.explanation: The pharynx is divided into three parts: the nasopharynx (behind the nasal cavity), the oropharynx (behind the mouth), and the laryngopharynx (also called the hypopharynx, leading to the larynx and esophagus). All three serve as a shared passage for air and food.
facts.visualDescription: Sagittal head section with nasopharynx, oropharynx, and laryngopharynx each labeled in distinct colours

variants.quizQuestion: The pharynx is subdivided into the nasopharynx, oropharynx, and ___.
variants.explanation: The laryngopharynx (hypopharynx) is the lowest of the three pharyngeal divisions, splitting into the larynx anteriorly and the esophagus posteriorly.

facts.quizQuestion: At which vertebral levels does the larynx sit in the adult neck?
facts.explanation: The larynx occupies the anterior neck from approximately C3 to C6. It sits below the pharynx and above the trachea, housing the vocal cords and serving as both the voice box and the guardian of the lower airway.
facts.visualDescription: Lateral neck diagram with larynx outlined and C3–C6 vertebrae labeled alongside

variants.quizQuestion: True or false: The larynx is located at approximately the C3–C6 vertebral level in adults.
variants.explanation: In adults the larynx spans C3 to C6; in children it sits slightly higher. Its position descends with age as the neck elongates.

facts.quizQuestion: Which leaf-shaped cartilage flap covers the laryngeal inlet during swallowing to prevent food entering the airway?
facts.explanation: The epiglottis is an elastic cartilage flap attached to the base of the tongue and the thyroid cartilage. During swallowing, muscles tilt it downward to seal the larynx, directing food and liquid into the esophagus. Between swallows it stands upright, keeping the airway open.
facts.visualDescription: Larynx cross-section showing epiglottis tilting downward like a trap-door over the laryngeal inlet during swallowing

variants.quizQuestion: During swallowing, which laryngeal structure acts as a lid to prevent aspiration into the trachea?
variants.explanation: The epiglottis tips posteriorly over the glottis during the pharyngeal phase of swallowing, directing a food bolus or liquid into the esophagus rather than the trachea.

facts.quizQuestion: Which laryngeal cartilage forms the visible prominence in the neck known as the 'Adam's apple'?
facts.explanation: The thyroid cartilage is the largest cartilage of the larynx. Its two fused plates form a V-shaped shield whose anterior angle projects forward, especially in post-pubertal males due to testosterone-driven growth, creating the externally visible Adam's apple.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior neck with thyroid cartilage prominence highlighted and labeled 'Adam's apple', arrows pointing to the V-angle

variants.quizQuestion: The 'Adam's apple' visible in the neck is formed by which cartilage?
variants.explanation: The thyroid cartilage's anterior angle protrudes visibly, more so in males after puberty. It protects the vocal cords housed just behind it.

facts.quizQuestion: Which laryngeal cartilage is unique in being the only complete ring in the entire airway?
facts.explanation: The cricoid cartilage forms a complete ring around the airway — unlike the C-shaped tracheal rings or the other laryngeal cartilages. It sits just below the thyroid cartilage and just above the first tracheal ring. Because it completely encircles the airway, pressure on it (the Sellick manoeuvre) can temporarily occlude the esophagus behind it.
facts.visualDescription: Larynx diagram with cricoid cartilage highlighted as a closed signet-ring shape, contrasted with open C-shape of tracheal rings below

variants.quizQuestion: True or false: The cricoid cartilage is the only complete cartilaginous ring in the respiratory tract.
variants.explanation: All tracheal rings are C-shaped (open posteriorly), but the cricoid is a closed ring — a clinically important landmark for surgical airway access and laryngeal pressure techniques.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate length of the adult trachea?
facts.explanation: The trachea extends approximately 12 cm from the lower border of the cricoid cartilage (C6) to the carina (T4–T5), where it bifurcates into the left and right main bronchi. It is kept patent by 16–20 C-shaped hyaline cartilage rings.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior torso diagram with trachea highlighted from larynx to carina, length measurement arrow showing ~12 cm

variants.quizQuestion: How many C-shaped cartilage rings reinforce the trachea?
variants.explanation: Between 16 and 20 incomplete hyaline cartilage rings keep the tracheal lumen open. The gaps face posteriorly, bridged by the trachealis smooth muscle.

facts.quizQuestion: What smooth muscle bridges the open posterior gap of each tracheal cartilage ring?
facts.explanation: The C-shaped tracheal rings are open at the back, and the trachealis muscle spans this gap against the anterior wall of the esophagus. This arrangement allows the esophagus to bulge into the trachea during swallowing of large food boluses, while the trachealis can contract to narrow the tracheal lumen during coughing — increasing airflow velocity to expel mucus.
facts.visualDescription: Trachea cross-section showing C-shaped cartilage ring with trachealis muscle closing the posterior gap, esophagus visible behind

variants.quizQuestion: The posterior wall of the trachea is closed not by cartilage but by which structure?
variants.explanation: The trachealis is smooth muscle connecting the free ends of the tracheal cartilage rings posteriorly. Its contraction during coughing narrows the trachea, increasing air velocity to clear secretions.

facts.quizQuestion: Foreign bodies inhaled into the airway are more likely to lodge in which main bronchus, and why?
facts.explanation: The right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical (more nearly aligned with the trachea) than the left, which angles more sharply to accommodate the heart. Because of this straighter, larger channel, aspirated objects — pills, peanuts, toy parts — fall preferentially into the right bronchus and its lower lobe branches.
facts.visualDescription: Bronchial tree diagram with right bronchus highlighted, angle measurements showing it deviates less from the tracheal axis than the left

variants.quizQuestion: Compared to the left main bronchus, the right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more ___.
variants.explanation: The right main bronchus branches from the trachea at a shallower angle than the left. This anatomical difference is clinically important because aspirated foreign bodies travel more directly into the right lung.

facts.quizQuestion: How many lobes does the right lung have?
facts.explanation: The right lung has three lobes — superior, middle, and inferior — separated by two fissures: the horizontal (minor) fissure separating the superior from the middle lobe, and the oblique (major) fissure separating the middle from the inferior lobe.
facts.visualDescription: Right lung lateral view with three lobes color-coded and both fissures labeled

variants.quizQuestion: The right lung is divided into superior, middle, and inferior lobes by which two fissures?
variants.explanation: The horizontal (minor) fissure divides the superior and middle lobes; the oblique (major) fissure divides the middle and inferior lobes. The left lung has only the oblique fissure.

facts.quizQuestion: How many lobes does the left lung have?
facts.explanation: The left lung has two lobes — superior and inferior — separated by a single oblique fissure. It is smaller than the right lung because the heart displaces space on the left side, creating the cardiac notch on its medial surface.
facts.visualDescription: Left lung medial view with two lobes labeled, cardiac notch indentation visible on its lower medial border

variants.quizQuestion: Why is the left lung smaller than the right lung?
variants.correctAnswer: Cardiac notch — the heart displaces space on the left
variants.explanation: The medial surface of the left lung has a cardiac notch where the heart sits. This reduces the left lung's volume relative to the right. The lingula, a tongue-like projection of the left superior lobe, is considered the anatomical equivalent of the right middle lobe.

facts.quizQuestion: Which tongue-shaped projection of the left lung's superior lobe is considered the anatomical equivalent of the right middle lobe?
facts.explanation: The lingula (Latin: 'little tongue') is a projection of the left superior lobe that extends antero-inferiorly. Because the left lung has no middle lobe, the lingula serves the same anatomical position and is often called the functional counterpart of the right middle lobe.
facts.visualDescription: Left lung anterior view with lingula highlighted as a downward-pointing tongue-shaped projection from the inferior border of the superior lobe

variants.quizQuestion: True or false: The lingula is a projection of the left lung's superior lobe that corresponds to the right middle lobe.
variants.explanation: The lingula occupies the same anatomical position as the right middle lobe but is a sub-segment of the left superior lobe, not a separate lobe.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the two layers of the pleura enclosing each lung?
facts.explanation: Each lung is enclosed in a double-layered sac. The visceral pleura adheres directly to the lung surface, while the parietal pleura lines the inner chest wall, diaphragm, and mediastinum. The pleural cavity between them contains approximately 5 mL of serous fluid that lubricates movement during breathing.
facts.visualDescription: Lung cross-section showing visceral pleura touching the lung surface, parietal pleura on the chest wall, and the pleural cavity gap between them

variants.quizQuestion: Which layer of the pleura directly covers the lung surface?
variants.explanation: The visceral pleura is firmly adhered to the lung parenchyma and cannot be easily separated from it. The parietal pleura lines the thoracic cavity and reflects around the lung root to become the visceral layer.

facts.quizQuestion: How much serous fluid is normally present in the pleural cavity?
facts.explanation: Under normal conditions the pleural cavity contains only about 5 mL of serous fluid. This thin film acts as a lubricant, allowing the visceral and parietal pleural surfaces to glide smoothly over each other during inspiration and expiration without friction.
facts.visualDescription: Magnified view of pleural cavity showing thin fluid layer between visceral and parietal layers

variants.quizQuestion: What condition results when air abnormally accumulates in the pleural cavity, causing the lung to collapse?
variants.explanation: A pneumothorax occurs when air enters the pleural space — through a chest wound, ruptured bleb, or spontaneously — disrupting the negative pressure that keeps the lung expanded. The lung collapses away from the chest wall.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many alveoli are present in the adult human lungs?
facts.explanation: Modern stereological estimates place the total alveolar count at approximately 480 million in adult lungs. Each alveolus is a tiny grape-like air sac, and together they create an enormous surface area for gas exchange.
facts.visualDescription: Microscopic cross-section of lung parenchyma with alveoli visible as densely packed bubbles, scale bar shown

variants.quizQuestion: The total gas-exchange surface area of both lungs is approximately how large — often compared to half a tennis court?
variants.explanation: The combined surface area of ~480 million alveoli is roughly 70 square metres — about half a tennis court — packed into a volume that fits inside the chest. This extraordinary surface-to-volume ratio enables rapid gas exchange.

facts.quizQuestion: Which alveolar cell type is extremely thin, covers ~95% of the alveolar surface, and is the primary site of gas exchange?
facts.explanation: Type I pneumocytes are flat squamous epithelial cells so thin (~0.1–0.2 µm) that gases diffuse through them almost instantaneously. Despite numbering only about 40% of alveolar cells, they cover ~95% of the inner surface area because of their enormous spread. Their thinness is essential — thick cells would slow diffusion and impair oxygenation.
facts.visualDescription: Alveolar wall cross-section with Type I pneumocyte drawn as very flat, thin cell spanning a large area, fused to capillary endothelium

variants.quizQuestion: True or false: Type I pneumocytes cover approximately 95% of the alveolar surface area.
variants.explanation: Despite being fewer in number than Type II pneumocytes, Type I cells are exceptionally thin and spread over a huge area, making them the dominant cell type by surface coverage and the main gas-exchange interface.

facts.quizQuestion: Which cuboidal alveolar cell type produces surfactant to reduce surface tension and prevent alveolar collapse?
facts.explanation: Type II pneumocytes are cuboidal cells containing lamellar bodies that secrete pulmonary surfactant — a mixture of phospholipids (mainly DPPC) and proteins. Surfactant coats the alveolar surface and dramatically reduces surface tension, preventing alveoli from collapsing on expiration. Without surfactant (as in premature infants with respiratory distress syndrome), breathing requires enormous effort.
facts.visualDescription: Alveolar wall with Type II pneumocyte shown as cuboidal cell with lamellar bodies (small dark vesicles), surfactant layer depicted on alveolar surface

variants.quizQuestion: Respiratory distress syndrome in premature infants is caused by deficiency of which substance produced by Type II pneumocytes?
variants.explanation: Surfactant production begins around 26 weeks of gestation and is adequate by ~35 weeks. Premature birth before this threshold leaves alveoli prone to collapse with each breath, causing infant respiratory distress syndrome (IRDS).

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate partial pressure of oxygen (PO₂) in the alveoli?
facts.explanation: Alveolar PO₂ is approximately 100 mmHg under normal conditions. Pulmonary capillary blood arriving from the right heart has a PO₂ of only ~40 mmHg, so oxygen diffuses rapidly down its concentration gradient from alveolus to blood across the thin respiratory membrane.
facts.visualDescription: Alveolus-capillary diagram with PO₂ values labeled: 100 mmHg in alveolar air, 40 mmHg in incoming venous blood, arrows showing O₂ diffusion direction

variants.quizQuestion: Oxygen diffuses from alveoli into pulmonary capillary blood because alveolar PO₂ (~100 mmHg) is higher than capillary PO₂ of approximately ___.
variants.explanation: Deoxygenated venous blood returning to the lungs has a PO₂ of ~40 mmHg. The 60 mmHg gradient drives rapid O₂ diffusion across the respiratory membrane until equilibrium is reached.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the average tidal volume — the amount of air moved in or out with each normal, resting breath?
facts.explanation: Tidal volume (TV) is approximately 500 mL in a resting adult. Of this, about 150 mL fills anatomical dead space (trachea and bronchi) where no gas exchange occurs, and ~350 mL reaches the alveoli. Tidal volume can increase several-fold during exercise.
facts.visualDescription: Spirogram graph with tidal volume section highlighted between two quiet breaths, 500 mL labeled

variants.quizQuestion: Of the ~500 mL tidal volume, approximately how much reaches the alveoli for gas exchange (alveolar ventilation per breath)?
variants.explanation: The ~150 mL of anatomical dead space (conducting airways from nose to terminal bronchioles) does not participate in gas exchange. Only the remaining ~350 mL reaches the alveoli.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate vital capacity of the lungs — the maximum volume of air that can be exhaled after a maximum inhalation?
facts.explanation: Vital capacity (VC) is the sum of tidal volume (~500 mL) + inspiratory reserve volume (~3100 mL) + expiratory reserve volume (~1200 mL) = approximately 4800 mL. It represents the maximum usable ventilatory range and is an important measure of respiratory function; reduced VC indicates restrictive lung disease.
facts.visualDescription: Spirogram with vital capacity bracket spanning from maximum inspiration to maximum expiration, sum of TV + IRV + ERV labeled

variants.quizQuestion: Vital capacity equals tidal volume plus inspiratory reserve volume plus which other lung volume?
variants.explanation: VC = TV + IRV + ERV. The residual volume (~1200 mL) is not included because it cannot be exhaled — it remains in the lungs even after maximum expiration and can only be measured indirectly.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate total lung capacity (TLC) in a healthy adult?
facts.explanation: Total lung capacity is the volume of air in the lungs after a maximum inhalation. TLC = vital capacity (~4800 mL) + residual volume (~1200 mL) ≈ 6000 mL (6 L). Because residual volume cannot be exhaled, TLC must be measured with specialised techniques such as helium dilution or whole-body plethysmography.
facts.visualDescription: Complete lung volume spirogram showing all subdivisions stacked, TLC bracket spanning the full height at ~6000 mL

variants.quizQuestion: Total lung capacity equals vital capacity plus which volume that cannot be measured by spirometry alone?
variants.explanation: Residual volume (~1200 mL) remains in the lungs after maximum expiration and cannot be expelled or directly measured by a spirometer. TLC = VC + RV ≈ 6000 mL.

facts.quizQuestion: Which dome-shaped muscle is the primary muscle of quiet inspiration?
facts.explanation: The diaphragm is a dome-shaped sheet of skeletal muscle separating the thoracic and abdominal cavities. During inspiration it contracts and flattens downward, increasing thoracic volume and drawing air in. It is responsible for approximately 70–80% of the work of quiet breathing and is innervated by the phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5 — 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive').
facts.visualDescription: Coronal torso cross-section with diaphragm shown as a dome, arrows indicating it flattens downward during inspiration, lungs expanding above

variants.quizQuestion: The diaphragm is innervated by the phrenic nerve, which arises from which spinal cord levels?
variants.explanation: The mnemonic 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive' reminds students of the phrenic nerve's origin. Spinal cord injuries above C3 paralyse the diaphragm, requiring mechanical ventilation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscles elevate the ribs during normal inspiration, assisting the diaphragm?
facts.explanation: The external intercostal muscles run obliquely from the rib above to the rib below (fibres directed inferiorly and anteriorly). When they contract they elevate and evert the ribs, increasing the anteroposterior and lateral dimensions of the thoracic cage and assisting inspiration. During forced expiration the internal intercostals depress the ribs.
facts.visualDescription: Rib cage diagram with external intercostal muscle fibres highlighted, directional arrows showing rib elevation during contraction

variants.quizQuestion: During forced expiration, which muscles depress the ribs to actively reduce thoracic volume?
variants.explanation: Internal intercostals have fibres that run opposite to the external intercostals. During forced expiration (exercise, coughing), they pull ribs downward, actively reducing thoracic volume along with the abdominal muscles.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate residual volume — the air remaining in the lungs after a maximum forced expiration?
facts.explanation: The residual volume (RV) is ~1200 mL. It cannot be expelled because airway closure and the elastic recoil of the chest wall prevent further deflation. RV keeps the alveoli partially open between breaths and prevents them from collapsing completely. Functional residual capacity (FRC) = RV + ERV ≈ 2400 mL — the volume in the lungs at end of a normal, passive expiration.
facts.visualDescription: Spirogram with residual volume shown below the zero expiration line, cannot-be-exhaled zone shaded, FRC bracket shown

variants.quizQuestion: Functional residual capacity (FRC) is the sum of residual volume and which other lung volume?
variants.explanation: FRC = RV (~1200 mL) + ERV (~1200 mL) ≈ 2400 mL. FRC is the equilibrium point of the respiratory system — the lung volume present at the end of a passive (relaxed) expiration.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate inspiratory reserve volume — the extra air that can be forcefully inhaled beyond a normal tidal breath?
facts.explanation: The inspiratory reserve volume (IRV) is approximately 3100 mL. After a normal resting inhalation of ~500 mL (tidal volume), an additional ~3100 mL can be drawn in by maximal contraction of the diaphragm and accessory inspiratory muscles (scalenes, sternocleidomastoid, pectoralis minor). IRV is the largest lung volume subdivision and provides significant reserve for increased oxygen demand during exercise.
facts.visualDescription: Spirogram with IRV bracket shown above the tidal volume peak, labeled ~3100 mL, accessory muscle icons alongside

variants.quizQuestion: Which lung volume is the largest subdivision at approximately 3100 mL, representing the extra air inhaled beyond a normal tidal breath?
variants.explanation: IRV (~3100 mL) is the largest of the four basic lung volumes. Combined with TV and ERV it forms vital capacity (~4800 mL), the maximum air that can be moved in one breath cycle.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the compound formed when oxygen binds to hemoglobin in red blood cells?
facts.explanation: When O₂ binds to the iron-containing heme groups of hemoglobin, it forms oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂). Each hemoglobin molecule has four heme groups and can carry up to four O₂ molecules. This reversible binding is the primary mechanism for oxygen transport — approximately 98% of blood oxygen is carried this way, with only ~2% dissolved in plasma.
facts.visualDescription: Hemoglobin tetramer diagram with four heme groups each binding an O₂ molecule, red coloring indicating oxygenated state

variants.quizQuestion: Approximately what percentage of oxygen in arterial blood is carried bound to hemoglobin as oxyhemoglobin?
variants.explanation: About 98% of blood O₂ is transported bound to hemoglobin; only ~2% is dissolved in plasma. This is why hemoglobin is essential — plasma alone cannot carry nearly enough oxygen to meet tissue demand.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the primary form in which carbon dioxide is transported in the blood, accounting for ~70% of CO₂ transport?
facts.explanation: Approximately 70% of CO₂ is carried as bicarbonate ions (HCO₃⁻) dissolved in plasma. CO₂ diffuses into red blood cells, where carbonic anhydrase rapidly catalyses: CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃ → H⁺ + HCO₃⁻. The bicarbonate exits into plasma via the chloride shift, while H⁺ is buffered by hemoglobin. This reaction reverses in the lungs.
facts.visualDescription: Red blood cell diagram showing CO₂ entry, carbonic anhydrase conversion arrow, HCO₃⁻ exiting into plasma via chloride shift, H⁺ binding to hemoglobin

variants.quizQuestion: Which enzyme in red blood cells catalyses the rapid conversion of CO₂ and water into carbonic acid during CO₂ transport?
variants.explanation: Carbonic anhydrase accelerates the reaction CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ by approximately 5000-fold. Without it, CO₂ would accumulate in tissues and the lungs could not expel it fast enough.

facts.quizQuestion: CO₂ that binds directly to the amino groups of hemoglobin protein chains forms which compound, accounting for ~23% of CO₂ transport?
facts.explanation: About 23% of CO₂ binds to the N-terminal amino groups of globin chains, forming carbaminohemoglobin (HbCO₂). This is distinct from carboxyhemoglobin (HbCO), which is the toxic compound formed when carbon monoxide — not CO₂ — binds to hemoglobin. The remaining ~7% of CO₂ is transported dissolved in plasma.
facts.visualDescription: Hemoglobin molecule with CO₂ molecules attached to terminal amino groups of globin chains, labeled carbaminohemoglobin

variants.quizQuestion: Approximately what percentage of CO₂ is transported dissolved directly in blood plasma?
variants.explanation: Only ~7% of CO₂ is simply dissolved in plasma (as CO₂ gas in solution). The majority is converted to bicarbonate (~70%) or carried as carbaminohemoglobin (~23%).

facts.quizQuestion: Which physiological effect describes how a decrease in blood pH (increased acidity) causes hemoglobin to release oxygen more readily to tissues?
facts.explanation: The Bohr effect, described by Christian Bohr in 1904, states that lower pH (higher H⁺ concentration) decreases hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen, shifting the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve to the right. Actively metabolising tissues produce CO₂ and lactic acid, lowering local pH — this triggers hemoglobin to unload more O₂ precisely where it is needed most.
facts.visualDescription: Oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve with two sigmoidal lines: one at normal pH shifted left, one at lower pH shifted right, arrows showing increased O₂ release at lower pH

variants.quizQuestion: The Bohr effect states that lower pH shifts the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve to the right. What does a rightward shift mean for oxygen delivery?
variants.explanation: A rightward shift means that at any given PO₂, hemoglobin holds less O₂ — it unloads more. This is beneficial in exercising muscle where pH falls, ensuring tissues receive more O₂ when demand is highest.

facts.quizQuestion: What shape is the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve, and why is this physiologically advantageous?
facts.explanation: The curve is sigmoidal (S-shaped) due to cooperative binding: as each O₂ molecule binds to hemoglobin it increases the affinity of remaining heme groups for more O₂ (T→R state transition). The flat upper portion (high PO₂ range) means hemoglobin loads O₂ efficiently in the lungs even if PO₂ drops somewhat. The steep lower portion means hemoglobin releases large amounts of O₂ to tissues for small drops in PO₂ — maximising delivery where demand is greatest.
facts.visualDescription: Classic S-shaped oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve with PO₂ on x-axis, % saturation on y-axis; flat upper region labeled 'loading zone (lungs)', steep lower region labeled 'unloading zone (tissues)'

variants.quizQuestion: The sigmoidal shape of the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve is caused by which property of hemoglobin binding?
variants.explanation: Cooperative binding means each O₂ that binds changes hemoglobin's shape, increasing affinity for the next O₂. This creates the steep mid-range of the curve. Myoglobin, with no cooperative binding, shows a hyperbolic curve.

facts.quizQuestion: Which brainstem center generates the basic, automatic rhythm of breathing?
facts.explanation: The medullary rhythmicity center (comprising the dorsal respiratory group and ventral respiratory group in the medulla oblongata) generates the fundamental cyclic rhythm of inspiration and expiration. It is the essential pacemaker for breathing — damage here is immediately fatal. The pontine centers modulate but do not generate the basic rhythm.
facts.visualDescription: Brainstem diagram with medulla oblongata highlighted, dorsal and ventral respiratory groups labeled, arrows showing rhythmic output to phrenic and intercostal nerves

variants.quizQuestion: True or false: The basic rhythm of breathing is generated in the medulla oblongata, not in the cerebral cortex.
variants.explanation: Breathing is autonomous — it continues during sleep and unconsciousness because the medullary rhythmicity center generates it automatically. The cortex can voluntarily override this (e.g., breath-holding) but cannot sustain the rhythm on its own.

facts.quizQuestion: Which pontine center inhibits the inspiratory center, helping to limit the depth of each breath and regulate respiratory rate?
facts.explanation: The pneumotaxic center (pontine respiratory group) in the upper pons sends inhibitory signals to the inspiratory neurons of the medulla, switching off inspiration before the lungs over-inflate. Stronger pneumotaxic signals shorten inspiration and increase breathing rate; weaker signals allow deeper, slower breaths.
facts.visualDescription: Brainstem diagram with pons highlighted, pneumotaxic center labeled at upper pons, inhibitory arrow pointing down to medullary inspiratory neurons

variants.quizQuestion: The apneustic center in the lower pons tends to prolong inspiration. Which pontine center acts against it, limiting inspiration?
variants.explanation: The apneustic center promotes prolonged inspiratory effort (apneusis if unchecked), while the pneumotaxic center in the upper pons overrides it, enabling smooth cycling between inspiration and expiration.

facts.quizQuestion: Which chemoreceptors, located on the ventral surface of the medulla, are the primary sensors for changes in blood CO₂ levels?
facts.explanation: Central chemoreceptors on the ventral medullary surface detect changes in the pH of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), which reflects arterial CO₂ levels. CO₂ crosses the blood-brain barrier freely and reacts with water to form H⁺, which these receptors detect. Even small rises in PCO₂ strongly stimulate ventilation. They do not detect O₂ directly.
facts.visualDescription: Brainstem ventral surface diagram with central chemoreceptor zones highlighted, CO₂ diffusing across blood-brain barrier shown as dashed arrows

variants.quizQuestion: Central chemoreceptors detect rising CO₂ levels indirectly by sensing a fall in the pH of which fluid?
variants.explanation: CO₂ crosses the blood-brain barrier and dissolves in CSF, forming carbonic acid and lowering CSF pH. Central chemoreceptors detect this pH drop and signal the medullary respiratory center to increase ventilation.

facts.quizQuestion: Where are the peripheral chemoreceptors located, and what do they primarily detect?
facts.explanation: Peripheral chemoreceptors are found in the carotid bodies (at the bifurcation of the common carotid arteries) and the aortic bodies (in the aortic arch). They respond to low PO₂ (hypoxia), high PCO₂, and low pH. Unlike central chemoreceptors, they are the primary sensors for arterial oxygen levels and become especially important at altitude or in severe hypoxia.
facts.visualDescription: Neck and chest diagram with carotid bodies highlighted at carotid bifurcations and aortic bodies at aortic arch, afferent nerve pathways labeled to brainstem

variants.quizQuestion: Peripheral chemoreceptors are the primary sensors for detecting low blood oxygen. At which two anatomical locations are they found?
variants.explanation: Carotid bodies sit at the fork of the common carotid arteries; aortic bodies sit near the aortic arch. Both relay signals via the glossopharyngeal (IX) and vagus (X) nerves to the respiratory centers in the medulla.

facts.correctAnswer: Hypercapnia (raised CO₂) is the primary driver
acceptableAlternatives: CO₂ / PCO₂ is the primary driver
facts.quizQuestion: Under normal physiological conditions, which factor is the primary driver of the urge to breathe — rising CO₂ or falling O₂?
facts.explanation: Rising arterial PCO₂ (hypercapnia) — not falling O₂ — is normally the dominant stimulus to breathe. Central chemoreceptors are exquisitely sensitive to small PCO₂ changes and respond within seconds. Falling O₂ (hypoxia) only becomes a significant breathing stimulus when PO₂ drops below ~60 mmHg. This is why breathing into a paper bag (rebreathing CO₂) causes urgent dyspnoea even when O₂ is still adequate.
facts.visualDescription: Graph comparing ventilatory response to increasing PCO₂ (steep, linear curve) versus decreasing PO₂ (flat until PO₂ < 60 mmHg, then steep), illustrating CO₂ dominance
distractors: Falling O₂ is the primary driver
Blood pH is the primary driver
Lung stretch receptors are the primary driver

variants.quizQuestion: At approximately what arterial PO₂ level does hypoxia become a significant stimulus to breathing via peripheral chemoreceptors?
variants.explanation: The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve is relatively flat above 60 mmHg, so hemoglobin saturation stays high even if PO₂ falls somewhat. Below 60 mmHg, saturation drops steeply and peripheral chemoreceptors fire strongly to increase ventilation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which obstructive airway disease is characterised by reversible bronchospasm — episodes of airway narrowing caused by smooth muscle contraction, mucosal oedema, and mucus hypersecretion?
facts.explanation: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory condition of the airways. Triggers (allergens, cold air, exercise, irritants) cause bronchial smooth muscle to contract (bronchospasm), the airway mucosa to swell, and mucus glands to hypersecrete — all narrowing the lumen. Airflow obstruction is typically reversible with bronchodilators (e.g., salbutamol/albuterol). In contrast to COPD, asthma obstruction fully reverses between episodes.
facts.visualDescription: Bronchus cross-section comparison: normal open lumen versus asthma attack showing thickened wall, contracted smooth muscle, mucus plugging, and narrowed lumen

variants.quizQuestion: A key feature distinguishing asthma from COPD is that asthma's airflow obstruction is typically ___.
variants.explanation: Asthma episodes resolve with bronchodilators and corticosteroids, and lung function returns to normal between attacks. COPD involves permanent structural damage (emphysema, chronic bronchitis) with irreversible airflow limitation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which progressive lung disease caused predominantly by cigarette smoking combines emphysema (destruction of alveolar walls) and chronic bronchitis (mucus hypersecretion and cough) to produce irreversible airflow limitation?
facts.explanation: COPD (Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease) is the third leading cause of death worldwide. Emphysema destroys alveolar walls, reducing gas-exchange surface area and losing elastic recoil — causing air trapping (hyperinflation). Chronic bronchitis is defined as productive cough for at least 3 months per year for 2 consecutive years. Unlike asthma, obstruction in COPD is not fully reversible. Smoking is responsible for ~80–90% of cases.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side: normal alveoli (tightly packed, intact walls) versus emphysematous alveoli (enlarged, broken-down walls, fewer and larger air spaces)

variants.quizQuestion: Chronic bronchitis is clinically defined as a productive cough lasting at least 3 months per year for how many consecutive years?
variants.explanation: This 3-months/2-years definition distinguishes chronic bronchitis from acute bronchitis. It reflects ongoing mucus hypersecretion from enlarged bronchial glands (increased Reid index) due to years of irritant exposure, typically cigarette smoke.

facts.quizQuestion: Which life-threatening condition occurs when a blood clot (usually originating in a deep leg vein) travels to the pulmonary arteries and blocks blood flow to part of the lung?
facts.explanation: Pulmonary embolism (PE) usually results from deep vein thrombosis (DVT), most commonly in the calf or thigh. The clot detaches, travels through the right heart, and lodges in a pulmonary artery. This creates dead space (ventilated but not perfused alveoli), right heart strain, and potentially haemodynamic collapse. Classic symptoms include sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, and tachycardia.
facts.visualDescription: Pulmonary artery diagram with saddle embolus lodged at the main bifurcation, blocked blood flow shown, right heart strain illustrated with dilated right ventricle

variants.quizQuestion: A pulmonary embolism creates areas of lung that are ventilated but not perfused. What is this called in respiratory physiology?
variants.explanation: Dead space is ventilation without perfusion. A PE blocks blood flow to a lung region that continues to receive air — the alveoli are inflated but cannot exchange gases with blood, wasting ventilation and impairing oxygenation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which lung infection causes alveoli to fill with fluid and inflammatory cells (consolidation), impairing gas exchange and causing fever, productive cough, and dyspnoea?
facts.explanation: Pneumonia is infection of the lung parenchyma — bacterial (most commonly Streptococcus pneumoniae), viral, or fungal. Infection triggers an inflammatory response that fills alveoli with exudate (fluid, white blood cells, debris) in a process called consolidation. Consolidated alveoli receive no airflow but remain perfused — creating a shunt (perfusion without ventilation) that drops blood oxygen. This is why pneumonia causes hypoxia.
facts.visualDescription: Chest X-ray-style diagram showing lobar consolidation as an opaque white area in one lung lobe, contrasted with normal translucent air-filled lung tissue

variants.quizQuestion: In pneumonia, alveoli that are perfused but not ventilated (filled with fluid) create which ventilation-perfusion abnormality?
variants.explanation: A shunt is the opposite of dead space: blood flows through the lung but past alveoli that cannot be ventilated (fluid-filled). This blood returns to the left heart deoxygenated, lowering arterial PO₂ — the mechanism of hypoxia in pneumonia.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate functional residual capacity (FRC) — the volume of air remaining in the lungs at the end of a normal passive expiration?
facts.explanation: FRC = expiratory reserve volume (~1200 mL) + residual volume (~1200 mL) ≈ 2400 mL. FRC represents the equilibrium point at which the outward recoil of the chest wall exactly balances the inward recoil of the lung. It is the resting lung volume — the position the chest returns to after each quiet breath. Reduced FRC indicates loss of lung volume (e.g., obesity, pleural effusion, atelectasis).
facts.visualDescription: Spirogram with FRC bracket spanning from end-tidal expiration line down to zero, composed of ERV + RV, ~2400 mL labeled

variants.quizQuestion: FRC is the equilibrium point where the outward recoil of the chest wall balances the inward recoil of the lungs. What approximate volume is this in adults?
variants.explanation: At FRC (~2400 mL), no respiratory muscle activity is needed to maintain lung volume. This is the natural rest position of the respiratory system. Anaesthesia and supine positioning reduce FRC, increasing risk of atelectasis.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate expiratory reserve volume (ERV) — the extra air forcefully exhaled beyond a normal tidal expiration?
facts.explanation: After a normal tidal expiration, an additional ~1200 mL can be forcefully expelled by active contraction of internal intercostal and abdominal muscles. This is the expiratory reserve volume. Together with residual volume (~1200 mL) it forms the functional residual capacity (~2400 mL).
facts.visualDescription: Spirogram with ERV bracket below the resting end-expiration line, showing extra volume expelled by forced expiration, ~1200 mL labeled

variants.quizQuestion: The expiratory reserve volume plus the residual volume equals which lung capacity?
variants.explanation: FRC = ERV (~1200 mL) + RV (~1200 mL) ≈ 2400 mL. FRC is the lung volume at rest after passive expiration — the balance point of respiratory mechanics.

facts.quizQuestion: During exercise, rising temperature and falling pH both shift the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve in which direction, and what is the physiological benefit?
facts.explanation: More O₂ released. Exercise raises muscle temperature and produces lactic acid, CO₂, and H⁺ — all of which shift the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve to the right (reduced O₂ affinity). At any given PO₂, hemoglobin releases more oxygen to the working muscle. This is the Bohr effect in action: the body automatically delivers more O₂ precisely where metabolic demand is greatest.
facts.visualDescription: Oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve showing baseline sigmoid curve and a rightward-shifted curve labeled 'exercise (high temp, low pH)', increased O₂ unloading at tissue PO₂ highlighted
distractors: No change — temperature has no effect
Left shift — more O₂ loaded in lungs

variants.quizQuestion: A leftward shift of the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve — for example caused by fetal hemoglobin (HbF) — means hemoglobin has a ___ affinity for oxygen.
variants.explanation: Left-shifted HbF has higher O₂ affinity than adult HbA, allowing the fetus to extract oxygen from maternal blood across the placenta. This is essential because fetal blood must compete with — and win oxygen from — maternal hemoglobin.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the three forms of CO₂ transport in blood and their approximate percentages?
facts.explanation: Carbon dioxide is transported in three forms: primarily as bicarbonate (via carbonic anhydrase in RBCs). Carbon dioxide is transported in three forms: ~70% as bicarbonate ions (HCO₃⁻) in plasma (converted by carbonic anhydrase in red blood cells), ~23% bound to hemoglobin protein chains as carbaminohemoglobin, and ~7% dissolved directly in plasma. The bicarbonate route is dominant because CO₂ is highly soluble and rapidly converted by carbonic anhydrase.
facts.visualDescription: Pie chart or stacked bar showing three segments: 70% bicarbonate (blue), 23% carbaminohemoglobin (red), 7% dissolved (grey)

variants.quizQuestion: The majority (~70%) of carbon dioxide transported in blood is converted to which ion by carbonic anhydrase inside red blood cells?
variants.explanation: CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃ → H⁺ + HCO₃⁻. The bicarbonate exits into plasma via the chloride shift (Hamburger phenomenon), while H⁺ is buffered by hemoglobin.

facts.correctAnswer: Reduces surface tension, prevents alveolar collapse
acceptableAlternatives: Reduces surface tension to prevent collapse
Surfactant reduces surface tension, preventing alveolar collapse
facts.quizQuestion: What is the primary function of pulmonary surfactant, and which law explains why alveoli without it would collapse?
facts.explanation: Surfactant is mainly dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC). Surfactant (mainly dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine, DPPC) lines the alveolar surface and reduces surface tension, preventing alveolar collapse on expiration. Without surfactant, Laplace's law (P = 2T/r) predicts that smaller alveoli (smaller r) would have higher pressure and empty into larger ones — causing progressive atelectasis. Surfactant preferentially reduces tension in smaller alveoli, equalising pressures and keeping all sizes patent. Pulmonary surfactant lowers alveolar surface tension, preventing collapse on expiration.
facts.visualDescription: Two alveoli of different sizes: without surfactant the smaller one collapses into the larger; with surfactant both remain open as surface tension is appropriately scaled
distractors: Surfactant increases surface tension to maintain alveolar shape
Surfactant transports oxygen across the respiratory membrane
Surfactant acts as an immune barrier against pathogens

variants.quizQuestion: By Laplace's law, which alveoli are at greatest risk of collapsing into adjacent larger ones if surfactant is absent?
variants.explanation: Laplace's law states P = 2T/r: pressure inside a sphere is inversely proportional to its radius. Smaller alveoli develop higher pressure and tend to deflate into larger ones. Surfactant counteracts this by reducing T more in smaller alveoli, stabilising them.

acceptableAlternatives: Terminal bronchioles, respiratory bronchioles, alveolar ducts, alveoli
Terminal bronchioles → respiratory bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveoli
facts.quizQuestion: What is the correct order of airway structures from the terminal bronchioles to the alveoli?
facts.explanation: After the terminal bronchioles (the last purely conducting airways, with no gas exchange), the airway transitions to the respiratory zone: respiratory bronchioles (have occasional alveolar outpouchings) → alveolar ducts (walls almost entirely alveoli) → alveolar sacs → individual alveoli. The transition from terminal bronchiole to respiratory bronchiole marks the start of gas exchange. Transition zone: terminal bronchioles → respiratory bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveoli.
facts.visualDescription: Branching airway tree with each generation labeled from terminal bronchiole to alveolus, respiratory zone shaded in pink starting at respiratory bronchioles
distractors: Terminal bronchioles → alveolar ducts → respiratory bronchioles → alveoli
Segmental bronchi → terminal bronchioles → alveolar sacs → alveoli
Respiratory bronchioles → terminal bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveoli

variants.quizQuestion: True or false: Terminal bronchioles participate in gas exchange.
variants.explanation: Terminal bronchioles are purely conducting — they transport air but have no alveoli in their walls and do not exchange gases. Gas exchange begins only in the respiratory bronchioles and beyond.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate partial pressure of CO₂ (PCO₂) in pulmonary capillary blood returning from the body tissues?
facts.explanation: Venous blood returning from the systemic circulation has a PCO₂ of approximately 46 mmHg, compared to alveolar PCO₂ of ~40 mmHg. This 6 mmHg gradient drives CO₂ diffusion from blood into the alveoli, where it is exhaled. The gradient for CO₂ is much smaller than for O₂ (~60 mmHg), but CO₂ diffuses ~20 times more readily through tissues than O₂, so exchange is equally efficient.
facts.visualDescription: Alveolus-capillary interface with PCO₂ gradient labeled: 46 mmHg in incoming venous blood, 40 mmHg in alveolar air, arrow showing CO₂ diffusion out of blood

variants.quizQuestion: CO₂ diffuses from pulmonary capillary blood (~46 mmHg) into the alveoli (~40 mmHg). Despite the small 6 mmHg gradient, why is CO₂ exchange still efficient?
variants.correctAnswer: CO₂ diffuses ~20 times more readily than O₂ through tissues
variants.explanation: CO₂ has much higher solubility in tissue fluids than O₂, so it diffuses far more readily across the respiratory membrane. This compensates for the smaller pressure gradient compared to O₂.

facts.quizQuestion: Which complementary effect to the Bohr effect describes how oxygenation of hemoglobin reduces its affinity for CO₂ and H⁺, promoting CO₂ release in the lungs?
facts.explanation: The Haldane effect (described by John Scott Haldane) states that oxygenated hemoglobin (HbO₂) is a weaker acid and a poorer CO₂ carrier than deoxygenated hemoglobin. When hemoglobin picks up O₂ in the lungs, it releases CO₂ and H⁺ — facilitating CO₂ elimination. This effect accounts for roughly half of the CO₂ transported between tissues and lungs, working in concert with the Bohr effect.
facts.visualDescription: Dual diagram: in tissues (low PO₂) deoxyHb picks up CO₂ and H⁺; in lungs (high PO₂) oxyHb releases CO₂ and H⁺, labeled Haldane effect

variants.quizQuestion: The Bohr and Haldane effects work together during gas exchange. The Bohr effect promotes O₂ unloading in tissues. What does the Haldane effect promote in the lungs?
variants.explanation: In the lungs, O₂ binding to hemoglobin (Haldane effect) causes it to release CO₂ and H⁺. Simultaneously, the released H⁺ is buffered, shifting carbonic acid back to CO₂, which is exhaled. The two effects are reciprocal and synergistic.

facts.quizQuestion: Which lower pontine center tends to prolong and sustain inspiration, and is normally kept in check by the pneumotaxic center?
facts.explanation: The apneustic center in the lower pons sends excitatory signals to the inspiratory neurons of the medulla, tending to sustain inspiration in a prolonged gasping pattern (apneusis). Under normal conditions it is continuously inhibited by the pneumotaxic center. If the pons is transected above the apneustic center but below the pneumotaxic center, prolonged inspiratory spasms (apneusis) occur.
facts.visualDescription: Brainstem sagittal diagram with apneustic center labeled in lower pons, pneumotaxic center labeled in upper pons, inhibitory arrow from pneumotaxic to apneustic center

variants.quizQuestion: If the brainstem is transected between the pneumotaxic and apneustic centers, what abnormal breathing pattern results?
variants.explanation: Without inhibition from the pneumotaxic center, the apneustic center drives sustained inspiration with only brief expirations. This is the experimental basis for understanding the roles of pontine respiratory centers.

facts.correctAnswer: Nose → pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchi → alveoli
facts.quizQuestion: What is the correct sequence of structures that air passes through from the nostrils to the alveoli?
facts.explanation: Air flows: nasal cavity (warms/humidifies/filters) → pharynx (3 parts) → larynx (voice box, guards airway) → trachea (~12 cm) → main bronchi → lobar bronchi → segmental bronchi → bronchioles → terminal bronchioles → respiratory bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveoli. The conducting zone ends at terminal bronchioles; the respiratory zone begins at respiratory bronchioles. Full respiratory pathway: nasal cavity → pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli. Complete respiratory pathway from nose to alveoli.
facts.visualDescription: Labeled diagram of the full respiratory tract from nose to alveoli with each major structure numbered in sequence
distractors: Pharynx → nasal cavity → trachea → larynx → bronchi → alveoli
Nasal cavity → larynx → pharynx → trachea → alveoli → bronchi
Trachea → pharynx → larynx → bronchi → nasal cavity → alveoli

variants.quizQuestion: At which point in the respiratory tract does the conducting zone end and the respiratory (gas-exchange) zone begin?
variants.explanation: Terminal bronchioles mark the end of the conducting zone — they have no alveoli and do no gas exchange. Respiratory bronchioles are the first structures to have alveoli in their walls, marking the start of the respiratory zone.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the total gas-exchange surface area provided by ~480 million alveoli — often described as roughly half a tennis court?
facts.explanation: The combined alveolar surface area is approximately 70 m² — about half a regulation tennis court (260 m²). This extraordinary surface-to-volume ratio is achieved by packing ~480 million tiny spherical alveoli into lungs that together weigh only about 1 kg. The enormous area, combined with the extremely thin respiratory membrane (~0.5 µm), allows the ~5 litres of blood in the body to become fully oxygenated in less than one second during each passage through the pulmonary capillaries.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side scale comparison: a tennis court outline beside the outline of both lungs, with '70 m² alveolar surface' label; inset microscopy image of packed alveoli

variants.quizQuestion: How thin is the respiratory membrane separating alveolar air from capillary blood, enabling near-instantaneous gas diffusion?
variants.explanation: The respiratory membrane — comprising alveolar epithelium (Type I pneumocyte), basement membrane, and capillary endothelium — is only ~0.5 micrometres thick. This extraordinary thinness, combined with the 70 m² surface area, allows full oxygenation of pulmonary blood in under one second.

facts.quizQuestion: The eyeball wall is composed of how many concentric tissue layers (tunics)?
facts.explanation: The eyeball wall has three concentric tunics (coats). The outermost fibrous tunic comprises the opaque white sclera (posterior 5/6) and the transparent cornea (anterior 1/6). The middle vascular tunic (uvea) comprises the choroid (posterior, supplies retina), ciliary body (controls lens shape), and iris (controls pupil diameter). The innermost nervous tunic is the retina, which contains the photoreceptors.
facts.statement: The eyeball has 3 tunics: fibrous (sclera + cornea), vascular/uvea (choroid + ciliary body + iris), and nervous (retina).
facts.wowFactor: The uvea — the middle vascular tunic — is the most blood-rich tissue in the entire body per unit volume. The choroid layer alone carries more blood flow per gram of tissue than even the brain or kidneys, all to supply the metabolically insatiable photoreceptors in the retina above it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of the eyeball showing three concentric color-coded rings: white outer sclera/cornea, dark-red middle uvea with choroid/ciliary body/iris labeled, and pale inner retina

variants.question: The middle tunic of the eye (the uvea) contains three structures: the ___, the ___ body, and the ___.
True or false: The cornea is part of the fibrous tunic of the eye, along with the sclera.

facts.quizQuestion: Which avascular, transparent structure accounts for 65–70% of the eye's total refractive power?
facts.explanation: The cornea is responsible for the majority of the eye's refracting power — roughly 43 diopters out of a total of ~60 diopters — far more than the lens (~17–20 diopters). Being avascular (no blood vessels), the cornea receives oxygen and nutrients from the aqueous humor anteriorly and tears on its surface. It has five layers: epithelium, Bowman's layer, stroma (90% of thickness), Descemet's membrane, and endothelium. Its high density of sensory nerve endings makes it the most sensitive structure in the body.
facts.statement: The cornea, avascular and 5-layered, provides 65–70% of the eye's refractive power (~43 of ~60 total diopters). The most densely innervated structure in the body.
facts.wowFactor: The cornea is the most densely innervated tissue in the human body — it has roughly 300–600 nerve endings per square millimeter, about 300–400 times more than skin. This is why even a tiny eyelash touching your cornea triggers an intense blink reflex and why corneal injuries are so exquisitely painful.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art eye front view with transparent dome-shaped cornea highlighted, with light ray arrows bending sharply as they pass through it, labeled '43 diopters'

variants.question: True or false: The cornea is avascular, receiving oxygen from the aqueous humor and tears rather than from blood vessels.

facts.quizQuestion: Which biconvex, transparent structure of the eye changes shape via ciliary muscle contraction to focus on near or distant objects (accommodation)?
facts.explanation: The lens is a biconvex, crystalline structure suspended by zonule fibers (suspensory ligaments) attached to the ciliary body. Accommodation — the process of changing focal length — occurs when the ciliary muscle contracts, reducing tension on the zonule fibers, allowing the elastic lens to become more spherical (higher power) for near vision. Relaxation of the ciliary muscle flattens the lens for distant vision. With aging, the lens becomes less elastic (presbyopia — difficulty with near vision). Clouding of the lens is called a cataract.
facts.statement: The biconvex lens adjusts shape via ciliary muscle contraction (accommodation). It becomes more spherical for near vision. Clouding = cataract; aging-related stiffness = presbyopia.
facts.wowFactor: When your ciliary muscle relaxes during distant focus, it creates tension in the zonule fibers that pull the lens flat. When it contracts for near focus, it releases tension and lets the lens spring back to its natural spherical shape. Cataract surgery replaces the natural lens with a rigid artificial one — which is why most cataract patients still need reading glasses after surgery: the artificial lens cannot accommodate.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section showing ciliary muscle relaxed with zonule fibers taut pulling lens flat (distant focus arrow), and ciliary muscle contracted with fibers loose allowing lens to round up (near focus arrow)

variants.question: When the ciliary muscle CONTRACTS, what happens to the lens?
variants.correctAnswer: Zonule fibers loosen and the lens becomes more spherical (near vision)
distractors: Zonule fibers tighten and the lens flattens (distant vision)
The iris dilates to let in more light
