facts.explanation: The lower limb has three key joints: (1) Hip — ball-and-socket (femoral head in acetabulum), allowing multi-axial movement; (2) Knee — modified hinge (femoral condyles on tibial plateau), allowing flexion/extension plus slight rotation when flexed; (3) Ankle — hinge/mortise (talus in tibia-fibula mortise), primarily allowing dorsiflexion and plantarflexion. Each joint type reflects its biomechanical role. Joints of the lower limb: hip (ball-and-socket), knee (modified hinge), ankle (hinge).
facts.visualDescription: Three-panel diagram showing hip, knee, and ankle joints with joint type labels
distractors: Hip: hinge, Knee: ball-and-socket, Ankle: pivot
Hip: saddle, Knee: condyloid, Ankle: hinge
Hip: ball-and-socket, Knee: pivot, Ankle: ball-and-socket

variants.quizQuestion: The hip joint is a ball-and-socket joint. What bone forms the 'ball' and what structure forms the 'socket'?
variants.correctAnswer: The femoral head is the ball; the acetabulum of the hip bone is the socket
variants.explanation: The spherical head of the femur articulates with the deep cup-shaped acetabulum, which is deepened further by a fibrocartilaginous rim called the acetabular labrum. This arrangement allows a large range of motion while maintaining stability — unlike the shoulder's shallower socket, which trades stability for even greater mobility.

facts.quizQuestion: The kidneys sit behind the peritoneum rather than inside the peritoneal cavity. What is the anatomical term for this position?
facts.explanation: The kidneys are retroperitoneal — they lie posterior to the peritoneum against the posterior abdominal wall. This distinguishes them from intraperitoneal organs like the stomach and liver. Their retroperitoneal position means they are not covered by peritoneum on their anterior surface and are embedded in perirenal fat.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of the abdomen showing the kidneys nestled behind the peritoneal membrane against the posterior abdominal wall, surrounded by fat

variants.question: True or false: the kidneys are intraperitoneal organs, meaning they are enclosed within the peritoneal cavity.
The kidneys lie ___ to the peritoneum, against the posterior abdominal wall.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the approximate dimensions (length × width × depth) of a single adult human kidney?
facts.explanation: Each adult kidney measures approximately 11 cm long, 6 cm wide, and 3 cm deep — roughly the size of a fist — and weighs about 150 g. These dimensions are clinically important: kidneys that are significantly smaller may indicate chronic renal disease, while enlargement can suggest obstruction, polycystic disease, or tumour.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art kidney next to a ruler showing 11 cm length, with cross-section inset showing 6 cm width and 3 cm depth labels

variants.question: An adult kidney weighs approximately how much?

facts.quizQuestion: The liver occupies most of the right upper quadrant of the abdomen. As a result, which kidney sits slightly lower than the other?
facts.explanation: The right kidney sits slightly lower than the left because the liver, positioned above and to the right, displaces it inferiorly. This asymmetry is clinically relevant: the lower position of the right kidney makes it slightly easier to palpate on abdominal examination, and explains why the right kidney is more accessible for percutaneous procedures.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art anterior view of abdominal organs: large brown liver in right upper quadrant pushing the right kidney visibly lower than the left kidney
distractors: neither — they are at the same level

variants.question: True or false: the left kidney sits lower than the right kidney because of the position of the liver.

facts.quizQuestion: The renal artery, renal vein, and ureter all enter or exit the kidney through a medial indentation. What is this structure called?
facts.explanation: The hilum is the concave medial indentation of the kidney through which the renal artery enters, the renal vein exits, and the ureter exits. It is the gateway to the renal sinus — the central cavity containing the renal pelvis, major and minor calyces, blood vessels, lymphatics, and nerves. Imaging of the hilum is essential for assessing renal vascular anatomy before surgery.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art kidney viewed from the front showing the concave medial border with arrows labeling the renal artery (entering), renal vein (exiting), and ureter (exiting) at the hilum

variants.question: The renal artery, vein, and ureter pass through the ___ on the medial surface of the kidney.

facts.quizQuestion: Moving from the outer surface of the kidney inward, which zone is encountered first and contains the glomeruli?
facts.explanation: The renal cortex is the outermost zone of the kidney parenchyma, lying just beneath the fibrous capsule. It contains the glomeruli, Bowman's capsules, proximal convoluted tubules, and distal convoluted tubules — the filtration and most of the reabsorptive work of the kidney occurs here. The cortex has a granular appearance in cross-section due to the dense packing of glomeruli.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art kidney cross-section with the outer granular cortex highlighted in lighter red, the inner medullary pyramids darker, arrows showing the layer order from capsule inward

variants.question: True or false: the glomeruli are located in the renal medulla.

facts.quizQuestion: The inner zone of the kidney, the medulla, is organized into cone-shaped structures that contain the collecting ducts. What are these structures called?
facts.explanation: The renal medulla is composed of 8–18 renal pyramids — cone-shaped structures whose tips (papillae) point toward the renal pelvis. Each pyramid contains the loops of Henle and collecting ducts that drain urine into the minor calyces at the papillae. The striped appearance of the pyramids reflects the parallel arrangement of tubules and blood vessels.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art kidney cross-section highlighting the dark striped medullary pyramids pointing inward, with labels for cortex above and renal pelvis below

variants.question: The renal medulla consists of cone-shaped ___ whose tips drain urine into the minor calyces.

facts.quizQuestion: Despite making up less than 1% of body weight, the kidneys receive a remarkably large share of cardiac output at rest. What percentage of cardiac output goes to the kidneys?
facts.explanation: At rest, the kidneys receive approximately 25% of cardiac output — about 1.25 L of blood per minute — despite weighing only about 300 g combined. This enormous blood flow is necessary to filter the entire blood volume roughly 300 times per day. This high flow rate also makes the kidneys particularly vulnerable to ischaemic injury during shock or hypotension.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art infographic showing a heart with 4 arrows of different sizes: kidneys getting a thick 25% arrow, brain 20%, other organs sharing the rest

variants.question: True or false: the kidneys receive approximately 25% of the heart's resting cardiac output.
The kidneys receive approximately ___% of resting cardiac output — about 1.25 L of blood per minute.

facts.quizQuestion: Each kidney contains a vast number of functional filtering units. Approximately how many nephrons does a single adult kidney contain?
facts.explanation: Each kidney contains approximately 1 million nephrons — the functional units responsible for filtration, reabsorption, secretion, and excretion. Humans are born with their full complement of nephrons; they cannot regenerate. This is why chronic kidney disease is progressive: once nephrons are lost to disease, injury, or ageing, the remaining nephrons must compensate by increasing their workload.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art kidney with a zoomed inset showing a single coiled nephron in bright blue, surrounded by thousands of tiny dot-nephrons filling the cortex

variants.question: True or false: humans can regenerate nephrons lost to kidney disease.

facts.quizQuestion: Each nephron begins with a filtration unit consisting of a glomerulus enclosed within a Bowman's capsule. What is this combined structure called?
facts.explanation: The renal corpuscle is the filtration unit of the nephron, consisting of the glomerulus (a tuft of fenestrated capillaries) surrounded by Bowman's capsule (a double-walled epithelial cup). Blood enters via the afferent arteriole, is filtered under pressure, and the ultrafiltrate enters the capsular space before flowing into the proximal convoluted tubule. The renal corpuscle is located in the cortex.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art diagram of a renal corpuscle: round Bowman's capsule containing the tangled glomerular capillary tuft, afferent arteriole entering and efferent arteriole leaving

variants.question: The ___ consists of the glomerulus plus Bowman's capsule and is the site of blood filtration in the nephron.

facts.quizQuestion: The glomerular filtration rate (GFR) is the volume of fluid filtered from blood into Bowman's capsule each minute. What is the normal GFR in a healthy adult?
facts.explanation: The normal GFR is approximately 125 mL/min, which means roughly 180 litres of filtrate are produced every day. Since the body only excretes 1–2 L of urine per day, about 99% of the filtrate must be reabsorbed by the tubules. GFR is the primary clinical measure of kidney function — a falling GFR indicates progressive kidney disease. It is estimated clinically using serum creatinine.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art diagram showing blood flowing into glomerulus, filtrate dripping out at rate of 125 mL per minute, a gauge showing GFR with green zone at 125 mL/min

variants.question: At a GFR of 125 mL/min, how many litres of filtrate does the kidney produce per day?
True or false: the normal GFR produces about 180 litres of filtrate per day.

facts.quizQuestion: The proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) is the primary site of reabsorption in the nephron. What feature of PCT cells dramatically increases their absorptive surface area?
facts.explanation: PCT cells are lined with a brush border — a dense array of microvilli on their apical surface facing the tubular lumen. This dramatically increases the surface area available for reabsorption. The PCT reabsorbs approximately 65% of the filtrate, including all filtered glucose and amino acids, most sodium, water, bicarbonate, potassium, and phosphate. The brush border also contains enzymes for further digestion of small peptides.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of a PCT tube showing columnar cells with a dense fringe of microvilli on the luminal surface, Na+ and glucose transporters labeled

variants.question: The PCT's apical surface is covered in a ___, which greatly increases its surface area for reabsorption.

facts.quizQuestion: The proximal convoluted tubule handles the bulk of filtrate reabsorption. Approximately what percentage of the filtered load does it reabsorb?
facts.explanation: The PCT reabsorbs approximately 65% of the filtered water and sodium, and 100% of filtered glucose and amino acids under normal conditions. This bulk reabsorption occurs via sodium–glucose co-transporters (SGLT2) and Na+/H+ exchangers on the brush border, driven by the Na+/K+-ATPase pump on the basolateral membrane. The PCT cannot concentrate urine — it reabsorbs solutes and water in proportion.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art nephron diagram with '65%' label and downward arrows on the PCT segment, showing filtered load being dramatically reduced before reaching the loop of Henle

variants.question: True or false: the PCT reabsorbs virtually all filtered glucose under normal blood glucose levels.

facts.quizQuestion: The loop of Henle has two limbs with opposite properties. Which limb is thin-walled, permeable to water, and concentrates the filtrate as it descends into the hypertonic medulla?
facts.explanation: The descending limb of the loop of Henle is thin and freely permeable to water but not to solutes. As the filtrate descends into the increasingly hypertonic renal medulla, water is drawn out by osmosis, concentrating the filtrate. By the hairpin turn at the bottom of the loop, the filtrate has become highly concentrated. This is the first step in the countercurrent multiplier mechanism that allows the kidney to produce concentrated urine.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art loop of Henle diagram: thin descending limb with water molecules exiting into the hypertonic medulla (shown in orange gradient), filtrate arrow pointing downward with increasing concentration
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — loop of Henle

variants.question: True or false: the descending limb of the loop of Henle is impermeable to water.

facts.quizQuestion: In the loop of Henle, one limb is thick-walled, impermeable to water, and actively pumps Na+, K+, and Cl- into the medullary interstitium. Which limb is this?
facts.explanation: The thick ascending limb is impermeable to water but actively transports Na+, K+, and Cl- out of the tubular lumen into the medullary interstitium via the NKCC2 co-transporter. This dilutes the filtrate while building the high-osmolarity medullary gradient needed to concentrate urine later in the collecting duct. Loop diuretics (furosemide) block NKCC2, preventing concentration of the medullary gradient and causing profound diuresis.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art loop of Henle showing the thick ascending limb with NKCC2 pump icons moving Na+/K+/Cl- outward, water molecules blocked, filtrate arrow pointing upward becoming dilute
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — loop of Henle

variants.question: The thick ___ limb of the loop of Henle actively pumps Na+/K+/Cl- out but is impermeable to water, diluting the filtrate.

facts.quizQuestion: The loop of Henle creates a progressively increasing osmotic gradient in the renal medulla by flowing in opposite directions in its two limbs. What is this mechanism called?
facts.explanation: The countercurrent multiplier is the mechanism by which the loop of Henle creates a high-osmolarity gradient in the renal medulla — up to ~1200 mOsm/kg at the papillary tip, compared to ~300 mOsm/kg in plasma. The descending limb loses water (concentrating filtrate), the ascending limb pumps out solutes (diluting filtrate and hyperosmotising the medulla). This gradient is then exploited by the collecting duct under ADH influence to produce concentrated urine.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art diagram of loop of Henle with gradient shading in the surrounding medulla from light (isotonic) at the cortex to very dark (hypertonic) at the papilla, countercurrent flow arrows labeled
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — loop of Henle

variants.question: True or false: the medullary osmotic gradient created by the loop of Henle can reach ~1200 mOsm/kg at the papillary tip.

facts.quizQuestion: The distal convoluted tubule fine-tunes sodium reabsorption under hormonal control. Which hormone acts on the DCT to increase Na+ reabsorption?
facts.explanation: Aldosterone, secreted by the adrenal cortex (zona glomerulosa), acts on the DCT and collecting duct to increase the expression of Na+/K+-ATPase and epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) on principal cells. This increases Na+ reabsorption (and water follows osmotically) while increasing K+ secretion. Aldosterone is the final effector of the RAAS axis and is the primary regulator of long-term sodium balance and blood pressure.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art DCT cell with aldosterone molecule binding a nuclear receptor, arrows showing Na+ moving inward and K+ moving outward into the tubule lumen
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — DCT hormonal regulation

variants.question: Aldosterone increases Na+ reabsorption in the DCT — but what ion does it simultaneously cause to be secreted?
True or false: aldosterone acts on the DCT to increase Na+ reabsorption and K+ secretion.

facts.quizQuestion: Which hormone acts on the distal convoluted tubule to increase calcium (Ca²+) reabsorption?
facts.explanation: Parathyroid hormone (PTH), secreted by the parathyroid glands in response to low plasma Ca²+, acts on the DCT to upregulate TRPV5 calcium channels, increasing Ca²+ reabsorption. PTH simultaneously acts on bone (releasing Ca²+) and the kidney (activating vitamin D via 1α-hydroxylase in the PCT), creating a coordinated response to restore plasma calcium. Hypoparathyroidism results in hypocalcaemia with tetany.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art DCT cell with PTH molecule binding receptor, Ca²+ ions moving from tubule lumen into the cell, with a small parathyroid gland inset in the corner
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — DCT hormonal regulation

variants.question: True or false: PTH acts on the DCT to promote Ca²+ reabsorption.

facts.quizQuestion: Water reabsorption in the collecting duct is controlled by a hormone from the posterior pituitary that inserts aquaporin channels into collecting duct cells. What is this hormone?
facts.explanation: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also called vasopressin, is released from the posterior pituitary when blood osmolarity rises or blood volume falls. ADH binds V2 receptors on collecting duct principal cells, triggering insertion of aquaporin-2 (AQP2) channels into the apical membrane. This makes the collecting duct permeable to water, which is reabsorbed into the hypertonic medullary interstitium, producing concentrated urine. Without ADH, the collecting duct is impermeable and large volumes of dilute urine are produced.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art collecting duct cell: ADH binding receptor on one side, aquaporin channels appearing on the apical membrane, water molecules rushing through into the hypertonic interstitium below
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — collecting duct / ADH

variants.question: ADH inserts which specific water channels into the collecting duct apical membrane to allow water reabsorption?
True or false: without ADH, the collecting duct is impermeable to water and large volumes of dilute urine are produced.

facts.quizQuestion: A deficiency of ADH (vasopressin) causes a condition where the kidneys cannot reabsorb water in the collecting duct, producing massive volumes of dilute urine. What is this condition called?
facts.explanation: Diabetes insipidus (DI) results from ADH deficiency (central DI) or resistance to ADH at the collecting duct (nephrogenic DI). Patients produce 3–20+ L of dilute urine per day (polyuria) and experience intense thirst (polydipsia). Despite the name, DI has nothing to do with blood sugar — the 'insipid' refers to the tasteless quality of the dilute urine, in contrast to the sweet urine of diabetes mellitus.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art patient surrounded by many full water glasses, a graph showing urine output spiking to 20L/day, with 'NO ADH' label and a broken aquaporin channel icon
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — ADH / diabetes insipidus

variants.question: True or false: diabetes insipidus is caused by excess blood sugar, just like diabetes mellitus.

facts.quizQuestion: The RAAS begins when juxtaglomerular cells in the kidney detect low blood pressure. What enzyme do they release to start the cascade?
facts.explanation: Juxtaglomerular (JG) cells are modified smooth muscle cells in the wall of the afferent arteriole. When they detect reduced stretch (low blood pressure) or when the macula densa senses reduced NaCl delivery, they release renin — an aspartyl protease. Renin cleaves angiotensinogen (a protein produced by the liver) into angiotensin I, initiating the RAAS cascade that ultimately raises blood pressure.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art juxtaglomerular cell releasing renin molecules (labeled R) into the bloodstream, with a low blood pressure gauge in the background and the afferent arteriole wall surrounding the cell

variants.question: Juxtaglomerular cells detect low blood pressure and release ___, the enzyme that starts the RAAS cascade.

facts.quizQuestion: Renin does not directly raise blood pressure — it cleaves a precursor protein made by the liver into angiotensin I. What is this liver-produced precursor called?
facts.explanation: Angiotensinogen is an alpha-2 globulin produced constitutively by the liver and released into the bloodstream. Renin cleaves angiotensinogen into the decapeptide angiotensin I, which is then converted by angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) in the pulmonary vasculature to the active octapeptide angiotensin II. ACE inhibitors (e.g., lisinopril) lower blood pressure by blocking this conversion step.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art RAAS flowchart: liver icon producing angiotensinogen → renin arrow cleaves it → angiotensin I → ACE (lungs icon) → angiotensin II

variants.question: In the RAAS, renin cleaves angiotensinogen into which inactive precursor?

facts.quizQuestion: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) converts angiotensin I to the active angiotensin II. In which organ does this conversion primarily occur?
facts.explanation: ACE is found on the luminal surface of endothelial cells throughout the vasculature, but the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II occurs predominantly in the pulmonary vasculature of the lungs, which receives the entire cardiac output. This is why ACE inhibitors are such effective antihypertensives — they block this key conversion step. The lungs also inactivate bradykinin via ACE, which is why ACE inhibitor-induced cough (from bradykinin accumulation) is a well-known side effect.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art lung with ACE enzyme icons on capillary endothelium converting angiotensin I (labeled ANG I) to angiotensin II (ANG II), blood flowing through

variants.question: True or false: ACE inhibitors lower blood pressure by blocking conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II.

facts.quizQuestion: Angiotensin II is the effector of the RAAS. What are its two main actions that raise blood pressure?
facts.explanation: Angiotensin II raises blood pressure through two complementary mechanisms: (1) direct vasoconstriction of arterioles (increasing peripheral resistance immediately), and (2) stimulation of aldosterone secretion from the adrenal cortex (increasing Na+ and water reabsorption over hours). Together these restore blood volume and pressure. Angiotensin II also stimulates thirst and ADH release, further conserving water.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art angiotensin II molecule with two arrows: one pointing to constricted blood vessel labeled 'vasoconstriction', another pointing to adrenal gland releasing aldosterone

variants.question: Angiotensin II raises blood pressure by causing ___ and stimulating aldosterone release from the adrenal cortex.

facts.quizQuestion: When blood volume is too high, atrial myocytes stretch and release a hormone that promotes sodium excretion and opposes RAAS. What is this hormone?
facts.explanation: Atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) is secreted by atrial cardiomyocytes in response to atrial stretch from high blood volume. ANP promotes Na+ excretion (natriuresis) by the kidneys — water follows, reducing blood volume and pressure. ANP also inhibits renin and aldosterone release, directly opposing the RAAS. It represents a cardiac-renal feedback loop: the heart monitors volume status and signals the kidney to correct it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art heart atrium stretched by high blood volume releasing ANP molecules, arrows pointing to kidney showing Na+ and water excretion, and a crossed-out RAAS pathway
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — ANP / hormonal regulation

variants.question: True or false: ANP is secreted by the atria of the heart in response to high blood volume and promotes sodium excretion.
ANP promotes natriuresis — meaning it causes the kidneys to excrete more of which ion?

facts.quizQuestion: In response to low oxygen levels (hypoxia), the kidneys produce a hormone that travels to bone marrow to stimulate red blood cell production. What is this hormone?
facts.explanation: Erythropoietin (EPO) is produced by peritubular interstitial fibroblasts in the renal cortex in response to hypoxia. EPO stimulates erythropoiesis — RBC production in bone marrow — increasing oxygen-carrying capacity. Chronic kidney disease (CKD) patients develop anaemia partly because diseased kidneys produce insufficient EPO; recombinant EPO is used as treatment. EPO is also infamously misused as a performance-enhancing drug in endurance sports.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art kidney releasing EPO molecules that travel via bloodstream to bone marrow, where new red blood cells are being produced in increasing numbers
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — EPO / hormonal regulation

variants.question: True or false: chronic kidney disease can cause anaemia because damaged kidneys produce less erythropoietin.

facts.quizQuestion: Each ureter transports urine from the renal pelvis to the bladder via peristalsis. What is the approximate length of each ureter?
facts.explanation: Each ureter is approximately 25–30 cm long and 3–4 mm in diameter. They run retroperitoneally from the renal pelvis, cross anterior to the psoas muscle, pass over the pelvic brim (crossing the iliac vessels), and enter the posterolateral wall of the bladder at an oblique angle. This oblique entry creates a valve-like mechanism that prevents vesicoureteral reflux (backflow of urine into the ureter during bladder contraction).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art urinary tract showing the right ureter running from kidney to bladder, with a ruler alongside indicating 25–30 cm length, the oblique bladder entry highlighted

variants.question: True or false: the ureters enter the bladder at a right angle, which allows the bladder to squeeze urine back up if too full.

facts.quizQuestion: Kidney stones passing down the ureter are most likely to lodge at narrow sites. How many sites of physiological narrowing are there in the ureter?
facts.explanation: There are 3 sites of physiological narrowing in the ureter where kidney stones most commonly become lodged: (1) the ureteropelvic junction (UPJ) — where the renal pelvis meets the ureter; (2) the crossing of the iliac vessels at the pelvic brim; and (3) the ureterovesical junction (UVJ) — where the ureter enters the bladder. The UVJ is the narrowest and most common site for stones to obstruct, causing severe colicky flank pain (ureteric colic).
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art ureter with three red X marks at the ureteropelvic junction, iliac vessel crossing, and ureterovesical junction, with a kidney stone icon at the UVJ
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — ureters / kidney stones

variants.question: There are ___ sites of physiological narrowing in the ureter where kidney stones most commonly lodge.
The narrowest and most common site for kidney stones to obstruct the ureter is which junction?

facts.quizQuestion: The urinary bladder is a hollow muscular reservoir. What is its approximate functional capacity in an adult?
facts.explanation: The adult bladder has a capacity of approximately 500 mL, although the first urge to urinate typically occurs at 200–300 mL. The wall of the bladder contains the detrusor muscle — three interlacing layers of smooth muscle that contract to expel urine during micturition. The bladder can be overstretched beyond 500 mL in conditions such as urinary retention, risking detrusor damage.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art bladder with a gauge showing capacity at 500 mL, a 'first urge' marker at 200–300 mL, and the three-layer detrusor muscle walls labeled

variants.question: The urge to urinate typically begins at what volume in the adult bladder?

facts.quizQuestion: The floor of the bladder contains a triangular smooth region defined by the two ureteral openings and the internal urethral orifice. What is this area called?
facts.explanation: The trigone is the triangular region of the bladder base (fundus) bounded by the two ureteral openings superolaterally and the internal urethral orifice inferiorly. Unlike the rest of the bladder mucosa, the trigone is smooth rather than rugated and is clinically important: it is the most common site for bladder tumours and cystitis, and its anatomical landmarks guide cystoscopy and ureteric catheterisation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art bladder interior view from above, showing the triangular trigone region highlighted at the base, two ureteral openings at the top corners and the urethral opening at the bottom point

variants.question: True or false: the trigone is the triangular smooth region of the bladder floor between the two ureteral openings and the internal urethral orifice.

facts.quizQuestion: The bladder wall contains a specialised smooth muscle arranged in three interlacing layers that contract during micturition to expel urine. What is this muscle called?
facts.explanation: The detrusor muscle makes up the thick muscular wall of the urinary bladder, consisting of three interlacing layers of smooth muscle (inner longitudinal, middle circular, outer longitudinal). During micturition, parasympathetic stimulation (via pelvic nerves, S2–S4) causes detrusor contraction while the internal urethral sphincter relaxes, expelling urine. Detrusor overactivity causes urgency incontinence; detrusor underactivity causes urinary retention.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art bladder cross-section showing the three interlacing smooth muscle layers of the detrusor, with parasympathetic nerve fiber labeled and contraction arrows

variants.question: The ___ muscle forms the three-layered smooth muscle wall of the bladder and contracts during micturition.

facts.quizQuestion: The male urethra has three segments (prostatic, membranous, spongy) and is much longer than the female urethra. What is its approximate total length?
facts.explanation: The male urethra is approximately 20 cm long and is divided into: the prostatic urethra (~3 cm, passes through the prostate), the membranous urethra (~2 cm, passes through the external urethral sphincter), and the spongy (penile) urethra (~15 cm, runs through the corpus spongiosum). The length of the male urethra means urinary tract infections are less common in males than females, but urethral catheterisation is more challenging.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art male pelvis cross-section showing the three urethra segments labeled with their lengths: prostatic, membranous, and spongy, total length indicated as ~20 cm

variants.question: What are the three named segments of the male urethra, in order from bladder to external opening?

facts.quizQuestion: The female urethra is dramatically shorter than the male's, contributing to a higher risk of urinary tract infections in women. How long is the female urethra?
facts.explanation: The female urethra is only approximately 4 cm long, running from the bladder neck to the external urethral orifice (located between the clitoris and vaginal opening). This short length means bacteria from the perineum can reach the bladder far more easily than in males, explaining why UTIs are ~50× more common in women. The shorter urethra also makes bladder catheterisation technically simpler in women.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art side-by-side comparison: female urethra labeled 4 cm vs male urethra labeled 20 cm, UTI bacteria icons crowding the short female urethra

variants.question: True or false: the female urethra is shorter than the male urethra, which contributes to a higher UTI risk in women.

facts.quizQuestion: Electrolyte balance is a core kidney function. Which ion is the most abundant in the extracellular fluid and is primarily regulated by aldosterone and ANP?
facts.explanation: Sodium (Na+) is the most abundant extracellular cation and the primary determinant of extracellular fluid volume and plasma osmolarity. It is regulated by aldosterone (promotes reabsorption in the DCT/collecting duct) and ANP (promotes excretion). Because water follows Na+ osmotically, sodium balance directly controls blood volume and blood pressure. A 1 mmol/L change in plasma Na+ can have profound neurological consequences.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cell diagram showing Na+ ions crowded outside a cell (extracellular space) and K+ ions inside, labels pointing to each compartment

variants.question: True or false: sodium is the most abundant cation in the extracellular fluid.

facts.quizQuestion: Which ion is the most abundant intracellular cation and is regulated by aldosterone-mediated secretion in the DCT?
facts.explanation: Potassium (K+) is the most abundant intracellular cation, with ~98% of total body K+ inside cells. Aldosterone promotes K+ secretion in the DCT/collecting duct in exchange for Na+ reabsorption. Hyperkalemia (elevated plasma K+) is a dangerous complication of kidney failure that can cause potentially fatal cardiac arrhythmias by depolarising cardiac myocytes. This is why monitoring serum K+ is essential in renal patients.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cell diagram with K+ ions densely packed inside and few outside, with a DCT cell inset showing aldosterone-driven K+ secretion into the tubule lumen

variants.question: Hyperkalemia — elevated plasma K+ — can cause which dangerous cardiac complication?

facts.quizQuestion: The kidneys play a central role in acid-base balance by excreting H+ and reabsorbing bicarbonate. What is the normal pH range of arterial blood?
facts.explanation: Normal arterial blood pH is 7.35–7.45 — a narrow range that must be maintained precisely. The kidneys contribute to acid-base balance by excreting H+ (via the intercalated cells of the collecting duct) and reabsorbing filtered HCO₃⁻ (bicarbonate) in the PCT. In acidosis (pH < 7.35), kidneys excrete more H+ and reabsorb more HCO₃⁻; in alkalosis (pH > 7.45), the opposite occurs. This renal compensation is slower than respiratory compensation but more powerful.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art pH scale from 7.0 to 7.8, with a green zone highlighting 7.35–7.45 as normal blood pH, red danger zones below 7.35 (acidosis) and above 7.45 (alkalosis)
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — pH / electrolyte balance

variants.question: True or false: the normal arterial blood pH range is 7.35–7.45.
A blood pH below ___ is defined as acidosis.

facts.quizQuestion: In metabolic acidosis, the kidneys respond by excreting more H+ and reabsorbing more of which buffer ion in the proximal convoluted tubule?
facts.explanation: Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) is the main extracellular buffer. In acidosis, the PCT increases HCO₃⁻ reabsorption (via H+/HCO₃⁻ exchange mechanisms) and intercalated cells in the collecting duct secrete more H+. This combination raises blood pH back toward normal. In alkalosis, the reverse occurs — less HCO₃⁻ is reabsorbed and it is excreted in urine (making urine alkaline). Renal compensation for acid-base disturbances takes hours to days, unlike respiratory compensation which is rapid.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art PCT cell showing H+ being secreted into the lumen while HCO₃⁻ moves into the peritubular capillary, an acidosis warning label above
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — pH / electrolyte balance

variants.question: True or false: in acidosis, the kidneys excrete more H+ and reabsorb more bicarbonate to raise blood pH.

facts.quizQuestion: The kidneys filter 180 litres of fluid per day but excrete far less. What is the normal daily urine output?
facts.explanation: Normal daily urine output is approximately 1–2 L/day, despite the kidneys filtering 180 L of fluid per day. This means ~99% of the filtered fluid is reabsorbed by the tubules. Urine output below 400 mL/day is oliguria (abnormally low — often indicating kidney failure or dehydration), and below 100 mL/day is anuria (typically indicating complete obstruction or severe renal failure). Above 3 L/day is polyuria.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art infographic: 180L funnel going into nephron, with 178–179 L reabsorption arrow and 1–2 L urine dripping into a collection vessel

variants.question: True or false: urine output below 400 mL/day is called oliguria and often indicates kidney injury or severe dehydration.

facts.quizQuestion: Urine is 95% water. What are the three main nitrogenous waste products found in the remaining 5%?
facts.explanation: The non-water components of urine are primarily nitrogenous wastes: urea (the main waste, from protein catabolism and the urea cycle), creatinine (from muscle creatine phosphate metabolism — used clinically to estimate GFR), and uric acid (from purine catabolism — elevated in gout). Urine also contains electrolytes (Na+, K+, Cl-), hormones, and various metabolites. Creatinine's constant production rate makes it a reliable GFR marker.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art urine composition pie chart: large blue 95% water slice, and small wedges labeled urea (largest waste), creatinine, and uric acid

variants.question: Which nitrogenous waste product of muscle metabolism is used clinically as a marker to estimate GFR?

facts.quizQuestion: The peritubular capillaries that dip alongside the loop of Henle into the renal medulla have a special name. These hairpin capillaries preserve the medullary osmotic gradient by using countercurrent exchange. What are they called?
facts.explanation: The vasa recta are long, straight, hairpin-shaped peritubular capillaries that parallel the loops of Henle deep into the medulla. They supply oxygen and nutrients to the medulla while preserving the osmotic gradient through countercurrent exchange: descending vessels lose water and gain solutes as they go deeper; ascending vessels gain water and lose solutes. If the vasa recta were straight-through capillaries, they would wash out the medullary gradient and the kidney could not concentrate urine.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art showing the vasa recta as hairpin capillaries running alongside the loop of Henle into the hypertonic medulla (orange gradient), water and solute exchange arrows labeled
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — nephron / blood supply

variants.question: True or false: the vasa recta are hairpin-shaped capillaries that preserve the medullary osmotic gradient through countercurrent exchange.

facts.quizQuestion: Blood flows through the glomerular capillaries between two arterioles. In correct order from entry to exit, what is the sequence?
facts.explanation: Blood enters the glomerulus via the afferent arteriole ('afferent' = carrying toward), is filtered under hydrostatic pressure across the fenestrated capillary wall and basement membrane into Bowman's capsule, then exits via the efferent arteriole ('efferent' = carrying away). Because both vessels are arterioles, the glomerulus is a portal system — the high pressure maintained between two resistance vessels drives filtration. Constriction of the efferent arteriole increases GFR; constriction of the afferent arteriole decreases it. Renal blood flow sequence: afferent arteriole → glomerular capillaries → efferent arteriole.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art glomerulus with a red afferent arteriole entering on the left, the capillary tuft in the centre, and a narrower efferent arteriole exiting on the right, arrows showing flow direction
facts.sourceName: Verified source data — glomerular filtration / blood supply
distractors: afferent arteriole → Bowman's capsule → efferent arteriole

variants.question: Blood enters the glomerular capillaries via the ___ arteriole and exits via the efferent arteriole.
True or false: constriction of the efferent arteriole increases glomerular filtration pressure and thus increases GFR.

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical structure.
facts.explanation: This is the abdominal aorta — the section of the aorta below the diaphragm that supplies blood to the abdominal organs. It begins at the aortic hiatus (T12) and bifurcates into the common iliac arteries at L4.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the abdominal aorta and its branches

facts.quizQuestion: What is the large vessel shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is the aorta — the largest artery in the body, arising from the left ventricle. It carries oxygenated blood and distributes it to the systemic circulation via its many branches.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the full aorta from heart to iliac bifurcation

facts.quizQuestion: Name the valve structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This is the aortic valve — the semilunar valve between the left ventricle and the aorta. It has three cusps and prevents backflow of blood into the left ventricle during diastole. This image depicts aortic regurgitation.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the aortic valve showing regurgitation

facts.quizQuestion: Which body system is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: This is the arterial system — the network of arteries that distributes oxygenated blood from the heart to the body's tissues. Arteries have thick muscular walls to withstand high pressure from cardiac output.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the full body arterial system

facts.quizQuestion: What does this full-body illustration depict?
facts.explanation: This is the arterial circulation — the full-body distribution of arteries carrying oxygenated blood. The aorta branches into successively smaller arteries reaching every organ and tissue.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration showing full body arterial distribution

facts.quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This shows the artery wall layers — tunica intima (inner endothelium), tunica media (smooth muscle), and tunica adventitia (outer connective tissue). The thick media gives arteries their elastic, pressure-resistant properties.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section illustration of an artery showing three wall layers

acceptableAlternatives: Deep veins of the lower limb
facts.quizQuestion: Which part of the cardiovascular system is this?
facts.explanation: This is the deep venous system — the network of deep veins in the lower limbs that carries deoxygenated blood toward the heart. Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) can occur in these vessels.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of deep veins of the lower extremities

facts.quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is the heart — the muscular organ that pumps blood through the circulatory system. It weighs 250–350 g, beats ~100,000 times per day, and is located in the mediastinum tilted slightly to the left.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the external heart

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical structure.
facts.explanation: This is the cardiac conduction system — the electrical pathway that coordinates heartbeats. It includes the SA node (pacemaker), AV node, Bundle of His, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the heart's electrical conduction pathway

facts.quizQuestion: What does this cross-sectional illustration show?
facts.explanation: This is a cross-section of the heart showing the four chambers (right/left atria and ventricles) and the valves between them. The interventricular septum separates the two ventricles.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional medical illustration of the heart's internal chambers

facts.quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This four-chamber view of the heart shows the right atrium and ventricle (pulmonary circuit) on the right, and the left atrium and ventricle (systemic circuit) on the left. The left ventricle has a thicker wall due to higher pressure demands.
facts.visualDescription: Four-chamber cross-sectional illustration of the heart

facts.quizQuestion: Which part of the cardiovascular system is this?
facts.explanation: This shows the heart wall layers — the outer epicardium (visceral pericardium), the thick muscular myocardium, and the inner endocardium lining the chambers. The pericardial sac surrounds the heart.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the three layers of the heart wall

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical structure.
facts.explanation: This is the myocardium — the thick muscular middle layer of the heart wall composed of cardiac muscle cells. It generates the contractile force needed to pump blood, and is thickest in the left ventricle.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the myocardial muscle layer

facts.quizQuestion: What structures are shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This shows the four heart valves: the tricuspid (right AV), mitral/bicuspid (left AV), pulmonary semilunar, and aortic semilunar valves. They ensure one-way blood flow through the cardiac chambers.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of all four heart valves viewed from above

facts.quizQuestion: Name the vascular structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: These are the coronary arteries — the blood vessels supplying oxygen and nutrients to the heart muscle. The left coronary artery (LCA) and right coronary artery (RCA) arise from the aortic root just above the aortic valve.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of coronary artery distribution on the heart surface

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this blood vessel structure.
facts.explanation: This is a vein — a blood vessel that returns deoxygenated blood to the heart. Veins have thinner walls and larger lumens than arteries, and contain one-way valves (especially in the limbs) to prevent backflow.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional illustration of a vein showing its wall layers and valves

facts.quizQuestion: What digestive process is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: This illustrates bile secretion — bile is produced by hepatocytes in the liver, stored and concentrated in the gallbladder, and released into the duodenum via the common bile duct to emulsify dietary fats.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the biliary system and bile secretion pathway

facts.quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is the colon (large intestine) — the final section of the GI tract, approximately 1.5 m long. It absorbs water and electrolytes, forming solid waste. Its sections are: cecum, ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid colon, and rectum.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the large intestine/colon

facts.quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This is a cross-section of the colon showing its wall layers: mucosa (with goblet cells), submucosa, muscularis externa (inner circular + outer longitudinal), and serosa. The inner mucosa lacks villi, unlike the small intestine.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section illustration of the colon wall layers

facts.quizQuestion: Which body system is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: This is the complete digestive system — the gastrointestinal tract from mouth to anus, plus accessory organs (liver, gallbladder, pancreas). It breaks down food, absorbs nutrients, and eliminates waste.
facts.visualDescription: Full-body medical illustration of the complete digestive system

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this part of the digestive tract.
facts.explanation: This is the duodenum — the first and shortest segment of the small intestine (~25 cm, C-shaped). It receives chyme from the stomach, bile from the common bile duct, and pancreatic enzymes, making it the primary site of chemical digestion.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the duodenum and its connections

facts.quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is the liver — the largest internal organ (~1.5 kg), located in the right upper quadrant. It produces bile, detoxifies blood, synthesizes plasma proteins, stores glycogen, and performs over 500 metabolic functions.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the liver showing its lobes

facts.quizQuestion: What pathological condition of the liver is shown here?
facts.explanation: This shows a liver with cirrhosis — diffuse scarring (fibrosis) and regenerative nodules that replace normal liver architecture. Causes include chronic alcohol use, hepatitis B/C, and NASH. It leads to portal hypertension and liver failure.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of cirrhotic liver with nodular scarring

facts.quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is the stomach — the J-shaped muscular organ between the esophagus and small intestine. It churns food with hydrochloric acid (pH 1.5–3.5) and pepsin, producing chyme. Regions: cardia, fundus, body, pylorus.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the stomach showing its regions

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this endocrine gland.
facts.explanation: This is the adrenal (suprarenal) gland — a triangular gland sitting atop each kidney. The cortex produces cortisol, aldosterone, and androgens; the medulla produces adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the adrenal gland atop the kidney

facts.quizQuestion: Name the structure depicting adrenal zones depicted here.
facts.explanation: This shows the adrenal gland zones from outside in: zona glomerulosa (aldosterone), zona fasciculata (cortisol), zona reticularis (androgens), and the central medulla (epinephrine/norepinephrine). Mnemonic: GFR — salt, sugar, sex.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the adrenal gland cellular zones

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this microscopic pancreatic structure.
facts.explanation: This is an islet of Langerhans — a cluster of endocrine cells scattered throughout the pancreas. Alpha cells secrete glucagon (raises blood glucose), beta cells secrete insulin (lowers blood glucose). Type 1 diabetes destroys beta cells.
facts.visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of a pancreatic islet of Langerhans

facts.quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is the pancreas — a glandular organ with both exocrine (digestive enzymes: lipase, amylase, proteases) and endocrine (insulin and glucagon from islets of Langerhans) functions. It lies behind the stomach in the retroperitoneum.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the pancreas showing its head, body, and tail

facts.quizQuestion: What does this cross-sectional illustration of the pancreas show?
facts.explanation: This cross-section of the pancreas shows its internal structure including the main pancreatic duct (duct of Wirsung), acinar cells (exocrine), and islets of Langerhans (endocrine). The duct joins the common bile duct at the ampulla of Vater.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section illustration of the pancreas showing ducts and islets

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this endocrine gland.
facts.explanation: This is the thyroid gland — a butterfly-shaped gland in the anterior neck, wrapped around the trachea. It produces T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine) regulating metabolism, and calcitonin regulating calcium levels.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the thyroid gland showing its two lobes and isthmus

facts.quizQuestion: What degree of burn is shown?
facts.explanation: This is a first-degree (superficial) burn — affecting only the epidermis. It causes redness, pain, and minor swelling but no blisters. It heals within 3–5 days without scarring. A typical sunburn is a first-degree burn.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of skin showing first-degree burn affecting only the epidermis

facts.quizQuestion: What degree of burn is shown?
facts.explanation: This is a second-degree (partial-thickness) burn — extending through the epidermis into the dermis. It causes blistering, intense pain, and a wet shiny appearance. Superficial second-degree burns heal in 2–3 weeks; deep ones may need skin grafting.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of skin showing second-degree burn with blistering into dermis

facts.quizQuestion: What degree of burn is shown?
facts.explanation: This is a third-degree (full-thickness) burn — destroying both the epidermis and dermis, and possibly deeper tissues. The area appears white, brown, or black and leathery. Nerve destruction means it is often painless. Requires skin grafting.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of skin showing full-thickness third-degree burn

facts.quizQuestion: What does this illustration show compared to burn injuries?
facts.explanation: This shows normal, healthy skin with an intact epidermis and dermis. Normal skin serves as a barrier against pathogens, regulates temperature, provides sensation, and synthesizes vitamin D — contrasted with the burn depth series.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of normal unburned skin layers for comparison

facts.quizQuestion: What does this full-body neurological map illustrate?
facts.explanation: This is a dermatome map — showing the sensory distribution of each spinal nerve on the body surface. Each dermatome corresponds to a spinal level (C2–S5). Used to localize disc herniations and spinal cord injuries clinically.
facts.visualDescription: Full-body illustration of spinal nerve dermatome distribution

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this skin layer structure.
facts.explanation: This is the epidermis — the outermost skin layer with 5 strata (from deep to superficial): stratum basale, spinosum, granulosum, lucidum (only in thick skin), and corneum. Keratinocytes produced in the basale migrate upward over ~4 weeks.
facts.visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of the five epidermal layers

facts.quizQuestion: Name the skin appendage structure shown here.
facts.explanation: This is a hair follicle — the skin structure that produces hair. It includes the hair shaft, sebaceous gland (sebum production), and arrector pili muscle (causes goosebumps). Hair grows at ~1 cm/month in the anagen (growth) phase.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a hair follicle with associated structures

facts.quizQuestion: Which body system is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: This is the integumentary system — comprising the epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis (subcutaneous layer), plus appendages (hair, nails, sweat glands, sebaceous glands). It is the body's largest organ system at ~2 m² surface area.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of skin cross-section showing epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this specialized immune cell found in the skin.
facts.explanation: This is a Langerhans cell — a dendritic antigen-presenting cell residing in the epidermis. It detects pathogens and allergens, then migrates to lymph nodes to activate T lymphocytes. Key to contact hypersensitivity reactions.
facts.visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of a Langerhans cell in the epidermis

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this specialized skin cell.
facts.explanation: This is a melanocyte — a melanin-producing cell located in the stratum basale of the epidermis. Melanin determines skin and hair color and provides UV protection. Melanocytes arise from neural crest cells and connect to ~36 keratinocytes each.
facts.visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of a melanocyte in the stratum basale

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this lymphoid organ.
facts.explanation: This is a lymph node — a bean-shaped secondary lymphoid organ (0.1–2.5 cm) that filters lymph. The cortex contains B cell follicles; the paracortex has T cells. About 600–700 lymph nodes are distributed throughout the body.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section illustration of a lymph node showing cortex and medulla

facts.quizQuestion: What circulatory pathway is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: This is the lymphatic circulation — the one-way network that collects interstitial fluid from tissues as lymph and returns it to the bloodstream via the thoracic duct (left side) and right lymphatic duct. Also transports dietary fats absorbed in the intestine.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the lymphatic circulation network

facts.quizQuestion: Which body system is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: This is the complete lymphatic system — including lymph nodes, lymphatic vessels, and lymphoid organs (spleen, thymus, tonsils, Peyer's patches). It maintains fluid balance, absorbs dietary fats, and houses immune cells.
facts.visualDescription: Full-body illustration of the lymphatic system and organs

facts.quizQuestion: Name the vessel structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This is a lymphatic vessel — a thin-walled vessel with one-way valves that carries lymph (filtered interstitial fluid) back to the venous system. Lymphatic capillaries are more permeable than blood capillaries, allowing protein uptake.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a lymphatic vessel showing its valves

facts.quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is the spleen — the largest lymphoid organ (~150 g), located in the left upper quadrant (LUQ) behind the 9th–11th ribs. It filters blood, destroys old red blood cells, and stores platelets and monocytes. Rich blood supply makes it vulnerable to traumatic rupture.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the spleen

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this molecular muscle component.
facts.explanation: This is the actin filament (thin filament) — a double-helical polymer of G-actin monomers in the sarcomere. Myosin heads bind to actin during the cross-bridge cycle, pulling the Z-discs closer together and shortening the sarcomere.
facts.visualDescription: Molecular illustration of an actin (thin) filament

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this type of muscle tissue.
facts.explanation: These are cardiac muscle cells (cardiomyocytes) — branched, striated, involuntary muscle cells connected by intercalated discs. The discs contain gap junctions for electrical coupling and desmosomes for mechanical strength, allowing the heart to contract in unison.
facts.visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of cardiac muscle cells showing striations and intercalated discs

facts.quizQuestion: What mechanism of muscle physiology is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: Muscle contraction (sliding filament). This illustrates the sliding filament mechanism of muscle contraction — myosin heads (thick filaments) bind actin (thin filaments), pivot, and pull them inward, shortening the sarcomere. ATP powers each cross-bridge cycle; calcium release from the SR triggers it.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the sliding filament mechanism of muscle contraction

facts.quizQuestion: What type of muscle tissue and vessel are shown here?
facts.explanation: This shows smooth muscle in an arteriole wall — involuntary, non-striated muscle cells arranged circumferentially. Sympathetic stimulation causes vasoconstriction; relaxation causes vasodilation. Controls blood pressure and tissue perfusion.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of smooth muscle cells in an arteriole wall

facts.quizQuestion: What does this lateral view illustration show?
facts.explanation: This is the lateral view of the brain showing the four cerebral lobes: frontal (motor/executive function), parietal (somatosensory), temporal (hearing/memory), and occipital (vision). The lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure) separates the temporal from the frontal and parietal lobes.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the brain in lateral view showing the four lobes

facts.quizQuestion: What does this medial cross-section of the brain show?
facts.explanation: This is the medial (sagittal) view of the brain revealing the corpus callosum (connecting hemispheres), cingulate gyrus, thalamus, hypothalamus, brainstem, and cerebellum. The medial view exposes structures hidden in the lateral view.
facts.visualDescription: Sagittal section illustration of the brain showing midline structures

facts.quizQuestion: What vascular structure supplying the brain is shown here?
facts.explanation: This shows the cerebral arteries — the brain receives blood from the internal carotid arteries (anterior circulation) and vertebral arteries (posterior circulation), which unite at the Circle of Willis (circulus arteriosus) at the base of the brain.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the cerebral arterial supply and Circle of Willis

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this part of the central nervous system.
facts.explanation: This is the brainstem — comprising the midbrain (cranial nerves III–IV), pons (V–VIII), and medulla oblongata (IX–XII). It controls vital functions (breathing, heart rate, blood pressure), relays signals between the cerebrum and spinal cord, and contains the reticular activating system.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the brainstem showing midbrain, pons, and medulla

facts.quizQuestion: What does this color-coded brain map depict?
facts.explanation: This shows brain functional zones — cortical areas mapped by function. Key areas include the primary motor cortex (precentral gyrus), somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus), Broca's area (speech production), Wernicke's area (speech comprehension), and visual cortex (occipital lobe).
facts.visualDescription: Color-coded illustration of the brain's functional cortical areas

facts.quizQuestion: What cortical mapping system is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: This shows the cerebral cortex regions including Brodmann areas and functional regions. The cortex is the outer gray matter layer (~2–4 mm thick) with ~16 billion neurons organized into six layers, responsible for higher cognitive functions.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of cerebral cortex Brodmann areas and functional regions

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this limbic system structure.
facts.explanation: This is the hippocampus — a seahorse-shaped structure in the medial temporal lobe essential for converting short-term memories to long-term memories (consolidation) and spatial navigation. Bilateral destruction causes anterograde amnesia (Korsakoff syndrome, H.M. case).
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the hippocampus within the temporal lobe

facts.quizQuestion: What type of cell is depicted in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is a neuron — the fundamental signal-transmitting cell of the nervous system. It consists of dendrites (receive signals), a cell body (soma with nucleus), axon (transmits signals), myelin sheath (speeds conduction), and axon terminals (release neurotransmitters).
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of a typical neuron with labeled components

facts.quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This detailed view of neuron structure shows the cell body (soma), nucleus, rough ER (Nissl substance), dendrites for input, and axon hillock (action potential initiation zone). Myelinated segments between nodes of Ranvier enable saltatory conduction.
facts.visualDescription: Detailed illustration of neural cell components and internal structure

facts.quizQuestion: Name the airway structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This is a bronchus — one of the two main airways branching from the trachea at the carina (T4 level) into the lungs. The right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left, making it the preferred path for aspirated foreign bodies.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a healthy bronchus cross-section

facts.quizQuestion: What respiratory process is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: This illustrates pulmonary (external) gas exchange — O2 diffuses from alveolar air across the thin alveolar-capillary membrane into pulmonary capillary blood, while CO2 diffuses in the opposite direction. The membrane is only ~0.5 μm thick for rapid diffusion.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of O2 and CO2 exchange at the alveolar-capillary membrane

facts.quizQuestion: What type of gas exchange is depicted here?
facts.explanation: This is systemic (internal) gas exchange — O2 is offloaded from hemoglobin to tissue cells, while CO2 produced by cellular respiration enters the capillary blood for transport back to the lungs. Tissue pO2 is ~40 mmHg vs arterial ~100 mmHg.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of O2 delivery and CO2 collection at tissue capillaries

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this portion of the respiratory anatomy.
facts.explanation: These are the intrapulmonary airways — the bronchial tree inside the lungs, branching from primary bronchi to secondary (lobar) bronchi, tertiary (segmental) bronchi, bronchioles, terminal bronchioles, and finally respiratory bronchioles leading to alveoli (~23 generations total).
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of bronchi branching into bronchioles within the lungs

facts.quizQuestion: What organ pair is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: These are the lungs — paired respiratory organs in the thoracic cavity. The right lung has 3 lobes (superior, middle, inferior); the left has 2 lobes (superior, inferior) plus the lingula. Total surface area for gas exchange is ~70 m² — roughly the size of a tennis court.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of both lungs in the thoracic cavity

facts.quizQuestion: Which part of the respiratory anatomy is shown here?
facts.explanation: This shows the lung lobes — the right lung is divided into 3 lobes by the oblique and horizontal fissures, while the left lung has 2 lobes divided by the oblique fissure. The right middle lobe is the smallest. Each lobe receives its own lobar bronchus.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of right (3 lobes) and left (2 lobes) lung with fissures labeled

facts.quizQuestion: What pathological finding in the ankle is shown here?
facts.explanation: This shows an ankle fracture — involving the bones of the ankle mortise: the medial malleolus (tibia), lateral malleolus (fibula), and posterior malleolus (tibia). The most common mechanism is inversion injury. The Ottawa Ankle Rules guide X-ray decisions.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of ankle bones showing fracture lines

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the bones shown in this upper limb illustration.
facts.explanation: These are the arm bones: the humerus (upper arm), radius (lateral forearm, rotates with thumb), and ulna (medial forearm, forms the elbow point). The radius and ulna articulate at the proximal and distal radioulnar joints, enabling pronation/supination.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the upper limb skeleton showing humerus, radius, and ulna

facts.quizQuestion: What process of skeletal development is illustrated here?
facts.explanation: This illustrates bone growth via endochondral ossification at the epiphyseal (growth) plate. Chondrocytes proliferate, hypertrophy, and are replaced by bone through primary and secondary ossification centers. Growth hormone and IGF-1 stimulate this process until plate closure in late adolescence.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of bone growth plate showing endochondral ossification zones

facts.quizQuestion: Name the ocular structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This is the cornea — the transparent, avascular anterior layer of the eye that refracts 65–70% of the eye's total focusing power. Its 5 layers include the epithelium, Bowman's layer, stroma (90% thickness), Descemet's membrane, and endothelium.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the cornea showing its layers

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this part of the eye.
facts.explanation: This is the iris — the colored muscular diaphragm that controls pupil diameter. The sphincter pupillae (parasympathetic) constricts the pupil (miosis) in bright light; the dilator pupillae (sympathetic) dilates it (mydriasis) in dim light.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the iris and pupil

facts.quizQuestion: Name the ocular structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This is the lens — a biconvex transparent structure suspended behind the iris by zonular fibers (zonules of Zinn). Ciliary muscle contraction (accommodation) rounds the lens to focus on near objects. Clouding of the lens is called a cataract.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the eye lens and zonular fibers

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the muscles shown controlling eye movement.
facts.explanation: These are the extraocular muscles — six muscles controlling eye movement: four recti (superior, inferior, medial, lateral) and two obliques (superior, inferior). CN III (oculomotor) controls most; CN IV controls superior oblique; CN VI controls lateral rectus.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the six extraocular muscles controlling eye movement

facts.quizQuestion: What does this cross-sectional illustration of the eye show?
facts.explanation: This is the complete eye structure — showing all three tunics: fibrous (cornea + sclera), vascular (choroid + ciliary body + iris), and neural (retina). The anterior chamber contains aqueous humor; the posterior chamber is filled with vitreous humor.
facts.visualDescription: Complete cross-section illustration of the eyeball showing all layers and structures

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this ocular structure.
facts.explanation: This is the vitreous body — the clear gel (99% water with collagen fibrils and hyaluronic acid) filling the posterior chamber of the eye between the lens and retina. It maintains the eye's shape and cannot be regenerated if lost. Floaters are condensed vitreous collagen.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the vitreous body filling the posterior eye chamber

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this inner ear structure responsible for hearing.
facts.explanation: This is the cochlea — the snail-shaped inner ear structure with 2.5 turns containing the organ of Corti. Sound vibrations transmitted from the stapes create pressure waves in perilymph, bending stereocilia on hair cells to generate auditory nerve signals.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the cochlea showing its spiral structure

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this ossicle — the smallest bone in the human body.
facts.explanation: This is the stapes (stirrup) — the smallest bone in the body (~3 mm), the innermost of the three middle ear ossicles. It transmits vibrations from the incus to the oval window of the cochlea. The stapedius muscle (CN VII) dampens loud sounds.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the stapes bone and the inner ear ossicles

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this renal filtration structure.
facts.explanation: This is the glomerulus — a tuft of capillaries within Bowman's capsule where blood is filtered under pressure. The filtration barrier has three layers: fenestrated capillary endothelium, glomerular basement membrane, and podocytes with filtration slits. Normal GFR ~125 mL/min.
facts.visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of the glomerular capillary tuft in Bowman's capsule

facts.quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is the kidney — a bean-shaped retroperitoneal organ (~11 cm) that filters blood to produce urine, regulates blood pressure (renin), maintains electrolyte balance, produces erythropoietin, and activates vitamin D. Each kidney contains ~1 million nephrons.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the kidney external view

facts.quizQuestion: What does this cross-section of the kidney reveal?
facts.explanation: This cross-section reveals the kidney's internal zones: the outer cortex (contains glomeruli and convoluted tubules), medulla (contains renal pyramids and loops of Henle), renal calyces (minor → major) collecting urine, and the renal pelvis draining to the ureter.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional illustration of the kidney showing cortex, medulla, and calyces

facts.quizQuestion: What does this illustration show sitting atop the kidney?
facts.explanation: This shows the kidney with the adrenal (suprarenal) gland perched on its superior pole. Despite the name 'suprarenal', the adrenal gland is not anatomically connected to the kidney — it has its own fascial compartment and separate blood supply from the phrenic, aortic, and renal arteries.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the kidney with adrenal gland on its superior pole

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this organ and its anatomical overview.
facts.explanation: This is an overview of kidney anatomy showing both external features (hilum, renal capsule, cortex) and internal structures (medullary pyramids, renal columns, calyces, pelvis, ureter). The hilum is the entry point for the renal artery, vein, and ureter.
facts.visualDescription: Combined external and internal overview illustration of the kidney

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this functional unit of the kidney.
facts.explanation: This is the nephron — the functional unit of the kidney. Each kidney has ~1 million nephrons consisting of: renal corpuscle (glomerulus + Bowman's capsule), proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule, and collecting duct. Together they filter, reabsorb, and secrete to produce urine.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a complete nephron from glomerulus to collecting duct

facts.quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
facts.explanation: This detailed view of nephron structure shows the distinct functional segments: the proximal tubule (bulk Na+ and water reabsorption), descending/ascending loop of Henle (countercurrent multiplier for urine concentration), distal convoluted tubule (aldosterone-regulated Na+ reabsorption), and collecting duct (ADH-regulated water reabsorption).
facts.visualDescription: Detailed illustration of nephron segments with functional annotations

facts.quizQuestion: Name the cranial nerve depicted leaving the inner ear.
facts.explanation: This is the auditory nerve (CN VIII), also called the vestibulocochlear nerve. It has two divisions: the cochlear nerve carrying hearing signals from the organ of Corti, and the vestibular nerve carrying balance signals from the semicircular canals and otolith organs.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the inner ear with the auditory/vestibulocochlear nerve highlighted

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle group is shown in this posterior view?
facts.explanation: This is the posterior musculature — the back muscles viewed from behind. Key muscles include the trapezius (upper back and neck), latissimus dorsi (broad back muscle), and erector spinae (deep column of muscles along the spine). Together they control posture, spinal extension, and arm movement.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the posterior (back) musculature of the human body

facts.quizQuestion: Which muscle group is shown in this anterior view?
facts.explanation: This is the anterior musculature — the front-facing body muscles. Key muscles include pectoralis major (chest), rectus abdominis (core), deltoid (shoulder), biceps brachii (arm), and quadriceps femoris (thigh). These muscles primarily govern pushing, flexion, and forward locomotion.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the anterior (front) musculature of the human body

facts.quizQuestion: What contractile structure of muscle is shown here?
facts.explanation: This is a myofibril — a cylindrical bundle of actin (thin) and myosin (thick) filaments that runs the length of a muscle fiber. Each myofibril is divided into repeating sarcomeres. During contraction, myosin heads walk along actin filaments in the sliding filament mechanism, shortening the sarcomere.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of a myofibril showing the arrangement of thin and thick filaments

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this thick protein filament involved in muscle contraction.
facts.explanation: This is myosin — the thick filament of muscle. Its globular heads (cross-bridges) bind to actin and use ATP hydrolysis to generate the power stroke that pulls actin filaments inward, shortening the sarcomere. Myosin is the molecular motor of skeletal muscle.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the myosin thick filament with its protruding motor heads

facts.quizQuestion: What is the fundamental contractile unit of skeletal muscle shown here?
facts.explanation: This is a sarcomere — the basic functional unit of a myofibril, bounded by two Z-lines. It contains thick myosin filaments in the A-band and thin actin filaments in the I-band. During contraction, the sliding filament mechanism shortens the distance between Z-lines without changing filament length.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of a sarcomere showing Z-lines, A-band, I-band, and filament arrangement

facts.quizQuestion: What does this cross-sectional view of bone reveal?
facts.explanation: This cross-section of bone shows the outer ring of compact (cortical) bone surrounding the inner spongy (trabecular) bone. At the center is the medullary cavity containing bone marrow. The periosteum covers the outer surface, and the endosteum lines the medullary cavity.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of a bone cross-section showing compact bone, spongy bone, and medullary cavity

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structural components shown in this bone illustration.
facts.explanation: This illustration shows bone internal architecture including cortical (compact) bone forming the dense outer shell and trabecular (cancellous) bone forming the inner lattice. The periosteum covers the outer surface, Haversian canals run through compact bone, and the medullary cavity houses yellow or red bone marrow depending on location and age.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of bone internal structure showing cortical and trabecular regions

facts.quizQuestion: What type of bone tissue is shown in this lattice-like structure?
facts.explanation: This is trabecular (spongy or cancellous) bone — a porous lattice of bony struts called trabeculae. Found in epiphyses of long bones and flat bones, it houses red bone marrow for hematopoiesis. Its architecture aligns with mechanical stress lines (Wolff's law), providing strength with minimal weight.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of trabecular spongy bone showing its open lattice structure

facts.quizQuestion: What stage of skeletal development is shown here?
facts.explanation: This is a child skeleton, distinguished by unfused epiphyseal (growth) plates — cartilaginous zones at bone ends where longitudinal growth occurs. Children have more bones than adults (around 270-300) due to incomplete fusion. The growth plates are vulnerable to Salter-Harris fractures and close in late adolescence under sex hormone influence.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of a child's skeleton showing growth plates and proportions

facts.quizQuestion: Which bone fracture is depicted in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is a clavicle (collarbone) fracture — the most commonly fractured bone in the body, accounting for ~5% of all fractures. About 80% occur at the middle third where the bone has the least support. It typically results from a fall on an outstretched hand or direct blow to the shoulder.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of a clavicle fracture at the middle third of the collarbone

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this large flat bone of the pelvis.
facts.explanation: This is the coxal (hip) bone, also called the os coxae or innominate bone. It forms from the fusion of three bones: the ilium (superior), ischium (posteroinferior), and pubis (anteroinferior). These three bones meet at the acetabulum — the cup-shaped socket for the femoral head. Two coxal bones plus the sacrum and coccyx form the bony pelvis.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the coxal (hip) bone showing ilium, ischium, and pubis regions
