facts.visualDescription: Diagram of the dorsal tongue surface showing the four types of papillae: filiform, fungiform, circumvallate, and foliate

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this overview illustration of skeletal muscle organization.
facts.explanation: Skeletal muscle is the voluntary, striated muscle attached to bone via tendons. Structural hierarchy: muscle → fascicles (bundles of fibers wrapped in perimysium) → muscle fibers (cells, wrapped in endomysium) → myofibrils → sarcomeres (functional units). The whole muscle is wrapped in epimysium. Each muscle fiber is a multinucleate syncytium formed by fusion of myoblasts. Motor units (one motor neuron + all muscle fibers it innervates) are the basic functional unit of force production. Large muscles have large motor units (coarse control); hand/eye muscles have small motor units (fine control).
facts.visualDescription: Hierarchical illustration of skeletal muscle organization from whole muscle to fascicle to fiber to myofibril

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this diagram illustrating the sliding filament mechanism of muscle contraction.
facts.explanation: Muscle contraction follows the sliding filament theory (Huxley, 1954): thick filaments (myosin) and thin filaments (actin) overlap and slide past each other without changing length. The cross-bridge cycle: (1) ATP binds myosin head → releases from actin; (2) ATP hydrolysis → myosin head cocks (high-energy state); (3) Myosin attaches to actin; (4) Power stroke — Pi release → myosin head pivots, pulling actin; (5) New ATP binds to restart. Trigger: Ca2+ release from SR binds troponin C → tropomyosin shifts → exposes actin binding sites. Rigor mortis occurs when ATP is depleted (step 1 cannot occur).
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of the cross-bridge cycle and sliding filament mechanism showing myosin heads, actin, and sarcomere shortening

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the connective tissue-wrapped bundle of muscle fibers shown in this cross-sectional diagram.
facts.explanation: A fascicle is a bundle of skeletal muscle fibers (cells) wrapped in perimysium (dense irregular connective tissue). Each individual fiber within is wrapped in endomysium; the whole muscle is wrapped in epimysium, continuous with the tendon. Fascicular arrangement determines muscle architecture and force-velocity characteristics: parallel (strap) muscles — long fibers, long ROM, less force; pennate muscles (unipennate, bipennate, multipennate) — shorter fibers at an angle to the tendon, more fibers per unit volume, more force but shorter ROM. The biceps brachii is a fusiform (spindle-shaped) muscle.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional diagram of a muscle fascicle showing individual muscle fibers surrounded by endomysium within a perimysium sheath

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the individual multinucleate skeletal muscle cell shown in this detailed illustration.
facts.explanation: A skeletal muscle fiber (cell) is a large, multinucleate syncytium (formed by myoblast fusion during development) up to 30 cm long, with peripherally located nuclei. The sarcolemma (plasma membrane) has T-tubule invaginations that carry action potentials deep into the cell. The sarcoplasm contains abundant mitochondria and the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) — specialized ER that stores and releases Ca2+. Myofibrils are the contractile elements; each myofibril is ~1–2 µm in diameter and contains sarcomeres in series. The striated appearance results from regular A-bands (myosin+actin overlap, dark) and I-bands (actin only, light).
facts.visualDescription: Detailed illustration of a single skeletal muscle fiber showing sarcolemma, T-tubules, sarcoplasmic reticulum, myofibrils, and peripheral nuclei

acceptableAlternatives: Anterior middle and posterior cranial fossae
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this superior view of the internal skull base showing the three cranial fossae.
facts.explanation: The internal base of the skull has three stepped fossae: (1) Anterior cranial fossa — frontal and ethmoid bones; houses frontal lobes and olfactory bulbs; cribriform plate transmits CN I; (2) Middle cranial fossa — sphenoid and temporal bones; houses temporal lobes; key foramina: optic canal (CN II, ophthalmic artery), superior orbital fissure (CN III/IV/VI/V1), foramen rotundum (V2), foramen ovale (V3), foramen spinosum (middle meningeal artery — laceration causes epidural hematoma); (3) Posterior cranial fossa — occipital and temporal bones; houses cerebellum and brainstem; foramen magnum, internal acoustic meatus (CN VII/VIII), jugular foramen (CN IX/X/XI).
facts.visualDescription: Superior view of the internal skull base showing the three cranial fossae and their key foramina

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this delicate, sieve-like midline skull bone that forms the roof of the nasal cavity.
facts.explanation: The ethmoid bone is an unpaired, delicate midline bone at the roof of the nasal cavity. Key features: (1) Cribriform plate — horizontal plate with many small foramina (cribriform = 'sieve-like') transmitting CN I (olfactory nerve) fibers from nasal epithelium to olfactory bulb. Fractures here cause CSF rhinorrhea and anosmia; (2) Crista galli — superior vertical projection anchoring the falx cerebri; (3) Perpendicular plate — forms the upper nasal septum; (4) Lateral masses (labyrinths) with superior and middle nasal conchae; ethmoid air cells (paranasal sinuses). The ethmoid forms part of the medial orbital wall (lamina papyracea — extremely thin, 'paper-thin').
facts.visualDescription: Isolated ethmoid bone illustration showing cribriform plate, crista galli, perpendicular plate, and lateral masses with air cells

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the only bone in the body that does not articulate with any other bone, located in the anterior neck.
facts.explanation: The hyoid bone is a U-shaped bone suspended in the anterior neck at the level of C3, held in place by muscles and ligaments — it has NO bony articulations. It consists of a body, two greater horns, and two lesser horns. The hyoid serves as an attachment point for muscles of the floor of the mouth, tongue, pharynx, and larynx. It is critical for swallowing and speech. Fracture of the hyoid is uncommon due to its protected position but is classically associated with strangulation (manual or ligature) — a key forensic finding. In embalming and laryngoscopy, hyoid position is a surgical landmark.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the isolated hyoid bone showing the body, greater horns, and lesser horns in anterior and lateral views

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the only moveable bone of the skull shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The mandible (lower jaw) is the only moveable skull bone and the largest, strongest facial bone. Key features: body (horizontal portion bearing the lower teeth), two rami (vertical portions), mandibular angle (junction of body and ramus), condylar process (condyle articulates with temporal bone at the TMJ — only true joint of the skull), and coronoid process (attachment of temporalis muscle). The mandibular foramen (on medial ramus) transmits the inferior alveolar nerve and artery — target for dental blocks (inferior alveolar nerve block). The mental foramen (lateral body) transmits the mental nerve (V3 branch). Mandible fracture is the second most common facial fracture.
facts.visualDescription: Lateral and anterior view illustrations of the mandible showing body, ramus, condyle, coronoid process, and key foramina

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the paired facial bones that form the upper jaw, floor of the orbit, and roof of the mouth.
facts.explanation: The maxillae (paired) fuse at the intermaxillary suture to form the upper jaw. The maxilla contributes to: (1) alveolar process — houses upper teeth; (2) palatine process — anterior 3/4 of hard palate (failure to fuse causes cleft palate); (3) orbital floor and inferior orbital rim; (4) anterior nasal floor; (5) medial orbital wall. The infraorbital foramen transmits the infraorbital nerve (V2 branch) — anesthetized for upper lip/anterior teeth blocks. The maxillary sinus (largest paranasal sinus) is housed within the maxilla — the largest sinus and most commonly affected by sinusitis. Le Fort fractures involve the maxilla.
facts.visualDescription: Isolated maxilla bone illustration showing alveolar process, palatine process, orbital surface, and infraorbital foramen

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this diagram showing the bony framework and nasal conchae of the nasal cavity.
facts.explanation: The bony nasal cavity is framed by: nasal bones (bridge), maxillae (floor and lateral walls), ethmoid (roof — cribriform plate, and superior/middle conchae), inferior nasal conchae (separate bones, largest conchae), vomer (inferior nasal septum), and perpendicular plate of ethmoid (superior septum). The three pairs of conchae (superior, middle, inferior) create turbulence to warm and humidify air and dramatically increase mucosal surface area. Beneath each concha is a meatus: inferior meatus receives the nasolacrimal duct (tearing into the nose). Nasal polyps most commonly arise from the middle meatus.
facts.visualDescription: Coronal or sagittal cross-section of the bony nasal cavity showing conchae, nasal septum, and bony contributions of surrounding bones

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the seven bones that form the bony orbit of the eye shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The bony orbit is formed by seven bones (mnemonic: 'My Flat Zeal Surprises Posh Elderly Ladies'): Maxilla (floor and medial wall), Frontal (roof), Zygomatic (lateral wall and floor), Sphenoid (posterior lateral wall and apex — optic canal and SOF), Palatine (posteromedial floor), Ethmoid (medial wall — lamina papyracea, very thin), Lacrimal (anteromedial wall — lacrimal fossa for lacrimal sac). Key openings: optic canal (CN II, ophthalmic artery), superior orbital fissure (CN III/IV/VI/V1, ophthalmic veins), inferior orbital fissure (V2, infraorbital vessels). The orbital floor is the weakest wall — blowout fractures here.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior and/or lateral view of the bony orbit with all seven contributing bones labeled and color-coded

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this diagram showing the four pairs of air-filled sinuses within the facial bones.
facts.explanation: The four paired paranasal sinuses: (1) Maxillary — largest, in maxilla; drain into middle meatus via ostium high on medial wall (poor gravitational drainage → most prone to infection); roof is floor of orbit; floor is alveolar process (upper molar roots may project into it); (2) Frontal — in frontal bone; drain via frontonasal duct into middle meatus; (3) Ethmoid — anterior/posterior cells between orbits; complex drainage; (4) Sphenoid — in sphenoid body; drain into sphenoethmoidal recess above superior concha. All lined with pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium. Functions: lighten skull, warm/humidify air, resonate voice, cushion facial trauma.
facts.visualDescription: Frontal and lateral skull views showing the locations and relative sizes of the four pairs of paranasal sinuses

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this frontal view of the adult human skull showing facial bones and sutures.
facts.explanation: The anterior skull shows: cranium (neurocranium) — frontal bone (forehead, with frontal sinuses); facial bones — nasal bones (bridge), zygomatic bones (cheekbones), maxillae (upper jaw), mandible (lower jaw, only moveable skull bone), and the orbits. Sutures visible anteriorly: coronal suture (frontal-parietal), frontonasal suture. Key foramina visible anteriorly: supraorbital foramen/notch (V1 branch, supraorbital nerve), infraorbital foramen (V2, infraorbital nerve), mental foramen (V3 branch, mental nerve). The skull has 22 bones total: 8 cranial and 14 facial.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior (frontal) view of the human skull with labeled bones, sutures, and foramina

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this inferior view of the skull base showing the foramina and occipital region.
facts.explanation: The external skull base (inferior view) shows key structures: occipital bone (foramen magnum — transmits medulla oblongata, vertebral arteries, CN XI spinal roots; occipital condyles articulate with C1/atlas), temporal bone (styloid process — attachment of styloid muscles; stylomastoid foramen transmits CN VII exiting skull; carotid canal; jugular foramen — CN IX/X/XI, sigmoid sinus), sphenoid (pterygoid plates, foramen ovale — CN V3, foramen spinosum — middle meningeal artery), and the hard palate (maxilla anteriorly, palatine posteriorly). The mastoid process (temporal bone) is the posterior attachment of the SCM.
facts.visualDescription: Inferior view of the external skull base with labeled bones, foramina, processes, and key landmarks

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this lateral view of the skull showing the cranial bones, sutures, and temporal region.
facts.explanation: The lateral skull shows the major cranial bones and sutures: frontal (anterior), parietal (superior and lateral), temporal (lateral — contains external acoustic meatus, mastoid process, zygomatic process), occipital (posterior), sphenoid (pterion region). Sutures: coronal (frontal-parietal), squamous/squamosal (temporal-parietal), lambdoid (parietal-occipital). The pterion (H-shaped junction of frontal, parietal, temporal, sphenoid) is the thinnest point of the skull — the middle meningeal artery runs here; trauma → epidural hematoma (biconvex on CT, arterial bleed, lucid interval). The zygomatic arch: temporal process of zygomatic + zygomatic process of temporal.
facts.visualDescription: Lateral view of the skull with all major bones, sutures, foramina, and landmarks labeled

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this labeled overview diagram identifying the major bones of the skull.
facts.explanation: The skull has 22 bones: Cranial bones (8) — frontal (1), parietal (2), temporal (2), occipital (1), sphenoid (1, 'keystone' of skull base), ethmoid (1); Facial bones (14) — mandible (1), maxillae (2), zygomatic (2), nasal (2), lacrimal (2), palatine (2), inferior nasal conchae (2), vomer (1). The cranial bones form the neurocranium protecting the brain; facial bones form the viscerocranium. At birth, the skull has fontanelles (fibrous membranes between incompletely ossified bones) — anterior fontanelle closes at ~18 months, posterior at ~3 months.
facts.visualDescription: Labeled diagram identifying all major skull bones from multiple views or an exploded diagram

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this posterior view of the skull showing the occipital bone and lambdoid suture.
facts.explanation: The posterior skull view features: occipital bone — the large posterior cranial bone bearing the external occipital protuberance (EOP/inion, palpable midline bump — nuchal ligament attachment), superior and inferior nuchal lines (attachment of suboccipital/neck muscles), and the foramen magnum (inferiorly). The lambdoid suture (inverted V-shape) separates the parietal bones from the occipital bone. Parietal foramina (emissary veins). The asterion is the landmark at the junction of lambdoid, occipitomastoid, and parietomastoid sutures — overlies the transverse-sigmoid sinus junction. Wormian (sutural) bones are accessory ossification centers within sutures; excess numbers suggest osteogenesis imperfecta.
facts.visualDescription: Posterior view of the skull showing the occipital bone, lambdoid suture, parietal bones, and key landmarks

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this midsagittal cross-section of the skull showing the internal bones and nasal septum.
facts.explanation: The midsagittal skull section reveals internal structures invisible from outside: (1) nasal septum — perpendicular plate of ethmoid (superior) + vomer (inferior) + septal cartilage (anterior); (2) hard palate — palatine process of maxilla (anterior 3/4) + horizontal plate of palatine bone (posterior 1/4); (3) internal occipital protuberance; (4) sella turcica — depression in sphenoid body housing the pituitary gland; (5) anterior cranial fossa floor (cribriform plate of ethmoid); (6) clivus — sloping posterior part of sphenoid/occipital bodies, where basilar artery lies against it.
facts.visualDescription: Midsagittal section of the skull showing nasal septum, hard palate, sella turcica, internal cranial base, and all contributing bones

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the butterfly-shaped bone that forms the central keystone of the skull base.
facts.explanation: The sphenoid is an unpaired 'keystone' bone articulating with all other cranial bones. Bat/butterfly shape: body (center — contains sphenoid sinuses; sella turcica houses pituitary; optic chiasm sits in optic groove), greater wings (form middle cranial fossa floor, pterion region, infratemporal and temporal fossae), lesser wings (form posterior part of anterior cranial fossa and superior orbital fissure — CN III/IV/VI/V1), and pterygoid processes (hang down — attachment for pterygoid muscles of mastication; medial pterygoid plate ends at pterygoid hamulus). Key foramina: optic canal (lesser wing), superior orbital fissure (between greater/lesser wings), foramen rotundum (V2), foramen ovale (V3), foramen spinosum (middle meningeal artery).
facts.visualDescription: Superior view and/or anterior view of the isolated sphenoid bone showing body, greater wings, lesser wings, pterygoid processes, and key foramina

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the complex paired bone forming the lateral skull, housing the organs of hearing and balance.
facts.explanation: The temporal bone consists of four parts: squamous (forms lateral skull wall, zygomatic process), petrous ('rocky' medial part — hardest bone in the body; houses cochlea and semicircular canals within the bony labyrinth; contains internal acoustic meatus transmitting CN VII and CN VIII), mastoid (posterior — mastoid process with air cells that communicate with middle ear; mastoiditis is a complication of otitis media), and tympanic (surrounds external acoustic meatus). CN VII exits via the stylomastoid foramen (between styloid and mastoid). The carotid canal transmits the internal carotid artery. Temporal bone fractures: longitudinal (90%, tympanic membrane tear, conductive hearing loss) vs transverse (sensorineural hearing loss, CN VII palsy).
facts.visualDescription: Medial and/or lateral view of the isolated temporal bone showing squamous, petrous, mastoid, and tympanic portions with labeled processes and foramina

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this illustration of the male bony pelvis showing its characteristic narrow shape.
facts.explanation: The male pelvis differs from the female in several clinically important ways: (1) Male pelvic inlet is heart-shaped (narrower); female is oval/round; (2) Male pubic arch angle <90° (acute); female >90° (obtuse, called subpubic angle); (3) Male ischial spines are more prominent and closer together (smaller interspinous diameter); (4) Male sacrum is narrower and more curved; (5) Overall, male pelvis is narrower, deeper, and heavier with more pronounced muscle markings. The pelvis consists of two hip bones (each = ilium + ischium + pubis, fused at triradiate cartilage), the sacrum, and the coccyx. The pelvic outlet is bounded by the coccyx (posteriorly), ischial tuberosities (laterally), and pubic arch (anteriorly).
facts.visualDescription: Anterior view illustration of the male bony pelvis showing the characteristic narrow inlet, acute pubic arch, and prominent ischial spines

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this cross-sectional diagram of the female breast showing its glandular and ductal anatomy.
facts.explanation: Breast and lactation anatomy. The breast (mammary gland) is a modified apocrine sweat gland consisting of 15–25 lobes of tubuloalveolar glands arranged radially around the nipple, separated by adipose tissue and Cooper's ligaments (suspensory ligaments attaching breast to overlying skin and underlying fascia). Each lobe drains via a lactiferous duct to the nipple; ducts dilate near the nipple as lactiferous sinuses. Blood supply: internal thoracic (internal mammary) artery and lateral thoracic artery. Lymphatic drainage is critical in breast cancer: ~75% drains to axillary nodes (sentinel node biopsy); medial breast drains to parasternal nodes. Prolactin (anterior pituitary) stimulates milk production; oxytocin triggers milk ejection ('let-down' reflex). Breast tissue extends to the axillary tail of Spence.
facts.visualDescription: Sagittal cross-section of the female breast showing lobes, lactiferous ducts, lactiferous sinuses, nipple, areola, Cooper's ligaments, and surrounding adipose tissue

facts.quizQuestion: Identify this diagram showing the cyclical hormonal changes that govern the female reproductive cycle.
facts.explanation: The female reproductive cycle is governed by a hormonal cascade: Hypothalamus → GnRH (pulsatile) → Anterior pituitary → FSH (follicle development) and LH (midcycle surge → ovulation; stimulates theca cells → androgens). Ovarian phases: Follicular phase (days 1–14) — rising estradiol from granulosa cells; Ovulation (day 14) — LH surge triggers follicle rupture; Luteal phase (days 15–28) — corpus luteum secretes progesterone + estrogen. Progesterone: prepares endometrium for implantation, inhibits further ovulation, raises basal body temperature. If no fertilization, corpus luteum degenerates → progesterone/estrogen fall → menstruation. Estrogen feedback: early follicular = negative; late follicular (high E2) = positive → LH surge.
facts.visualDescription: Multi-panel diagram showing FSH, LH, estrogen, and progesterone levels across the menstrual cycle alongside follicular and uterine changes

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ system shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: This is an overview of the female reproductive system. The ovaries produce ova and sex hormones (estrogen, progesterone). Each ovary connects to the uterus via a fallopian tube; fertilization typically occurs in the ampulla of the fallopian tube. The uterus is the site of implantation and fetal development. The cervix is the narrow lower portion opening into the vagina, the muscular canal to the exterior.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the female reproductive system showing ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix, and vagina

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ system shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: This overview shows the male reproductive system. The testes (in the scrotum) produce sperm and testosterone. Sperm mature in the epididymis, then travel through the vas deferens during ejaculation. The seminal vesicles, prostate gland, and bulbourethral glands add secretions to form semen. The penis delivers semen into the female reproductive tract and also carries urine to the exterior via the urethra.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the male reproductive system showing testes, epididymis, vas deferens, prostate, and penis

facts.quizQuestion: This diagram correlates hormonal changes with cyclic events in the female reproductive system. What cycle is depicted?
facts.explanation: The menstrual cycle (approximately 28 days) integrates the ovarian cycle and uterine cycle, coordinated by hormones. Rising FSH stimulates follicle development (follicular phase). The LH surge triggers ovulation (~day 14). The ruptured follicle becomes the corpus luteum (luteal phase), secreting progesterone to maintain the endometrium. If no fertilization occurs, the corpus luteum regresses, progesterone falls, and menstruation begins.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram correlating FSH, LH, estrogen, and progesterone levels with follicular development and uterine endometrial changes across a 28-day cycle

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the process of female gamete development illustrated here.
facts.explanation: Oogenesis is the process of producing mature ova. Oogonia (2n) undergo mitosis before birth to form primary oocytes, which arrest in prophase I. At puberty, each menstrual cycle one primary oocyte completes meiosis I to yield a secondary oocyte (n) and a first polar body. Meiosis II completes only upon fertilization, producing the ovum (n) and a second polar body. Thus, one functional egg and up to three polar bodies are produced.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing progression from oogonium through primary oocyte, meiosis I arrest, secondary oocyte, and ovum formation with polar bodies

facts.quizQuestion: This illustration shows sequential stages within the ovary. What process is depicted?
facts.explanation: Ovarian follicle development (folliculogenesis) proceeds through distinct stages: primordial follicle → primary follicle → secondary follicle → antral (tertiary) follicle → Graafian (preovulatory) follicle. After ovulation, the ruptured follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone. If unfertilized, it degenerates into the corpus albicans (fibrous scar tissue).
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section of ovary showing primordial, primary, secondary, and Graafian follicles plus corpus luteum and corpus albicans

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the transverse section of the male external genitalia shown here.
facts.explanation: The penis contains three cylinders of erectile tissue: two corpora cavernosa (dorsal pair, larger) and one corpus spongiosum (ventral, surrounds the urethra). During erection, parasympathetic stimulation causes nitric oxide release, relaxing smooth muscle in the helicine arteries, engorging the sinusoids with blood. The tunica albuginea compresses emissary veins, maintaining rigidity. The corpus spongiosum stays softer to keep the urethra patent for ejaculation.
facts.visualDescription: Transverse cross-section of the penis showing corpora cavernosa, corpus spongiosum, urethra, and surrounding fascia layers

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structures of the male external genitalia shown in this sectional view.
facts.explanation: The scrotum is a cutaneous sac housing the testes outside the abdominal cavity, keeping them ~2–3°C below core body temperature for optimal spermatogenesis. Key layers from outside in: skin → dartos muscle (wrinkles skin for thermoregulation) → external spermatic fascia → cremaster muscle (elevates testes) → tunica vaginalis (serous sac). The testis itself is covered by the tunica albuginea and divided into lobules containing seminiferous tubules.
facts.visualDescription: Sectional view of the scrotum and testes showing dartos muscle, cremasteric fascia, tunica vaginalis, tunica albuginea, and testicular lobules

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the histological section of the testis showing the sites of sperm production.
facts.explanation: Seminiferous tubules are the site of spermatogenesis. The tubule wall contains Sertoli cells (supporting cells — form the blood-testis barrier, nourish developing sperm, secrete inhibin) and spermatogenic cells at various stages. Between tubules in the interstitium, Leydig cells (interstitial cells) synthesize testosterone in response to LH. Mature sperm are released into the tubule lumen and move to the rete testis, then the epididymis.
facts.visualDescription: Histological cross-sections of seminiferous tubules showing Sertoli cells, spermatogenic cells at various stages, and Leydig cells in the interstitium

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structure of the male gamete shown in this diagram.
facts.explanation: A spermatozoon has three main parts. The head contains the haploid nucleus (condensed DNA) capped by the acrosome — a lysosome-like vesicle with enzymes (acrosin, hyaluronidase) for penetrating the zona pellucida of the egg. The midpiece is packed with mitochondria arranged in a helix around the axoneme, providing ATP for motility. The tail (flagellum) drives propulsion via the 9+2 axonemal microtubule arrangement powered by dynein ATPase.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of a sperm cell labeling the acrosome, nucleus (head), mitochondrial sheath (midpiece), and flagellum (tail)

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the process of male gamete development illustrated here.
facts.explanation: Spermatogenesis occurs in the seminiferous tubules and takes ~74 days. Spermatogonia (2n) undergo mitosis to renew the stem cell pool and produce primary spermatocytes (2n). Each primary spermatocyte undergoes meiosis I to form two secondary spermatocytes (n), then meiosis II to form four spermatids (n). Spermatids then undergo spermiogenesis — a dramatic morphological transformation — to become mature spermatozoa. Unlike oogenesis, all four products are functional.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing the stages from spermatogonium through primary and secondary spermatocytes, spermatids, to mature spermatozoa with meiosis I and II indicated

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the uterine structures and their connections to the ovaries shown here.
facts.explanation: The uterus is a pear-shaped muscular organ with three regions: fundus (dome above fallopian tube openings), body, and cervix. Each fallopian tube has four segments from ovary to uterus: infundibulum (with fimbriae that sweep ova in), ampulla (most common site of fertilization), isthmus, and intramural portion. The broad ligament suspends the uterus and tubes. Ciliated epithelium and smooth muscle peristalsis move the ovum toward the uterus.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior view of the uterus and fallopian tubes with labeled fimbriae, infundibulum, ampulla, isthmus, fundus, body, and cervix

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the external female genitalia shown in this anatomical illustration.
facts.explanation: The vulva comprises the external female genitalia: mons pubis (fatty pad over pubic symphysis), labia majora (outer folds, homologous to scrotum), labia minora (inner folds, highly vascular), clitoris (erectile tissue, homologous to glans penis), vestibule (space between labia minora containing urethral and vaginal openings), Bartholin's glands (greater vestibular glands — lubricate vestibule, homologous to Cowper's glands), and Skene's glands (periurethral).
facts.visualDescription: Anatomical illustration of the external female genitalia labeling the mons pubis, labia majora, labia minora, clitoris, urethral opening, vaginal opening, and vestibular glands

facts.quizQuestion: This diagram illustrates hormonal control of the male reproductive system. What axis or system is shown?
facts.explanation: The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis regulates male reproduction. GnRH from the hypothalamus stimulates anterior pituitary release of FSH and LH. FSH acts on Sertoli cells to support spermatogenesis and promote inhibin secretion. LH acts on Leydig cells to stimulate testosterone synthesis. Testosterone and inhibin provide negative feedback to the hypothalamus and pituitary, maintaining homeostatic hormone levels. Testosterone drives secondary sex characteristics and anabolism.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing the HPG axis with GnRH, FSH and LH pathways, Sertoli and Leydig cell targets, testosterone and inhibin feedback loops

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the vascular supply of the kidney shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Renal blood flow follows a precise pathway: renal artery → segmental arteries → interlobar arteries (between renal pyramids) → arcuate arteries (arch at corticomedullary junction) → interlobular (cortical radiate) arteries → afferent arterioles → glomerular capillaries → efferent arterioles → peritubular capillaries (cortex) and vasa recta (medulla) → venous system in reverse. The kidneys receive ~20–25% of cardiac output despite being <1% of body mass.
facts.visualDescription: Coronal section of kidney with arterial and venous supply labeled from renal artery through segmental, interlobar, arcuate, and interlobular vessels

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the internal anatomy of the kidney shown in this coronal section.
facts.explanation: A coronal kidney section reveals: renal cortex (outer, granular — contains glomeruli and convoluted tubules), renal medulla (inner, striated — contains renal pyramids with collecting ducts and loops of Henle), renal columns (cortical tissue between pyramids), renal papilla (apex of each pyramid draining into a minor calyx), minor calyces → major calyces → renal pelvis → ureter. The renal sinus is the central fatty space containing the pelvis, calyces, and vessels.
facts.visualDescription: Coronal section of kidney labeling cortex, medulla, renal pyramids, renal columns, minor and major calyces, renal pelvis, and hilum

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the position of the kidneys relative to the abdominal cavity shown here.
facts.explanation: Kidney position in abdomen. The kidneys are retroperitoneal organs located on the posterior abdominal wall between T12 and L3 vertebrae. The right kidney sits slightly lower than the left (due to the liver above). Each kidney is embedded in perinephric fat within the renal fascia (Gerota's fascia). The adrenal glands sit atop each kidney. Key relations: right kidney contacts liver, duodenum, hepatic flexure of colon; left kidney contacts stomach, pancreas, spleen, splenic flexure.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior view of the abdomen showing bilateral retroperitoneal kidney positions relative to vertebral levels, liver, and other abdominal organs

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the microcirculation of the kidney functional unit shown here.
facts.explanation: Nephron blood flow: afferent arteriole → glomerulus (high-pressure filtration) → efferent arteriole → peritubular capillaries (cortical nephrons, surrounds proximal/distal tubules for reabsorption/secretion) or vasa recta (juxtamedullary nephrons, thin-walled vessels parallel to loops of Henle enabling countercurrent exchange for urinary concentration). The two-capillary-bed arrangement (glomerulus + peritubular/vasa recta) is unique to the kidney and enables independent regulation of filtration and reabsorption.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of a nephron with associated blood vessels showing afferent and efferent arterioles, glomerular capillaries, peritubular capillaries, and vasa recta

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the internal cardiac structures that anchor the atrioventricular valves shown here.
facts.explanation: Chordae tendineae and papillary muscles. The chordae tendineae ('heartstrings') are fibrous cords connecting the atrioventricular valve leaflets (mitral and tricuspid) to the papillary muscles on the ventricular floor. The papillary muscles contract during systole, tensioning the chordae to prevent valve leaflet prolapse into the atrium (regurgitation). Mitral valve has two papillary muscles (anterolateral and posteromedial); tricuspid has three. Rupture of papillary muscle (e.g., post-MI) causes acute severe mitral regurgitation.
facts.visualDescription: Internal view of the left ventricle showing mitral valve leaflets connected by chordae tendineae to the anterolateral and posteromedial papillary muscles

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the category of structural cardiac abnormalities shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Congenital heart defects (CHDs) are structural abnormalities present at birth. Common examples include: ventricular septal defect (VSD — most common CHD), atrial septal defect (ASD), patent ductus arteriosus (PDA — DA fails to close after birth causing L→R shunt), tetralogy of Fallot (VSD + pulmonary stenosis + overriding aorta + RVH — most common cyanotic CHD), and coarctation of the aorta. Left-to-right shunts cause pulmonary hypertension; right-to-left shunts cause cyanosis.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration comparing normal heart structure with common congenital defects such as VSD, ASD, and PDA

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the blood vessels supplying the heart muscle shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The coronary arteries arise from the aortic sinuses just above the aortic valve. The left main coronary artery divides into the left anterior descending (LAD — supplies anterior LV, IVS, anterior LV apex) and left circumflex (LCx — supplies lateral and posterior LV). The right coronary artery (RCA) supplies the right heart and, in right-dominant systems (70%), gives the posterior descending artery (PDA). The cardiac veins drain into the coronary sinus → right atrium.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior and posterior views of the heart showing the right coronary artery, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and cardiac veins with the coronary sinus

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the two-loop blood circulation diagram shown here.
facts.explanation: Mammals have a dual (double) circulatory system: pulmonary circulation (right heart → pulmonary arteries → lungs → pulmonary veins → left heart) and systemic circulation (left heart → aorta → body tissues → venae cavae → right heart). These circuits are in series, ensuring all blood is oxygenated before systemic distribution. The right ventricle generates lower pressure (~25/10 mmHg) than the left (~120/80 mmHg), reflecting pulmonary low-resistance vs. systemic high-resistance.
facts.visualDescription: Schematic diagram showing pulmonary and systemic circulation loops with heart, lungs, and body tissues, with arrows indicating oxygenated and deoxygenated blood flow

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the internal anatomy of the heart shown in this coronal section.
facts.explanation: A coronal section of the heart reveals four chambers: right atrium (receives deoxygenated blood from SVC/IVC, contains SA node) → tricuspid valve → right ventricle → pulmonary valve → pulmonary trunk; and left atrium (receives oxygenated blood from pulmonary veins) → mitral (bicuspid) valve → left ventricle (thick wall for systemic pressure) → aortic valve → aorta. The interventricular septum separates the ventricles; the interatrial septum separates the atria (contains the fossa ovalis — remnant of foramen ovale).
facts.visualDescription: Coronal section of the heart showing all four chambers, four valves, interatrial and interventricular septa, and major vessels

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the anatomical position of the heart within the chest cavity shown here.
facts.explanation: The heart lies in the middle mediastinum within the pericardial sac, positioned obliquely behind the sternum. It is 2/3 to the left of the midline. Its apex (tip of the left ventricle) points inferiorly and to the left, typically at the 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line — where the apical impulse (point of maximal impulse, PMI) is palpated. The base (great vessel end) faces superiorly and posteriorly. It lies anterior to the esophagus and thoracic aorta, posterior to the sternum.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior thorax illustration showing the heart's position relative to sternum, ribs, lungs, and mediastinal structures with the apex pointing to the left 5th ICS

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the external surface features of the heart shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The external heart surfaces include: the sternocostal (anterior) surface dominated by the right ventricle, the diaphragmatic (inferior) surface formed mainly by the left ventricle, and the base (posterior) formed by the atria. The coronary sulcus (atrioventricular groove) encircles the heart separating atria from ventricles and carries the coronary arteries. The anterior and posterior interventricular sulci mark the IVS position. The right and left auricles are ear-like appendages of each atrium.
facts.visualDescription: Anterior and posterior views of the heart exterior showing sulci, auricles, coronary vessels in their grooves, and the great vessels

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the four cardiac valves shown in this superior view of the heart with the atria removed.
facts.explanation: Heart valves (superior view). A superior (basal) view with atria removed reveals all four valves simultaneously. The atrioventricular valves (mitral — bicuspid, left; tricuspid — right) open during diastole to allow ventricular filling. The semilunar valves (aortic — between LV and aorta; pulmonary — between RV and pulmonary trunk) open during systole for ejection. All valves are associated with the fibrous cardiac skeleton (annuli fibrosi), which provides mechanical support, prevents overdistension, and electrically insulates atria from ventricles.
facts.visualDescription: Superior (basal) view of the heart base with atria removed showing the tricuspid, mitral, aortic, and pulmonary valves in their relative positions

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the layers of the heart wall shown in this cross-sectional diagram.
facts.explanation: The heart wall has three layers. The epicardium (visceral pericardium) is the outer serous membrane composed of simple squamous epithelium and connective tissue; the coronary vessels run within it. The myocardium is the thick middle muscular layer of cardiac muscle (uninucleate, striated, branched, connected by intercalated discs with gap junctions for electrical coupling). The endocardium is the thin inner layer of simple squamous epithelium lining the chambers, continuous with the endothelium of vessels. Pericarditis, myocarditis, and endocarditis each affect different layers.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional diagram of the heart wall labeling the epicardium (visceral pericardium), myocardium, endocardium, and the parietal pericardium with pericardial cavity

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the type of vascular dilation shown in this medical illustration.
facts.explanation: A fusiform aneurysm is a symmetrical, spindle-shaped dilation involving the entire circumference of the vessel wall, contrasting with a saccular aneurysm (asymmetric outpouching). Most aortic aneurysms are fusiform (abdominal aortic aneurysm, AAA, is most common — often at the infrarenal aorta). Caused by atherosclerosis, hypertension, or connective tissue disorders (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos). Risk of rupture increases with diameter >5.5 cm. Repair options: open surgical or endovascular (EVAR).
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of a blood vessel showing symmetric fusiform (spindle-shaped) dilation of the aorta

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the arterial supply to the head and neck shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The head and neck receive blood from two main sources: the common carotid arteries (right from the brachiocephalic trunk; left directly from the aortic arch) dividing into external carotid (face/scalp/neck) and internal carotid (brain/orbit); and the vertebral arteries (from subclavian arteries, ascending through transverse foramina C1–C6, merging intracranially to form the basilar artery). The circle of Willis connects internal carotid and vertebrobasilar systems, providing collateral flow.
facts.visualDescription: Lateral and anterior view of the head and neck showing common carotid bifurcation, external and internal carotid arteries, vertebral arteries, and their branches

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ shown in this detailed medical illustration.
facts.explanation: This detailed illustration of the heart highlights its external and surface anatomy with the coronary vessels. The heart is the muscular pump of the cardiovascular system, beating approximately 100,000 times per day. It weighs 250–350 g in adults. The right side handles deoxygenated blood (pulmonary circuit); the left side handles oxygenated blood (systemic circuit). The cardiac output at rest is ~5 L/min, increasing to 20–25 L/min during intense exercise via increases in heart rate and stroke volume.
facts.visualDescription: High-detail medical illustration of the heart showing external surface with coronary vessels, great vessels, and major anatomical landmarks

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the plane of section used in this cardiac illustration.
facts.explanation: A sagittal (parasagittal) section of the heart provides a lateral view revealing the relationship between anterior structures (right ventricle, right atrium) and posterior structures (left ventricle, aorta, pulmonary veins). This view is useful for understanding the anterior position of the right ventricle (predisposing it to trauma injury), the posterior position of the left atrium (explaining why left atrial enlargement causes dysphagia), and the spatial relationship between the aorta and pulmonary trunk.
facts.visualDescription: Sagittal or parasagittal section through the heart showing anterior-to-posterior relationships of the cardiac chambers and great vessels

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the CNS glial cell shown wrapping myelin around multiple axons.
facts.explanation: Oligodendrocytes are the myelin-producing glial cells of the CNS (contrast: Schwann cells in the PNS). A single oligodendrocyte can myelinate up to 50 axon segments simultaneously, sending out flat processes that wrap around axons. Myelin increases conduction velocity via saltatory conduction (action potentials 'jump' between nodes of Ranvier). Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune demyelinating disease targeting CNS myelin; remyelination by oligodendrocyte precursors is limited.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of an oligodendrocyte with multiple processes wrapping myelin sheaths around several CNS axons simultaneously

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the spinal cord level shown in this cross-section based on its characteristic gray and white matter proportions.
facts.explanation: At C5 (cervical enlargement), the spinal cord cross-section shows a large amount of white matter relative to gray matter, reflecting the many ascending and descending tracts passing through this high level. The gray matter has prominent anterior horns (containing large alpha motor neurons for the brachial plexus/upper limb innervation) and smaller posterior horns. The cervical enlargement (C5–T1) supplies the upper limb via the brachial plexus. C5 injury causes deltoid and biceps weakness (shoulder abduction, elbow flexion).
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section of the spinal cord at the C5 cervical level showing large white matter volume, prominent anterior horns, and oval overall shape

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the spinal cord level shown in this cross-section based on its gray-to-white matter ratio.
facts.explanation: At L1, the spinal cord cross-section shows a relatively rounder shape with a higher gray-to-white matter ratio than cervical levels — fewer ascending/descending tracts remain at this caudal level. The anterior horns are enlarged (lumbar enlargement L1–S3 supplies lower limb via lumbosacral plexus), but smaller than at cervical enlargement. L1 is clinically important: the spinal cord typically ends at L1–L2 (conus medullaris); below this is the cauda equina. L1 compression affects hip flexors and knee extensors.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section of the spinal cord at the L1 lumbar level showing rounder shape, proportionally larger gray matter relative to white matter compared to cervical levels

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the spinal cord level shown in this cross-section with its distinctive thoracic morphology.
facts.explanation: At T2, the spinal cord cross-section has a characteristic rounded oval shape with relatively small gray matter horns and proportionally large white matter. The thoracic cord (T1–T12) has small anterior and posterior horns (few limb motor/sensory neurons) but a unique lateral horn containing preganglionic sympathetic neurons (intermediolateral cell column, IML) — absent in cervical and lumbar cord. This is the hallmark of thoracic cord sections. The thoracic cord is the narrowest part of the cord.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section of the spinal cord at T2 thoracic level showing oval shape, small anterior and posterior horns, lateral horn (IML), and large white matter columns

acceptableAlternatives: Spinal cord gray and white matter
facts.quizQuestion: Identify the CNS structure shown in this transverse section with butterfly-shaped gray matter.
facts.explanation: The spinal cord in cross-section shows a characteristic H-shaped or butterfly-shaped gray matter (containing neuron cell bodies) surrounded by white matter (myelinated axon tracts). Gray matter horns: anterior (ventral) horn — lower motor neurons; posterior (dorsal) horn — sensory relay neurons; lateral horn (T1–L2, S2–S4) — autonomic preganglionic neurons. White matter columns: dorsal (posterior) columns (fine touch, proprioception), lateral columns (corticospinal, spinothalamic), anterior (ventral) columns (motor).
facts.visualDescription: Transverse cross-section of the spinal cord showing butterfly-shaped gray matter with anterior and posterior horns surrounded by white matter columns

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the diffuse lymphoid tissue found in mucosal surfaces shown here.
facts.explanation: MALT (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue). Mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) is non-encapsulated lymphoid tissue distributed throughout mucosal surfaces. It constitutes the largest component of the immune system by mass. Subtypes include GALT (gut-associated — Peyer's patches in ileum, appendix), BALT (bronchus-associated), NALT (nasal), and MALT of the urogenital tract. MALT contains B cells (especially IgA-secreting plasma cells), T cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages. IgA secretion is the primary mucosal defense mechanism.
facts.visualDescription: Histological illustration of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue in a mucosal surface showing lymphoid follicles, B and T cell zones, and overlying epithelium

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the diagram showing the lineage of blood cell development from a common precursor.
facts.explanation: Hematopoiesis occurs primarily in red bone marrow (adults: axial skeleton, proximal long bones). A pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) divides into a common myeloid progenitor (→ erythrocytes, platelets via megakaryocytes, granulocytes, monocytes) or a common lymphoid progenitor (→ B lymphocytes, T lymphocytes, NK cells). Erythropoiesis is stimulated by erythropoietin (EPO) from the kidney. Thrombopoietin drives megakaryocyte/platelet production. G-CSF drives neutrophil production.
facts.visualDescription: Branching diagram showing hematopoietic stem cells differentiating into myeloid and lymphoid lineages with all mature blood cell types at the branch ends

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the histological section of this secondary lymphoid organ showing follicles and sinuses.
facts.explanation: Lymph node histology shows: outer cortex with lymphoid follicles (primary = quiescent B cells; secondary with germinal centers = active B cell proliferation/somatic hypermutation for affinity maturation), paracortex (deep cortex — T cell zone, also where dendritic cells present antigen), and medulla (medullary cords with plasma cells + macrophages; medullary sinuses filtering lymph). Afferent lymphatics enter the convex surface; efferent lymphatics and vessels exit at the hilum. Sentinel nodes are the first to receive tumor metastases.
facts.visualDescription: Histological section of a lymph node showing the capsule, subcapsular sinus, cortical follicles with germinal centers, paracortex, medullary cords, and hilum

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the blind-ended vessels of the lymphatic system shown here alongside blood capillaries.
facts.explanation: Lymphatic capillaries are blind-ended, thin-walled vessels in the interstitium that absorb excess tissue fluid (lymph), proteins, and cellular debris. They are more permeable than blood capillaries due to overlapping endothelial cells with anchoring filaments that open inter-endothelial junctions when interstitial pressure rises ('one-way valves'). Lacteals are specialized lymphatic capillaries in intestinal villi that absorb dietary fats (chylomicrons) and form chyle. They drain into collecting lymphatics → lymph nodes → trunks → right lymphatic duct or thoracic duct → venous system.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing lymphatic capillaries alongside blood capillaries and arterioles in the interstitium, with arrows indicating fluid absorption from tissues

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the body-wide system of vessels and organs shown in this overview illustration.
facts.explanation: The lymphatic system serves three functions: fluid homeostasis (returns ~3 L/day of interstitial fluid to the venous system), fat absorption (lacteals absorb dietary fat in the gut), and immune defense. Major organs: lymph nodes (filter lymph), spleen (filters blood), thymus (T cell maturation), tonsils and MALT (mucosal defense). Lymph flows one-way via intrinsic smooth muscle contractions, extrinsic skeletal muscle compression, and respiratory pressure changes. The thoracic duct (largest lymphatic vessel) drains into the left subclavian vein.
facts.visualDescription: Full-body anterior view showing the lymphatic system including the thoracic duct, major lymph node chains (cervical, axillary, inguinal), spleen, and thymus

acceptableAlternatives: Thoracic duct and right lymphatic duct
facts.quizQuestion: Identify the large lymphatic collecting vessels draining into the venous system shown here.
facts.explanation: Lymphatic trunks and ducts. Lymphatic vessels merge into trunks (lumbar, intestinal, bronchomediastinal, subclavian, jugular), which converge into two terminal ducts. The thoracic duct (the largest) begins at the cisterna chyli (L1–L2), ascends through the thorax, and empties into the left subclavian/internal jugular junction — draining the entire body below the diaphragm, and the left arm and left side of the head/thorax. The right lymphatic duct drains only the right arm and right head/thorax into the right subclavian/jugular junction.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of the upper body showing lymphatic trunks converging into the thoracic duct (left side) and right lymphatic duct, with drainage territories indicated

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the secondary lymphoid organ shown here that filters blood and recycles erythrocytes.
facts.explanation: The spleen is the largest secondary lymphoid organ, weighing ~150 g, located in the left upper quadrant beneath the 9th–11th ribs. It has two functional compartments: white pulp (lymphoid tissue with periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths — T cells, and lymphoid follicles — B cells; mounts immune responses to blood-borne antigens) and red pulp (venous sinusoids and splenic cords — filters blood, removes aged/defective RBCs via erythrophagocytosis by macrophages, stores platelets). Splenectomy increases susceptibility to encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenzae).
facts.visualDescription: External view and internal histological diagram of the spleen showing white pulp (periarteriolar sheaths and follicles) and red pulp (sinusoids and cords)

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the primary lymphoid organ shown here that is responsible for T cell maturation.
facts.explanation: The thymus is a bilobed primary lymphoid organ in the superior mediastinum (anterior to the great vessels). Each lobe is subdivided into lobules with two zones: cortex (dense, dark — immature T cells (thymocytes) undergo positive selection for MHC recognition) and medulla (pale — mature T cells undergo negative selection to eliminate self-reactive clones; contains Hassall's corpuscles). The thymus is largest at puberty and involutes with age. Thymic aplasia (DiGeorge syndrome) causes T cell deficiency.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of the thymus showing bilobed structure, cortex with dense thymocytes, medulla with Hassall's corpuscles, and lobule organization

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the lymphoid tissue forming the immune ring at the entrance to the pharynx.
facts.explanation: Waldeyer's ring is a ring of lymphoid tissue guarding the entrance to the respiratory and digestive tracts: palatine tonsils (lateral oropharynx — most commonly removed in tonsillectomy), pharyngeal tonsil (adenoid — posterior nasopharynx; enlarges in children causing nasal obstruction), lingual tonsil (base of tongue), and tubal tonsils (around Eustachian tube openings). All are MALT components covered by epithelium with crypts that trap antigens. They initiate mucosal immune responses without capsules.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram or illustration showing the tonsillar ring in the pharynx: palatine tonsils, adenoid, and lingual tonsil locations

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the two structural layers of the dermis shown in this histological section.
facts.explanation: The dermis has two layers. The papillary dermis (superficial, ~20% of dermis thickness) consists of loose areolar connective tissue with thin collagen and elastic fibers, contains dermal papillae (increasing epidermal contact), capillary loops (nourishing avascular epidermis), and free nerve endings (Meissner's corpuscles for light touch). The reticular dermis (deep, ~80%) consists of dense irregular connective tissue with thick collagen bundles, contains hair follicles, sweat glands, sebaceous glands, Pacinian corpuscles (deep pressure/vibration), and Ruffini endings (stretch).
facts.visualDescription: Histological section of skin showing the papillary dermis (loose connective tissue with dermal papillae) and reticular dermis (dense irregular connective tissue) below the epidermis

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the different cell types found within the epidermis shown in this diagram.
facts.explanation: The epidermis contains four main cell types: Keratinocytes (~90%) — synthesize keratin (structural protein), undergo terminal differentiation from stratum basale to stratum corneum, connected by desmosomes. Melanocytes (~8%) — neural crest-derived, located in stratum basale, produce melanin in melanosomes transferred to keratinocytes for UV protection. Langerhans cells (~2%) — bone marrow-derived dendritic cells in stratum spinosum, antigen-presenting cells of the skin. Merkel cells (<1%) — mechanoreceptors in stratum basale, slow-adapting type I, for fine touch discrimination.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of the epidermis highlighting the four cell types: keratinocytes, melanocytes, Langerhans cells, and Merkel cells with their characteristic appearances and locations

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the detailed cellular architecture of the outermost body covering shown here.
facts.explanation: Detailed epidermal cellular anatomy shows keratinocytes in progressive stages of keratinization (cornification). In the stratum basale, mitotically active cells are attached to the basement membrane by hemidesmosomes. As cells move upward, they accumulate keratin intermediate filaments. In the stratum spinosum, cells are connected by desmosomes (giving the spiny appearance in fixed preparations). Lamellar granules (membrane-coating granules) are prominent in stratum granulosum — they secrete lipids (ceramides) that form the water-impermeable epidermal barrier.
facts.visualDescription: High-magnification diagram of epidermal cells showing ultrastructural features: desmosomes, tonofilaments, lamellar granules, and basement membrane attachments

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the five layers of the outermost skin layer shown in this histological diagram.
facts.explanation: The epidermis has five layers (from deep to superficial): Stratum basale (germinativum) — single layer of mitotically active columnar keratinocytes, contains melanocytes and Merkel cells. Stratum spinosum — several layers of polygonal keratinocytes with prominent desmosomes, contains Langerhans cells. Stratum granulosum — 3–5 layers with keratohyalin granules and lamellar granules. Stratum lucidum — present only in thick skin (palms, soles), translucent layer of dead cells. Stratum corneum — 15–30 layers of anucleate, dead cornified cells (corneocytes) — the barrier layer.
facts.visualDescription: Histological diagram of thick skin epidermis labeling all five strata from stratum basale through stratum corneum

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the specialized pigment-producing cells shown in this skin diagram.
facts.explanation: Melanocytes are neural crest-derived cells residing in the stratum basale. They synthesize melanin (from tyrosine via tyrosinase) in organelles called melanosomes. Melanocytes donate melanosomes to surrounding keratinocytes via cytoplasmic dendrites ('melanocyte unit': 1 melanocyte serves ~36 keratinocytes). Melanin forms a supranuclear cap over keratinocyte nuclei, shielding DNA from UV radiation. Two types: eumelanin (brown-black) and pheomelanin (yellow-red). Melanocyte count is equal across races; skin color varies due to melanosome size, number, and distribution.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing melanocytes in the stratum basale with long dendritic processes transferring melanosomes to surrounding keratinocytes

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ system shown in this comprehensive cross-sectional illustration.
facts.explanation: A comprehensive skin section shows three main layers: epidermis (stratified squamous keratinized epithelium — barrier and sensory function), dermis (connective tissue — structural support, vasculature, nerves, appendages), and hypodermis/subcutis (adipose connective tissue — insulation, energy storage, shock absorption). Skin appendages include hair follicles with arrector pili muscles, sebaceous glands (holocrine, secrete sebum), eccrine sweat glands (merocrine, thermoregulation), and apocrine sweat glands (axillary/genital, modified by bacteria into odor compounds). Nails are specialized keratinized structures.
facts.visualDescription: Detailed cross-sectional illustration of skin showing the epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis with hair follicles, sebaceous glands, sweat glands, blood vessels, and sensory receptors

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Abdominal aorta?
facts.explanation: This is the abdominal aorta — the section of the aorta below the diaphragm that supplies blood to the abdominal organs. It begins at the aortic hiatus (T12) and bifurcates into the common iliac arteries at L4.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Aorta?
facts.explanation: This is the aorta — the largest artery in the body, arising from the left ventricle. It carries oxygenated blood and distributes it to the systemic circulation via its many branches.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Aortic valve?
facts.explanation: This is the aortic valve — the semilunar valve between the left ventricle and the aorta. It has three cusps and prevents backflow of blood into the left ventricle during diastole. This image depicts aortic regurgitation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Arterial system?
facts.explanation: This is the arterial system — the network of arteries that distributes oxygenated blood from the heart to the body's tissues. Arteries have thick muscular walls to withstand high pressure from cardiac output.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Arterial circulation?
facts.explanation: This is the arterial circulation — the full-body distribution of arteries carrying oxygenated blood. The aorta branches into successively smaller arteries reaching every organ and tissue.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Artery wall layers?
facts.explanation: This shows the artery wall layers — tunica intima (inner endothelium), tunica media (smooth muscle), and tunica adventitia (outer connective tissue). The thick media gives arteries their elastic, pressure-resistant properties.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Deep venous system?
facts.explanation: This is the deep venous system — the network of deep veins in the lower limbs that carries deoxygenated blood toward the heart. Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) can occur in these vessels.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Heart?
facts.explanation: This is the heart — the muscular organ that pumps blood through the circulatory system. It weighs 250–350 g, beats ~100,000 times per day, and is located in the mediastinum tilted slightly to the left.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Cardiac conduction system?
facts.explanation: This is the cardiac conduction system — the electrical pathway that coordinates heartbeats. It includes the SA node (pacemaker), AV node, Bundle of His, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Heart (cross-section)?
facts.explanation: This is a cross-section of the heart showing the four chambers (right/left atria and ventricles) and the valves between them. The interventricular septum separates the two ventricles.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Heart chambers?
facts.explanation: This four-chamber view of the heart shows the right atrium and ventricle (pulmonary circuit) on the right, and the left atrium and ventricle (systemic circuit) on the left. The left ventricle has a thicker wall due to higher pressure demands.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Heart wall layers?
facts.explanation: This shows the heart wall layers — the outer epicardium (visceral pericardium), the thick muscular myocardium, and the inner endocardium lining the chambers. The pericardial sac surrounds the heart.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Myocardium?
facts.explanation: This is the myocardium — the thick muscular middle layer of the heart wall composed of cardiac muscle cells. It generates the contractile force needed to pump blood, and is thickest in the left ventricle.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Heart valves?
facts.explanation: This shows the four heart valves: the tricuspid (right AV), mitral/bicuspid (left AV), pulmonary semilunar, and aortic semilunar valves. They ensure one-way blood flow through the cardiac chambers.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Coronary arteries?
facts.explanation: These are the coronary arteries — the blood vessels supplying oxygen and nutrients to the heart muscle. The left coronary artery (LCA) and right coronary artery (RCA) arise from the aortic root just above the aortic valve.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Vein?
facts.explanation: This is a vein — a blood vessel that returns deoxygenated blood to the heart. Veins have thinner walls and larger lumens than arteries, and contain one-way valves (especially in the limbs) to prevent backflow.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Bile secretion?
facts.explanation: This illustrates bile secretion — bile is produced by hepatocytes in the liver, stored and concentrated in the gallbladder, and released into the duodenum via the common bile duct to emulsify dietary fats.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Colon (large intestine)?
facts.explanation: This is the colon (large intestine) — the final section of the GI tract, approximately 1.5 m long. It absorbs water and electrolytes, forming solid waste. Its sections are: cecum, ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid colon, and rectum.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Colon cross-section?
facts.explanation: This is a cross-section of the colon showing its wall layers: mucosa (with goblet cells), submucosa, muscularis externa (inner circular + outer longitudinal), and serosa. The inner mucosa lacks villi, unlike the small intestine.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Digestive system?
facts.explanation: This is the complete digestive system — the gastrointestinal tract from mouth to anus, plus accessory organs (liver, gallbladder, pancreas). It breaks down food, absorbs nutrients, and eliminates waste.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Duodenum?
facts.explanation: This is the duodenum — the first and shortest segment of the small intestine (~25 cm, C-shaped). It receives chyme from the stomach, bile from the common bile duct, and pancreatic enzymes, making it the primary site of chemical digestion.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Liver?
facts.explanation: This is the liver — the largest internal organ (~1.5 kg), located in the right upper quadrant. It produces bile, detoxifies blood, synthesizes plasma proteins, stores glycogen, and performs over 500 metabolic functions.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Liver (cirrhosis)?
facts.explanation: This shows a liver with cirrhosis — diffuse scarring (fibrosis) and regenerative nodules that replace normal liver architecture. Causes include chronic alcohol use, hepatitis B/C, and NASH. It leads to portal hypertension and liver failure.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Stomach (overview)?
facts.explanation: This is the stomach — the J-shaped muscular organ between the esophagus and small intestine. It churns food with hydrochloric acid (pH 1.5–3.5) and pepsin, producing chyme. Regions: cardia, fundus, body, pylorus.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Adrenal gland?
facts.explanation: This is the adrenal (suprarenal) gland — a triangular gland sitting atop each kidney. The cortex produces cortisol, aldosterone, and androgens; the medulla produces adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Adrenal gland cell types?
facts.explanation: This shows the adrenal gland zones from outside in: zona glomerulosa (aldosterone), zona fasciculata (cortisol), zona reticularis (androgens), and the central medulla (epinephrine/norepinephrine). Mnemonic: GFR — salt, sugar, sex.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Islet of Langerhans?
facts.explanation: This is an islet of Langerhans — a cluster of endocrine cells scattered throughout the pancreas. Alpha cells secrete glucagon (raises blood glucose), beta cells secrete insulin (lowers blood glucose). Type 1 diabetes destroys beta cells.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Pancreas?
facts.explanation: This is the pancreas — a glandular organ with both exocrine (digestive enzymes: lipase, amylase, proteases) and endocrine (insulin and glucagon from islets of Langerhans) functions. It lies behind the stomach in the retroperitoneum.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Pancreas (cross-section)?
facts.explanation: This cross-section of the pancreas shows its internal structure including the main pancreatic duct (duct of Wirsung), acinar cells (exocrine), and islets of Langerhans (endocrine). The duct joins the common bile duct at the ampulla of Vater.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Thyroid gland?
facts.explanation: This is the thyroid gland — a butterfly-shaped gland in the anterior neck, wrapped around the trachea. It produces T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine) regulating metabolism, and calcitonin regulating calcium levels.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the First-degree burn?
facts.explanation: This is a first-degree (superficial) burn — affecting only the epidermis. It causes redness, pain, and minor swelling but no blisters. It heals within 3–5 days without scarring. A typical sunburn is a first-degree burn.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Second-degree burn?
facts.explanation: This is a second-degree (partial-thickness) burn — extending through the epidermis into the dermis. It causes blistering, intense pain, and a wet shiny appearance. Superficial second-degree burns heal in 2–3 weeks; deep ones may need skin grafting.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Third-degree burn?
facts.explanation: This is a third-degree (full-thickness) burn — destroying both the epidermis and dermis, and possibly deeper tissues. The area appears white, brown, or black and leathery. Nerve destruction means it is often painless. Requires skin grafting.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Normal skin?
facts.explanation: This shows normal, healthy skin with an intact epidermis and dermis. Normal skin serves as a barrier against pathogens, regulates temperature, provides sensation, and synthesizes vitamin D — contrasted with the burn depth series.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Dermatome map?
facts.explanation: This is a dermatome map — showing the sensory distribution of each spinal nerve on the body surface. Each dermatome corresponds to a spinal level (C2–S5). Used to localize disc herniations and spinal cord injuries clinically.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Epidermis?
facts.explanation: This is the epidermis — the outermost skin layer with 5 strata (from deep to superficial): stratum basale, spinosum, granulosum, lucidum (only in thick skin), and corneum. Keratinocytes produced in the basale migrate upward over ~4 weeks.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Hair follicle?
facts.explanation: This is a hair follicle — the skin structure that produces hair. It includes the hair shaft, sebaceous gland (sebum production), and arrector pili muscle (causes goosebumps). Hair grows at ~1 cm/month in the anagen (growth) phase.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Integumentary system?
facts.explanation: This is the integumentary system — comprising the epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis (subcutaneous layer), plus appendages (hair, nails, sweat glands, sebaceous glands). It is the body's largest organ system at ~2 m² surface area.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Langerhans cell?
facts.explanation: This is a Langerhans cell — a dendritic antigen-presenting cell residing in the epidermis. It detects pathogens and allergens, then migrates to lymph nodes to activate T lymphocytes. Key to contact hypersensitivity reactions.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Melanocyte?
facts.explanation: This is a melanocyte — a melanin-producing cell located in the stratum basale of the epidermis. Melanin determines skin and hair color and provides UV protection. Melanocytes arise from neural crest cells and connect to ~36 keratinocytes each.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Lymph node?
facts.explanation: This is a lymph node — a bean-shaped secondary lymphoid organ (0.1–2.5 cm) that filters lymph. The cortex contains B cell follicles; the paracortex has T cells. About 600–700 lymph nodes are distributed throughout the body.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Lymphatic circulation?
facts.explanation: This is the lymphatic circulation — the one-way network that collects interstitial fluid from tissues as lymph and returns it to the bloodstream via the thoracic duct (left side) and right lymphatic duct. Also transports dietary fats absorbed in the intestine.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Lymphatic system?
facts.explanation: This is the complete lymphatic system — including lymph nodes, lymphatic vessels, and lymphoid organs (spleen, thymus, tonsils, Peyer's patches). It maintains fluid balance, absorbs dietary fats, and houses immune cells.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Lymphatic vessel?
facts.explanation: This is a lymphatic vessel — a thin-walled vessel with one-way valves that carries lymph (filtered interstitial fluid) back to the venous system. Lymphatic capillaries are more permeable than blood capillaries, allowing protein uptake.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Spleen?
facts.explanation: This is the spleen — the largest lymphoid organ (~150 g), located in the left upper quadrant (LUQ) behind the 9th–11th ribs. It filters blood, destroys old red blood cells, and stores platelets and monocytes. Rich blood supply makes it vulnerable to traumatic rupture.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Actin filament?
facts.explanation: This is the actin filament (thin filament) — a double-helical polymer of G-actin monomers in the sarcomere. Myosin heads bind to actin during the cross-bridge cycle, pulling the Z-discs closer together and shortening the sarcomere.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Cardiac muscle cells?
facts.explanation: These are cardiac muscle cells (cardiomyocytes) — branched, striated, involuntary muscle cells connected by intercalated discs. The discs contain gap junctions for electrical coupling and desmosomes for mechanical strength, allowing the heart to contract in unison.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Muscle contraction (sliding filament)?
facts.explanation: This illustrates the sliding filament mechanism of muscle contraction — myosin heads (thick filaments) bind actin (thin filaments), pivot, and pull them inward, shortening the sarcomere. ATP powers each cross-bridge cycle; calcium release from the SR triggers it.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Smooth muscle (arteriole)?
facts.explanation: This shows smooth muscle in an arteriole wall — involuntary, non-striated muscle cells arranged circumferentially. Sympathetic stimulation causes vasoconstriction; relaxation causes vasodilation. Controls blood pressure and tissue perfusion.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Brain (lateral view)?
facts.explanation: This is the lateral view of the brain showing the four cerebral lobes: frontal (motor/executive function), parietal (somatosensory), temporal (hearing/memory), and occipital (vision). The lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure) separates the temporal from the frontal and parietal lobes.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Brain (medial view)?
facts.explanation: This is the medial (sagittal) view of the brain revealing the corpus callosum (connecting hemispheres), cingulate gyrus, thalamus, hypothalamus, brainstem, and cerebellum. The medial view exposes structures hidden in the lateral view.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Cerebral arteries?
facts.explanation: This shows the cerebral arteries — the brain receives blood from the internal carotid arteries (anterior circulation) and vertebral arteries (posterior circulation), which unite at the Circle of Willis (circulus arteriosus) at the base of the brain.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Brainstem (overview)?
facts.explanation: This is the brainstem — comprising the midbrain (cranial nerves III–IV), pons (V–VIII), and medulla oblongata (IX–XII). It controls vital functions (breathing, heart rate, blood pressure), relays signals between the cerebrum and spinal cord, and contains the reticular activating system.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Brain functional zones?
facts.explanation: This shows brain functional zones — cortical areas mapped by function. Key areas include the primary motor cortex (precentral gyrus), somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus), Broca's area (speech production), Wernicke's area (speech comprehension), and visual cortex (occipital lobe).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Cerebral cortex regions?
facts.explanation: This shows the cerebral cortex regions including Brodmann areas and functional regions. The cortex is the outer gray matter layer (~2–4 mm thick) with ~16 billion neurons organized into six layers, responsible for higher cognitive functions.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Hippocampus?
facts.explanation: This is the hippocampus — a seahorse-shaped structure in the medial temporal lobe essential for converting short-term memories to long-term memories (consolidation) and spatial navigation. Bilateral destruction causes anterograde amnesia (Korsakoff syndrome, H.M. case).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Neuron?
facts.explanation: This is a neuron — the fundamental signal-transmitting cell of the nervous system. It consists of dendrites (receive signals), a cell body (soma with nucleus), axon (transmits signals), myelin sheath (speeds conduction), and axon terminals (release neurotransmitters).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Neuron structure?
facts.explanation: This detailed view of neuron structure shows the cell body (soma), nucleus, rough ER (Nissl substance), dendrites for input, and axon hillock (action potential initiation zone). Myelinated segments between nodes of Ranvier enable saltatory conduction.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Bronchus?
