Inner hair cells (3,500) are the primary sensory transducers. Outer hair cells (12,000) amplify basilar membrane motion. Hair cells do NOT regenerate in mammals — aminoglycoside antibiotics and cisplatin are ototoxic because they destroy hair cells.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the maculae (vestibular equilibrium)?
facts.explanation: The maculae are sensory patches in the utricle (horizontal plane, detects horizontal acceleration and head tilt) and saccule (vertical plane, detects vertical acceleration). They consist of hair cells embedded in a gelatinous otolithic membrane containing calcium carbonate crystals (otoliths/otoconia). Gravity and linear acceleration shift the otolithic membrane relative to hair cells, bending stereocilia → signal via vestibular branch of CN VIII. Dislodged otoconia entering the semicircular canals cause benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) — treated with the Epley maneuver.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the olfaction (smell pathway)?
facts.explanation: Olfactory receptor neurons (ORNs) in the nasal roof's olfactory epithelium have cilia bearing G-protein-coupled olfactory receptors (~400 functional types in humans). Odorant binding → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → cyclic nucleotide-gated channels → depolarization. Unmyelinated ORN axons bundle as the olfactory nerve (CN I — the only cranial nerve with neurogenesis capability), pass through the cribriform plate of the ethmoid, and synapse in the olfactory bulb (glomeruli). Signal travels to piriform cortex and amygdala without a thalamic relay — unique among sensory systems. Anosmia can be an early sign of Parkinson's disease or COVID-19.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the sensory receptor types?
facts.explanation: Sensory receptors are classified by adequate stimulus: mechanoreceptors (touch, pressure, vibration, proprioception — Meissner's, Pacinian, Merkel, Ruffini corpuscles; muscle spindles), thermoreceptors (temperature), nociceptors (pain; free nerve endings), photoreceptors (light; rods and cones), and chemoreceptors (taste, smell, oxygen/CO2 levels). By location: exteroceptors (external stimuli), interoceptors (visceral), proprioceptors (body position). By structure: encapsulated (Meissner's, Pacinian) vs. free nerve endings. Rapidly vs. slowly adapting receptors govern which sensations persist vs. habituate.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the rods and cones?
facts.explanation: Rods (~120 million): cylindrical outer segments containing stacks of discs loaded with rhodopsin (opsin + 11-cis retinal); mediate scotopic (dim-light, black-and-white) vision; absent from fovea; abundant in periphery. Cones (~6 million): conical outer segments; mediate photopic (bright-light, color) vision; densely packed in the fovea; three types (S/M/L) for trichromatic color vision. Phototransduction: light isomerizes retinal → conformation change in opsin → activates transducin (G-protein) → phosphodiesterase → hydrolyzes cGMP → Na+ channels close → hyperpolarization → less glutamate released onto bipolar cells.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the semicircular canals?
facts.explanation: The three semicircular canals (anterior/superior, posterior, lateral/horizontal) lie in three orthogonal planes, allowing detection of rotational acceleration in any direction. Each canal has an ampulla at one end containing the crista ampullaris — a sensory ridge with hair cells embedded in a gelatinous cupula. Rotational head movement causes endolymph to lag behind (inertia), deflecting the cupula and bending hair cells. Signal travels via vestibular branch of CN VIII. The lateral canal is tested by the caloric test (cold/warm water irrigation of external ear canal). Lesions cause nystagmus and vertigo.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the sound transmission and ossicles?
facts.explanation: The three ossicles — malleus (hammer), incus (anvil), and stapes (stirrup) — form a mechanical lever chain transmitting tympanic membrane vibration to the oval window. The stapes footplate seats in the oval window, compressing cochlear perilymph. The ossicular lever system provides ~1.3× mechanical advantage; the large-to-small area ratio (tympanic membrane:oval window ≈ 17:1) provides impedance matching between air and fluid. This amplifies pressure ~22× total. The stapedius (CN VII) and tensor tympani (CN V3) muscles dampen ossicle movement to protect against loud sounds. Conductive hearing loss results from ossicular dysfunction (e.g., otosclerosis — abnormal bone growth fixing the stapes).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the tongue papillae?
facts.explanation: Four papillae types: (1) Filiform — most numerous, no taste buds, provide texture/sensation (CN V3); (2) Fungiform — mushroom-shaped, scattered across anterior 2/3, contain taste buds (CN VII via chorda tympani); (3) Circumvallate/vallate — 7–12 large papillae forming a V-row at the junction of anterior 2/3 and posterior 1/3, contain hundreds of taste buds (CN IX); (4) Foliate — ridges on lateral tongue, some taste buds (CN IX). Five basic tastes: sweet, salty, sour, bitter, umami. Taste receptor cells (TRCs) in taste buds are chemoreceptors; they synapse onto CN VII, IX, and X fibers.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the skeletal muscle anatomy?
facts.explanation: Skeletal muscle is the voluntary, striated muscle attached to bone via tendons. Structural hierarchy: muscle → fascicles (bundles of fibers wrapped in perimysium) → muscle fibers (cells, wrapped in endomysium) → myofibrils → sarcomeres (functional units). The whole muscle is wrapped in epimysium. Each muscle fiber is a multinucleate syncytium formed by fusion of myoblasts. Motor units (one motor neuron + all muscle fibers it innervates) are the basic functional unit of force production. Large muscles have large motor units (coarse control); hand/eye muscles have small motor units (fine control).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the muscle contraction mechanism?
facts.explanation: Muscle contraction follows the sliding filament theory (Huxley, 1954): thick filaments (myosin) and thin filaments (actin) overlap and slide past each other without changing length. The cross-bridge cycle: (1) ATP binds myosin head → releases from actin; (2) ATP hydrolysis → myosin head cocks (high-energy state); (3) Myosin attaches to actin; (4) Power stroke — Pi release → myosin head pivots, pulling actin; (5) New ATP binds to restart. Trigger: Ca2+ release from SR binds troponin C → tropomyosin shifts → exposes actin binding sites. Rigor mortis occurs when ATP is depleted (step 1 cannot occur).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the muscle fascicle?
facts.explanation: A fascicle is a bundle of skeletal muscle fibers (cells) wrapped in perimysium (dense irregular connective tissue). Each individual fiber within is wrapped in endomysium; the whole muscle is wrapped in epimysium, continuous with the tendon. Fascicular arrangement determines muscle architecture and force-velocity characteristics: parallel (strap) muscles — long fibers, long ROM, less force; pennate muscles (unipennate, bipennate, multipennate) — shorter fibers at an angle to the tendon, more fibers per unit volume, more force but shorter ROM. The biceps brachii is a fusiform (spindle-shaped) muscle.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the muscle fiber?
facts.explanation: A skeletal muscle fiber (cell) is a large, multinucleate syncytium (formed by myoblast fusion during development) up to 30 cm long, with peripherally located nuclei. The sarcolemma (plasma membrane) has T-tubule invaginations that carry action potentials deep into the cell. The sarcoplasm contains abundant mitochondria and the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) — specialized ER that stores and releases Ca2+. Myofibrils are the contractile elements; each myofibril is ~1–2 µm in diameter and contains sarcomeres in series. The striated appearance results from regular A-bands (myosin+actin overlap, dark) and I-bands (actin only, light).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the cranial fossae?
facts.explanation: The internal base of the skull has three stepped fossae: (1) Anterior cranial fossa — frontal and ethmoid bones; houses frontal lobes and olfactory bulbs; cribriform plate transmits CN I; (2) Middle cranial fossa — sphenoid and temporal bones; houses temporal lobes; key foramina: optic canal (CN II, ophthalmic artery), superior orbital fissure (CN III/IV/VI/V1), foramen rotundum (V2), foramen ovale (V3), foramen spinosum (middle meningeal artery — laceration causes epidural hematoma); (3) Posterior cranial fossa — occipital and temporal bones; houses cerebellum and brainstem; foramen magnum, internal acoustic meatus (CN VII/VIII), jugular foramen (CN IX/X/XI).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the ethmoid bone?
facts.explanation: The ethmoid bone is an unpaired, delicate midline bone at the roof of the nasal cavity. Key features: (1) Cribriform plate — horizontal plate with many small foramina (cribriform = 'sieve-like') transmitting CN I (olfactory nerve) fibers from nasal epithelium to olfactory bulb. Fractures here cause CSF rhinorrhea and anosmia; (2) Crista galli — superior vertical projection anchoring the falx cerebri; (3) Perpendicular plate — forms the upper nasal septum; (4) Lateral masses (labyrinths) with superior and middle nasal conchae; ethmoid air cells (paranasal sinuses). The ethmoid forms part of the medial orbital wall (lamina papyracea — extremely thin, 'paper-thin').

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the hyoid bone?
facts.explanation: The hyoid bone is a U-shaped bone suspended in the anterior neck at the level of C3, held in place by muscles and ligaments — it has NO bony articulations. It consists of a body, two greater horns, and two lesser horns. The hyoid serves as an attachment point for muscles of the floor of the mouth, tongue, pharynx, and larynx. It is critical for swallowing and speech. Fracture of the hyoid is uncommon due to its protected position but is classically associated with strangulation (manual or ligature) — a key forensic finding. In embalming and laryngoscopy, hyoid position is a surgical landmark.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the mandible?
facts.explanation: The mandible (lower jaw) is the only moveable skull bone and the largest, strongest facial bone. Key features: body (horizontal portion bearing the lower teeth), two rami (vertical portions), mandibular angle (junction of body and ramus), condylar process (condyle articulates with temporal bone at the TMJ — only true joint of the skull), and coronoid process (attachment of temporalis muscle). The mandibular foramen (on medial ramus) transmits the inferior alveolar nerve and artery — target for dental blocks (inferior alveolar nerve block). The mental foramen (lateral body) transmits the mental nerve (V3 branch). Mandible fracture is the second most common facial fracture.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the maxilla?
facts.explanation: The maxillae (paired) fuse at the intermaxillary suture to form the upper jaw. The maxilla contributes to: (1) alveolar process — houses upper teeth; (2) palatine process — anterior 3/4 of hard palate (failure to fuse causes cleft palate); (3) orbital floor and inferior orbital rim; (4) anterior nasal floor; (5) medial orbital wall. The infraorbital foramen transmits the infraorbital nerve (V2 branch) — anesthetized for upper lip/anterior teeth blocks. The maxillary sinus (largest paranasal sinus) is housed within the maxilla — the largest sinus and most commonly affected by sinusitis. Le Fort fractures involve the maxilla.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the nasal cavity (skeletal)?
facts.explanation: The bony nasal cavity is framed by: nasal bones (bridge), maxillae (floor and lateral walls), ethmoid (roof — cribriform plate, and superior/middle conchae), inferior nasal conchae (separate bones, largest conchae), vomer (inferior nasal septum), and perpendicular plate of ethmoid (superior septum). The three pairs of conchae (superior, middle, inferior) create turbulence to warm and humidify air and dramatically increase mucosal surface area. Beneath each concha is a meatus: inferior meatus receives the nasolacrimal duct (tearing into the nose). Nasal polyps most commonly arise from the middle meatus.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the orbital bones?
facts.explanation: The bony orbit is formed by seven bones (mnemonic: 'My Flat Zeal Surprises Posh Elderly Ladies'): Maxilla (floor and medial wall), Frontal (roof), Zygomatic (lateral wall and floor), Sphenoid (posterior lateral wall and apex — optic canal and SOF), Palatine (posteromedial floor), Ethmoid (medial wall — lamina papyracea, very thin), Lacrimal (anteromedial wall — lacrimal fossa for lacrimal sac). Key openings: optic canal (CN II, ophthalmic artery), superior orbital fissure (CN III/IV/VI/V1, ophthalmic veins), inferior orbital fissure (V2, infraorbital vessels). The orbital floor is the weakest wall — blowout fractures here.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the paranasal sinuses (skeletal)?
facts.explanation: The four paired paranasal sinuses: (1) Maxillary — largest, in maxilla; drain into middle meatus via ostium high on medial wall (poor gravitational drainage → most prone to infection); roof is floor of orbit; floor is alveolar process (upper molar roots may project into it); (2) Frontal — in frontal bone; drain via frontonasal duct into middle meatus; (3) Ethmoid — anterior/posterior cells between orbits; complex drainage; (4) Sphenoid — in sphenoid body; drain into sphenoethmoidal recess above superior concha. All lined with pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium. Functions: lighten skull, warm/humidify air, resonate voice, cushion facial trauma.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the anterior skull (frontal view)?
facts.explanation: The anterior skull shows: cranium (neurocranium) — frontal bone (forehead, with frontal sinuses); facial bones — nasal bones (bridge), zygomatic bones (cheekbones), maxillae (upper jaw), mandible (lower jaw, only moveable skull bone), and the orbits. Sutures visible anteriorly: coronal suture (frontal-parietal), frontonasal suture. Key foramina visible anteriorly: supraorbital foramen/notch (V1 branch, supraorbital nerve), infraorbital foramen (V2, infraorbital nerve), mental foramen (V3 branch, mental nerve). The skull has 22 bones total: 8 cranial and 14 facial.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the skull base (external)?
facts.explanation: The external skull base (inferior view) shows key structures: occipital bone (foramen magnum — transmits medulla oblongata, vertebral arteries, CN XI spinal roots; occipital condyles articulate with C1/atlas), temporal bone (styloid process — attachment of styloid muscles; stylomastoid foramen transmits CN VII exiting skull; carotid canal; jugular foramen — CN IX/X/XI, sigmoid sinus), sphenoid (pterygoid plates, foramen ovale — CN V3, foramen spinosum — middle meningeal artery), and the hard palate (maxilla anteriorly, palatine posteriorly). The mastoid process (temporal bone) is the posterior attachment of the SCM.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the lateral skull?
facts.explanation: The lateral skull shows the major cranial bones and sutures: frontal (anterior), parietal (superior and lateral), temporal (lateral — contains external acoustic meatus, mastoid process, zygomatic process), occipital (posterior), sphenoid (pterion region). Sutures: coronal (frontal-parietal), squamous/squamosal (temporal-parietal), lambdoid (parietal-occipital). The pterion (H-shaped junction of frontal, parietal, temporal, sphenoid) is the thinnest point of the skull — the middle meningeal artery runs here; trauma → epidural hematoma (biconvex on CT, arterial bleed, lucid interval). The zygomatic arch: temporal process of zygomatic + zygomatic process of temporal.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the skull parts overview?
facts.explanation: The skull has 22 bones: Cranial bones (8) — frontal (1), parietal (2), temporal (2), occipital (1), sphenoid (1, 'keystone' of skull base), ethmoid (1); Facial bones (14) — mandible (1), maxillae (2), zygomatic (2), nasal (2), lacrimal (2), palatine (2), inferior nasal conchae (2), vomer (1). The cranial bones form the neurocranium protecting the brain; facial bones form the viscerocranium. At birth, the skull has fontanelles (fibrous membranes between incompletely ossified bones) — anterior fontanelle closes at ~18 months, posterior at ~3 months.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the posterior skull?
facts.explanation: The posterior skull view features: occipital bone — the large posterior cranial bone bearing the external occipital protuberance (EOP/inion, palpable midline bump — nuchal ligament attachment), superior and inferior nuchal lines (attachment of suboccipital/neck muscles), and the foramen magnum (inferiorly). The lambdoid suture (inverted V-shape) separates the parietal bones from the occipital bone. Parietal foramina (emissary veins). The asterion is the landmark at the junction of lambdoid, occipitomastoid, and parietomastoid sutures — overlies the transverse-sigmoid sinus junction. Wormian (sutural) bones are accessory ossification centers within sutures; excess numbers suggest osteogenesis imperfecta.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the skull sagittal section?
facts.explanation: The midsagittal skull section reveals internal structures invisible from outside: (1) nasal septum — perpendicular plate of ethmoid (superior) + vomer (inferior) + septal cartilage (anterior); (2) hard palate — palatine process of maxilla (anterior 3/4) + horizontal plate of palatine bone (posterior 1/4); (3) internal occipital protuberance; (4) sella turcica — depression in sphenoid body housing the pituitary gland; (5) anterior cranial fossa floor (cribriform plate of ethmoid); (6) clivus — sloping posterior part of sphenoid/occipital bodies, where basilar artery lies against it.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the sphenoid bone?
facts.explanation: The sphenoid is an unpaired 'keystone' bone articulating with all other cranial bones. Bat/butterfly shape: body (center — contains sphenoid sinuses; sella turcica houses pituitary; optic chiasm sits in optic groove), greater wings (form middle cranial fossa floor, pterion region, infratemporal and temporal fossae), lesser wings (form posterior part of anterior cranial fossa and superior orbital fissure — CN III/IV/VI/V1), and pterygoid processes (hang down — attachment for pterygoid muscles of mastication; medial pterygoid plate ends at pterygoid hamulus). Key foramina: optic canal (lesser wing), superior orbital fissure (between greater/lesser wings), foramen rotundum (V2), foramen ovale (V3), foramen spinosum (middle meningeal artery).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the temporal bone?
facts.explanation: The temporal bone consists of four parts: squamous (forms lateral skull wall, zygomatic process), petrous ('rocky' medial part — hardest bone in the body; houses cochlea and semicircular canals within the bony labyrinth; contains internal acoustic meatus transmitting CN VII and CN VIII), mastoid (posterior — mastoid process with air cells that communicate with middle ear; mastoiditis is a complication of otitis media), and tympanic (surrounds external acoustic meatus). CN VII exits via the stylomastoid foramen (between styloid and mastoid). The carotid canal transmits the internal carotid artery. Temporal bone fractures: longitudinal (90%, tympanic membrane tear, conductive hearing loss) vs transverse (sensorineural hearing loss, CN VII palsy).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the male pelvis?
facts.explanation: The male pelvis differs from the female in several clinically important ways: (1) Male pelvic inlet is heart-shaped (narrower); female is oval/round; (2) Male pubic arch angle <90° (acute); female >90° (obtuse, called subpubic angle); (3) Male ischial spines are more prominent and closer together (smaller interspinous diameter); (4) Male sacrum is narrower and more curved; (5) Overall, male pelvis is narrower, deeper, and heavier with more pronounced muscle markings. The pelvis consists of two hip bones (each = ilium + ischium + pubis, fused at triradiate cartilage), the sacrum, and the coccyx. The pelvic outlet is bounded by the coccyx (posteriorly), ischial tuberosities (laterally), and pubic arch (anteriorly).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the breast and lactation anatomy?
facts.explanation: The breast (mammary gland) is a modified apocrine sweat gland consisting of 15–25 lobes of tubuloalveolar glands arranged radially around the nipple, separated by adipose tissue and Cooper's ligaments (suspensory ligaments attaching breast to overlying skin and underlying fascia). Each lobe drains via a lactiferous duct to the nipple; ducts dilate near the nipple as lactiferous sinuses. Blood supply: internal thoracic (internal mammary) artery and lateral thoracic artery. Lymphatic drainage is critical in breast cancer: ~75% drains to axillary nodes (sentinel node biopsy); medial breast drains to parasternal nodes. Prolactin (anterior pituitary) stimulates milk production; oxytocin triggers milk ejection ('let-down' reflex). Breast tissue extends to the axillary tail of Spence.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the female reproductive hormones?
facts.explanation: The female reproductive cycle is governed by a hormonal cascade: Hypothalamus → GnRH (pulsatile) → Anterior pituitary → FSH (follicle development) and LH (midcycle surge → ovulation; stimulates theca cells → androgens). Ovarian phases: Follicular phase (days 1–14) — rising estradiol from granulosa cells; Ovulation (day 14) — LH surge triggers follicle rupture; Luteal phase (days 15–28) — corpus luteum secretes progesterone + estrogen. Progesterone: prepares endometrium for implantation, inhibits further ovulation, raises basal body temperature. If no fertilization, corpus luteum degenerates → progesterone/estrogen fall → menstruation. Estrogen feedback: early follicular = negative; late follicular (high E2) = positive → LH surge.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the female reproductive system?
facts.explanation: This is an overview of the female reproductive system. The ovaries produce ova and sex hormones (estrogen, progesterone). Each ovary connects to the uterus via a fallopian tube; fertilization typically occurs in the ampulla of the fallopian tube. The uterus is the site of implantation and fetal development. The cervix is the narrow lower portion opening into the vagina, the muscular canal to the exterior.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the male reproductive system?
facts.explanation: This overview shows the male reproductive system. The testes (in the scrotum) produce sperm and testosterone. Sperm mature in the epididymis, then travel through the vas deferens during ejaculation. The seminal vesicles, prostate gland, and bulbourethral glands add secretions to form semen. The penis delivers semen into the female reproductive tract and also carries urine to the exterior via the urethra.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image depicts the hormonal and cyclic changes of the menstrual cycle?
facts.explanation: The menstrual cycle (approximately 28 days) integrates the ovarian cycle and uterine cycle, coordinated by hormones. Rising FSH stimulates follicle development (follicular phase). The LH surge triggers ovulation (~day 14). The ruptured follicle becomes the corpus luteum (luteal phase), secreting progesterone to maintain the endometrium. If no fertilization occurs, the corpus luteum regresses, progesterone falls, and menstruation begins.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image illustrates oogenesis — the development of female gametes?
facts.explanation: Oogenesis is the process of producing mature ova. Oogonia (2n) undergo mitosis before birth to form primary oocytes, which arrest in prophase I. At puberty, each menstrual cycle one primary oocyte completes meiosis I to yield a secondary oocyte (n) and a first polar body. Meiosis II completes only upon fertilization, producing the ovum (n) and a second polar body. Thus, one functional egg and up to three polar bodies are produced.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows ovarian follicle development from primordial follicle to corpus luteum?
facts.explanation: Ovarian follicle development (folliculogenesis) proceeds through distinct stages: primordial follicle → primary follicle → secondary follicle → antral (tertiary) follicle → Graafian (preovulatory) follicle. After ovulation, the ruptured follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone. If unfertilized, it degenerates into the corpus albicans (fibrous scar tissue).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a cross-section of the penis with corpora cavernosa and corpus spongiosum?
facts.explanation: The penis contains three cylinders of erectile tissue: two corpora cavernosa (dorsal pair, larger) and one corpus spongiosum (ventral, surrounds the urethra). During erection, parasympathetic stimulation causes nitric oxide release, relaxing smooth muscle in the helicine arteries, engorging the sinusoids with blood. The tunica albuginea compresses emissary veins, maintaining rigidity. The corpus spongiosum stays softer to keep the urethra patent for ejaculation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the scrotum and testes with dartos muscle and tunica vaginalis?
facts.explanation: The scrotum is a cutaneous sac housing the testes outside the abdominal cavity, keeping them ~2–3°C below core body temperature for optimal spermatogenesis. Key layers from outside in: skin → dartos muscle (wrinkles skin for thermoregulation) → external spermatic fascia → cremaster muscle (elevates testes) → tunica vaginalis (serous sac). The testis itself is covered by the tunica albuginea and divided into lobules containing seminiferous tubules.

facts.quizQuestion: Which histological image shows seminiferous tubules with Sertoli cells and Leydig cells?
facts.explanation: Seminiferous tubules are the site of spermatogenesis. The tubule wall contains Sertoli cells (supporting cells — form the blood-testis barrier, nourish developing sperm, secrete inhibin) and spermatogenic cells at various stages. Between tubules in the interstitium, Leydig cells (interstitial cells) synthesize testosterone in response to LH. Mature sperm are released into the tubule lumen and move to the rete testis, then the epididymis.

facts.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows the structure of a sperm cell with acrosome, midpiece, and tail?
facts.explanation: A spermatozoon has three main parts. The head contains the haploid nucleus (condensed DNA) capped by the acrosome — a lysosome-like vesicle with enzymes (acrosin, hyaluronidase) for penetrating the zona pellucida of the egg. The midpiece is packed with mitochondria arranged in a helix around the axoneme, providing ATP for motility. The tail (flagellum) drives propulsion via the 9+2 axonemal microtubule arrangement powered by dynein ATPase.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image illustrates spermatogenesis from spermatogonium to spermatozoa?
facts.explanation: Spermatogenesis occurs in the seminiferous tubules and takes ~74 days. Spermatogonia (2n) undergo mitosis to renew the stem cell pool and produce primary spermatocytes (2n). Each primary spermatocyte undergoes meiosis I to form two secondary spermatocytes (n), then meiosis II to form four spermatids (n). Spermatids then undergo spermiogenesis — a dramatic morphological transformation — to become mature spermatozoa. Unlike oogenesis, all four products are functional.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the uterus with fallopian tubes labeled from fimbriae to the uterine wall?
facts.explanation: The uterus is a pear-shaped muscular organ with three regions: fundus (dome above fallopian tube openings), body, and cervix. Each fallopian tube has four segments from ovary to uterus: infundibulum (with fimbriae that sweep ova in), ampulla (most common site of fertilization), isthmus, and intramural portion. The broad ligament suspends the uterus and tubes. Ciliated epithelium and smooth muscle peristalsis move the ovum toward the uterus.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the vulva — the external female genitalia?
facts.explanation: The vulva comprises the external female genitalia: mons pubis (fatty pad over pubic symphysis), labia majora (outer folds, homologous to scrotum), labia minora (inner folds, highly vascular), clitoris (erectile tissue, homologous to glans penis), vestibule (space between labia minora containing urethral and vaginal openings), Bartholin's glands (greater vestibular glands — lubricate vestibule, homologous to Cowper's glands), and Skene's glands (periurethral).

facts.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows the male HPG axis with FSH, LH, testosterone, and inhibin feedback?
facts.explanation: The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis regulates male reproduction. GnRH from the hypothalamus stimulates anterior pituitary release of FSH and LH. FSH acts on Sertoli cells to support spermatogenesis and promote inhibin secretion. LH acts on Leydig cells to stimulate testosterone synthesis. Testosterone and inhibin provide negative feedback to the hypothalamus and pituitary, maintaining homeostatic hormone levels. Testosterone drives secondary sex characteristics and anabolism.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows renal blood flow from renal artery through arcuate and interlobular vessels?
facts.explanation: Renal blood flow follows a precise pathway: renal artery → segmental arteries → interlobar arteries (between renal pyramids) → arcuate arteries (arch at corticomedullary junction) → interlobular (cortical radiate) arteries → afferent arterioles → glomerular capillaries → efferent arterioles → peritubular capillaries (cortex) and vasa recta (medulla) → venous system in reverse. The kidneys receive ~20–25% of cardiac output despite being <1% of body mass.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a kidney cross-section with cortex, medulla, renal pyramids, and calyces?
facts.explanation: A coronal kidney section reveals: renal cortex (outer, granular — contains glomeruli and convoluted tubules), renal medulla (inner, striated — contains renal pyramids with collecting ducts and loops of Henle), renal columns (cortical tissue between pyramids), renal papilla (apex of each pyramid draining into a minor calyx), minor calyces → major calyces → renal pelvis → ureter. The renal sinus is the central fatty space containing the pelvis, calyces, and vessels.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the retroperitoneal position of the kidneys relative to the spine and abdominal organs?
facts.explanation: The kidneys are retroperitoneal organs located on the posterior abdominal wall between T12 and L3 vertebrae. The right kidney sits slightly lower than the left (due to the liver above). Each kidney is embedded in perinephric fat within the renal fascia (Gerota's fascia). The adrenal glands sit atop each kidney. Key relations: right kidney contacts liver, duodenum, hepatic flexure of colon; left kidney contacts stomach, pancreas, spleen, splenic flexure.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows nephron blood flow with afferent/efferent arterioles, peritubular capillaries, and vasa recta?
facts.explanation: Nephron blood flow: afferent arteriole → glomerulus (high-pressure filtration) → efferent arteriole → peritubular capillaries (cortical nephrons, surrounds proximal/distal tubules for reabsorption/secretion) or vasa recta (juxtamedullary nephrons, thin-walled vessels parallel to loops of Henle enabling countercurrent exchange for urinary concentration). The two-capillary-bed arrangement (glomerulus + peritubular/vasa recta) is unique to the kidney and enables independent regulation of filtration and reabsorption.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows chordae tendineae connecting valve leaflets to papillary muscles?
facts.explanation: The chordae tendineae ('heartstrings') are fibrous cords connecting the atrioventricular valve leaflets (mitral and tricuspid) to the papillary muscles on the ventricular floor. The papillary muscles contract during systole, tensioning the chordae to prevent valve leaflet prolapse into the atrium (regurgitation). Mitral valve has two papillary muscles (anterolateral and posteromedial); tricuspid has three. Rupture of papillary muscle (e.g., post-MI) causes acute severe mitral regurgitation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image illustrates congenital heart defects including VSD, ASD, and PDA?
facts.explanation: Congenital heart defects (CHDs) are structural abnormalities present at birth. Common examples include: ventricular septal defect (VSD — most common CHD), atrial septal defect (ASD), patent ductus arteriosus (PDA — DA fails to close after birth causing L→R shunt), tetralogy of Fallot (VSD + pulmonary stenosis + overriding aorta + RVH — most common cyanotic CHD), and coarctation of the aorta. Left-to-right shunts cause pulmonary hypertension; right-to-left shunts cause cyanosis.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the coronary arteries (LAD, LCx, RCA) and cardiac veins on the heart surface?
facts.explanation: The coronary arteries arise from the aortic sinuses just above the aortic valve. The left main coronary artery divides into the left anterior descending (LAD — supplies anterior LV, IVS, anterior LV apex) and left circumflex (LCx — supplies lateral and posterior LV). The right coronary artery (RCA) supplies the right heart and, in right-dominant systems (70%), gives the posterior descending artery (PDA). The cardiac veins drain into the coronary sinus → right atrium.

facts.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows the dual circulatory system with separate pulmonary and systemic loops?
facts.explanation: Mammals have a dual (double) circulatory system: pulmonary circulation (right heart → pulmonary arteries → lungs → pulmonary veins → left heart) and systemic circulation (left heart → aorta → body tissues → venae cavae → right heart). These circuits are in series, ensuring all blood is oxygenated before systemic distribution. The right ventricle generates lower pressure (~25/10 mmHg) than the left (~120/80 mmHg), reflecting pulmonary low-resistance vs. systemic high-resistance.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the heart's internal anatomy with all four chambers and valves in coronal section?
facts.explanation: A coronal section of the heart reveals four chambers: right atrium (receives deoxygenated blood from SVC/IVC, contains SA node) → tricuspid valve → right ventricle → pulmonary valve → pulmonary trunk; and left atrium (receives oxygenated blood from pulmonary veins) → mitral (bicuspid) valve → left ventricle (thick wall for systemic pressure) → aortic valve → aorta. The interventricular septum separates the ventricles; the interatrial septum separates the atria (contains the fossa ovalis — remnant of foramen ovale).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the heart's position in the thorax relative to the sternum and lungs?
facts.explanation: The heart lies in the middle mediastinum within the pericardial sac, positioned obliquely behind the sternum. It is 2/3 to the left of the midline. Its apex (tip of the left ventricle) points inferiorly and to the left, typically at the 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line — where the apical impulse (point of maximal impulse, PMI) is palpated. The base (great vessel end) faces superiorly and posteriorly. It lies anterior to the esophagus and thoracic aorta, posterior to the sternum.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the external surface anatomy of the heart with coronary sulcus and interventricular sulci?
facts.explanation: The external heart surfaces include: the sternocostal (anterior) surface dominated by the right ventricle, the diaphragmatic (inferior) surface formed mainly by the left ventricle, and the base (posterior) formed by the atria. The coronary sulcus (atrioventricular groove) encircles the heart separating atria from ventricles and carries the coronary arteries. The anterior and posterior interventricular sulci mark the IVS position. The right and left auricles are ear-like appendages of each atrium.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows all four heart valves in a superior view with the atria removed?
facts.explanation: A superior (basal) view with atria removed reveals all four valves simultaneously. The atrioventricular valves (mitral — bicuspid, left; tricuspid — right) open during diastole to allow ventricular filling. The semilunar valves (aortic — between LV and aorta; pulmonary — between RV and pulmonary trunk) open during systole for ejection. All valves are associated with the fibrous cardiac skeleton (annuli fibrosi), which provides mechanical support, prevents overdistension, and electrically insulates atria from ventricles.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the three layers of the heart wall: epicardium, myocardium, and endocardium?
facts.explanation: The heart wall has three layers. The epicardium (visceral pericardium) is the outer serous membrane composed of simple squamous epithelium and connective tissue; the coronary vessels run within it. The myocardium is the thick middle muscular layer of cardiac muscle (uninucleate, striated, branched, connected by intercalated discs with gap junctions for electrical coupling). The endocardium is the thin inner layer of simple squamous epithelium lining the chambers, continuous with the endothelium of vessels. Pericarditis, myocarditis, and endocarditis each affect different layers.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a fusiform aneurysm — a symmetric spindle-shaped vascular dilation?
facts.explanation: A fusiform aneurysm is a symmetrical, spindle-shaped dilation involving the entire circumference of the vessel wall, contrasting with a saccular aneurysm (asymmetric outpouching). Most aortic aneurysms are fusiform (abdominal aortic aneurysm, AAA, is most common — often at the infrarenal aorta). Caused by atherosclerosis, hypertension, or connective tissue disorders (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos). Risk of rupture increases with diameter >5.5 cm. Repair options: open surgical or endovascular (EVAR).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the head and neck arteries including carotid and vertebral vessels?
facts.explanation: The head and neck receive blood from two main sources: the common carotid arteries (right from the brachiocephalic trunk; left directly from the aortic arch) dividing into external carotid (face/scalp/neck) and internal carotid (brain/orbit); and the vertebral arteries (from subclavian arteries, ascending through transverse foramina C1–C6, merging intracranially to form the basilar artery). The circle of Willis connects internal carotid and vertebrobasilar systems, providing collateral flow.

facts.quizQuestion: Which detailed illustration shows the heart with coronary vessels and great vessels?
facts.explanation: This detailed illustration of the heart highlights its external and surface anatomy with the coronary vessels. The heart is the muscular pump of the cardiovascular system, beating approximately 100,000 times per day. It weighs 250–350 g in adults. The right side handles deoxygenated blood (pulmonary circuit); the left side handles oxygenated blood (systemic circuit). The cardiac output at rest is ~5 L/min, increasing to 20–25 L/min during intense exercise via increases in heart rate and stroke volume.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a sagittal section through the heart?
facts.explanation: A sagittal (parasagittal) section of the heart provides a lateral view revealing the relationship between anterior structures (right ventricle, right atrium) and posterior structures (left ventricle, aorta, pulmonary veins). This view is useful for understanding the anterior position of the right ventricle (predisposing it to trauma injury), the posterior position of the left atrium (explaining why left atrial enlargement causes dysphagia), and the spatial relationship between the aorta and pulmonary trunk.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows an oligodendrocyte myelinating multiple CNS axons?
facts.explanation: Oligodendrocytes are the myelin-producing glial cells of the CNS (contrast: Schwann cells in the PNS). A single oligodendrocyte can myelinate up to 50 axon segments simultaneously, sending out flat processes that wrap around axons. Myelin increases conduction velocity via saltatory conduction (action potentials 'jump' between nodes of Ranvier). Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune demyelinating disease targeting CNS myelin; remyelination by oligodendrocyte precursors is limited.

facts.quizQuestion: Which cross-section shows the spinal cord at the C5 cervical level with large white matter and prominent anterior horns?
facts.explanation: At C5 (cervical enlargement), the spinal cord cross-section shows a large amount of white matter relative to gray matter, reflecting the many ascending and descending tracts passing through this high level. The gray matter has prominent anterior horns (containing large alpha motor neurons for the brachial plexus/upper limb innervation) and smaller posterior horns. The cervical enlargement (C5–T1) supplies the upper limb via the brachial plexus. C5 injury causes deltoid and biceps weakness (shoulder abduction, elbow flexion).

facts.quizQuestion: Which cross-section shows the spinal cord at the L1 lumbar level near the conus medullaris?
facts.explanation: At L1, the spinal cord cross-section shows a relatively rounder shape with a higher gray-to-white matter ratio than cervical levels — fewer ascending/descending tracts remain at this caudal level. The anterior horns are enlarged (lumbar enlargement L1–S3 supplies lower limb via lumbosacral plexus), but smaller than at cervical enlargement. L1 is clinically important: the spinal cord typically ends at L1–L2 (conus medullaris); below this is the cauda equina. L1 compression affects hip flexors and knee extensors.

facts.quizQuestion: Which cross-section shows the thoracic spinal cord at T2 level with the lateral horn (sympathetic IML)?
facts.explanation: At T2, the spinal cord cross-section has a characteristic rounded oval shape with relatively small gray matter horns and proportionally large white matter. The thoracic cord (T1–T12) has small anterior and posterior horns (few limb motor/sensory neurons) but a unique lateral horn containing preganglionic sympathetic neurons (intermediolateral cell column, IML) — absent in cervical and lumbar cord. This is the hallmark of thoracic cord sections. The thoracic cord is the narrowest part of the cord.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a spinal cord cross-section with butterfly-shaped gray matter and surrounding white matter columns?
facts.explanation: The spinal cord in cross-section shows a characteristic H-shaped or butterfly-shaped gray matter (containing neuron cell bodies) surrounded by white matter (myelinated axon tracts). Gray matter horns: anterior (ventral) horn — lower motor neurons; posterior (dorsal) horn — sensory relay neurons; lateral horn (T1–L2, S2–S4) — autonomic preganglionic neurons. White matter columns: dorsal (posterior) columns (fine touch, proprioception), lateral columns (corticospinal, spinothalamic), anterior (ventral) columns (motor).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows MALT — mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue with follicles at a mucosal surface?
facts.explanation: Mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) is non-encapsulated lymphoid tissue distributed throughout mucosal surfaces. It constitutes the largest component of the immune system by mass. Subtypes include GALT (gut-associated — Peyer's patches in ileum, appendix), BALT (bronchus-associated), NALT (nasal), and MALT of the urogenital tract. MALT contains B cells (especially IgA-secreting plasma cells), T cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages. IgA secretion is the primary mucosal defense mechanism.

facts.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows hematopoiesis — blood cell development from a pluripotent stem cell?
facts.explanation: Hematopoiesis occurs primarily in red bone marrow (adults: axial skeleton, proximal long bones). A pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) divides into a common myeloid progenitor (→ erythrocytes, platelets via megakaryocytes, granulocytes, monocytes) or a common lymphoid progenitor (→ B lymphocytes, T lymphocytes, NK cells). Erythropoiesis is stimulated by erythropoietin (EPO) from the kidney. Thrombopoietin drives megakaryocyte/platelet production. G-CSF drives neutrophil production.

facts.quizQuestion: Which histological image shows a lymph node with follicles, germinal centers, paracortex, and medullary sinuses?
facts.explanation: Lymph node histology shows: outer cortex with lymphoid follicles (primary = quiescent B cells; secondary with germinal centers = active B cell proliferation/somatic hypermutation for affinity maturation), paracortex (deep cortex — T cell zone, also where dendritic cells present antigen), and medulla (medullary cords with plasma cells + macrophages; medullary sinuses filtering lymph). Afferent lymphatics enter the convex surface; efferent lymphatics and vessels exit at the hilum. Sentinel nodes are the first to receive tumor metastases.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows lymphatic capillaries as blind-ended vessels absorbing interstitial fluid alongside blood capillaries?
facts.explanation: Lymphatic capillaries are blind-ended, thin-walled vessels in the interstitium that absorb excess tissue fluid (lymph), proteins, and cellular debris. They are more permeable than blood capillaries due to overlapping endothelial cells with anchoring filaments that open inter-endothelial junctions when interstitial pressure rises ('one-way valves'). Lacteals are specialized lymphatic capillaries in intestinal villi that absorb dietary fats (chylomicrons) and form chyle. They drain into collecting lymphatics → lymph nodes → trunks → right lymphatic duct or thoracic duct → venous system.

facts.quizQuestion: Which full-body illustration shows the lymphatic system with the thoracic duct, lymph nodes, spleen, and thymus?
facts.explanation: The lymphatic system serves three functions: fluid homeostasis (returns ~3 L/day of interstitial fluid to the venous system), fat absorption (lacteals absorb dietary fat in the gut), and immune defense. Major organs: lymph nodes (filter lymph), spleen (filters blood), thymus (T cell maturation), tonsils and MALT (mucosal defense). Lymph flows one-way via intrinsic smooth muscle contractions, extrinsic skeletal muscle compression, and respiratory pressure changes. The thoracic duct (largest lymphatic vessel) drains into the left subclavian vein.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the thoracic duct and right lymphatic duct draining into the venous system at the subclavian junctions?
facts.explanation: Lymphatic vessels merge into trunks (lumbar, intestinal, bronchomediastinal, subclavian, jugular), which converge into two terminal ducts. The thoracic duct (the largest) begins at the cisterna chyli (L1–L2), ascends through the thorax, and empties into the left subclavian/internal jugular junction — draining the entire body below the diaphragm, and the left arm and left side of the head/thorax. The right lymphatic duct drains only the right arm and right head/thorax into the right subclavian/jugular junction.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the spleen with white pulp and red pulp compartments?
facts.explanation: The spleen is the largest secondary lymphoid organ, weighing ~150 g, located in the left upper quadrant beneath the 9th–11th ribs. It has two functional compartments: white pulp (lymphoid tissue with periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths — T cells, and lymphoid follicles — B cells; mounts immune responses to blood-borne antigens) and red pulp (venous sinusoids and splenic cords — filters blood, removes aged/defective RBCs via erythrophagocytosis by macrophages, stores platelets). Splenectomy increases susceptibility to encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenzae).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the thymus with its cortex and medulla containing Hassall's corpuscles?
facts.explanation: The thymus is a bilobed primary lymphoid organ in the superior mediastinum (anterior to the great vessels). Each lobe is subdivided into lobules with two zones: cortex (dense, dark — immature T cells (thymocytes) undergo positive selection for MHC recognition) and medulla (pale — mature T cells undergo negative selection to eliminate self-reactive clones; contains Hassall's corpuscles). The thymus is largest at puberty and involutes with age. Thymic aplasia (DiGeorge syndrome) causes T cell deficiency.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the tonsils forming Waldeyer's ring at the entrance to the pharynx?
facts.explanation: Waldeyer's ring is a ring of lymphoid tissue guarding the entrance to the respiratory and digestive tracts: palatine tonsils (lateral oropharynx — most commonly removed in tonsillectomy), pharyngeal tonsil (adenoid — posterior nasopharynx; enlarges in children causing nasal obstruction), lingual tonsil (base of tongue), and tubal tonsils (around Eustachian tube openings). All are MALT components covered by epithelium with crypts that trap antigens. They initiate mucosal immune responses without capsules.

facts.quizQuestion: Which histological image shows the papillary and reticular layers of the dermis?
facts.explanation: The dermis has two layers. The papillary dermis (superficial, ~20% of dermis thickness) consists of loose areolar connective tissue with thin collagen and elastic fibers, contains dermal papillae (increasing epidermal contact), capillary loops (nourishing avascular epidermis), and free nerve endings (Meissner's corpuscles for light touch). The reticular dermis (deep, ~80%) consists of dense irregular connective tissue with thick collagen bundles, contains hair follicles, sweat glands, sebaceous glands, Pacinian corpuscles (deep pressure/vibration), and Ruffini endings (stretch).

facts.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows the four epidermal cell types: keratinocytes, melanocytes, Langerhans cells, and Merkel cells?
facts.explanation: The epidermis contains four main cell types: Keratinocytes (~90%) — synthesize keratin (structural protein), undergo terminal differentiation from stratum basale to stratum corneum, connected by desmosomes. Melanocytes (~8%) — neural crest-derived, located in stratum basale, produce melanin in melanosomes transferred to keratinocytes for UV protection. Langerhans cells (~2%) — bone marrow-derived dendritic cells in stratum spinosum, antigen-presenting cells of the skin. Merkel cells (<1%) — mechanoreceptors in stratum basale, slow-adapting type I, for fine touch discrimination.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the detailed cellular structure of the epidermis including desmosomes and lamellar granules?
facts.explanation: Detailed epidermal cellular anatomy shows keratinocytes in progressive stages of keratinization (cornification). In the stratum basale, mitotically active cells are attached to the basement membrane by hemidesmosomes. As cells move upward, they accumulate keratin intermediate filaments. In the stratum spinosum, cells are connected by desmosomes (giving the spiny appearance in fixed preparations). Lamellar granules (membrane-coating granules) are prominent in stratum granulosum — they secrete lipids (ceramides) that form the water-impermeable epidermal barrier.

facts.quizQuestion: Which histological diagram shows all five epidermal strata from stratum basale to stratum corneum?
facts.explanation: The epidermis has five layers (from deep to superficial): Stratum basale (germinativum) — single layer of mitotically active columnar keratinocytes, contains melanocytes and Merkel cells. Stratum spinosum — several layers of polygonal keratinocytes with prominent desmosomes, contains Langerhans cells. Stratum granulosum — 3–5 layers with keratohyalin granules and lamellar granules. Stratum lucidum — present only in thick skin (palms, soles), translucent layer of dead cells. Stratum corneum — 15–30 layers of anucleate, dead cornified cells (corneocytes) — the barrier layer.

facts.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows melanocytes with dendritic processes transferring melanosomes to keratinocytes?
facts.explanation: Melanocytes are neural crest-derived cells residing in the stratum basale. They synthesize melanin (from tyrosine via tyrosinase) in organelles called melanosomes. Melanocytes donate melanosomes to surrounding keratinocytes via cytoplasmic dendrites ('melanocyte unit': 1 melanocyte serves ~36 keratinocytes). Melanin forms a supranuclear cap over keratinocyte nuclei, shielding DNA from UV radiation. Two types: eumelanin (brown-black) and pheomelanin (yellow-red). Melanocyte count is equal across races; skin color varies due to melanosome size, number, and distribution.

facts.quizQuestion: Which cross-sectional illustration shows the complete skin structure with epidermis, dermis, hypodermis, and appendages?
facts.explanation: A comprehensive skin section shows three main layers: epidermis (stratified squamous keratinized epithelium — barrier and sensory function), dermis (connective tissue — structural support, vasculature, nerves, appendages), and hypodermis/subcutis (adipose connective tissue — insulation, energy storage, shock absorption). Skin appendages include hair follicles with arrector pili muscles, sebaceous glands (holocrine, secrete sebum), eccrine sweat glands (merocrine, thermoregulation), and apocrine sweat glands (axillary/genital, modified by bacteria into odor compounds). Nails are specialized keratinized structures.
Gross anatomy of the lungs showing lobes, fissures, and surface landmarks including the hilum where bronchi and vessels enter.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Pleura (parietal and visceral layers). The pleura consists of two serous membrane layers: the visceral pleura adhering to the lung surface and the parietal pleura lining the thoracic wall, with the pleural cavity between them.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Adrenal glands (suprarenal glands). The adrenal (suprarenal) glands sit atop each kidney and consist of an outer cortex (producing steroid hormones) and an inner medulla (producing catecholamines).
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Thyroid gland (with parathyroid glands). The thyroid gland is a butterfly-shaped endocrine gland in the anterior neck with two lobes connected by an isthmus. The four parathyroid glands are embedded on its posterior surface.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Optic pathways (optic chiasm, optic tract). The optic pathways carry visual information from the retina through the optic nerves, optic chiasm (where nasal fibers decussate), optic tracts, lateral geniculate nucleus, and optic radiations to the visual cortex.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Retina (layers and photoreceptors). The retina contains 10 layers including photoreceptors (rods and cones), bipolar cells, and ganglion cells whose axons form the optic nerve. The macula lutea contains the fovea centralis for highest visual acuity.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Anterior neck muscles (infrahyoid and suprahyoid groups). The anterior neck muscles include the suprahyoid group (digastric, mylohyoid, geniohyoid, stylohyoid) and infrahyoid strap muscles (sternohyoid, sternothyroid, thyrohyoid, omohyoid) that move the hyoid bone and larynx.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Extraocular muscles (six muscles of the orbit). The six extraocular muscles (superior/inferior/medial/lateral rectus and superior/inferior oblique) move the eyeball. The levator palpebrae superioris is often shown as a seventh muscle that elevates the upper eyelid.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Facial muscles (muscles of facial expression). The muscles of facial expression are derived from the second pharyngeal arch (CN VII) and include orbicularis oculi, orbicularis oris, zygomaticus major/minor, buccinator, frontalis, and many others that move skin rather than bone.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Intrinsic muscles of the hand (thenar eminence)
Extrinsic muscles acting on the foot (peroneal group)
Muscles of the dorsum of the foot (extensor digitorum brevis)
facts.explanation: Intrinsic muscles of the foot (plantar layers). The intrinsic foot muscles are organized in four plantar layers. They include the flexor digitorum brevis, quadratus plantae, lumbricals, and interossei, which support the longitudinal arch and assist toe movements.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Forearm muscles (flexor and extensor compartments). The forearm contains an anterior (flexor/pronator) compartment and a posterior (extensor/supinator) compartment, separated by the interosseous membrane. Key muscles include flexor carpi radialis, pronator teres, extensor carpi radialis, and supinator.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Gluteal muscles (gluteus maximus, medius, minimus). The gluteal muscles form the buttock. Gluteus maximus is the largest and strongest hip extensor. Gluteus medius and minimus are hip abductors and medial rotators. The deep lateral rotators (piriformis, obturator internus/externus, gemelli, quadratus femoris) lie deep to gluteus maximus.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Intrinsic muscles of the foot (plantar layers)
Extrinsic flexors of the digits (flexor digitorum superficialis)
Extrinsic extensors of the digits (extensor digitorum)
facts.explanation: Intrinsic hand muscles (thenar, hypothenar, lumbricals). The intrinsic hand muscles include the thenar group (abductor/flexor/opponens pollicis, adductor pollicis), hypothenar group (abductor/flexor/opponens digiti minimi), four lumbricals, and seven interossei (3 palmar, 4 dorsal).
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Intrinsic muscles of the hand (thenar, hypothenar, lumbricals, interossei)

facts.explanation: Jaw muscles (muscles of mastication). The four muscles of mastication are the masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid, and lateral pterygoid, all innervated by the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V3). They close the jaw (elevation), protrude, retract, and grind.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: The leg has three compartments: anterior (tibialis anterior, extensor digitorum longus, extensor hallucis longus — dorsiflexion), posterior (gastrocnemius, soleus, tibialis posterior — plantarflexion), and lateral (fibularis longus and brevis — eversion). Shows anterior, posterior, and lateral muscle compartments of the leg.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Gap junction (electrical synapse between cardiac cells)
facts.explanation: Motor end plate (neuromuscular junction). The motor end plate is the specialized region of the muscle fiber membrane (sarcolemma) where the motor neuron terminal synapses. Acetylcholine released from synaptic vesicles binds nicotinic receptors, triggering an end-plate potential and muscle contraction.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Muscle connective tissue layers (epimysium, perimysium, endomysium)
facts.explanation: Muscle fiber structure (myofibrils, T-tubules, SR). A skeletal muscle fiber contains parallel myofibrils surrounded by the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR), a specialized ER that stores and releases Ca²⁺. T-tubules (transverse tubules) invaginate the sarcolemma and contact the SR at triads to transmit the action potential deep into the fiber.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Muscle fiber structure (myofibrils, T-tubules, sarcoplasmic reticulum)

facts.explanation: The posterior neck and back muscles include the superficial erector spinae column (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis), the transversospinalis group (semispinalis, multifidus, rotatores), and the suboccipital muscles. They extend and stabilize the vertebral column. Shows erector spinae and deep posterior muscle groups.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Neck and back muscles (erector spinae and deep posterior group)

distractors: Rotator cuff muscles (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis)
Muscles of the arm (biceps brachii, coracobrachialis, brachialis)
Muscles of the back (latissimus dorsi, rhomboids, levator scapulae)
facts.explanation: Muscles acting on the pectoral girdle stabilize and move the scapula and clavicle. Key muscles include pectoralis minor (protracts/depresses scapula), serratus anterior (protracts/rotates scapula upward), and trapezius (elevates, retracts, and rotates scapula). Shows pec major/minor, serratus anterior, and trapezius.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Pectoral girdle muscles (pectoralis major/minor, serratus anterior, trapezius)

distractors: Erector spinae group (thoracic and lumbar regions)
Deep cervical flexors (longus colli, longus capitis)
facts.explanation: The posterior neck muscles include the suboccipital muscles (rectus capitis posterior major/minor, obliquus capitis superior/inferior) forming the suboccipital triangle, and the semispinalis capitis/cervicis group. They extend, rotate, and tilt the head. Shows suboccipital triangle and semispinalis muscle group.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Posterior neck muscles (suboccipital triangle and semispinalis group)

facts.explanation: Sarcomere (Z-line, A-band, I-band, H-zone). The sarcomere is the functional unit of skeletal muscle, bounded by Z-lines. The A-band (dark) contains thick myosin filaments and overlapping actin. The I-band (light) contains only thin actin filaments. The H-zone in the center of the A-band contains only myosin; the M-line bisects it.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Axial skeleton (skull, vertebral column, thoracic cage)
facts.explanation: Appendicular skeleton (limb bones plus limb girdles). The appendicular skeleton comprises 126 bones: the pectoral girdles (clavicles and scapulae), upper limbs (humeri, radii, ulnae, carpals, metacarpals, phalanges), pelvic girdle (hip bones), and lower limbs (femora, patellae, tibiae, fibulae, tarsals, metatarsals, phalanges).
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Appendicular skeleton (bones of upper and lower limbs plus girdles)

distractors: Bone marrow (red versus yellow marrow distribution)
facts.explanation: Long bone blood supply arrives via a nutrient artery entering the diaphysis through the nutrient foramen, periosteal arteries supplying the cortex, and metaphyseal/epiphyseal vessels. Venous drainage parallels arterial supply. Disruption can cause avascular necrosis. Shows nutrient artery, periosteal vessels, and metaphyseal vessels supplying bone.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Bone blood supply (nutrient artery, periosteal vessels, metaphyseal vessels)

distractors: Connective tissue cells (fibroblast, macrophage, mast cell)
facts.explanation: Bone cells (osteoblast, osteocyte, osteoclast, osteogenic). Four main bone cell types: osteogenic cells (stem cells in periosteum/endosteum), osteoblasts (secrete osteoid matrix, become trapped as osteocytes), osteocytes (mature cells in lacunae maintaining matrix), and osteoclasts (multinucleated macrophage derivatives that resorb bone via acidification).
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Bone cells (osteoblast, osteocyte, osteoclast, osteogenic cell)

facts.explanation: Bone markings are surface features that indicate sites of muscle attachment, joint articulation, or neurovascular passage. Projections include processes, trochanters, tuberosities, and tubercles. Depressions include fossae, grooves, and sulci. Openings include foramina and canals. Shows processes, fossae, foramina, and facets as key bone surface markings.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Cubital tunnel (ulnar nerve at medial epicondyle)
facts.explanation: Carpal tunnel (transverse carpal ligament and contents). The carpal tunnel is an osseofibrous passage at the wrist formed by the carpal bones posteriorly and the transverse carpal ligament (flexor retinaculum) anteriorly. It transmits the median nerve and nine flexor tendons (flexor digitorum superficialis ×4, profundus ×4, flexor pollicis longus).
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Thoracic vertebrae (costal facets, long spinous process)
Lumbar vertebrae (large body, no costal facets)
facts.explanation: Cervical vertebrae (atlas, axis, and typical C3–C7). The seven cervical vertebrae are distinguished by bifid spinous processes (C2–C6) and transverse foramina for the vertebral arteries. C1 (atlas) has no body or spinous process; C2 (axis) has the dens (odontoid process); C7 (vertebra prominens) has the longest non-bifid spinous process.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Compact bone (osteon/Haversian system structure). Compact bone is organized into cylindrical osteons (Haversian systems). Each osteon consists of concentric lamellae surrounding a central Haversian canal carrying blood vessels. Osteocytes in lacunae communicate via canaliculi. Perforating (Volkmann's) canals run perpendicular, connecting Haversian canals.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Tibia and fibula (proximal and distal ends)
facts.explanation: Femur and patella (anterior and posterior surfaces). The femur is the longest bone in the body. Proximal landmarks include the head, neck, greater and lesser trochanters, and intertrochanteric line/crest. Distal landmarks include the medial and lateral condyles and epicondyles. The patella is a sesamoid bone in the quadriceps tendon.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Long bone cross-section (compact cortex with medullary cavity)
Short bone (spongy core with thin compact shell)
Sesamoid bone (embedded in tendon, small and round)
facts.explanation: Flat bone structure. Flat bones (skull, sternum, ribs, scapula) consist of two layers of compact bone (outer and inner tables) sandwiching a layer of spongy bone (diploë). The diploë contains red bone marrow. The inner table of the calvaria is thinner and more brittle. Shows inner and outer compact tables with diplöe (spongy bone) between them.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Hand and wrist bones (carpals, metacarpals, and phalanges)
Ankle joint (distal tibia, fibula, and talus)
Leg bones (tibia and fibula with interosseous membrane)
facts.explanation: Foot bones (tarsals, metatarsals, and phalanges). The foot contains 26 bones: 7 tarsals (calcaneus, talus, navicular, cuboid, 3 cuneiforms), 5 metatarsals, and 14 phalanges (2 in the hallux, 3 in each lesser toe). The calcaneus is the largest tarsal bone.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Distal forearm (distal radius, ulna, and radiocarpal joint)
facts.explanation: The hand contains 27 bones: 8 carpals in two rows (proximal: scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum, pisiform; distal: trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, hamate), 5 metacarpals, and 14 phalanges (2 in the thumb, 3 in each finger). Shows carpals, metacarpals, and phalanges of the hand.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Femur proximal end (femoral head and neck)
Pelvis overview (full pelvic girdle with sacrum)
facts.explanation: Hip bone (ilium, ischium, pubis, and acetabulum). The hip (coxal) bone is formed by fusion of three bones at the acetabulum: the ilium (superior, forms the iliac crest and greater sciatic notch), ischium (posteroinferior, forms the ischial tuberosity and spine), and pubis (anterior, forms the pubic symphysis).
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Femur and knee joint (condyles, intercondylar notch)
Radius and ulna (proximal ends, radial head, coronoid process)
facts.explanation: The humerus articulates at the elbow with the radius (capitulum) and ulna (trochlea). Key landmarks include the medial and lateral epicondyles, olecranon fossa (posterior), coronoid fossa (anterior), radial groove (spiral groove for radial nerve), and deltoid tuberosity. Shows epicondyles, condyles, and olecranon fossa of the humerus and elbow.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Humerus and elbow joint (epicondyles, condyles, olecranon fossa)

distractors: Costovertebral joint (rib articulation with thoracic vertebra)
facts.explanation: The intervertebral disc is a fibrocartilaginous secondary cartilaginous joint. The central nucleus pulposus (gelatinous, high water content) absorbs compressive loads. The annulus fibrosus (concentric fibrocartilage lamellae) resists tension. Herniation occurs when the nucleus pulposus protrudes through a tear in the annulus. Shows nucleus pulposus (gelatinous center) and annulus fibrosus (fibrous ring).
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Flat bone structure (inner/outer compact tables with diplöe)
Short bone anatomy (e.g., carpal bone cross-section)
facts.explanation: Long bone anatomy. A long bone consists of the diaphysis (shaft of compact bone enclosing the medullary cavity), two epiphyses (articular ends of spongy bone covered by hyaline cartilage), the metaphysis (flared zone between diaphysis and epiphysis containing the growth plate in children), periosteum (outer fibrous layer), and endosteum (inner lining of medullary cavity). Shows diaphysis, epiphysis, metaphysis, medullary cavity, and related structures.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Long bone anatomy (diaphysis, epiphysis, metaphysis, medullary cavity)

distractors: Thoracic vertebra (costal facets on body and transverse process)
Cervical vertebra (bifid spinous process, transverse foramen)
facts.explanation: Lumbar vertebra (wide body/processes, no costal facets). Lumbar vertebrae (L1–L5) are the largest moveable vertebrae and bear the most body weight. They are distinguished by their kidney-shaped bodies, short stout spinous processes projecting horizontally, absence of costal facets, and lack of transverse foramina. Mammillary processes are unique to lumbar vertebrae.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Lumbar vertebra (large body, transverse and spinous processes, no costal facets)

distractors: Pediatric versus adult pelvis (ossification and proportions)
Osteoporotic pelvis (trabecular loss and fracture risk)
facts.explanation: Male and female pelvis comparison. The female pelvis is adapted for childbirth: wider pelvic inlet (oval vs. heart-shaped), larger subpubic angle (>80° vs. <70°), more circular pubic arch, wider greater sciatic notch, and a more shallow overall structure. The male pelvis is narrower and deeper with a more prominent sacral promontory. Shows anatomical differences between male (narrower) and female (wider) pelvis.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: Pectoral girdle (clavicle and scapula). The pectoral girdle consists of two bones on each side: the clavicle (S-shaped, connecting sternum to scapula via the sternoclavicular and acromioclavicular joints) and the scapula (triangular flat bone with glenoid cavity, spine, acromion, coracoid process, and three angles).
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Hip bone (individual os coxae with ilium, ischium, pubis)
facts.explanation: Pelvis overview. The pelvis is formed by two hip bones, the sacrum, and the coccyx. The pelvic brim (arcuate line, iliopectineal line, sacral promontory) divides the greater (false) pelvis above from the lesser (true) pelvis below. Key landmarks include the ischial spines, used to gauge fetal descent during labor. Shows pelvic inlet, outlet, and key bony landmarks of the pelvis.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Compact bone osteon (Haversian system with lamellae)
Bone blood supply (nutrient artery, periosteal arterioles)
Articular cartilage (hyaline cartilage at joint surface)
facts.explanation: The periosteum is a fibrous connective tissue sheath covering the outer bone surface (except articular surfaces). It has an outer fibrous layer and an inner osteogenic layer with osteoprogenitor cells. The endosteum lines internal bony surfaces (medullary cavity, Haversian canals) and also contains osteoprogenitor cells. Shows outer periosteum and inner endosteum as bone membrane layers.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Periosteum and endosteum (outer and inner bone membrane layers)

facts.explanation: Rib cage (sternum, ribs, and costal cartilages). The thoracic cage consists of 12 pairs of ribs, 12 thoracic vertebrae, and the sternum (manubrium, body, xiphoid process). Ribs 1–7 are true ribs (attach directly to sternum via costal cartilage); ribs 8–10 are false ribs (join costal cartilage of rib 7); ribs 11–12 are floating ribs with no anterior attachment.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Rib cage (thoracic cage with sternum, ribs, and costal cartilages)

distractors: Pelvis overview (full pelvic ring including hip bones)
Coccygeal plexus (nerve supply to pelvic floor)
facts.explanation: The sacrum is formed by fusion of 5 sacral vertebrae. It has an anterior (pelvic) surface with anterior sacral foramina for ventral rami, and a posterior surface with dorsal sacral foramina for dorsal rami. The sacral promontory (S1 superior edge) is the most prominent point of the pelvic brim. The coccyx consists of 3–5 fused rudimentary vertebrae. Shows fused vertebral segments with sacral foramina and the coccyx.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

facts.explanation: The scapula is a triangular flat bone with three angles (superior, inferior, lateral), three borders (superior, medial/vertebral, lateral/axillary), and key processes: the spine (dividing supraspinous and infraspinous fossae), the acromion (roof of shoulder), and the coracoid process (attachment for pectoralis minor, short head of biceps, coracobrachialis). Shows spine, acromion, coracoid process, and glenoid cavity of the scapula.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Flat bone structure (diplöe sandwiched between compact tables)
facts.explanation: Spongy (cancellous) bone (trabeculae and red bone marrow). Spongy (cancellous) bone consists of an interconnecting lattice of bony trabeculae (plates and rods of bone tissue) with spaces filled by red bone marrow in actively hematopoietic sites. The trabeculae align along lines of mechanical stress, providing strength with minimal mass. It is found in epiphyses and diplöe.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Fibrous joint (suture or gomphosis — no cavity)
Primary cartilaginous joint (synchondrosis — e.g., epiphyseal plate)
Secondary cartilaginous joint (symphysis — e.g., pubic symphysis)
facts.explanation: Synovial joint structure (cartilage, membrane, capsule). A synovial (diarthrosis) joint is characterized by a joint cavity filled with synovial fluid, articular cartilage (hyaline) covering the bone ends, a fibrous joint capsule lined by synovial membrane, and accessory structures (ligaments, menisci, bursae). It allows the greatest range of motion.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Synovial joint structure (articular cartilage, synovial membrane, joint capsule)

distractors: Tendon sheath types (synovial sheaths around tendons)
facts.explanation: The six types of synovial joints are classified by the shape of their articular surfaces and the movements they permit: plane (gliding), hinge (uniaxial flexion/extension), pivot (uniaxial rotation), condyloid (biaxial: flexion/extension + abduction/adduction), saddle (biaxial + opposition), and ball-and-socket (multiaxial). Shows hinge, ball-and-socket, pivot, and condyloid synovial joint examples.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Synovial joint types (ball-and-socket, hinge, pivot, condyloid, saddle, plane)

distractors: Lumbar vertebra (large body, no costal facets, no transverse foramen)
Cervical vertebra (transverse foramen, bifid spinous process)
facts.explanation: Thoracic vertebra (costal facets on body/transverse). Thoracic vertebrae (T1–T12) are distinguished by costal facets (demifacets on the body for articulation with rib heads) and costal facets on the transverse processes (for rib tubercle articulation, T1–T10). Their spinous processes are long and angled sharply inferiorly. The vertebral foramen is roughly circular and smaller than cervical vertebrae.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Thoracic vertebra (costal facets on body and transverse process)

distractors: Radius and ulna (forearm bones with landmarks)
Femur (thigh bone with condyles and trochanters)
Humerus (arm bone with epicondyles and tuberosities)
facts.explanation: Tibia and fibula (medial and lateral leg bones). The tibia (medial leg bone) is the weight-bearing bone with the tibial plateau (medial/lateral condyles), tibial tuberosity (patellar tendon attachment), medial malleolus (ankle mortise), and anterior border (shin). The fibula (lateral leg bone) is slender and non-weight-bearing, with the fibular head (peroneal nerve at risk) and lateral malleolus.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Tibia and fibula (medial and lateral leg bones with landmarks)

distractors: Tibia and fibula (leg bones with interosseous membrane)
Humerus and elbow joint (distal humerus with condyles)
Wrist and hand bones (carpals, metacarpals, phalanges)
facts.explanation: The ulna (medial forearm bone) has the olecranon process (forms the elbow point), coronoid process, trochlear notch, and styloid process. The radius (lateral forearm bone) has the radial head (articulates with capitulum), radial tuberosity (biceps tendon), and a broader distal end. The interosseous membrane connects them and transmits forces. Shows forearm bones with interosseous membrane between them.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Intervertebral disc (nucleus pulposus and annulus fibrosus)
Vertebral ligaments (anterior longitudinal, posterior longitudinal, ligamentum flavum)
Spinal cord cross-section (gray and white matter horns)
facts.explanation: A typical vertebra consists of a body (weight-bearing), a vertebral arch (formed by two pedicles and two laminae), and seven processes: one spinous process (posterior), two transverse processes (lateral), and four articular processes (two superior, two inferior) forming zygapophyseal (facet) joints. Shows body, vertebral arch, pedicles, laminae, and various processes.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.

distractors: Spinal cord (with nerve roots and meninges)
Axial skeleton overview (skull, vertebral column, thoracic cage combined)
