Intervertebral foramina (lateral view with nerve root exit)
facts.explanation: Vertebral column (cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral). The adult vertebral column has 33 vertebrae (7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 fused sacral, 4 fused coccygeal) and four curvatures: cervical lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis (secondary curvatures that develop postnatally), and sacral kyphosis (primary curvature present at birth).
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Vertebral column (cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal regions with curvatures)

distractors: Osteocyte (mature bone cell in lacuna with canaliculi)
facts.explanation: Osteoclast (multinucleated, ruffled border). Osteoclasts are large multinucleated cells (up to 50 nuclei) derived from monocyte/macrophage precursors. They resorb bone by creating a sealed acidic microenvironment via the ruffled border (extensive membrane infolding), secreting H⁺ (via V-ATPase) and cathepsin K to dissolve mineral and matrix. Bisphosphonates inhibit osteoclast function.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Osteoclast (multinucleated bone-resorbing cell with ruffled border)

distractors: Osteoblast (cuboidal bone-forming cell on osteoid surface)
Chondrocyte (cartilage cell within lacuna, no canaliculi)
facts.explanation: Osteocyte (bone cell in lacuna, connected via canaliculi). Osteocytes are former osteoblasts that have become embedded in the mineralized bone matrix within lacunae. They extend long cytoplasmic processes through canaliculi that form gap junctions with neighboring osteocytes, creating a syncytial network for mechanosensing, Ca²⁺ signaling, and communication with surface osteoblasts and osteoclasts.
facts.quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical image.
acceptableAlternatives: Osteocyte (mature bone cell within lacuna, connected via canaliculi)

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structures shown in this digestive system illustration.
facts.explanation: The accessory digestive organs — liver, gallbladder, and pancreas — support digestion without food passing through them. The liver produces bile, which emulsifies dietary fats. Bile is stored and concentrated in the gallbladder, then released into the duodenum via the common bile duct. The pancreas secretes digestive enzymes (amylase, lipase, proteases) and bicarbonate into the duodenum. Gallstones form when bile becomes supersaturated with cholesterol, a key USMLE clinical correlation.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the accessory digestive organs including liver, gallbladder, and pancreas with their ductal connections to the duodenum

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structure shown in this GI tract illustration.
facts.explanation: The esophagus is a muscular tube approximately 25 cm long connecting the pharynx to the stomach. It passes through the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm. Its wall layers (mucosa, submucosa, muscularis, adventitia) propel food via peristalsis. The upper third contains skeletal muscle, the lower third smooth muscle, and the middle third a mixture. The lower esophageal sphincter (LES) prevents gastroesophageal reflux; LES incompetence underlies GERD.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional and longitudinal illustration of the esophagus showing wall layers and relationship to surrounding structures

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structure shown in this detailed illustration.
facts.explanation: The gallbladder is a pear-shaped sac on the inferior surface of the liver that stores and concentrates bile up to 10-fold. Bile enters via the cystic duct and is released into the common bile duct in response to cholecystokinin (CCK) from the duodenum. The gallbladder wall consists of mucosa, muscularis, and serosa. Acute cholecystitis typically results from cystic duct obstruction by gallstones and presents with right upper quadrant pain (Murphy's sign).
facts.visualDescription: Detailed illustration of the gallbladder showing its fundus, body, neck, and cystic duct connecting to the biliary tree

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ shown in this GI illustration.
facts.explanation: The large intestine (~1.5 m) consists of the cecum, colon (ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid), rectum, and anal canal. Its primary role is water and electrolyte absorption and compaction of feces. Distinguishing features include haustra (pouches), taeniae coli (three longitudinal muscle bands), and epiploic appendages (fat tags). The ileocecal valve separates the small from large intestine. Colorectal cancer most commonly arises in the sigmoid and rectum.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the large intestine showing cecum, ascending colon, transverse colon, descending colon, sigmoid colon, and rectum

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the microscopic structure shown in this liver slide.
facts.explanation: The classic hepatic lobule is a hexagonal unit with a central vein at its core and portal triads (portal vein branch, hepatic artery branch, bile ductule) at the corners. Hepatocytes radiate as cords between sinusoids lined with Kupffer cells (resident macrophages). Blood flows from portal triads inward to the central vein; bile flows outward to bile ductules. Zone 1 (periportal) is most oxygenated; Zone 3 (centrilobular) is most susceptible to ischemia and acetaminophen toxicity.
facts.visualDescription: Microscopic cross-section of liver tissue showing hepatic lobule architecture with central vein, portal triads, and hepatocyte cords

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the anatomical region shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The oral cavity includes the lips, cheeks, hard and soft palate, uvula, tongue, teeth, and gingiva. The vestibule lies between the cheeks/lips and the teeth; the oral cavity proper lies posterior to the teeth. The hard palate (palatine processes of maxilla + horizontal plates of palatine bones) separates the oral and nasal cavities. The soft palate, bearing the uvula, elevates during swallowing to prevent nasopharyngeal regurgitation. Cleft palate results from failed fusion of palatal shelves.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the open oral cavity showing teeth, tongue, hard and soft palate, uvula, and associated structures

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ and its dual function shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Pancreas (exocrine and endocrine). The pancreas has both exocrine (acinar cells, ~95% of mass) and endocrine (islets of Langerhans, ~2%) components. Acinar cells secrete digestive enzymes into ducts emptying at the ampulla of Vater. Islets contain alpha cells (glucagon), beta cells (insulin), delta cells (somatostatin), and PP cells (pancreatic polypeptide). Destruction of beta cells by autoimmune attack causes Type 1 diabetes mellitus. Pancreatic head carcinoma classically obstructs the common bile duct, causing painless jaundice.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the pancreas showing its head, body, and tail, with inset of islets of Langerhans and surrounding acinar tissue

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structure shown at the crossroads of the respiratory and digestive tracts.
facts.explanation: The pharynx is a funnel-shaped musculomembranous tube divided into three parts: nasopharynx (posterior to nasal cavity, contains pharyngeal tonsil and eustachian tube openings), oropharynx (posterior to oral cavity, contains palatine tonsils), and laryngopharynx/hypopharynx (opens into both the larynx anteriorly and esophagus posteriorly). During swallowing, the epiglottis folds over the laryngeal inlet to prevent aspiration. Peritonsillar abscess is a complication of tonsillitis, often caused by Streptococcus pyogenes.
facts.visualDescription: Sagittal illustration of the pharynx showing the three regions, epiglottis, and connections to nasal cavity, oral cavity, larynx, and esophagus

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the glands shown in this head and neck illustration.
facts.explanation: The three paired major salivary glands produce ~1–1.5 L of saliva daily. The parotid glands (serous secretion) drain via Stensen's duct opposite the upper second molar. The submandibular glands (mixed, predominantly serous) drain via Wharton's duct at the sublingual caruncle. The sublingual glands (mixed, predominantly mucous) drain via multiple ducts. Saliva contains amylase, lingual lipase, mucins, IgA, and lysozyme. Mumps virus preferentially infects the parotid glands; Sjögren syndrome causes autoimmune destruction of salivary and lacrimal glands.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the lateral face and neck showing the three major salivary glands and their ducts

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ shown in this digestive system illustration.
facts.explanation: The small intestine (~6–7 m) is the primary site of nutrient digestion and absorption. It is divided into the duodenum (25 cm; receives bile and pancreatic juices), jejunum (~2.4 m; major absorption site), and ileum (~3.5 m; absorbs vitamin B12 and bile salts). Surface area is amplified ~600-fold by plicae circulares, villi, and microvilli. Celiac disease (gluten-induced autoimmune enteropathy) destroys villi, causing malabsorption. Meckel's diverticulum is the most common congenital GI anomaly, found in the ileum.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the small intestine coiled within the abdominal cavity, with labels for duodenum, jejunum, and ileum

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the microscopic structure shown in this intestinal slide.
facts.explanation: Small intestinal histology shows finger-like villi projecting into the lumen, each with a core of lamina propria containing a lacteal (lymphatic capillary for fat absorption) and blood capillaries. Crypts of Lieberkühn lie between villi and harbor intestinal stem cells, Paneth cells (antimicrobial secretion), and goblet cells (mucus). The enterocyte brush border (microvilli) expresses disaccharidases and peptidases. Lactase deficiency causes lactose intolerance; brush border enzyme loss occurs in rotavirus infection.
facts.visualDescription: Histological cross-section of small intestine showing villi, crypts of Lieberkühn, goblet cells, and submucosal layers

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ shown in this digestive illustration.
facts.explanation: The stomach is a J-shaped muscular organ in the left upper quadrant with four regions: cardia (near esophageal junction), fundus (dome above cardia), body (main portion), and pylorus (distal funnel leading to duodenum). Its muscularis has three layers (oblique, circular, longitudinal) for churning. Parietal cells secrete HCl and intrinsic factor; chief cells secrete pepsinogen. H. pylori infection (colonizes antrum) causes most peptic ulcers. Intrinsic factor deficiency causes pernicious anemia.
facts.visualDescription: Gross anatomical illustration of the stomach showing fundus, body, cardia, pylorus, greater and lesser curvatures

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the microscopic structure shown in this gastric slide.
facts.explanation: Stomach histology reveals gastric pits that open into gastric glands. The mucosa is lined by surface mucous cells secreting protective alkaline mucus. Gastric glands contain mucous neck cells, parietal cells (eosinophilic, HCl and intrinsic factor), chief cells (basophilic, pepsinogen), and enteroendocrine cells (G cells in antrum secrete gastrin; D cells secrete somatostatin). The thick muscularis externa has three layers. NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandins, disrupting the mucus-bicarbonate barrier and predisposing to ulcers.
facts.visualDescription: Histological section of the stomach wall showing gastric pits, glands, and distinct cell types within the mucosa

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the dental structures shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Teeth (dental anatomy overview). Adult dentition comprises 32 permanent teeth: 8 incisors, 4 canines, 8 premolars, and 12 molars (including 4 third molars/wisdom teeth). Each tooth type has a distinct morphology suited to its function — incisors cut, canines tear, premolars and molars grind. Primary (deciduous) dentition has 20 teeth. Each tooth has a crown (visible), neck (at gum line), and root (embedded in alveolar bone). Third molar impaction is the most common dental surgical procedure.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration showing the upper and lower dental arches with labeled tooth types including incisors, canines, premolars, and molars

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structure shown in this oral anatomy illustration.
facts.explanation: The tongue is a muscular organ with intrinsic (within tongue) and extrinsic muscles (genioglossus, hyoglossus, styloglossus, palatoglossus). The dorsal surface has four types of papillae: filiform (most numerous, no taste buds), fungiform (scattered, taste buds), circumvallate/vallate (row of ~7–12 at junction of anterior 2/3 and posterior 1/3, taste buds), and foliate (lateral edges). Taste (CN VII anterior 2/3; CN IX posterior 1/3) and general sensation (CN V3 anterior 2/3; CN IX posterior 1/3) are carried by distinct nerves.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the tongue from above showing papillae distributions, taste zones, and underlying musculature

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structure shown in this detailed cross-sectional illustration.
facts.explanation: A cross-sectioned tooth reveals enamel (hardest substance in the body, produced by ameloblasts) covering the crown; dentin (mineralized connective tissue produced by odontoblasts) beneath the enamel; pulp cavity (loose connective tissue, vessels, nerves) in the center; cementum (covering the root, produced by cementoblasts) anchoring to the periodontal ligament; and the root canal carrying neurovascular structures to the apical foramen. Dental caries (tooth decay) begins with demineralization of enamel by bacterial acid, progressing to dentin and pulp.
facts.visualDescription: Longitudinal cross-section of a molar tooth showing enamel, dentin, pulp cavity, cementum, root, and periodontal structures

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the terminal GI structure shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The rectum (approximately 12–15 cm) connects the sigmoid colon to the anal canal. It has three lateral curves creating three transverse folds (Houston's valves). The anal canal (~4 cm) has an internal anal sphincter (smooth muscle, involuntary) and external anal sphincter (skeletal muscle, voluntary). The pectinate (dentate) line divides the upper (visceral innervation, columnar epithelium) from the lower (somatic innervation, stratified squamous epithelium) anal canal. Hemorrhoids above the dentate line are internal; below are external.
facts.visualDescription: Medical illustration of the rectum and anal canal showing Houston's valves, sphincter muscles, and the dentate line

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the glands shown in this head and neck illustration.
facts.explanation: The three paired major salivary glands are the parotid (largest, located anterior to the ear, purely serous), submandibular (under the mandible floor, mixed with predominantly serous secretion), and sublingual (under the tongue, mixed with predominantly mucous secretion). The facial nerve (CN VII) passes through the parotid gland, making it vulnerable during parotid surgery. Sjögren syndrome is characterized by autoimmune destruction of salivary and lacrimal glands, producing dry mouth (xerostomia) and dry eyes (keratoconjunctivitis sicca).
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art illustration of the head showing the three major salivary glands and their anatomical positions

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structure shown in this cross-sectional illustration.
facts.explanation: A cross-section of the stomach reveals its four wall layers: mucosa (gastric pits and glands), submucosa (connective tissue with vessels and Meissner's plexus), muscularis externa (three layers: inner oblique, middle circular, outer longitudinal — unique to the stomach — with Auerbach's plexus between the inner two), and serosa (visceral peritoneum). The thick muscularis allows vigorous churning to produce chyme. Hiatal hernias occur when the stomach herniates through the esophageal hiatus into the thorax.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional medical illustration of the stomach wall showing distinct muscle layers and mucosal folding (rugae)

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the muscular organ shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The tongue comprises intrinsic muscles (superior and inferior longitudinal, transverse, vertical — altering tongue shape) and extrinsic muscles (genioglossus, hyoglossus, styloglossus, palatoglossus — positioning the tongue). All tongue muscles are innervated by CN XII (hypoglossal nerve), except palatoglossus (CN X). The tongue is the primary organ of taste; taste buds on the circumvallate papillae are innervated by CN IX. A unilateral CN XII lesion causes the tongue to deviate toward the side of the lesion.
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art illustration of the tongue showing its dorsal surface and anatomical position within the oral cavity

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the structure shown in this cross-sectional medical illustration.
facts.explanation: The tooth's internal architecture includes enamel (96% mineral, hardest biological material), dentin (70% mineral, slightly elastic, produced throughout life by odontoblasts), pulp (central cavity with nerves and blood vessels through the root canal), cementum (covers the root, anchoring to alveolar bone via the periodontal ligament). The periodontal ligament acts as a shock absorber during chewing. Dental pulpitis causes severe pain; untreated, infection spreads apically, forming a periapical abscess requiring root canal treatment.
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art cross-section of a tooth clearly showing enamel, dentin, pulp, and root anatomy

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the renal microstructure shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Juxtaglomerular apparatus and glomerulus. The juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) consists of three components: macula densa (specialized distal tubule cells sensing NaCl concentration), juxtaglomerular (granular) cells (modified smooth muscle in afferent arteriole wall, secreting renin), and extraglomerular mesangial (lacis) cells. Renin cleaves angiotensinogen → angiotensin I, initiating the RAAS cascade. The glomerulus is a tuft of capillaries enclosed in Bowman's capsule, driving ultrafiltration. Diabetic nephropathy causes glomerular basement membrane thickening and Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodular glomerulosclerosis.
facts.visualDescription: Detailed illustration of the glomerulus and juxtaglomerular apparatus showing afferent/efferent arterioles, macula densa, and Bowman's capsule

acceptableAlternatives: Visceral epithelial cells of the glomerulus
facts.quizQuestion: Identify the specialized renal cells shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Podocytes are highly specialized visceral epithelial cells that wrap around glomerular capillaries with interdigitating foot processes (pedicels). The narrow filtration slits between pedicels are bridged by slit diaphragms (nephrin, podocin) that are critical for selective filtration. Podocyte injury — whether by immune complexes, minimal change disease, or FSGS — leads to foot process effacement and proteinuria. Minimal change disease (lipoid nephrosis), the most common nephrotic syndrome in children, shows diffuse foot process effacement on electron microscopy.
facts.visualDescription: Electron microscopy-style illustration of podocytes showing foot processes wrapping around a glomerular capillary with filtration slits

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ system shown in this female anatomy illustration.
facts.explanation: The female urinary system consists of the kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder, and urethra. Key difference from males: the female urethra is only ~4 cm long (vs ~20 cm in males), making women significantly more susceptible to ascending urinary tract infections (UTIs). Escherichia coli is the most common UTI pathogen. The bladder neck in females is supported by pubourethral ligaments; pelvic floor weakness can cause stress urinary incontinence. The trigone of the bladder (between ureteral orifices and internal urethral orifice) is embryologically distinct from the rest of the bladder.
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art illustration of the female urinary system showing kidneys, ureters, and urinary bladder in anatomical position

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the organ system shown in this male anatomy illustration.
facts.explanation: The male urinary system includes kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder, and a ~20 cm urethra. The male urethra passes through three regions: prostatic (passes through prostate, receives ejaculatory ducts), membranous (passes through urogenital diaphragm, site of external sphincter), and spongy/penile (traverses corpus spongiosum). Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — hyperplasia of the transitional zone — compresses the prostatic urethra, causing obstructive lower urinary tract symptoms. PSA screening detects prostate cancer arising most often from the peripheral zone.
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art illustration of the male urinary system showing kidneys, ureters, and bladder with the prostate gland visible

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the smallest blood vessel type shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels (diameter 5–10 µm), consisting of a single layer of endothelium on a thin basement membrane. Three types exist: continuous (most common, tight junctions — muscle, skin, lung), fenestrated (pores for rapid exchange — kidney, intestinal villi, endocrine glands), and sinusoidal/discontinuous (large gaps — liver, spleen, bone marrow). Starling forces govern fluid exchange: hydrostatic pressure drives filtration at the arteriolar end; oncotic pressure drives reabsorption at the venular end. Edema results when these forces are imbalanced.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a capillary showing single-layer endothelium, pericytes, and its relationship to surrounding tissue

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the cerebrovascular structure shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The Circle of Willis is an anastomotic ring of arteries at the base of the brain formed by the internal carotid arteries, anterior cerebral arteries (ACAs), anterior communicating artery (ACoA), posterior cerebral arteries (PCAs), and posterior communicating arteries (PCoAs). It provides collateral circulation between the anterior and posterior circulations. Berry aneurysms occur most commonly at bifurcation points, especially the ACoA (most common), MCA bifurcation, and PCoA junction. ACoA aneurysm rupture causes subarachnoid hemorrhage with possible visual field deficits.
facts.visualDescription: Inferior view illustration of the brain showing the Circle of Willis with labeled arteries including ACA, MCA, PCA, ACoA, and PCoA

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the blood cell shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Erythrocyte (red blood cell). Erythrocytes (RBCs) are biconcave discs (~7–8 µm diameter) lacking a nucleus and organelles, maximizing space for hemoglobin (~280 million molecules per cell). The biconcave shape maximizes surface area-to-volume ratio for gas exchange and allows deformation through narrow capillaries. RBC lifespan is ~120 days; senescent RBCs are destroyed in the spleen. Hemoglobin carries O2 (cooperative binding via T→R state shift) and CO2. Sickle cell disease (HbS mutation) causes RBCs to polymerize under low O2, producing vaso-occlusive crises.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of an erythrocyte showing its characteristic biconcave disc shape from multiple perspectives

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the vascular structures shown in this head and neck illustration.
facts.explanation: Veins of the head and neck. Venous drainage of the head and neck includes the external and internal jugular veins. Cerebral blood drains via dural venous sinuses (superior sagittal, inferior sagittal, transverse, sigmoid) into the internal jugular vein. The external jugular vein drains the scalp and face into the subclavian vein. The internal jugular vein is used for central venous access. Elevated intracranial pressure is transmitted to the internal jugular, causing jugular venous distension (JVD). Cavernous sinus thrombosis is a dangerous complication of facial infections spreading via the facial and ophthalmic veins.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of the head and neck showing external and internal jugular veins, facial vein, and connections to dural sinuses
distractors: Arteries of the head and neck

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the cardiac valve shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The mitral (bicuspid) valve guards the left atrioventricular orifice, with two leaflets (anterior and posterior) attached to the left ventricular papillary muscles via chordae tendineae. During systole, it closes to prevent regurgitation into the left atrium. Mitral stenosis (often rheumatic fever sequela) restricts flow from LA to LV, causing LA enlargement, atrial fibrillation, and pulmonary hypertension. Mitral regurgitation produces a holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla. Mitral valve prolapse (myxomatous degeneration) causes a mid-systolic click.
facts.visualDescription: Detailed illustration of the mitral valve showing its two leaflets, chordae tendineae, and papillary muscle attachments

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the immature blood cell shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Reticulocytes are immature red blood cells that have extruded their nucleus but retain residual ribosomal RNA (visible as a blue reticular network with new methylene blue staining). They circulate for ~1–2 days before maturing into erythrocytes. The reticulocyte count is a key marker of erythropoietic activity: elevated in hemolytic anemia or acute blood loss (regenerative anemia), and low in aplastic anemia or iron/B12/folate deficiency (hypoproliferative anemia). Erythropoietin (produced by peritubular cells in the kidney) drives reticulocyte production.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a reticulocyte showing its residual ribosomal network compared to a mature erythrocyte

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the vascular system shown in this full-body illustration.
facts.explanation: The systemic venous system returns deoxygenated blood to the right atrium via the superior vena cava (SVC) and inferior vena cava (IVC). The SVC drains the head, neck, upper limbs, and thorax. The IVC drains the abdomen, pelvis, and lower limbs. At any given time, about 65% of total blood volume resides in the venous system, making veins the primary capacitance (reservoir) vessels. Deep vein thrombosis (DVT), most commonly in the lower extremities, risks pulmonary embolism if a clot propagates and embolizes to the pulmonary circulation.
facts.visualDescription: Full body illustration showing the major veins including jugular, subclavian, cephalic, basilic, portal, femoral, saphenous veins, SVC and IVC

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the deep brain structures shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Basal nuclei (basal ganglia). The basal nuclei (basal ganglia) include the caudate nucleus, putamen (together = striatum), globus pallidus (internal and external segments), subthalamic nucleus, and substantia nigra. They form circuits that modulate voluntary movement, motor learning, and habit formation via the direct (facilitatory) and indirect (inhibitory) pathways. Parkinson's disease results from loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, causing bradykinesia, tremor, rigidity, and postural instability. Huntington's disease preferentially destroys the striatum (caudate and putamen).
facts.visualDescription: Coronal or 3D illustration of the brain showing the basal nuclei including caudate, putamen, globus pallidus, and their spatial relationships

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the brain region shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The brainstem consists of the midbrain (mesencephalon), pons, and medulla oblongata. The midbrain contains the cerebral peduncles, superior and inferior colliculi (visual and auditory reflexes), red nucleus, and substantia nigra. The pons relays signals between cerebrum and cerebellum and houses CN V–VIII nuclei and respiratory control centers. The medulla controls vital functions (cardiac, respiratory, vasomotor centers) and houses CN IX–XII nuclei. Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome — posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) occlusion — causes ipsilateral facial numbness, contralateral body loss of pain/temperature, dysphasia, and Horner syndrome.
facts.visualDescription: Posterior or lateral illustration of the brainstem showing midbrain, pons, and medulla with cranial nerve exit points

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the cortical mapping system shown in this brain illustration.
facts.explanation: Brodmann areas are regions of the cerebral cortex defined by cytoarchitecture (cell layer composition and thickness). Key areas: 1, 2, 3 (primary somatosensory cortex, postcentral gyrus), 4 (primary motor cortex, precentral gyrus), 6 (premotor and supplementary motor cortex), 17 (primary visual cortex, calcarine sulcus), 22 (Wernicke's area in dominant hemisphere), 41/42 (primary auditory cortex), 44/45 (Broca's area in dominant hemisphere). Lesions of Broca's area (44/45) cause expressive aphasia; Wernicke's area (22) lesions cause receptive aphasia.
facts.visualDescription: Lateral and medial view of the cerebral cortex with numbered Brodmann areas color-coded by function

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the brain structure shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The cerebellum is located in the posterior cranial fossa and accounts for ~10% of brain weight but ~50% of neurons. It coordinates movement, balance, and motor learning via the spinocerebellar, vestibulocerebellar, and corticocerebellar circuits. It is organized into three lobes (anterior, posterior, flocculonodular) and three functional divisions: vestibulocerebellum (balance), spinocerebellum (limb and trunk coordination), and cerebrocerebellum (planning, fine motor). Cerebellar lesions cause ipsilateral deficits: ataxia, dysmetria (past-pointing), dysdiadochokinesia, intention tremor, and nystagmus (DANISH mnemonic).
facts.visualDescription: Posterior or inferior view of the cerebellum showing folia, vermis, hemispheres, and fissures

acceptableAlternatives: Frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital lobes
facts.quizQuestion: Identify the cortical regions shown in this lateral brain illustration.
facts.explanation: The cerebral cortex is divided into four lobes: frontal (anterior to central sulcus — motor cortex, prefrontal cortex, Broca's area), parietal (posterior to central sulcus — somatosensory cortex, spatial processing), temporal (below lateral sulcus — auditory cortex, Wernicke's area, memory), and occipital (posterior pole — primary and association visual cortex). The insula is a fifth lobe hidden within the lateral sulcus. Middle cerebral artery (MCA) stroke is the most common stroke syndrome, affecting the lateral cortex (frontal, parietal, temporal) and causing contralateral hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, and aphasia (dominant hemisphere).
facts.visualDescription: Lateral view of the left cerebral hemisphere with color-coded frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital lobes

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the largest brain structure shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The cerebrum is the largest part of the brain (~85% of total brain weight), consisting of two hemispheres connected by the corpus callosum. The cerebral cortex (gray matter, ~3 mm thick) contains 6 layers in most regions (neocortex). Gyri are folds; sulci are grooves; fissures are deep sulci. The longitudinal fissure divides the hemispheres. The left hemisphere is dominant for language in ~95% of right-handed individuals. Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM), the most aggressive primary brain tumor, most commonly arises in the cerebral hemispheres.
facts.visualDescription: Superior or lateral view of the cerebrum showing the two hemispheres, major gyri and sulci, and the longitudinal fissure

acceptableAlternatives: Astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglia, and ependymal cells
facts.quizQuestion: Identify the non-neuronal brain cells shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: CNS glial cells include: astrocytes (star-shaped, blood-brain barrier maintenance, scar formation, metabolic support — GFAP marker), oligodendrocytes (myelinate multiple CNS axons — each oligodendrocyte myelinates up to 30 axons; destroyed in multiple sclerosis), microglia (resident CNS macrophages derived from mesoderm, surveillance and phagocytosis), and ependymal cells (line ventricles, produce and circulate CSF). Gliomas (astrocytoma, glioblastoma, oligodendroglioma) are the most common primary brain tumors. Reactive astrogliosis forms glial scars after CNS injury.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration showing the different types of CNS glial cells: astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglia, and ependymal cells with distinguishing features

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the peripheral nervous system structures shown in this inferior brain illustration.
facts.explanation: The 12 cranial nerves (CN I–XII) emerge directly from the brain and brainstem. Mnemonic for their nature (S=sensory, M=motor, B=both): 'Some Say Marry Money But My Brother Says Big Brains Matter More' (CN I–XII: S,S,M,M,B,B,B,S,B,M,B,B). Key clinical correlations: CN III palsy (posterior communicating artery aneurysm, herniation — 'down and out' eye with ptosis and mydriasis); CN VII palsy (Bell's palsy, Lyme disease, parotid tumor); CN X (vagus, parasympathetic to thoracoabdominal organs).
facts.visualDescription: Inferior view of the brain base showing the 12 cranial nerve origins with labels

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the deep brain structure shown in this midsagittal illustration.
facts.explanation: The diencephalon consists of the thalamus, hypothalamus, epithalamus (pineal gland, habenula), and subthalamus. The thalamus is the relay station for all sensory information (except smell) going to the cortex; it also relays motor information from the basal ganglia and cerebellum. The hypothalamus controls the autonomic nervous system, endocrine function (via pituitary), temperature regulation, appetite, and circadian rhythms. The pineal gland produces melatonin for sleep-wake cycles. Parinaud's syndrome (vertical gaze palsy, loss of accommodation, convergence nystagmus) results from pineal mass compressing the superior colliculus.
facts.visualDescription: Midsagittal section of the brain showing the diencephalon with thalamus, hypothalamus, and epithalamus labeled

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the nerve cell structure shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Myelinated neurons have their axons wrapped by myelin sheaths, formed by Schwann cells (PNS) or oligodendrocytes (CNS). Nodes of Ranvier are gaps between adjacent myelin segments where the axon membrane is exposed and voltage-gated Na+ channels are concentrated, enabling saltatory conduction (signal jumps node to node), greatly increasing conduction velocity. Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune demyelinating disease destroying oligodendrocytes in the CNS, causing plaques of demyelination with slowed conduction. Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) demyelinates peripheral nerves.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a myelinated neuron showing axon, myelin sheath segments, nodes of Ranvier, and terminal boutons

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the peripheral nerve networks shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The major nerve plexuses are formed by the ventral rami of spinal nerves: cervical plexus (C1–C4 — phrenic nerve to diaphragm, cutaneous neck/shoulder), brachial plexus (C5–T1 — all upper limb innervation), lumbar plexus (L1–L4 — femoral, obturator nerves), and sacral plexus (L4–S3 — sciatic nerve, largest nerve in the body). Erb's palsy (upper trunk C5–C6 injury — 'waiter's tip') and Klumpke's palsy (lower trunk C8–T1 — claw hand, Horner syndrome) are brachial plexus injuries seen in birth trauma.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration showing the major peripheral nerve plexuses including the brachial plexus, lumbar plexus, and sacral plexus

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the cross-sectional structure shown in this nerve illustration.
facts.explanation: A peripheral nerve is organized into nested layers: individual nerve fibers (axons with myelin) wrapped by endoneurium; bundles of fibers (fascicles) wrapped by perineurium; and the entire nerve wrapped by epineurium. The perineurium forms a blood-nerve barrier analogous to the blood-brain barrier. Nerve injuries are classified by Seddon's system: neuropraxia (conduction block, intact axon — best prognosis), axonotmesis (axon disrupted, endoneurium intact — Wallerian degeneration but can regenerate), neurotmesis (complete nerve transection — requires surgical repair).
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional illustration of a peripheral nerve showing epineurium, perineurium, endoneurium, fascicles, and individual myelinated fibers

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the fundamental unit of the nervous system shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: A neuron consists of a cell body (soma, containing nucleus and organelles), dendrites (short branched processes receiving inputs — contain Nissl bodies/rough ER), and a single axon (conducts impulses away from soma, wrapped in myelin). The axon hillock is where action potentials are initiated (lowest threshold). Synaptic terminals at the axon endings release neurotransmitters. Neurons cannot regenerate after death (with limited exceptions in olfactory epithelium and hippocampus). ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis) destroys both upper and lower motor neurons, causing progressive weakness without sensory loss.
facts.visualDescription: Classic illustration of a multipolar neuron showing soma, multiple dendrites, axon hillock, myelinated axon, and terminal boutons

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the classification of nerve cells shown in this comparative illustration.
facts.explanation: Neurons are classified by number of processes: unipolar (one process — dorsal root ganglion sensory neurons in adults, really pseudounipolar with one process that divides into central and peripheral branches), bipolar (two processes — retinal bipolar cells, vestibular and cochlear neurons), and multipolar (one axon + multiple dendrites — most CNS neurons, motor neurons). They can also be classified functionally: sensory (afferent), motor (efferent), and interneurons (association). Interneurons constitute ~99% of all neurons.
facts.visualDescription: Comparative illustration showing the structural differences between unipolar, bipolar, and multipolar neuron types

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the non-neuronal cells of the peripheral nervous system shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: PNS glial cells include Schwann cells and satellite cells. Schwann cells myelinate peripheral axons (one Schwann cell per myelin segment, unlike oligodendrocytes which myelinate multiple axons). They also guide peripheral nerve regeneration after injury. Satellite cells surround neuron cell bodies in dorsal root and autonomic ganglia, providing metabolic support. Schwannomas (benign Schwann cell tumors) most commonly affect the vestibulocochlear nerve (acoustic neuroma / CN VIII), causing unilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Neurofibromatosis type 1 (von Recklinghausen's) involves multiple peripheral nerve neurofibromas (Schwann cell + fibroblast).
facts.visualDescription: Illustration showing Schwann cells wrapping peripheral nerve axons and satellite cells surrounding neuronal somata in ganglia

acceptableAlternatives: Gray and white matter of the spinal cord
facts.quizQuestion: Identify the CNS structure shown in this cross-sectional illustration.
facts.explanation: The spinal cord cross-section has a butterfly-shaped central gray matter (H-shape) surrounded by white matter. The gray matter dorsal horns contain sensory neurons; ventral horns contain motor neurons (alpha and gamma motor neurons); lateral horns (T1–L2 and S2–S4) contain autonomic preganglionic neurons. White matter columns carry ascending (sensory) and descending (motor) tracts. Key tracts: dorsal columns (ipsilateral fine touch, proprioception), spinothalamic tract (contralateral pain/temperature), corticospinal tract (ipsilateral motor). Brown-Séquard syndrome (hemisection) causes ipsilateral motor loss + proprioception loss with contralateral pain/temperature loss below the lesion.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-sectional illustration of the spinal cord showing butterfly-shaped gray matter, white matter columns, and labeled tracts

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the CNS glial cell shown in this Servier illustration.
facts.explanation: Astrocytes are the most abundant cells in the CNS, identifiable by their star-like shape with numerous radiating processes. Functions: (1) Blood-brain barrier maintenance — astrocytic endfeet cover >99% of brain capillary surface; (2) Neurotransmitter recycling (glutamate-glutamine cycle, GABA uptake); (3) Potassium buffering; (4) Metabolic support via lactate shuttle; (5) Reactive astrogliosis and glial scar formation after injury. GFAP (glial fibrillary acidic protein) is the astrocyte marker. Astrocytomas (WHO grade I–IV) range from pilocytic astrocytoma (benign, children) to glioblastoma multiforme (GBM, most malignant).
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art illustration of an astrocyte showing its characteristic star shape with processes contacting both neurons and blood vessels

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the brain structure shown in this Servier medical illustration.
facts.explanation: The cerebellum ('little brain') lies in the posterior fossa and connects to the brainstem via three cerebellar peduncles: superior (output to thalamus/red nucleus), middle (input from pons/cortex), and inferior (input/output from medulla/spinal cord). Its cortex has three layers: molecular, Purkinje cell (only output), and granular. Purkinje cells project inhibitory signals to deep cerebellar nuclei (dentate, interposed, fastigial). The cerebellum fine-tunes movement by comparing intended vs. actual motor output (comparator function). Medulloblastoma, a malignant tumor arising from cerebellar germinal matrix, is the most common brain tumor in children.
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art illustration of the cerebellum showing its folia, vermis, and hemispheres from a posterior or lateral perspective

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the cerebrovascular anastomosis shown in this Servier illustration.
facts.explanation: The Circle of Willis ensures collateral cerebral blood supply by connecting the anterior and posterior circulations. The anterior circulation (internal carotid arteries → ACAs via ACoA) supplies the frontal, parietal, and medial temporal lobes. The posterior circulation (vertebral arteries → basilar artery → PCAs via PCoAs) supplies the occipital lobe, brainstem, and cerebellum. Anatomical variants are common — a complete circle is present in only ~20–25% of individuals. PCoA aneurysm is a classic cause of a painful CN III palsy (blown pupil) — an emergency requiring neurosurgical intervention.
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art inferior view of the brain showing the Circle of Willis with arterial components labeled

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the CNS fluid circulation system shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) circulation. CSF (~150 mL total, produced at ~500 mL/day) is produced by choroid plexus in the lateral ventricles, flows through the interventricular foramina of Monro into the third ventricle, through the cerebral aqueduct (aqueduct of Sylvius) into the fourth ventricle, through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie into the subarachnoid space, and is reabsorbed by arachnoid granulations into the dural venous sinuses. CSF pressure is ~10–15 cmH2O. Communicating hydrocephalus results from impaired reabsorption; non-communicating (obstructive) from obstruction within the ventricular system (e.g., aqueductal stenosis).
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art sagittal illustration showing the ventricular system and CSF flow pathway from production through reabsorption

facts.quizQuestion: Identify the largest peripheral nerve shown in this illustration.
facts.explanation: The sciatic nerve (L4–S3) is the largest and longest peripheral nerve in the body (~2 cm wide at its origin). It exits the greater sciatic foramen below the piriformis muscle, descends through the posterior thigh, and divides at the popliteal fossa into the tibial nerve (innervates posterior leg and sole) and common fibular (peroneal) nerve (innervates anterior and lateral leg and dorsum of foot). Piriformis syndrome compresses the sciatic nerve, causing sciatica. The most common cause of true sciatica is lumbar disc herniation (most commonly L4/L5 or L5/S1 levels), compressing the nerve root.
facts.visualDescription: Servier medical art illustration of the sciatic nerve tracing from the pelvis through the posterior thigh to its bifurcation at the knee

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Lungs (gross anatomy)?
facts.explanation: Gross anatomy of the lungs showing lobes, fissures, and surface landmarks including the hilum where bronchi and vessels enter.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Pleura (parietal and visceral layers)?
facts.explanation: The pleura consists of two serous membrane layers: the visceral pleura adhering to the lung surface and the parietal pleura lining the thoracic wall, with the pleural cavity between them.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Adrenal glands (suprarenal glands)?
facts.explanation: The adrenal (suprarenal) glands sit atop each kidney and consist of an outer cortex (producing steroid hormones) and an inner medulla (producing catecholamines).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Thyroid gland (with parathyroid glands)?
facts.explanation: The thyroid gland is a butterfly-shaped endocrine gland in the anterior neck with two lobes connected by an isthmus. The four parathyroid glands are embedded on its posterior surface.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Optic pathways (optic chiasm, optic tract)?
facts.explanation: The optic pathways carry visual information from the retina through the optic nerves, optic chiasm (where nasal fibers decussate), optic tracts, lateral geniculate nucleus, and optic radiations to the visual cortex.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Retina (layers and photoreceptors)?
facts.explanation: The retina contains 10 layers including photoreceptors (rods and cones), bipolar cells, and ganglion cells whose axons form the optic nerve. The macula lutea contains the fovea centralis for highest visual acuity.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Anterior neck muscles (infrahyoid and suprahyoid groups)?
facts.explanation: The anterior neck muscles include the suprahyoid group (digastric, mylohyoid, geniohyoid, stylohyoid) and infrahyoid strap muscles (sternohyoid, sternothyroid, thyrohyoid, omohyoid) that move the hyoid bone and larynx.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Extraocular muscles (six muscles of the orbit)?
facts.explanation: The six extraocular muscles (superior/inferior/medial/lateral rectus and superior/inferior oblique) move the eyeball. The levator palpebrae superioris is often shown as a seventh muscle that elevates the upper eyelid.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Facial muscles (muscles of facial expression)?
facts.explanation: The muscles of facial expression are derived from the second pharyngeal arch (CN VII) and include orbicularis oculi, orbicularis oris, zygomaticus major/minor, buccinator, frontalis, and many others that move skin rather than bone.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Intrinsic muscles of the foot (plantar layers)?
facts.explanation: The intrinsic foot muscles are organized in four plantar layers. They include the flexor digitorum brevis, quadratus plantae, lumbricals, and interossei, which support the longitudinal arch and assist toe movements.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Forearm muscles (flexor and extensor compartments)?
facts.explanation: The forearm contains an anterior (flexor/pronator) compartment and a posterior (extensor/supinator) compartment, separated by the interosseous membrane. Key muscles include flexor carpi radialis, pronator teres, extensor carpi radialis, and supinator.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Gluteal muscles (gluteus maximus, medius, minimus)?
facts.explanation: The gluteal muscles form the buttock. Gluteus maximus is the largest and strongest hip extensor. Gluteus medius and minimus are hip abductors and medial rotators. The deep lateral rotators (piriformis, obturator internus/externus, gemelli, quadratus femoris) lie deep to gluteus maximus.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Intrinsic muscles of the hand (thenar, hypothenar, lumbricals, interossei)?
facts.explanation: The intrinsic hand muscles include the thenar group (abductor/flexor/opponens pollicis, adductor pollicis), hypothenar group (abductor/flexor/opponens digiti minimi), four lumbricals, and seven interossei (3 palmar, 4 dorsal).
acceptableAlternatives: Intrinsic muscles of the hand (thenar, hypothenar, lumbricals, interossei)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Jaw muscles (muscles of mastication)?
facts.explanation: The four muscles of mastication are the masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid, and lateral pterygoid, all innervated by the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V3). They close the jaw (elevation), protrude, retract, and grind.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Leg muscles (anterior, posterior, and lateral compartments)?
facts.explanation: The leg has three compartments: anterior (tibialis anterior, extensor digitorum longus, extensor hallucis longus — dorsiflexion), posterior (gastrocnemius, soleus, tibialis posterior — plantarflexion), and lateral (fibularis longus and brevis — eversion). Shows anterior, posterior, and lateral muscle compartments of the leg.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Motor end plate (neuromuscular junction)?
facts.explanation: The motor end plate is the specialized region of the muscle fiber membrane (sarcolemma) where the motor neuron terminal synapses. Acetylcholine released from synaptic vesicles binds nicotinic receptors, triggering an end-plate potential and muscle contraction.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Muscle fiber structure (myofibrils, T-tubules, sarcoplasmic reticulum)?
facts.explanation: A skeletal muscle fiber contains parallel myofibrils surrounded by the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR), a specialized ER that stores and releases Ca²⁺. T-tubules (transverse tubules) invaginate the sarcolemma and contact the SR at triads to transmit the action potential deep into the fiber.
acceptableAlternatives: Muscle fiber structure (myofibrils, T-tubules, sarcoplasmic reticulum)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Neck and back muscles (erector spinae and deep posterior group)?
facts.explanation: The posterior neck and back muscles include the superficial erector spinae column (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis), the transversospinalis group (semispinalis, multifidus, rotatores), and the suboccipital muscles. They extend and stabilize the vertebral column. Shows erector spinae and deep posterior muscle groups.
acceptableAlternatives: Neck and back muscles (erector spinae and deep posterior group)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Pectoral girdle muscles (pectoralis major/minor, serratus anterior, trapezius)?
facts.explanation: Muscles acting on the pectoral girdle stabilize and move the scapula and clavicle. Key muscles include pectoralis minor (protracts/depresses scapula), serratus anterior (protracts/rotates scapula upward), and trapezius (elevates, retracts, and rotates scapula). Shows pec major/minor, serratus anterior, and trapezius.
acceptableAlternatives: Pectoral girdle muscles (pectoralis major/minor, serratus anterior, trapezius)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Posterior neck muscles (suboccipital triangle and semispinalis group)?
facts.explanation: The posterior neck muscles include the suboccipital muscles (rectus capitis posterior major/minor, obliquus capitis superior/inferior) forming the suboccipital triangle, and the semispinalis capitis/cervicis group. They extend, rotate, and tilt the head. Shows suboccipital triangle and semispinalis muscle group.
acceptableAlternatives: Posterior neck muscles (suboccipital triangle and semispinalis group)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Sarcomere (Z-line, A-band, I-band, H-zone)?
facts.explanation: The sarcomere is the functional unit of skeletal muscle, bounded by Z-lines. The A-band (dark) contains thick myosin filaments and overlapping actin. The I-band (light) contains only thin actin filaments. The H-zone in the center of the A-band contains only myosin; the M-line bisects it.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Appendicular skeleton (bones of upper and lower limbs plus girdles)?
facts.explanation: The appendicular skeleton comprises 126 bones: the pectoral girdles (clavicles and scapulae), upper limbs (humeri, radii, ulnae, carpals, metacarpals, phalanges), pelvic girdle (hip bones), and lower limbs (femora, patellae, tibiae, fibulae, tarsals, metatarsals, phalanges).
acceptableAlternatives: Appendicular skeleton (bones of upper and lower limbs plus girdles)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Bone blood supply (nutrient artery, periosteal vessels, metaphyseal vessels)?
facts.explanation: Long bone blood supply arrives via a nutrient artery entering the diaphysis through the nutrient foramen, periosteal arteries supplying the cortex, and metaphyseal/epiphyseal vessels. Venous drainage parallels arterial supply. Disruption can cause avascular necrosis. Shows nutrient artery, periosteal vessels, and metaphyseal vessels supplying bone.
acceptableAlternatives: Bone blood supply (nutrient artery, periosteal vessels, metaphyseal vessels)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Bone cells (osteoblast, osteocyte, osteoclast, osteogenic cell)?
facts.explanation: Four main bone cell types: osteogenic cells (stem cells in periosteum/endosteum), osteoblasts (secrete osteoid matrix, become trapped as osteocytes), osteocytes (mature cells in lacunae maintaining matrix), and osteoclasts (multinucleated macrophage derivatives that resorb bone via acidification).
acceptableAlternatives: Bone cells (osteoblast, osteocyte, osteoclast, osteogenic cell)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Bone surface markings (processes, fossae, foramina, facets)?
facts.explanation: Bone markings are surface features that indicate sites of muscle attachment, joint articulation, or neurovascular passage. Projections include processes, trochanters, tuberosities, and tubercles. Depressions include fossae, grooves, and sulci. Openings include foramina and canals. Shows processes, fossae, foramina, and facets as key bone surface markings.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Carpal tunnel (transverse carpal ligament and contents)?
facts.explanation: The carpal tunnel is an osseofibrous passage at the wrist formed by the carpal bones posteriorly and the transverse carpal ligament (flexor retinaculum) anteriorly. It transmits the median nerve and nine flexor tendons (flexor digitorum superficialis ×4, profundus ×4, flexor pollicis longus).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Cervical vertebrae (atlas, axis, and typical C3–C7)?
facts.explanation: The seven cervical vertebrae are distinguished by bifid spinous processes (C2–C6) and transverse foramina for the vertebral arteries. C1 (atlas) has no body or spinous process; C2 (axis) has the dens (odontoid process); C7 (vertebra prominens) has the longest non-bifid spinous process.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Compact bone (osteon/Haversian system structure)?
facts.explanation: Compact bone is organized into cylindrical osteons (Haversian systems). Each osteon consists of concentric lamellae surrounding a central Haversian canal carrying blood vessels. Osteocytes in lacunae communicate via canaliculi. Perforating (Volkmann's) canals run perpendicular, connecting Haversian canals.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Femur and patella (anterior and posterior surfaces)?
facts.explanation: The femur is the longest bone in the body. Proximal landmarks include the head, neck, greater and lesser trochanters, and intertrochanteric line/crest. Distal landmarks include the medial and lateral condyles and epicondyles. The patella is a sesamoid bone in the quadriceps tendon.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Flat bone structure (inner/outer compact tables with diplöe)?
facts.explanation: Flat bones (skull, sternum, ribs, scapula) consist of two layers of compact bone (outer and inner tables) sandwiching a layer of spongy bone (diploë). The diploë contains red bone marrow. The inner table of the calvaria is thinner and more brittle. Shows inner and outer compact tables with diplöe (spongy bone) between them.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Foot bones (tarsals, metatarsals, and phalanges)?
facts.explanation: The foot contains 26 bones: 7 tarsals (calcaneus, talus, navicular, cuboid, 3 cuneiforms), 5 metatarsals, and 14 phalanges (2 in the hallux, 3 in each lesser toe). The calcaneus is the largest tarsal bone.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Hand and wrist bones (carpals, metacarpals, and phalanges)?
facts.explanation: The hand contains 27 bones: 8 carpals in two rows (proximal: scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum, pisiform; distal: trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, hamate), 5 metacarpals, and 14 phalanges (2 in the thumb, 3 in each finger). Shows carpals, metacarpals, and phalanges of the hand.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Hip bone (ilium, ischium, pubis, and acetabulum)?
facts.explanation: The hip (coxal) bone is formed by fusion of three bones at the acetabulum: the ilium (superior, forms the iliac crest and greater sciatic notch), ischium (posteroinferior, forms the ischial tuberosity and spine), and pubis (anterior, forms the pubic symphysis).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Humerus and elbow joint (epicondyles, condyles, olecranon fossa)?
facts.explanation: The humerus articulates at the elbow with the radius (capitulum) and ulna (trochlea). Key landmarks include the medial and lateral epicondyles, olecranon fossa (posterior), coronoid fossa (anterior), radial groove (spiral groove for radial nerve), and deltoid tuberosity. Shows epicondyles, condyles, and olecranon fossa of the humerus and elbow.
acceptableAlternatives: Humerus and elbow joint (epicondyles, condyles, olecranon fossa)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Intervertebral disc (nucleus pulposus and annulus fibrosus)?
facts.explanation: The intervertebral disc is a fibrocartilaginous secondary cartilaginous joint. The central nucleus pulposus (gelatinous, high water content) absorbs compressive loads. The annulus fibrosus (concentric fibrocartilage lamellae) resists tension. Herniation occurs when the nucleus pulposus protrudes through a tear in the annulus. Shows nucleus pulposus (gelatinous center) and annulus fibrosus (fibrous ring).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Long bone anatomy (diaphysis, epiphysis, metaphysis, medullary cavity)?
facts.explanation: A long bone consists of the diaphysis (shaft of compact bone enclosing the medullary cavity), two epiphyses (articular ends of spongy bone covered by hyaline cartilage), the metaphysis (flared zone between diaphysis and epiphysis containing the growth plate in children), periosteum (outer fibrous layer), and endosteum (inner lining of medullary cavity). Shows diaphysis, epiphysis, metaphysis, medullary cavity, and related structures.
acceptableAlternatives: Long bone anatomy (diaphysis, epiphysis, metaphysis, medullary cavity)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Lumbar vertebra (large body, transverse and spinous processes, no costal facets)?
facts.explanation: Lumbar vertebrae (L1–L5) are the largest moveable vertebrae and bear the most body weight. They are distinguished by their kidney-shaped bodies, short stout spinous processes projecting horizontally, absence of costal facets, and lack of transverse foramina. Mammillary processes are unique to lumbar vertebrae.
acceptableAlternatives: Lumbar vertebra (large body, transverse and spinous processes, no costal facets)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Male and female pelvis comparison (anatomical differences)?
facts.explanation: The female pelvis is adapted for childbirth: wider pelvic inlet (oval vs. heart-shaped), larger subpubic angle (>80° vs. <70°), more circular pubic arch, wider greater sciatic notch, and a more shallow overall structure. The male pelvis is narrower and deeper with a more prominent sacral promontory. Shows anatomical differences between male (narrower) and female (wider) pelvis.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Pectoral girdle (clavicle and scapula)?
facts.explanation: The pectoral girdle consists of two bones on each side: the clavicle (S-shaped, connecting sternum to scapula via the sternoclavicular and acromioclavicular joints) and the scapula (triangular flat bone with glenoid cavity, spine, acromion, coracoid process, and three angles).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Pelvis overview (pelvic inlet, outlet, and bony landmarks)?
facts.explanation: The pelvis is formed by two hip bones, the sacrum, and the coccyx. The pelvic brim (arcuate line, iliopectineal line, sacral promontory) divides the greater (false) pelvis above from the lesser (true) pelvis below. Key landmarks include the ischial spines, used to gauge fetal descent during labor. Shows pelvic inlet, outlet, and key bony landmarks of the pelvis.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Periosteum and endosteum (outer and inner bone membrane layers)?
facts.explanation: The periosteum is a fibrous connective tissue sheath covering the outer bone surface (except articular surfaces). It has an outer fibrous layer and an inner osteogenic layer with osteoprogenitor cells. The endosteum lines internal bony surfaces (medullary cavity, Haversian canals) and also contains osteoprogenitor cells. Shows outer periosteum and inner endosteum as bone membrane layers.
acceptableAlternatives: Periosteum and endosteum (outer and inner bone membrane layers)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Rib cage (thoracic cage with sternum, ribs, and costal cartilages)?
facts.explanation: The thoracic cage consists of 12 pairs of ribs, 12 thoracic vertebrae, and the sternum (manubrium, body, xiphoid process). Ribs 1–7 are true ribs (attach directly to sternum via costal cartilage); ribs 8–10 are false ribs (join costal cartilage of rib 7); ribs 11–12 are floating ribs with no anterior attachment.
acceptableAlternatives: Rib cage (thoracic cage with sternum, ribs, and costal cartilages)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Sacrum and coccyx (fused vertebral segments with foramina)?
facts.explanation: The sacrum is formed by fusion of 5 sacral vertebrae. It has an anterior (pelvic) surface with anterior sacral foramina for ventral rami, and a posterior surface with dorsal sacral foramina for dorsal rami. The sacral promontory (S1 superior edge) is the most prominent point of the pelvic brim. The coccyx consists of 3–5 fused rudimentary vertebrae. Shows fused vertebral segments with sacral foramina and the coccyx.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Scapula (spine, acromion, coracoid process, glenoid cavity)?
facts.explanation: The scapula is a triangular flat bone with three angles (superior, inferior, lateral), three borders (superior, medial/vertebral, lateral/axillary), and key processes: the spine (dividing supraspinous and infraspinous fossae), the acromion (roof of shoulder), and the coracoid process (attachment for pectoralis minor, short head of biceps, coracobrachialis). Shows spine, acromion, coracoid process, and glenoid cavity of the scapula.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Spongy (cancellous) bone (trabeculae and red bone marrow)?
facts.explanation: Spongy (cancellous) bone consists of an interconnecting lattice of bony trabeculae (plates and rods of bone tissue) with spaces filled by red bone marrow in actively hematopoietic sites. The trabeculae align along lines of mechanical stress, providing strength with minimal mass. It is found in epiphyses and diplöe.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Synovial joint structure (articular cartilage, synovial membrane, joint capsule)?
facts.explanation: A synovial (diarthrosis) joint is characterized by a joint cavity filled with synovial fluid, articular cartilage (hyaline) covering the bone ends, a fibrous joint capsule lined by synovial membrane, and accessory structures (ligaments, menisci, bursae). It allows the greatest range of motion.
acceptableAlternatives: Synovial joint structure (articular cartilage, synovial membrane, joint capsule)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Synovial joint types (ball-and-socket, hinge, pivot, condyloid, saddle, plane)?
facts.explanation: The six types of synovial joints are classified by the shape of their articular surfaces and the movements they permit: plane (gliding), hinge (uniaxial flexion/extension), pivot (uniaxial rotation), condyloid (biaxial: flexion/extension + abduction/adduction), saddle (biaxial + opposition), and ball-and-socket (multiaxial). Shows hinge, ball-and-socket, pivot, and condyloid synovial joint examples.
acceptableAlternatives: Synovial joint types (ball-and-socket, hinge, pivot, condyloid, saddle, plane)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Thoracic vertebra (costal facets on body and transverse process)?
facts.explanation: Thoracic vertebrae (T1–T12) are distinguished by costal facets (demifacets on the body for articulation with rib heads) and costal facets on the transverse processes (for rib tubercle articulation, T1–T10). Their spinous processes are long and angled sharply inferiorly. The vertebral foramen is roughly circular and smaller than cervical vertebrae.
acceptableAlternatives: Thoracic vertebra (costal facets on body and transverse process)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Tibia and fibula (medial and lateral leg bones with landmarks)?
facts.explanation: The tibia (medial leg bone) is the weight-bearing bone with the tibial plateau (medial/lateral condyles), tibial tuberosity (patellar tendon attachment), medial malleolus (ankle mortise), and anterior border (shin). The fibula (lateral leg bone) is slender and non-weight-bearing, with the fibular head (peroneal nerve at risk) and lateral malleolus.
acceptableAlternatives: Tibia and fibula (medial and lateral leg bones with landmarks)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Ulna and radius (forearm bones with interosseous membrane)?
facts.explanation: The ulna (medial forearm bone) has the olecranon process (forms the elbow point), coronoid process, trochlear notch, and styloid process. The radius (lateral forearm bone) has the radial head (articulates with capitulum), radial tuberosity (biceps tendon), and a broader distal end. The interosseous membrane connects them and transmits forces. Shows forearm bones with interosseous membrane between them.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Typical vertebra (body, arch, pedicles, laminae, processes)?
facts.explanation: A typical vertebra consists of a body (weight-bearing), a vertebral arch (formed by two pedicles and two laminae), and seven processes: one spinous process (posterior), two transverse processes (lateral), and four articular processes (two superior, two inferior) forming zygapophyseal (facet) joints. Shows body, vertebral arch, pedicles, laminae, and various processes.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Vertebral column (cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal regions with curvatures)?
facts.explanation: The adult vertebral column has 33 vertebrae (7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 fused sacral, 4 fused coccygeal) and four curvatures: cervical lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis (secondary curvatures that develop postnatally), and sacral kyphosis (primary curvature present at birth).
acceptableAlternatives: Vertebral column (cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal regions with curvatures)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Osteoclast (multinucleated bone-resorbing cell with ruffled border)?
facts.explanation: Osteoclasts are large multinucleated cells (up to 50 nuclei) derived from monocyte/macrophage precursors. They resorb bone by creating a sealed acidic microenvironment via the ruffled border (extensive membrane infolding), secreting H⁺ (via V-ATPase) and cathepsin K to dissolve mineral and matrix. Bisphosphonates inhibit osteoclast function.
acceptableAlternatives: Osteoclast (multinucleated bone-resorbing cell with ruffled border)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Osteocyte (mature bone cell within lacuna, connected via canaliculi)?
facts.explanation: Osteocytes are former osteoblasts that have become embedded in the mineralized bone matrix within lacunae. They extend long cytoplasmic processes through canaliculi that form gap junctions with neighboring osteocytes, creating a syncytial network for mechanosensing, Ca²⁺ signaling, and communication with surface osteoblasts and osteoclasts.
acceptableAlternatives: Osteocyte (mature bone cell within lacuna, connected via canaliculi)

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the accessory digestive organs?
facts.explanation: The accessory digestive organs — liver, gallbladder, and pancreas — support digestion without food passing through them. The liver produces bile, which emulsifies dietary fats. Bile is stored and concentrated in the gallbladder, then released into the duodenum via the common bile duct. The pancreas secretes digestive enzymes (amylase, lipase, proteases) and bicarbonate into the duodenum. Gallstones form when bile becomes supersaturated with cholesterol, a key USMLE clinical correlation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Esophagus (clinical detail view)?
facts.explanation: The esophagus is a muscular tube approximately 25 cm long connecting the pharynx to the stomach. It passes through the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm. Its wall layers (mucosa, submucosa, muscularis, adventitia) propel food via peristalsis. The upper third contains skeletal muscle, the lower third smooth muscle, and the middle third a mixture. The lower esophageal sphincter (LES) prevents gastroesophageal reflux; LES incompetence underlies GERD.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the gallbladder in detail?
facts.explanation: The gallbladder is a pear-shaped sac on the inferior surface of the liver that stores and concentrates bile up to 10-fold. Bile enters via the cystic duct and is released into the common bile duct in response to cholecystokinin (CCK) from the duodenum. The gallbladder wall consists of mucosa, muscularis, and serosa. Acute cholecystitis typically results from cystic duct obstruction by gallstones and presents with right upper quadrant pain (Murphy's sign).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the large intestine?
facts.explanation: The large intestine (~1.5 m) consists of the cecum, colon (ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid), rectum, and anal canal. Its primary role is water and electrolyte absorption and compaction of feces. Distinguishing features include haustra (pouches), taeniae coli (three longitudinal muscle bands), and epiploic appendages (fat tags). The ileocecal valve separates the small from large intestine. Colorectal cancer most commonly arises in the sigmoid and rectum.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows liver histology?
facts.explanation: The classic hepatic lobule is a hexagonal unit with a central vein at its core and portal triads (portal vein branch, hepatic artery branch, bile ductule) at the corners. Hepatocytes radiate as cords between sinusoids lined with Kupffer cells (resident macrophages). Blood flows from portal triads inward to the central vein; bile flows outward to bile ductules. Zone 1 (periportal) is most oxygenated; Zone 3 (centrilobular) is most susceptible to ischemia and acetaminophen toxicity.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the oral cavity structures?
facts.explanation: The oral cavity includes the lips, cheeks, hard and soft palate, uvula, tongue, teeth, and gingiva. The vestibule lies between the cheeks/lips and the teeth; the oral cavity proper lies posterior to the teeth. The hard palate (palatine processes of maxilla + horizontal plates of palatine bones) separates the oral and nasal cavities. The soft palate, bearing the uvula, elevates during swallowing to prevent nasopharyngeal regurgitation. Cleft palate results from failed fusion of palatal shelves.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the pancreas with its exocrine and endocrine components?
facts.explanation: The pancreas has both exocrine (acinar cells, ~95% of mass) and endocrine (islets of Langerhans, ~2%) components. Acinar cells secrete digestive enzymes into ducts emptying at the ampulla of Vater. Islets contain alpha cells (glucagon), beta cells (insulin), delta cells (somatostatin), and PP cells (pancreatic polypeptide). Destruction of beta cells by autoimmune attack causes Type 1 diabetes mellitus. Pancreatic head carcinoma classically obstructs the common bile duct, causing painless jaundice.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the pharynx?
facts.explanation: The pharynx is a funnel-shaped musculomembranous tube divided into three parts: nasopharynx (posterior to nasal cavity, contains pharyngeal tonsil and eustachian tube openings), oropharynx (posterior to oral cavity, contains palatine tonsils), and laryngopharynx/hypopharynx (opens into both the larynx anteriorly and esophagus posteriorly). During swallowing, the epiglottis folds over the laryngeal inlet to prevent aspiration. Peritonsillar abscess is a complication of tonsillitis, often caused by Streptococcus pyogenes.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the major salivary glands?
facts.explanation: The three paired major salivary glands produce ~1–1.5 L of saliva daily. The parotid glands (serous secretion) drain via Stensen's duct opposite the upper second molar. The submandibular glands (mixed, predominantly serous) drain via Wharton's duct at the sublingual caruncle. The sublingual glands (mixed, predominantly mucous) drain via multiple ducts. Saliva contains amylase, lingual lipase, mucins, IgA, and lysozyme. Mumps virus preferentially infects the parotid glands; Sjögren syndrome causes autoimmune destruction of salivary and lacrimal glands.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Small intestine (clinical pathology view)?
facts.explanation: The small intestine (~6–7 m) is the primary site of nutrient digestion and absorption. It is divided into the duodenum (25 cm; receives bile and pancreatic juices), jejunum (~2.4 m; major absorption site), and ileum (~3.5 m; absorbs vitamin B12 and bile salts). Surface area is amplified ~600-fold by plicae circulares, villi, and microvilli. Celiac disease (gluten-induced autoimmune enteropathy) destroys villi, causing malabsorption. Meckel's diverticulum is the most common congenital GI anomaly, found in the ileum.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows small intestine histology?
facts.explanation: Small intestinal histology shows finger-like villi projecting into the lumen, each with a core of lamina propria containing a lacteal (lymphatic capillary for fat absorption) and blood capillaries. Crypts of Lieberkühn lie between villi and harbor intestinal stem cells, Paneth cells (antimicrobial secretion), and goblet cells (mucus). The enterocyte brush border (microvilli) expresses disaccharidases and peptidases. Lactase deficiency causes lactose intolerance; brush border enzyme loss occurs in rotavirus infection.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Stomach (clinical detail view)?
facts.explanation: The stomach is a J-shaped muscular organ in the left upper quadrant with four regions: cardia (near esophageal junction), fundus (dome above cardia), body (main portion), and pylorus (distal funnel leading to duodenum). Its muscularis has three layers (oblique, circular, longitudinal) for churning. Parietal cells secrete HCl and intrinsic factor; chief cells secrete pepsinogen. H. pylori infection (colonizes antrum) causes most peptic ulcers. Intrinsic factor deficiency causes pernicious anemia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows stomach histology?
facts.explanation: Stomach histology reveals gastric pits that open into gastric glands. The mucosa is lined by surface mucous cells secreting protective alkaline mucus. Gastric glands contain mucous neck cells, parietal cells (eosinophilic, HCl and intrinsic factor), chief cells (basophilic, pepsinogen), and enteroendocrine cells (G cells in antrum secrete gastrin; D cells secrete somatostatin). The thick muscularis externa has three layers. NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandins, disrupting the mucus-bicarbonate barrier and predisposing to ulcers.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows dental anatomy (teeth overview)?
facts.explanation: Adult dentition comprises 32 permanent teeth: 8 incisors, 4 canines, 8 premolars, and 12 molars (including 4 third molars/wisdom teeth). Each tooth type has a distinct morphology suited to its function — incisors cut, canines tear, premolars and molars grind. Primary (deciduous) dentition has 20 teeth. Each tooth has a crown (visible), neck (at gum line), and root (embedded in alveolar bone). Third molar impaction is the most common dental surgical procedure.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the tongue anatomy?
facts.explanation: The tongue is a muscular organ with intrinsic (within tongue) and extrinsic muscles (genioglossus, hyoglossus, styloglossus, palatoglossus). The dorsal surface has four types of papillae: filiform (most numerous, no taste buds), fungiform (scattered, taste buds), circumvallate/vallate (row of ~7–12 at junction of anterior 2/3 and posterior 1/3, taste buds), and foliate (lateral edges). Taste (CN VII anterior 2/3; CN IX posterior 1/3) and general sensation (CN V3 anterior 2/3; CN IX posterior 1/3) are carried by distinct nerves.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a tooth cross-section?
facts.explanation: A cross-sectioned tooth reveals enamel (hardest substance in the body, produced by ameloblasts) covering the crown; dentin (mineralized connective tissue produced by odontoblasts) beneath the enamel; pulp cavity (loose connective tissue, vessels, nerves) in the center; cementum (covering the root, produced by cementoblasts) anchoring to the periodontal ligament; and the root canal carrying neurovascular structures to the apical foramen. Dental caries (tooth decay) begins with demineralization of enamel by bacterial acid, progressing to dentin and pulp.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the rectum?
facts.explanation: The rectum (approximately 12–15 cm) connects the sigmoid colon to the anal canal. It has three lateral curves creating three transverse folds (Houston's valves).
