The anal canal (~4 cm) has an internal anal sphincter (smooth muscle, involuntary) and external anal sphincter (skeletal muscle, voluntary). The pectinate (dentate) line divides the upper (visceral innervation, columnar epithelium) from the lower (somatic innervation, stratified squamous epithelium) anal canal. Hemorrhoids above the dentate line are internal; below are external.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the salivary glands (Servier illustration)?
facts.explanation: The three paired major salivary glands are the parotid (largest, located anterior to the ear, purely serous), submandibular (under the mandible floor, mixed with predominantly serous secretion), and sublingual (under the tongue, mixed with predominantly mucous secretion). The facial nerve (CN VII) passes through the parotid gland, making it vulnerable during parotid surgery. Sjögren syndrome is characterized by autoimmune destruction of salivary and lacrimal glands, producing dry mouth (xerostomia) and dry eyes (keratoconjunctivitis sicca).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a stomach cross-section?
facts.explanation: A cross-section of the stomach reveals its four wall layers: mucosa (gastric pits and glands), submucosa (connective tissue with vessels and Meissner's plexus), muscularis externa (three layers: inner oblique, middle circular, outer longitudinal — unique to the stomach — with Auerbach's plexus between the inner two), and serosa (visceral peritoneum). The thick muscularis allows vigorous churning to produce chyme. Hiatal hernias occur when the stomach herniates through the esophageal hiatus into the thorax.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the tongue (Servier illustration)?
facts.explanation: The tongue comprises intrinsic muscles (superior and inferior longitudinal, transverse, vertical — altering tongue shape) and extrinsic muscles (genioglossus, hyoglossus, styloglossus, palatoglossus — positioning the tongue). All tongue muscles are innervated by CN XII (hypoglossal nerve), except palatoglossus (CN X). The tongue is the primary organ of taste; taste buds on the circumvallate papillae are innervated by CN IX. A unilateral CN XII lesion causes the tongue to deviate toward the side of the lesion.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a tooth cross-section (Servier illustration)?
facts.explanation: The tooth's internal architecture includes enamel (96% mineral, hardest biological material), dentin (70% mineral, slightly elastic, produced throughout life by odontoblasts), pulp (central cavity with nerves and blood vessels through the root canal), cementum (covers the root, anchoring to alveolar bone via the periodontal ligament). The periodontal ligament acts as a shock absorber during chewing. Dental pulpitis causes severe pain; untreated, infection spreads apically, forming a periapical abscess requiring root canal treatment.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the juxtaglomerular apparatus and glomerulus?
facts.explanation: The juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) consists of three components: macula densa (specialized distal tubule cells sensing NaCl concentration), juxtaglomerular (granular) cells (modified smooth muscle in afferent arteriole wall, secreting renin), and extraglomerular mesangial (lacis) cells. Renin cleaves angiotensinogen → angiotensin I, initiating the RAAS cascade. The glomerulus is a tuft of capillaries enclosed in Bowman's capsule, driving ultrafiltration. Diabetic nephropathy causes glomerular basement membrane thickening and Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodular glomerulosclerosis.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows podocytes?
facts.explanation: Podocytes are highly specialized visceral epithelial cells that wrap around glomerular capillaries with interdigitating foot processes (pedicels). The narrow filtration slits between pedicels are bridged by slit diaphragms (nephrin, podocin) that are critical for selective filtration. Podocyte injury — whether by immune complexes, minimal change disease, or FSGS — leads to foot process effacement and proteinuria. Minimal change disease (lipoid nephrosis), the most common nephrotic syndrome in children, shows diffuse foot process effacement on electron microscopy.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the female urinary system?
facts.explanation: The female urinary system consists of the kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder, and urethra. Key difference from males: the female urethra is only ~4 cm long (vs ~20 cm in males), making women significantly more susceptible to ascending urinary tract infections (UTIs). Escherichia coli is the most common UTI pathogen. The bladder neck in females is supported by pubourethral ligaments; pelvic floor weakness can cause stress urinary incontinence. The trigone of the bladder (between ureteral orifices and internal urethral orifice) is embryologically distinct from the rest of the bladder.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the male urinary system?
facts.explanation: The male urinary system includes kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder, and a ~20 cm urethra. The male urethra passes through three regions: prostatic (passes through prostate, receives ejaculatory ducts), membranous (passes through urogenital diaphragm, site of external sphincter), and spongy/penile (traverses corpus spongiosum). Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — hyperplasia of the transitional zone — compresses the prostatic urethra, causing obstructive lower urinary tract symptoms. PSA screening detects prostate cancer arising most often from the peripheral zone.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a capillary?
facts.explanation: Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels (diameter 5–10 µm), consisting of a single layer of endothelium on a thin basement membrane. Three types exist: continuous (most common, tight junctions — muscle, skin, lung), fenestrated (pores for rapid exchange — kidney, intestinal villi, endocrine glands), and sinusoidal/discontinuous (large gaps — liver, spleen, bone marrow). Starling forces govern fluid exchange: hydrostatic pressure drives filtration at the arteriolar end; oncotic pressure drives reabsorption at the venular end. Edema results when these forces are imbalanced.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Circle of Willis?
facts.explanation: The Circle of Willis is an anastomotic ring of arteries at the base of the brain formed by the internal carotid arteries, anterior cerebral arteries (ACAs), anterior communicating artery (ACoA), posterior cerebral arteries (PCAs), and posterior communicating arteries (PCoAs). It provides collateral circulation between the anterior and posterior circulations. Berry aneurysms occur most commonly at bifurcation points, especially the ACoA (most common), MCA bifurcation, and PCoA junction. ACoA aneurysm rupture causes subarachnoid hemorrhage with possible visual field deficits.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows an erythrocyte (red blood cell)?
facts.explanation: Erythrocytes (RBCs) are biconcave discs (~7–8 µm diameter) lacking a nucleus and organelles, maximizing space for hemoglobin (~280 million molecules per cell). The biconcave shape maximizes surface area-to-volume ratio for gas exchange and allows deformation through narrow capillaries. RBC lifespan is ~120 days; senescent RBCs are destroyed in the spleen. Hemoglobin carries O2 (cooperative binding via T→R state shift) and CO2. Sickle cell disease (HbS mutation) causes RBCs to polymerize under low O2, producing vaso-occlusive crises.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the veins of the head and neck?
facts.explanation: Venous drainage of the head and neck includes the external and internal jugular veins. Cerebral blood drains via dural venous sinuses (superior sagittal, inferior sagittal, transverse, sigmoid) into the internal jugular vein. The external jugular vein drains the scalp and face into the subclavian vein. The internal jugular vein is used for central venous access. Elevated intracranial pressure is transmitted to the internal jugular, causing jugular venous distension (JVD). Cavernous sinus thrombosis is a dangerous complication of facial infections spreading via the facial and ophthalmic veins.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the mitral valve?
facts.explanation: The mitral (bicuspid) valve guards the left atrioventricular orifice, with two leaflets (anterior and posterior) attached to the left ventricular papillary muscles via chordae tendineae. During systole, it closes to prevent regurgitation into the left atrium. Mitral stenosis (often rheumatic fever sequela) restricts flow from LA to LV, causing LA enlargement, atrial fibrillation, and pulmonary hypertension. Mitral regurgitation produces a holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla. Mitral valve prolapse (myxomatous degeneration) causes a mid-systolic click.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a reticulocyte?
facts.explanation: Reticulocytes are immature red blood cells that have extruded their nucleus but retain residual ribosomal RNA (visible as a blue reticular network with new methylene blue staining). They circulate for ~1–2 days before maturing into erythrocytes. The reticulocyte count is a key marker of erythropoietic activity: elevated in hemolytic anemia or acute blood loss (regenerative anemia), and low in aplastic anemia or iron/B12/folate deficiency (hypoproliferative anemia). Erythropoietin (produced by peritubular cells in the kidney) drives reticulocyte production.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the systemic venous system?
facts.explanation: The systemic venous system returns deoxygenated blood to the right atrium via the superior vena cava (SVC) and inferior vena cava (IVC). The SVC drains the head, neck, upper limbs, and thorax. The IVC drains the abdomen, pelvis, and lower limbs. At any given time, about 65% of total blood volume resides in the venous system, making veins the primary capacitance (reservoir) vessels. Deep vein thrombosis (DVT), most commonly in the lower extremities, risks pulmonary embolism if a clot propagates and embolizes to the pulmonary circulation.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the basal nuclei (basal ganglia)?
facts.explanation: The basal nuclei (basal ganglia) include the caudate nucleus, putamen (together = striatum), globus pallidus (internal and external segments), subthalamic nucleus, and substantia nigra. They form circuits that modulate voluntary movement, motor learning, and habit formation via the direct (facilitatory) and indirect (inhibitory) pathways. Parkinson's disease results from loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, causing bradykinesia, tremor, rigidity, and postural instability. Huntington's disease preferentially destroys the striatum (caudate and putamen).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Brainstem (detailed structures view)?
facts.explanation: The brainstem consists of the midbrain (mesencephalon), pons, and medulla oblongata. The midbrain contains the cerebral peduncles, superior and inferior colliculi (visual and auditory reflexes), red nucleus, and substantia nigra. The pons relays signals between cerebrum and cerebellum and houses CN V–VIII nuclei and respiratory control centers. The medulla controls vital functions (cardiac, respiratory, vasomotor centers) and houses CN IX–XII nuclei. Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome — posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) occlusion — causes ipsilateral facial numbness, contralateral body loss of pain/temperature, dysphasia, and Horner syndrome.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Brodmann areas of the cortex?
facts.explanation: Brodmann areas are regions of the cerebral cortex defined by cytoarchitecture (cell layer composition and thickness). Key areas: 1, 2, 3 (primary somatosensory cortex, postcentral gyrus), 4 (primary motor cortex, precentral gyrus), 6 (premotor and supplementary motor cortex), 17 (primary visual cortex, calcarine sulcus), 22 (Wernicke's area in dominant hemisphere), 41/42 (primary auditory cortex), 44/45 (Broca's area in dominant hemisphere). Lesions of Broca's area (44/45) cause expressive aphasia; Wernicke's area (22) lesions cause receptive aphasia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the cerebellum?
facts.explanation: The cerebellum is located in the posterior cranial fossa and accounts for ~10% of brain weight but ~50% of neurons. It coordinates movement, balance, and motor learning via the spinocerebellar, vestibulocerebellar, and corticocerebellar circuits. It is organized into three lobes (anterior, posterior, flocculonodular) and three functional divisions: vestibulocerebellum (balance), spinocerebellum (limb and trunk coordination), and cerebrocerebellum (planning, fine motor). Cerebellar lesions cause ipsilateral deficits: ataxia, dysmetria (past-pointing), dysdiadochokinesia, intention tremor, and nystagmus (DANISH mnemonic).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the cerebral lobes?
facts.explanation: The cerebral cortex is divided into four lobes: frontal (anterior to central sulcus — motor cortex, prefrontal cortex, Broca's area), parietal (posterior to central sulcus — somatosensory cortex, spatial processing), temporal (below lateral sulcus — auditory cortex, Wernicke's area, memory), and occipital (posterior pole — primary and association visual cortex). The insula is a fifth lobe hidden within the lateral sulcus. Middle cerebral artery (MCA) stroke is the most common stroke syndrome, affecting the lateral cortex (frontal, parietal, temporal) and causing contralateral hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, and aphasia (dominant hemisphere).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the cerebrum?
facts.explanation: The cerebrum is the largest part of the brain (~85% of total brain weight), consisting of two hemispheres connected by the corpus callosum. The cerebral cortex (gray matter, ~3 mm thick) contains 6 layers in most regions (neocortex). Gyri are folds; sulci are grooves; fissures are deep sulci. The longitudinal fissure divides the hemispheres. The left hemisphere is dominant for language in ~95% of right-handed individuals. Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM), the most aggressive primary brain tumor, most commonly arises in the cerebral hemispheres.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows CNS glial cells?
facts.explanation: CNS glial cells include: astrocytes (star-shaped, blood-brain barrier maintenance, scar formation, metabolic support — GFAP marker), oligodendrocytes (myelinate multiple CNS axons — each oligodendrocyte myelinates up to 30 axons; destroyed in multiple sclerosis), microglia (resident CNS macrophages derived from mesoderm, surveillance and phagocytosis), and ependymal cells (line ventricles, produce and circulate CSF). Gliomas (astrocytoma, glioblastoma, oligodendroglioma) are the most common primary brain tumors. Reactive astrogliosis forms glial scars after CNS injury.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the cranial nerves?
facts.explanation: The 12 cranial nerves (CN I–XII) emerge directly from the brain and brainstem. Mnemonic for their nature (S=sensory, M=motor, B=both): 'Some Say Marry Money But My Brother Says Big Brains Matter More' (CN I–XII: S,S,M,M,B,B,B,S,B,M,B,B). Key clinical correlations: CN III palsy (posterior communicating artery aneurysm, herniation — 'down and out' eye with ptosis and mydriasis); CN VII palsy (Bell's palsy, Lyme disease, parotid tumor); CN X (vagus, parasympathetic to thoracoabdominal organs).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the diencephalon?
facts.explanation: The diencephalon consists of the thalamus, hypothalamus, epithalamus (pineal gland, habenula), and subthalamus. The thalamus is the relay station for all sensory information (except smell) going to the cortex; it also relays motor information from the basal ganglia and cerebellum. The hypothalamus controls the autonomic nervous system, endocrine function (via pituitary), temperature regulation, appetite, and circadian rhythms. The pineal gland produces melatonin for sleep-wake cycles. Parinaud's syndrome (vertical gaze palsy, loss of accommodation, convergence nystagmus) results from pineal mass compressing the superior colliculus.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a myelinated neuron?
facts.explanation: Myelinated neurons have their axons wrapped by myelin sheaths, formed by Schwann cells (PNS) or oligodendrocytes (CNS). Nodes of Ranvier are gaps between adjacent myelin segments where the axon membrane is exposed and voltage-gated Na+ channels are concentrated, enabling saltatory conduction (signal jumps node to node), greatly increasing conduction velocity. Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune demyelinating disease destroying oligodendrocytes in the CNS, causing plaques of demyelination with slowed conduction. Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) demyelinates peripheral nerves.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the nerve plexuses?
facts.explanation: The major nerve plexuses are formed by the ventral rami of spinal nerves: cervical plexus (C1–C4 — phrenic nerve to diaphragm, cutaneous neck/shoulder), brachial plexus (C5–T1 — all upper limb innervation), lumbar plexus (L1–L4 — femoral, obturator nerves), and sacral plexus (L4–S3 — sciatic nerve, largest nerve in the body). Erb's palsy (upper trunk C5–C6 injury — 'waiter's tip') and Klumpke's palsy (lower trunk C8–T1 — claw hand, Horner syndrome) are brachial plexus injuries seen in birth trauma.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows peripheral nerve structure?
facts.explanation: A peripheral nerve is organized into nested layers: individual nerve fibers (axons with myelin) wrapped by endoneurium; bundles of fibers (fascicles) wrapped by perineurium; and the entire nerve wrapped by epineurium. The perineurium forms a blood-nerve barrier analogous to the blood-brain barrier. Nerve injuries are classified by Seddon's system: neuropraxia (conduction block, intact axon — best prognosis), axonotmesis (axon disrupted, endoneurium intact — Wallerian degeneration but can regenerate), neurotmesis (complete nerve transection — requires surgical repair).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a neuron?
facts.explanation: A neuron consists of a cell body (soma, containing nucleus and organelles), dendrites (short branched processes receiving inputs — contain Nissl bodies/rough ER), and a single axon (conducts impulses away from soma, wrapped in myelin). The axon hillock is where action potentials are initiated (lowest threshold). Synaptic terminals at the axon endings release neurotransmitters. Neurons cannot regenerate after death (with limited exceptions in olfactory epithelium and hippocampus). ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis) destroys both upper and lower motor neurons, causing progressive weakness without sensory loss.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the different types of neurons?
facts.explanation: Neurons are classified by number of processes: unipolar (one process — dorsal root ganglion sensory neurons in adults, really pseudounipolar with one process that divides into central and peripheral branches), bipolar (two processes — retinal bipolar cells, vestibular and cochlear neurons), and multipolar (one axon + multiple dendrites — most CNS neurons, motor neurons). They can also be classified functionally: sensory (afferent), motor (efferent), and interneurons (association). Interneurons constitute ~99% of all neurons.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows PNS glial cells?
facts.explanation: PNS glial cells include Schwann cells and satellite cells. Schwann cells myelinate peripheral axons (one Schwann cell per myelin segment, unlike oligodendrocytes which myelinate multiple axons). They also guide peripheral nerve regeneration after injury. Satellite cells surround neuron cell bodies in dorsal root and autonomic ganglia, providing metabolic support. Schwannomas (benign Schwann cell tumors) most commonly affect the vestibulocochlear nerve (acoustic neuroma / CN VIII), causing unilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Neurofibromatosis type 1 (von Recklinghausen's) involves multiple peripheral nerve neurofibromas (Schwann cell + fibroblast).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows a spinal cord cross-section?
facts.explanation: The spinal cord cross-section has a butterfly-shaped central gray matter (H-shape) surrounded by white matter. The gray matter dorsal horns contain sensory neurons; ventral horns contain motor neurons (alpha and gamma motor neurons); lateral horns (T1–L2 and S2–S4) contain autonomic preganglionic neurons. White matter columns carry ascending (sensory) and descending (motor) tracts. Key tracts: dorsal columns (ipsilateral fine touch, proprioception), spinothalamic tract (contralateral pain/temperature), corticospinal tract (ipsilateral motor). Brown-Séquard syndrome (hemisection) causes ipsilateral motor loss + proprioception loss with contralateral pain/temperature loss below the lesion.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows an astrocyte?
facts.explanation: Astrocytes are the most abundant cells in the CNS, identifiable by their star-like shape with numerous radiating processes. Functions: (1) Blood-brain barrier maintenance — astrocytic endfeet cover >99% of brain capillary surface; (2) Neurotransmitter recycling (glutamate-glutamine cycle, GABA uptake); (3) Potassium buffering; (4) Metabolic support via lactate shuttle; (5) Reactive astrogliosis and glial scar formation after injury. GFAP (glial fibrillary acidic protein) is the astrocyte marker. Astrocytomas (WHO grade I–IV) range from pilocytic astrocytoma (benign, children) to glioblastoma multiforme (GBM, most malignant).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the cerebellum (Servier illustration)?
facts.explanation: The cerebellum ('little brain') lies in the posterior fossa and connects to the brainstem via three cerebellar peduncles: superior (output to thalamus/red nucleus), middle (input from pons/cortex), and inferior (input/output from medulla/spinal cord). Its cortex has three layers: molecular, Purkinje cell (only output), and granular. Purkinje cells project inhibitory signals to deep cerebellar nuclei (dentate, interposed, fastigial). The cerebellum fine-tunes movement by comparing intended vs. actual motor output (comparator function). Medulloblastoma, a malignant tumor arising from cerebellar germinal matrix, is the most common brain tumor in children.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the Circle of Willis (Servier illustration)?
facts.explanation: The Circle of Willis ensures collateral cerebral blood supply by connecting the anterior and posterior circulations. The anterior circulation (internal carotid arteries → ACAs via ACoA) supplies the frontal, parietal, and medial temporal lobes. The posterior circulation (vertebral arteries → basilar artery → PCAs via PCoAs) supplies the occipital lobe, brainstem, and cerebellum. Anatomical variants are common — a complete circle is present in only ~20–25% of individuals. PCoA aneurysm is a classic cause of a painful CN III palsy (blown pupil) — an emergency requiring neurosurgical intervention.

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the CSF circulation pathway?
facts.explanation: CSF (~150 mL total, produced at ~500 mL/day) is produced by choroid plexus in the lateral ventricles, flows through the interventricular foramina of Monro into the third ventricle, through the cerebral aqueduct (aqueduct of Sylvius) into the fourth ventricle, through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie into the subarachnoid space, and is reabsorbed by arachnoid granulations into the dural venous sinuses. CSF pressure is ~10–15 cmH2O. Communicating hydrocephalus results from impaired reabsorption; non-communicating (obstructive) from obstruction within the ventricular system (e.g., aqueductal stenosis).

facts.quizQuestion: Which image shows the sciatic nerve?
facts.explanation: The sciatic nerve (L4–S3) is the largest and longest peripheral nerve in the body (~2 cm wide at its origin). It exits the greater sciatic foramen below the piriformis muscle, descends through the posterior thigh, and divides at the popliteal fossa into the tibial nerve (innervates posterior leg and sole) and common fibular (peroneal) nerve (innervates anterior and lateral leg and dorsum of foot). Piriformis syndrome compresses the sciatic nerve, causing sciatica. The most common cause of true sciatica is lumbar disc herniation (most commonly L4/L5 or L5/S1 levels), compressing the nerve root.
